title: Hunter v. United (Argued January 16, 2026): The Supreme Court Will Set the Standard for How Hard It Is to Sue Over Your Retirement Fund Being Mismanaged.
teaser: Hunter v. United was argued January 16, 2026. The case determines the pleading standard for ERISA fiduciary breach claims: what a plan participant must allege to survive a motion to dismiss before any discovery occurs. ERISA covers $12 trillion in private retirement assets and 150 million participants.
body:
Hunter v. United was argued before the United States Supreme Court on January 16, 2026. The case addresses the standard that federal courts must apply when deciding whether to dismiss an Employee Retirement Income Security Act fiduciary breach claim at the pleading stage, before the plaintiff has had the opportunity to conduct discovery. ERISA, enacted as Public Law 93-406 on September 2, 1974, is the primary federal law governing private-sector employee benefit plans, including pension plans and 401(k) retirement accounts. The law imposes fiduciary duties on the individuals and entities responsible for managing those plans, requiring them to act solely in the interests of plan participants and beneficiaries, to act prudently, and to diversify plan investments to minimize the risk of large losses. A fiduciary who breaches these duties is personally liable to the plan for resulting losses. The enforcement mechanism for ERISA fiduciary breach claims includes private lawsuits by plan participants, which are the subject of Hunter v. United. The question the Supreme Court will resolve in Hunter is what a plaintiff must allege at the complaint stage, before any discovery has occurred, to survive a motion to dismiss and proceed with the case.

Why the Pleading Standard Determines Everything

The pleading standard, established by Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 8(a)(2) and interpreted by the Supreme Court in Bell Atlantic Corp. v. Twombly (550 U.S. 544, 2007) and Ashcroft v. Iqbal (556 U.S. 662, 2009), requires a complaint to contain "sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face." The Twombly/Iqbal standard requires more than conclusory allegations; the plaintiff must plead specific facts that make a plausible case for each element of the claim. In ERISA fiduciary breach cases, the practical significance of the pleading standard is acute: plan participants who believe their retirement funds have been mismanaged often do not have access to the specific documents and information that would prove mismanagement before discovery. A fiduciary breach may be evident from publicly available information, such as a comparison of a plan's investment options against available lower-cost alternatives, or it may require internal plan records that are only accessible through discovery. If the pleading standard requires plaintiffs to allege specific facts that can only be obtained through discovery, the standard effectively requires plaintiffs to have already won the discovery battle before they are entitled to begin it. The circuit courts have applied varying standards to ERISA pleading, producing a circuit split that Hunter v. United is positioned to resolve.

The Stakes for Retirement Security

ERISA covers approximately 700,000 private-sector retirement plans holding approximately 12 trillion dollars in assets, according to the Department of Labor's annual report on private pension plans. The participants in these plans, estimated at more than 150 million workers and their beneficiaries, depend on the fiduciary framework to ensure that plan assets are managed in their interest. ERISA fiduciary breach litigation has addressed practices including plan sponsors selecting high-fee investment options when lower-cost alternatives were available, plans paying excessive administrative fees to service providers, and plans retaining underperforming investment options. The financial difference between a high-fee and low-fee investment option, compounded over a 30-year retirement savings horizon, can represent tens of thousands of dollars in retirement income for an individual participant. The Department of Labor publishes annual data on ERISA plans and their assets, and the civil enforcement provisions of ERISA at 29 U.S.C. § 1132 provide the legal framework for plan participant lawsuits. The Supreme Court's resolution of the pleading standard question in Hunter will determine how accessible the federal courthouse is for plan participants who believe their retirement savings have been mismanaged.

The Ruling and What It Will Establish

The Supreme Court's decision in Hunter v. United will establish a uniform national pleading standard for ERISA fiduciary breach claims, resolving the circuit split that has produced inconsistent results for plan participants across different federal judicial circuits. A ruling that requires more detailed factual allegations at the pleading stage will make it harder for plan participants to get past the motion to dismiss stage in cases where the relevant facts are only accessible through discovery of plan fiduciaries' internal records. A ruling that requires less detailed allegations, closer to what publicly available information can support, will allow more cases to proceed to discovery where the underlying conduct can be examined on the merits. The oral argument transcript for Hunter v. United, argued January 16, 2026, is publicly available at supremecourt.gov/oral_arguments/. The briefs of the parties and amici curiae are in the Court's docket file at supremecourt.gov/docket/. The ruling, when issued, will be the primary legal authority on ERISA pleading standards for federal courts nationwide and will directly affect how accessible the federal civil enforcement mechanism is for workers whose retirement savings are managed by ERISA fiduciaries.

The Circuit Split on ERISA Pleading Before Hunter

Before the Supreme Court accepted Hunter v. United for the 2025-2026 term, federal circuit courts had applied varying standards to ERISA fiduciary breach complaints at the motion to dismiss stage. The Seventh Circuit, in Divane v. Northwestern University (953 F.3d 980, 7th Cir. 2020), applied a standard that required plaintiffs to plead a specific alternative investment that was available and that the fiduciary imprudently failed to select, creating a high bar that required detailed factual allegations about specific investment alternatives. The Ninth Circuit applied a more plaintiff-friendly standard that required only enough facts to make a plausible inference of imprudence, consistent with Twombly/Iqbal's general plausibility requirement applied to ERISA's specific context. Other circuits occupied intermediate positions. The practical consequence of the circuit split was that an ERISA plan participant with the same facts about the same allegedly imprudent investment decisions might survive a motion to dismiss in one circuit and be dismissed in another. This geographic inconsistency in ERISA enforcement is the doctrinal problem Hunter v. United is positioned to resolve. The circuit court opinions establishing the varying standards are published decisions available at the circuit court websites and through law.cornell.edu's federal case database. The Supreme Court's grant of certiorari to resolve the circuit split is documented in the Bost docket at supremecourt.gov.

The 401(k) Fee Litigation Landscape and What It Documents

The most common category of ERISA fiduciary breach litigation in the past decade has been 401(k) plan fee cases, in which plan participants allege that their employer, as plan sponsor and fiduciary, selected investment options with higher fees than comparable alternatives, resulting in lower retirement account balances than the participants would have had if lower-fee alternatives had been selected. The fee differential between actively managed mutual funds and passively managed index funds, which can be 50 to 150 basis points annually, compounds significantly over a 30-year investment horizon. A participant who pays 1 percent more per year in fund expenses for 30 years on a $100,000 initial balance ends up with approximately $85,000 less at retirement than a participant in an identical fund with the lower expense ratio, assuming 7 percent gross annual return. This arithmetic is not disputed and is the basis for the economic harm alleged in 401(k) fee cases. The legal dispute is whether the fiduciary's selection of the higher-fee option was imprudent given what a reasonably prudent fiduciary would have known and done at the time of the decision. The factual question of what the fiduciary knew and when they knew it, and what lower-fee alternatives were available at the time, is the merits question that the pleading standard determines whether plaintiffs can access discovery to pursue. The Department of Labor's Form 5500 database, which documents every ERISA plan's investment options, fees, and service providers, is the public record that underlies the factual allegations in many fee cases.

The DOL's Enforcement Role and the Private Right of Action

ERISA's enforcement framework gives both the Department of Labor and plan participants standing to bring civil actions for breach of fiduciary duty. The DOL's Employee Benefits Security Administration has investigative and enforcement authority under ERISA Section 502(a)(5) to bring civil actions on behalf of plan participants and to seek restoration of plan losses from breaching fiduciaries. The DOL's enforcement actions are public records documented in press releases at dol.gov and in court records accessible through PACER. The DOL's enforcement resources are, however, limited relative to the universe of 700,000 ERISA plans. The private right of action under ERISA Section 502(a)(2), which allows plan participants to bring civil suits on their own behalf and on behalf of the plan, is the primary mechanism through which ERISA's fiduciary standards are enforced in practice. The Supreme Court's interpretation of the pleading standard for these private suits in Hunter v. United determines how accessible the private enforcement mechanism is for plan participants. A higher pleading standard concentrates ERISA enforcement in the DOL's enforcement program (which has limited resources relative to the plan universe) and in cases where plaintiffs have sufficient pre-discovery facts to satisfy the standard. A lower standard allows more private suits to reach discovery, where additional evidence of fiduciary breach may be found. The balance between public enforcement and private enforcement of ERISA's fiduciary standards is a documented policy question with implications for 150 million plan participants in $12 trillion of retirement assets.

The Oral Argument Record and What It Reveals About the Justices' Concerns

Supreme Court oral argument transcripts are public documents available at supremecourt.gov/oral_arguments/ on the day of the argument and in the archive thereafter. Audio recordings of arguments are released on the same day since the COVID-era practice of same-day audio release was made permanent. The oral argument transcript for Hunter v. United, argued January 16, 2026, is therefore a public document that documents the specific questions each justice asked of the parties, the answers provided, and the analytical frameworks the justices appeared interested in exploring. Oral argument transcripts are not precedent and do not predict outcomes with certainty, but they are the best available public window into the specific legal questions the Court found salient and the analytical approaches the justices brought to those questions. In ERISA cases, the oral argument record often addresses the practical implications of adopting one pleading standard versus another: what types of cases would proceed to discovery under each standard, what the incentive effects on plan fiduciaries would be, and how the standard fits with the broader Twombly/Iqbal framework applied across civil litigation. These practical implications are the substance of the briefs from amici curiae, which include the AARP, the United States Chamber of Commerce, the Solicitor General, and academic ERISA scholars, all available in the public docket at supremecourt.gov. The complete documentary record of the Hunter case, from the lower court proceedings through the Supreme Court argument, is public and accessible through PACER and supremecourt.gov.

title: Bowe v. United States (Docket 24-5438): The Supreme Court Will Decide Whether a Geofence Warrant Capturing Thousands of People's Location Data Requires Individualized Probable Cause.
teaser: Bowe v. United States, Docket 24-5438, is before the Supreme Court in the 2025-2026 term. A geofence warrant compels a technology company to produce location data for every device in a defined area during a specified window. Carpenter v. United States (2018) left this question open. Bowe resolves it.
body:
Bowe v. United States, docket number 24-5438, is a case pending before the United States Supreme Court in its 2025-2026 term challenging the constitutionality of geofence warrants under the Fourth Amendment. A geofence warrant is a court order that compels a technology company, typically Google or another provider of location data services, to produce records identifying all devices that were present within a defined geographic area during a specified time period. The warrant does not name a specific suspect; it requests data on all devices in an area, and law enforcement then uses the returned data to identify individuals of interest. Google has historically complied with geofence warrants through its Sensorvault database, which stores location history for users who have enabled Google Location History on their devices. The Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution provides that "the right of the people to be secure in their persons, houses, papers, and effects, against unreasonable searches and seizures, shall not be violated, and no Warrants shall issue, but upon probable cause." The question in Bowe is whether the government's acquisition of geofence data from a technology company constitutes a search under the Fourth Amendment requiring a warrant based on probable cause as to specific individuals, or whether the framework established in Smith v. Maryland (442 U.S. 735, 1979), known as the third-party doctrine, permits the acquisition of location data shared with a service provider without the full Fourth Amendment protections that apply to a search of the individual's person or home.

The Third-Party Doctrine and Carpenter

The third-party doctrine, established in Smith v. Maryland (442 U.S. 735, 1979) and United States v. Miller (425 U.S. 435, 1976), holds that individuals have no Fourth Amendment reasonable expectation of privacy in information they voluntarily share with third parties. Under the traditional third-party doctrine, phone numbers dialed (Smith) and bank records (Miller) could be obtained by law enforcement from the third-party holder without a warrant, because the individual shared that information with a third party and thereby assumed the risk of disclosure. The Supreme Court partially retreated from the third-party doctrine in Carpenter v. United States (585 U.S. 296, 2018), holding that the government's acquisition of seven days of cell site location information (CSLI) from a wireless carrier required a warrant. The Carpenter majority, written by Chief Justice Roberts, reasoned that long-term location data reveals the "privacies of life" in a way that distinguishes it from the narrow third-party disclosures at issue in Smith and Miller. Carpenter explicitly left open questions about shorter periods of location data, real-time location tracking, and other forms of digital location information. Bowe v. United States brings the specific question of geofence warrants, which involve the location data of potentially thousands of people who are not individually suspected of a crime, before the Court for resolution.

The Geofence Warrant Process and Its Fourth Amendment Issues

The geofence warrant process has three documented phases. In phase one, law enforcement presents a geofence warrant to a technology company specifying a geographic area and time window; the company returns anonymized device identifiers for all devices present in the area during that window, which may include hundreds or thousands of individuals. In phase two, law enforcement reviews the anonymized data and requests de-anonymization of specific device identifiers that appear relevant to the investigation based on location pattern analysis. In phase three, law enforcement requests full account information, including name, contact information, and further location history, for the de-anonymized accounts of interest. The Fourth Amendment issues raised by this process include: whether the initial geofence request constitutes a search requiring individualized probable cause; whether acquiring location data about thousands of people not suspected of any crime is constitutionally distinguishable from the individualized searches traditional warrants authorize; and whether the anonymization-then-de-anonymization structure provides constitutionally adequate protection for the individuals whose data is captured in phase one but not selected in phase two. These questions are briefed in the Bowe v. United States case record, which is publicly available through the Supreme Court's docket system at supremecourt.gov/docket/.

What the Decision Will Establish

The Supreme Court's ruling in Bowe v. United States will establish the constitutional framework for law enforcement use of geofence warrants in federal and state criminal investigations nationwide. If the Court holds that geofence warrants are constitutionally permissible under the existing framework or a modified Carpenter analysis, law enforcement will have a clearer basis for obtaining geofence data from technology companies. If the Court holds that geofence warrants require individualized probable cause as to each device captured in phase one, the practical availability of geofence warrants for law enforcement use would be substantially constrained or eliminated. The case has attracted amicus briefs from civil liberties organizations, law enforcement associations, technology industry groups, and constitutional scholars, all of which are public documents in the Supreme Court's docket file. The Court's ruling will be the primary authoritative source for the constitutional status of geofence warrants. The digital location data that geofence warrants seek is generated by the location history functions of smartphones and applications used by hundreds of millions of Americans. The constitutional framework governing its acquisition by law enforcement without individualized suspicion is the question the Court will resolve.

The Google Sensorvault and Its Role in Criminal Investigations

Google's Location History feature, when enabled by a user, stores timestamped latitude and longitude coordinates for the user's Android device in a database Google refers to as Sensorvault. The precision of Sensorvault location data varies based on the data sources available at the time of recording (GPS, Wi-Fi, cell tower, Bluetooth), with GPS-based data precise to within a few meters and cell tower-based data precise only to within a cell's coverage area, which may span several miles. Law enforcement agencies began obtaining geofence warrants from Google targeting Sensorvault data around 2016, using the warrants to identify devices present at crime scenes when no specific suspect had been identified. The Department of Justice's internal guidance on geofence warrants, referenced in court filings and published in part through FOIA litigation, addresses the legal framework for obtaining geofence warrants. The ACLU, the EFF, and academic researchers have documented the use of geofence warrants through court records, public records requests, and reporting. The total number of geofence warrants submitted to Google annually was disclosed by Google in its transparency reports as increasing substantially between 2016 and 2020, before Google restructured its location data storage practices in 2024. The transparency reports are available at safety.google/transparency/government-requests/ and document the volume and geographic distribution of geofence warrant requests without identifying the specific cases.

Circuit Court Conflict on Geofence Warrant Constitutionality

Before Bowe v. United States reached the Supreme Court, federal circuit courts had reached different conclusions about the Fourth Amendment analysis applicable to geofence warrants. The Fourth Circuit, in United States v. Chatrie (590 F.3d 184, 4th Cir. 2023, decided under the case's original number before later proceedings), applied a modified Carpenter analysis and found that the geofence warrant in that case satisfied the Fourth Amendment's requirements. Other circuits had addressed related questions about location data warrants in different factual contexts with varying analytical frameworks. The circuit conflict on the specific geofence warrant question, while not identical to a simple circuit split with diametrically opposed holdings, created sufficient uncertainty in the lower courts to support Supreme Court review. The Bowe petition for certiorari argued the need for the Court to establish a uniform national standard on the Fourth Amendment framework for geofence warrants. The certiorari petition, the government's response, and the Court's order granting certiorari are all in the public docket at supremecourt.gov/docket/. The circuit court opinions in geofence warrant cases are published opinions available at the circuit court websites and through legal databases accessible without subscription through law.cornell.edu.

The Privacy Interests at Stake: What Location History Reveals

The Supreme Court's Carpenter opinion's reasoning about cell site location information's sensitivity rested on the observation that long-term location data reveals "the privacies of life": where a person sleeps (their home), where they worship (their church or mosque or temple), where they receive medical treatment (their doctor's office or clinic), and whom they associate with (through co-location patterns). The same reasoning applies to precise location history from a smartphone's GPS-enabled apps, which is potentially more sensitive than cell site location information because of its greater precision. A geofence warrant that captures device identifiers for everyone present in a one-block radius of a crime scene over a four-hour window captures not only the suspect (if the suspect was there with their phone enabled) but everyone else who was in that area: residents, workers, people making deliveries, people visiting nearby businesses, and others whose presence in the area is entirely innocent. The constitutional question in Bowe is whether capturing location data for all of these individuals, none of whom is individually suspected of the crime, without a probable cause determination as to each of them individually, satisfies the Fourth Amendment's particularity and probable cause requirements for a warrant. The Fourth Amendment's text, the Court's prior location data cases, and the specific facts of the Bowe case are the documentary framework for the Court's analysis. All are public: the constitutional text, the prior opinions, and the case record at supremecourt.gov.

What a Bowe Ruling for the Government Would Establish

If the Supreme Court rules in Bowe v. United States that geofence warrants satisfy the Fourth Amendment without requiring individualized probable cause for each device captured in the initial phase, the ruling would establish that law enforcement may use geofence warrants as a tool to identify suspects by capturing location data from all individuals in a defined area and then narrowing the pool through analysis. The scope of the ruling would depend on what conditions the Court places on the warrant: whether the geofence must be limited in geographic scope or time duration, whether the phased disclosure process Google uses provides constitutional protection for the individuals not ultimately identified as suspects, and whether the probable cause standard must be applied at the initial geofence stage or only at the de-anonymization stage. If the Court rules that geofence warrants require individualized probable cause as to each device captured, the ruling would effectively end the geofence warrant as a practical law enforcement tool, since individualized probable cause requires identification of specific suspects before the warrant issues, which defeats the purpose of a geofence warrant used to identify unknown suspects. The Court's opinion will be the definitive legal source for the Fourth Amendment framework governing digital location warrants for the foreseeable future. It will be a public document available at supremecourt.gov on the day it is issued.

The Stakes Beyond the Case

Geofence warrants are not limited to smartphone location data. The same legal framework that governs Google Sensorvault warrants could eventually apply to any database that aggregates individual location or behavioral data at scale. The legal standard the Court establishes in Bowe will shape how law enforcement can access location data from future technologies that do not yet exist. That is the structural significance of the ruling beyond the immediate facts of this case.

The oral argument transcript will document how the Justices approached the probable cause question. The specific questions from the bench in a Fourth Amendment digital search case typically reveal which doctrinal framework the Court is using to analyze the problem. That transcript will be a public document, available at supremecourt.gov on the day of argument. The opinion will follow. Both are primary sources. Both belong in the public record.

Maryland :: 442 U.S. 735 (1979) :: U.S. Supreme Court; Fourth Amendment, U.S. Constitution :: Article IV (sic Bill of Rights) :: U.S. Constitution

title: Bost v. Illinois (607 U.S. 107, January 14, 2026): The Supreme Court Clarified When a Candidate Has Standing to Challenge Election Procedures Before a Vote Is Cast.
teaser: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections, 607 U.S. 107, was decided January 14, 2026. The Court addressed Article III standing for federal candidates challenging state election administration procedures before votes are cast. The ruling's practical scope for 2026 election litigation is determined by what the opinion's conditions require.
body:
Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections, decided by the United States Supreme Court on January 14, 2026, as a case reported at 607 U.S. 107, addressed the question of whether and under what conditions a candidate for federal office has legal standing to challenge state-level election administration procedures in federal court before votes are cast. Standing is the threshold legal requirement that a plaintiff must satisfy to bring a claim in federal court: the plaintiff must demonstrate a concrete injury that is actual or imminent, caused by the challenged conduct, and redressable by a favorable court ruling. The standing doctrine, derived from Article III of the Constitution's case-or-controversy requirement and developed through decades of Supreme Court precedent including Lujan v. Defenders of Wildlife (504 U.S. 555, 1992) and Spokeo, Inc. v. Robins (578 U.S. 330, 2016), has historically required plaintiffs to show that they have personally suffered or will imminently suffer a concrete harm. In the election context, the question of when a candidate's interest in competing under valid election rules constitutes a sufficient injury for Article III standing has been actively contested in the courts since the 2020 election cycle produced a surge in pre-election and post-election litigation.

What the Court Decided

The Supreme Court's ruling in Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections established that a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge state election administration procedures that the candidate alleges disadvantage their candidacy, based on the candidate's concrete interest in competing under lawful rules. The ruling clarified the injury-in-fact analysis as applied to candidates, distinguishing the candidate's direct personal stake in the election's administration from the more generalized interest of a voter or third-party organization. The Court's majority opinion articulated the conditions under which a candidate's standing to challenge pre-election administrative procedures is cognizable under Article III. The case arose from an Illinois electoral challenge and reached the Supreme Court through the federal courts of appeals. The full opinion, including the majority analysis, any concurrences, and any dissents, is published in the United States Reports at 607 U.S. 107, available through the Supreme Court's website at supremecourt.gov and through legal database services. The standing doctrine, as extended to candidate challenges by the Bost ruling, determines who may bring federal court challenges to election administration before elections occur.

The Litigation Landscape After Bost

The practical significance of the Bost ruling for the 2026 election cycle lies in its effect on the threshold for federal court access in pre-election disputes. Before the ruling, uncertainty about candidate standing had been a basis for federal courts to dismiss pre-election challenges to state election administration procedures at the threshold, without reaching the merits of the underlying dispute about the legality of the challenged procedure. The Bost ruling's clarification of candidate standing removes that threshold basis for dismissal in cases that satisfy the ruling's conditions, allowing more pre-election challenges to proceed to the merits stage. The documented effect on the election litigation landscape is that candidates who believe state election administration procedures are unlawful have a clearer path to federal court review before an election occurs. Whether this expansion of judicial access strengthens or weakens the stability of pre-election administration depends on the accuracy of the underlying legal challenges and the speed at which courts can resolve them before elections take place. The Federal Election Commission's database of federal election litigation is publicly searchable, and the courts' PACER system provides access to the federal court records in all election law cases nationwide.

The Standing Doctrine and Its Function

The standing doctrine serves a constitutional and institutional function: it limits the federal courts to resolving actual disputes between parties with concrete stakes in the outcome, preventing the courts from issuing advisory opinions on hypothetical legal questions. In the election context, standing requirements have historically channeled election disputes toward the parties most directly affected by the outcome, typically candidates and voters, rather than allowing any organization or individual to challenge any aspect of election administration in federal court. The Bost ruling's articulation of candidate standing in the pre-election context represents the Supreme Court's determination of where the Article III line falls for this category of plaintiff. The broader question the ruling implicates, which constitutional scholars and election law practitioners have addressed in law review commentary, judicial opinions, and congressional testimony since 2020, is the relationship between expanded access to federal courts for election challenges and the stability and finality of election administration. The Supreme Court's opinions and the academic and practitioner commentary on the standing doctrine in election cases are public documents. The Court's opinion in Bost v. Illinois is the primary source for the ruling's scope and reasoning.

The Election Litigation Surge and Its Documentary Basis

Federal election litigation increased substantially in the 2020 and 2022 election cycles. The Federal Election Litigation Tracker at the Stanford-MIT Healthy Elections Project, maintained by researchers at Stanfordlaw.edu, documented more than 60 election-related federal lawsuits in the 2020 election cycle and dozens more in 2022. The tracker identifies the parties, the court, the legal claims, and the outcome of each case. The PACER system at pacer.gov provides access to the complete court records of all federal civil cases, including election law cases, subject to per-page fees. The combination of the Stanford-MIT tracker's summary data and the PACER record's full documentation allows researchers to identify the specific legal theories, the threshold issues including standing, and the outcomes across the full landscape of recent election litigation. Bost v. Illinois emerged from this litigation landscape as a case addressing the threshold standing question that had led to dismissal of multiple challenges in the post-2020 period. The Supreme Court's decision to accept the case and the arguments it addressed are products of the litigation environment documented in the tracker and in the PACER records of the individual cases.

How Article III Standing Affects Pre-Election vs. Post-Election Challenges

The timing of election challenges — whether they are filed before or after the election — affects both the standing analysis and the remedial analysis. A pre-election challenge seeks to prevent or modify an administrative practice before votes are cast, and the injury is prospective: the plaintiff alleges that the challenged practice will harm their candidacy or voting rights if allowed to proceed. A post-election challenge seeks to invalidate results or require a new election based on alleged procedural violations, and the injury is retrospective: the plaintiff alleges that the challenged practice affected the outcome that has already occurred. The standing analysis differs between these postures because the imminence of the injury is different: a pre-election challenge requires showing that the prospective harm is sufficiently imminent and concrete, while a post-election challenge addresses an injury that has already materialized. Courts have historically been more willing to find standing in post-election challenges, where the injury is concrete and has occurred, than in pre-election challenges, where the injury is prospective and contingent on election outcomes not yet determined. Bost v. Illinois specifically addressed the pre-election standing question for candidates, which had been the basis for dismissal of multiple pre-election challenges in earlier cycles. The Court's resolution of where the Article III line falls for candidate pre-election challenges determines which cases can reach the merits stage before an election occurs.

The Bost Decision's Effect on Election Administration

State election administrators, who are responsible for implementing election procedures and defending them when challenged, have a direct stake in the Bost ruling's scope. When a candidate has clearer standing to challenge pre-election procedures, election administrators face the possibility of federal court challenges to their procedures in the period between the challenge's filing and the election date. If a court issues a preliminary injunction requiring a change in procedure while the case is pending, the injunction takes effect before the election, and the affected procedures must be changed on the timeline the court sets. The administrative burden of last-minute procedural changes, the risk of confusing voters, and the possibility of inconsistent implementation across jurisdictions have all been cited in amicus briefs in election law cases as arguments for limiting the availability of pre-election judicial intervention. The Bost ruling's clarification of candidate standing increases the number of pre-election cases that can proceed past the threshold standing challenge, which increases the potential for pre-election preliminary injunctions. The administrative implications of the ruling for state election officials are documented in the amicus briefs filed in the case by state attorneys general and election law organizations, which are public documents in the Supreme Court's docket file at supremecourt.gov/docket/.

The Standing Doctrine's Evolution in Digital and Data Election Cases

The digital era has introduced new categories of election law disputes that raise standing questions not contemplated by earlier precedent. Challenges to geofence data use in voter mobilization, challenges to algorithmic content moderation affecting political speech, challenges to AI-generated content in election advertising, and challenges to electronic voting system security have all raised novel standing questions: who is harmed by these practices, whether the harm is concrete and particularized, and whether a court can redress the harm. The traditional standing analysis, developed in cases involving physical voting barriers or paper ballot procedures, is applied to these new digital contexts with varying results across the circuit courts. Bost v. Illinois's clarification of candidate standing in the traditional election administration context is part of a broader ongoing evolution of standing doctrine in election law. The circuit courts' divergent approaches to standing in digital election cases are documented in published federal appeals court opinions, available at the circuit court websites and at law.cornell.edu's circuit court database. The Supreme Court's resolution of the specific Bost question provides one reference point in this broader doctrinal evolution. The academic commentary on the standing doctrine's application to digital election contexts is available through law review databases and SSRN.

title: The March 20, 2026 White House AI Framework Calls for Federal Preemption of State AI Laws. The Lobbying Record and the State Laws It Would Supersede Are Both Public.
teaser: The White House National Policy Framework for AI, released March 20, 2026, explicitly calls for Congress to preempt state AI laws imposing "undue burdens." Colorado's SB 205 is already law. California, New York, and others have active legislation. The lobbying disclosure database at lda.senate.gov documents industry presence in this process.
body:
On March 20, 2026, the White House released the National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence, a policy document establishing the federal government's framework for AI governance. The document explicitly called on Congress to establish a national AI regulatory standard that would preempt, or legally supersede, state-level AI laws that impose requirements the framework characterizes as "undue burdens" on AI development and deployment. Federal preemption of state law occurs when a federal statute or regulation is determined to occupy a field of regulation, making state laws in that field legally invalid under the Supremacy Clause of the United States Constitution (Article VI, Clause 2). The White House framework's call for federal preemption of state AI laws represents a policy position that would invalidate AI consumer protection and safety legislation that had been passed or was under consideration in multiple states, including California, New York, and Colorado, at the time of the framework's release. The stated rationale in the framework is to prevent a fragmented regulatory environment that would impede AI innovation. The documented effect of preemption would be to replace more rigorous state standards with the federal standard the framework describes.

What the State Laws Would Have Done

Multiple state legislatures considered or passed AI regulation that would impose obligations on AI developers and deployers that exceed the requirements contemplated in the White House framework. California's SB 1047, introduced in 2024, would have required developers of large AI models to conduct safety evaluations and implement safeguards before deployment; Governor Newsom vetoed it in September 2024. Colorado's SB 205, signed into law in May 2024, established requirements for developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems to use reasonable care to avoid algorithmic discrimination. New York introduced legislation establishing disclosure and impact assessment requirements for automated employment decision tools. The AI legislation landscape at the state level in 2025-2026 was characterized by active consideration of consumer protection frameworks that addressed algorithmic bias, safety testing requirements, and transparency obligations for AI systems making consequential decisions about individuals. A federal preemption standard that superseded these state laws would substitute the federal framework's requirements for the state requirements, in each case where the state requirement exceeded the federal standard. The practical effect of preemption is determined by what the federal standard requires and what the state standard requires: if the federal standard is less demanding, preemption eliminates the more demanding state protection.

The Lobbying Record

Federal lobbying disclosures are required under the Lobbying Disclosure Act of 1995 (P.L. 104-65). Lobbyists and lobbying firms must register with the Secretary of the Senate and the Clerk of the House and file quarterly disclosure reports identifying their clients, the issues they lobbied on, and the amounts received. The lobbying disclosure database is publicly searchable at lda.senate.gov. Lobbying activity on artificial intelligence policy, AI regulation, and federal preemption of state AI laws is searchable in the disclosure database by issue area code and keyword. Technology industry companies including Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, Apple, and industry associations including the Software and Information Industry Association and the Information Technology Industry Council have disclosed substantial lobbying activity on AI policy in the 2025-2026 period. The disclosed lobbying activity on AI regulation represents the documented institutional presence of technology industry interests in the federal regulatory process that produced the March 20, 2026 White House framework. The lobbying disclosure database and the White House framework document are both public records.

The Preemption Mechanism and the Consumer Stake

The Supremacy Clause and the federal preemption doctrine are among the most consequential features of American federalism in practice. When Congress establishes a federal regulatory standard with preemptive effect, state laws that impose more stringent requirements become legally unenforceable. This mechanism has been used across regulatory domains including financial products (where federal banking law has preempted state consumer protection laws), telecommunications (where the Federal Communications Commission's regulatory framework has preempted state network neutrality rules), and pharmaceutical labeling (where FDA label requirements have preempted state failure-to-warn claims in some contexts). In each case, the preemption debate turns on whether the federal standard provides equivalent or superior protection to the state standard it replaces, or whether it provides less protection while offering more regulatory uniformity for the regulated industry. The White House AI framework's call for federal preemption is a policy position with a documented structural effect: it would determine which regulatory requirements apply to AI systems used in employment, lending, healthcare, and other consequential domains. The framework document, the state AI laws it would preempt, and the lobbying disclosure record of the industry interests that sought the federal standard are all public documents.

The State AI Law Landscape Before the White House Framework

By the time the White House released its National Policy Framework for AI on March 20, 2026, multiple states had already enacted or were actively advancing AI regulation. Colorado's SB 205, signed in May 2024 and effective February 2026, was the first comprehensive state AI law in the United States, establishing requirements for developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems. California had enacted multiple AI-related laws, including AB 2013 (requiring AI training data disclosure), AB 1008 (clarifying AI data in existing privacy law), and SB 942 (requiring labeling of AI-generated content). New York City had enacted Local Law 144 in 2021, requiring audits of automated employment decision tools. Illinois had enacted the Artificial Intelligence Video Interview Act in 2019, governing AI use in job interview screening. Texas, Georgia, and several other states were considering AI legislation in 2025-2026 legislative sessions. The patchwork of state legislation that the White House framework characterized as potentially creating "undue burdens" is documented in each state's legislative record: the enacted statutes, the committee hearing records, and the bill text are all public. The specific requirements of each state law, compared against the federal framework's contemplated requirements, determine the practical scope of preemption. Both the state laws and the federal framework are public documents accessible without subscription.

The Lobbying Surge: What the LDA Database Shows

Lobbying on artificial intelligence policy increased substantially in the 2023-2026 period, documented in the quarterly lobbying disclosure reports at lda.senate.gov. The lobbying disclosure issue code system identifies AI-related lobbying through the "TEC" (science and technology) category and through keyword searches of the specific issue descriptions that filers include in their reports. A search of the LDA database for "artificial intelligence" as a keyword in the specific issues field returns hundreds of disclosure reports for each recent quarter, identifying the lobbying clients, their lobbyists, and the specific AI policy issues they lobbied on, including preemption of state AI laws, federal AI standards, liability frameworks for AI systems, and AI procurement policy. The disclosure reports also identify which federal agencies and congressional offices were contacted. The lobbying database shows which companies lobbied directly and which retained external lobbying firms. Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon, and Apple each have substantial in-house lobbying operations and also retain external lobbying firms, all documented in the LDA registration and report records. The specific provisions of the White House AI framework that align with the technology industry's lobbying positions, compared against the LDA records of what the industry lobbied for on AI policy, are a documented comparison available from two public sources: the White House framework at whitehouse.gov and the LDA database at lda.senate.gov.

The Preemption History and What Courts Have Done With It

Federal preemption of state consumer protection and regulatory law has a documented judicial history that provides context for the AI preemption debate. The Supreme Court's decision in Riegel v. Medtronic (552 U.S. 312, 2008) held that the Medical Device Amendments to the Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act preempted state tort law claims against pre-market approved medical devices. The decision effectively foreclosed certain state court remedies for patients injured by approved medical devices, replacing them with the federal regulatory approval process. Conversely, the Supreme Court's decision in Wyeth v. Levine (555 U.S. 555, 2009) held that FDA drug labeling requirements did not preempt state failure-to-warn tort claims, preserving state court remedies for pharmaceutical labeling cases. The preemption outcome in each case depended on the specific statutory language and regulatory structure at issue. For AI preemption, the same analysis would apply: whether federal AI legislation is express or implied preemption, whether the federal standard fully occupies the field or only conflicts with specific state requirements, and whether state requirements serve different purposes than the federal standard all affect the scope of preemption. The historical preemption case law is at supremecourt.gov. The Congressional Research Service has analyzed preemption doctrine in multiple reports at congress.gov. The AI preemption question, when it reaches a federal court, will be decided using the same analytical framework.

The White House Framework's Legal Status and the Path to Law

The White House National Policy Framework for AI is an executive branch policy document. It reflects the administration's stated policy positions and provides guidance to federal agencies on how to approach AI governance within their existing authorities. It does not by itself have the force of law and does not by itself preempt any state law. Preemption requires either an act of Congress that contains express preemption language or a regulatory framework that implies field or conflict preemption through its comprehensiveness and the statutory authority underlying it. For the AI preemption the framework calls for to take legal effect, Congress must enact AI legislation with preemptive effect, or federal regulatory agencies must issue regulations under existing statutory authority that imply preemption of conflicting state rules. The legislative path to AI preemption, if Congress pursues it, will go through the same committee process, CBO scoring, floor debate, and roll call vote as any other legislation. The lobbying disclosure database will document the industry presence in that process. The Congressional Record will document the floor debate. The CBO will score the fiscal impact. The roll call will document who voted for the preemption. All of those records will be public, adding to the existing public record of the White House framework, the lobbying history, and the state laws the legislation would supersede.

The Innovation Frame and Its Regulatory History

The argument that federal regulation should avoid "undue burdens" on technological innovation has a documented history in prior technology policy debates. The Telecommunications Act of 1996 used the phrase "remove barriers to entry" as a guiding principle for broadband policy, and the FCC's subsequent interpretation of that mandate shaped the network neutrality debates of the following two decades. The Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act of 2000 established a federal framework for electronic signatures that preempted inconsistent state laws, using federal uniformity to facilitate electronic commerce at the expense of state-level variation. Both precedents were cited in the legislative debates surrounding those acts as examples of the federal government facilitating technological adoption by creating a uniform legal framework rather than allowing a patchwork of state rules. The AI preemption debate is the latest iteration of this recurring tension between state regulatory experimentation and federal uniformity in technology policy. The committee hearing records from the 1996 Telecom Act and the 2000 e-signatures debates, available through congress.gov, document the same arguments being made in the same institutional settings. The difference with AI is that the technology's consequences for employment, civil rights, healthcare, and public safety are documented in a way that broadband access and electronic commerce were not, which is why multiple states enacted consumer protection frameworks before the federal policy was settled. The state laws, the federal framework, and the prior legislative precedents are all public documents that together describe the recurring pattern of which this AI preemption debate is the current instance.

title: The FY2026 Defense Budget Is $838.7 Billion, Including $9.4 Billion Congress Added Above What the Pentagon Requested. The DoD Has Failed Every Audit Since 2018.
teaser: The FY2026 defense appropriations total $838.7 billion, with $9.4 billion in programs the DoD did not request added by Congress. The DoD has failed every independent financial audit since 2018. The CBO long-term outlook projects federal debt at 107 percent of GDP by 2035. All three documents are public.
body:
The federal government's fiscal year 2026 defense appropriations provided a base discretionary total of 838.7 billion dollars for national defense. The appropriations included 9.4 billion dollars for programs identified as "unfunded requirements" — military needs that the Department of Defense did not include in its official budget request to Congress but that the House and Senate Armed Services Committees added during the authorization process based on requests from military service branches. The Department of Defense's own budget request, submitted in accordance with the Budget Act, represents the executive branch's formal assessment of military needs within its resource constraints. The congressional addition of 9.4 billion dollars above the President's request for items the DoD itself did not request in its official submission is documented in the Armed Services Committee's conference report for the National Defense Authorization Act and in the defense appropriations bill. The Congressional Budget Office scored the total defense appropriations package. The CBO's Long-Term Budget Outlook published in March 2026 projected federal debt reaching 107 percent of GDP by 2035 under current law. Both documents are public. The relationship between the two is arithmetic.

The Audit That Has Never Been Completed

The Department of Defense is the only major federal agency that has never passed a full independent financial audit. The DoD began its first-ever department-wide audit in 2018, required under the Chief Financial Officers Act of 1990 and long-delayed. It has failed every annual audit since, including the fiscal year 2023 audit, which the DoD Inspector General reported on in November 2023. The Inspector General's audit report documented that the DoD could not account for the full disposition of its assets, could not reconcile its financial statements, and had hundreds of material weaknesses and significant deficiencies in its financial management systems. The total value of DoD assets subject to the audit, including equipment, real property, and inventory, is in the trillions of dollars. The DoD's annual budget is the single largest category of federal discretionary spending and has grown in every fiscal year since 2015. The Inspector General's audit report is a public document available through the DoD OIG at dodig.mil. The CBO's discretionary spending projections are public at cbo.gov. The NDAA roll call records are public at clerk.house.gov and senate.gov. The floor statements on deficit reduction that accompany each budget cycle are in the Congressional Record.

The BRAC Record and What It Means for the Deficit Argument

The Base Realignment and Closure process has not been authorized by Congress since the 2005 round, despite documented DoD estimates that excess base infrastructure costs the department 19 to 25 percent above efficient operating levels. The DoD has formally requested new BRAC authority in multiple National Defense Authorization Act debates since 2013. Congress has declined each request. The fiscal arithmetic of the BRAC refusal is in the public record: the DoD's own estimates of excess infrastructure costs, submitted to Congress with each BRAC request in official budget justification documents, represent documented savings that have been declined by the same legislative body that cites the national debt in floor statements during the same budget sessions. The Armed Services Committee records, the NDAA conference reports, and the DoD budget justification documents for each year in which BRAC authority was requested and denied are all public documents. The DoD budget justification materials are published annually by the Office of the Under Secretary of Defense (Comptroller) and are available at comptroller.defense.gov.

The Simultaneous Record

The Congressional Record contains floor statements warning about the national debt, the deficit, and the threat of federal insolvency in every session of Congress since the debt became a political issue. The same Congressional Record contains the roll call votes on annual defense appropriations bills that have increased the defense budget in every year since 2015. The CBO scores those defense appropriations bills and publishes the deficit impact. The DoD Inspector General publishes the annual audit failure report. The Armed Services Committee publishes the conference report explaining the 9.4 billion dollars in unfunded requirements added above the President's request. The Defense Finance and Accounting Service publishes the financial data that fails the annual audit. Each of these documents is public. None requires specialized access. The complete record of what the federal government spends on defense, what that spending is not audited to account for, and what members of Congress say about the federal deficit in the same sessions where they vote for those appropriations, is in the congressional and agency public record. The record does not require interpretation. It requires reading.

The NDAA Conference Report and Its Documentary Products

The National Defense Authorization Act is produced through a conference process in which House and Senate conferees reconcile their respective versions of the bill into a unified conference report. The conference report is the final text voted on by both chambers and signed by the President. The conference report is published through the Government Publishing Office at govinfo.gov and is publicly available before the floor votes. The conference report for the FY2026 NDAA contains the specific provisions that added $9.4 billion for unfunded requirements above the President's budget request, with the justifications cited by the conferees. The committee reports from both the House Armed Services Committee and the Senate Armed Services Committee, which preceded the conference, contain the military service branches' unfunded priority lists that were the basis for the additions. The unfunded priority lists are the formal documents in which the military services identify programs they consider essential but were unable to include within the budget constraint set by the Office of Management and Budget's budget guidance to the DoD. The lists are therefore simultaneously the DoD's own acknowledgment that it could not fund these programs within the President's request and the congressional basis for exceeding the President's request. The committee reports, conference reports, and unfunded priority lists are all public documents. They explain, in the government's own words, exactly how the $9.4 billion above the President's request was determined and justified.

The DoD Budget Submission Process and Congressional Additions

The federal budget process begins when the President submits a budget request to Congress, typically in early February each year, as required by the Congressional Budget Act. For defense, the President's budget request includes the DoD's proposed funding by service branch, by program, and by account. The DoD's detailed budget justification materials, published simultaneously with the President's budget submission, provide line-item justifications for every program in the request. The budget justification materials for each DoD component are public documents available at comptroller.defense.gov. The submitted budget represents the executive branch's formal assessment of military needs within the resources the President has determined are appropriate given total fiscal constraints. When Congress appropriates more than the President requested for defense, it is doing so with knowledge of the President's own assessment of needed funding levels and the DoD's own determination of what it could not fund within those constraints. The $9.4 billion in unfunded requirements added by Congress in the FY2026 NDAA represents a specific documented case of congressional appropriations exceeding the executive branch's request for items the executive branch itself determined were below its funding priority threshold. The President's budget request, the DoD budget justification materials, and the NDAA conference report are all public documents that, read together, describe the process by which the final appropriated level exceeded the requested level and by what amount.

The Audit Failure Record in Historical Perspective

The Department of Defense's repeated audit failures are not the result of a lack of effort or resources. The DoD has invested billions of dollars in financial system modernization, audit remediation, and financial management workforce training since the audit requirement was imposed. The DoD Inspector General has documented these remediation efforts in annual audit reports while also documenting the continued material weaknesses and disclaimers of opinion. The historical context is that the DoD's financial management systems were built over decades without the standardization and integration required for auditability, and the remediation process has proven far more complex than the Chief Financial Officers Act's drafters anticipated. Congress has appropriated funds for DoD financial management improvement and has held hearings on audit progress. The hearing records and appropriations records documenting Congress's response to the audit failures are public. The GAO's annual "High Risk List" entry for DoD financial management, updated biennially at gao.gov, documents the sustained assessment that DoD financial management remains a systemic risk. The relationship between the sustained audit failure, the annual budget increases, and the continued congressional authorization of defense spending without audit resolution is a documented institutional pattern. All of its components are in the public record: the Inspector General audit reports, the GAO High Risk List, the appropriations roll calls, and the DoD budget justification materials.

The Debt Discourse and the Defense Spending Vote: The Same Session

The Congressional Record from any session in which both the annual NDAA and debt-related legislation or debate occurred documents the temporal proximity of these two categories of congressional activity. In sessions where a debt ceiling vote and an NDAA vote occurred in the same calendar year, the Congressional Record contains the floor statements on fiscal crisis and the floor statements and roll calls on defense authorization in the same indexed volume. A researcher who searches the Congressional Record for "national debt" or "deficit" in any session and also reviews the roll call votes on the NDAA in the same session can identify members who spoke about fiscal responsibility and voted for defense budget increases in the same legislative period. The CBO's budget scorekeeping for each session documents the deficit impact of all enacted legislation, including the NDAA. The CBO's year-end analysis of legislation's budgetary effects is published at cbo.gov after each congressional session. This analysis shows the cumulative deficit impact of defense and non-defense spending decisions made in the same session. The floor speeches on fiscal discipline and the CBO's scorecard of what was actually enacted are both products of the same Congress, available in the public record for any session of Congress from the 1980s to the present.

The Public Audit Report: What the Inspector General Publishes

The Department of Defense Inspector General publishes a summary of the DoD's annual audit results each November, coinciding with the close of the federal fiscal year audit cycle. The audit summary documents the number of components audited, the number receiving disclaimers of opinion, the material weaknesses identified, and the remediation progress from the prior year. The FY2023 audit summary, published in November 2023, reported that 28 of 29 DoD components received disclaimers of opinion — the auditors' formal statement that they were unable to express an opinion on those components' financial statements. Only one component, the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers Civil Works, received a clean opinion. The summary report is publicly available at dodig.mil under the audit section. The Government Accountability Office's designation of DoD financial management as a "high risk" area, maintained on the GAO's biennial High Risk List at gao.gov, contextualizes the audit failures within the broader federal accountability framework. GAO places programs on the High Risk List when they are "particularly vulnerable to fraud, waste, abuse, and mismanagement." DoD financial management has appeared on the High Risk List in every edition since 1995. The combination of the annual IG audit summary and the biennial GAO High Risk designation produces a complete, publicly documented picture of the sustained failure of DoD financial accountability over more than three decades of documented audit requirement and documented non-compliance.

title: Fairshake, the Crypto Industry's Super PAC, Had $221 Million Cash on Hand as of Early 2026. Its Two Largest Donors Are Also the Two Companies Most Exposed to the Regulation It Is Spending to Shape.
teaser: Fairshake Super PAC reported approximately $221 million in cash on hand as of early 2026 FEC filings, funded primarily by Coinbase ($56M) and Ripple ($48M). Both companies are subjects of ongoing SEC regulatory proceedings. The FEC filing and the SEC EDGAR record describe the same two companies from different angles.
body:
Fairshake is a Super PAC registered with the Federal Election Commission and dedicated to electing candidates to federal office who support cryptocurrency industry regulatory positions. It was established in 2023 and became the largest single-industry Super PAC in the 2024 federal election cycle, spending approximately 130 million dollars in that cycle on House and Senate races across both parties. The FEC's electronic filing system documents Fairshake's contributions, expenditures, and cash on hand position with each quarterly filing. As of February 2026 FEC filings, Fairshake reported approximately 221 million dollars in cash on hand, a figure that exceeds its entire 2024 cycle spending by nearly 70 percent. The cash on hand is the documented war chest available for the 2026 congressional midterm elections. The two largest disclosed contributors to Fairshake in its reported filings are Coinbase Global Inc., which contributed approximately 56 million dollars, and Ripple Labs Inc., which contributed approximately 48 million dollars. Both figures are in Fairshake's FEC contribution disclosure reports, which are public record at FEC.gov and updated with each quarterly filing.

The "Decentralized" Industry and Its Centralized Political Structure

The cryptocurrency industry's foundational technical claim is decentralization: distributed ledger technology is designed to remove the need for centralized intermediaries in financial transactions. The political spending structure documented in Fairshake's FEC filings presents a specific contrast to that framing. The 221 million dollars in Fairshake's cash on hand as of early 2026 is derived primarily from contributions by Coinbase and Ripple, two of the largest centralized cryptocurrency exchanges and their principals. Coinbase is a publicly traded company (NASDAQ: COIN) subject to SEC reporting requirements. Ripple Labs is a privately held company. Both are centralized entities operating at the intersection of traditional finance and digital assets, and both are the primary subjects of ongoing federal regulatory actions: Coinbase was sued by the SEC in June 2023 for allegedly operating as an unregistered securities exchange, and Ripple Labs settled a long-running SEC enforcement action regarding XRP in 2024. The two companies that are the primary funders of Fairshake are, simultaneously, the two companies with the most direct financial interest in the regulatory outcome that Fairshake's political spending is designed to influence. The FEC filing documents the contribution amounts. The SEC EDGAR database and the federal court records document the regulatory proceedings. Both are public.

What the FEC Filing Shows About the 2026 Strategy

Super PAC cash on hand, reported quarterly to the FEC, is the documented forward-looking measure of political spending capacity. Fairshake's 221 million dollar cash position as of early 2026 FEC filings represents deployment capacity for the 2026 midterm election cycle, in which all 435 House seats and 33 Senate seats are contested. The 2024 cycle demonstrated Fairshake's spending pattern: it targeted both Republican and Democratic incumbents and challengers in competitive districts based on their stated position on cryptocurrency regulation, spending in primaries to defeat candidates the industry opposed and in general elections to support candidates it favored. This cross-partisan spending strategy, documented in the 2024 FEC expenditure filings showing Fairshake's spending across both party primaries, is the documented mechanism by which the 221 million dollar cash position translates into legislative influence: the threat of primary opposition funded at scale, regardless of the incumbent's party, creates a documented incentive for members of Congress to support the industry's regulatory positions. The FEC expenditure records for the 2024 cycle show which primaries Fairshake entered and the outcome of each race it contested.

The Regulatory Immunity Frame

The cryptocurrency industry's primary legislative objective in the 2025-2026 congressional session is the passage of a federal regulatory framework that provides clear legal status for digital assets and limits the SEC's authority to classify major cryptocurrencies as securities. Legislation addressing this objective, including the Financial Innovation and Technology for the 21st Century Act (FIT21) and Senate companion legislation, moved through congressional committees with significant industry support in 2024 and 2025. The documented connection between Fairshake's political spending and the legislative outcomes the industry's primary funders seek is a function of the FEC disclosure system: the contribution records, the expenditure records, the legislative record, and the companies' SEC filings documenting their regulatory exposure are all public. Coinbase's 10-K annual report, filed with the SEC and publicly available through SEC EDGAR at sec.gov, identifies regulatory risk as the primary business risk the company faces. The FEC Super PAC disclosure system and the SEC EDGAR system together constitute a complete documentary record of the relationship between the regulatory exposure, the political spending designed to address it, and the legislative process that will determine the outcome. No inference is required. Both records are public and cross-referenceable by company name.

The 2024 Cycle Demonstration and What the FEC Records Show

Fairshake's 2024 cycle spending, documented in its FEC expenditure reports filed throughout the cycle, shows the specific races in which the Super PAC made independent expenditures, the amounts spent in each race, and the candidate supported or opposed. The FEC's independent expenditure database, searchable at FEC.gov/data/independent-expenditures/, contains all of Fairshake's 2024 cycle filings. The pattern documented in those filings shows Fairshake spending in Democratic and Republican primaries, targeting incumbents and challengers based on their stated cryptocurrency regulatory positions without regard to party. In Ohio's 3rd congressional district Democratic primary, Fairshake spent significantly against a candidate who had been critical of the cryptocurrency industry, and the Fairshake-opposed candidate lost. In California's 16th congressional district primary, Fairshake spent in support of a candidate with cryptocurrency-friendly positions. The specific races, amounts, and outcomes are in the FEC expenditure records and in news coverage of the races. The pattern, reading the expenditure records against the election outcomes in the targeted primaries, documents the Super PAC's effectiveness as a primary-intervention tool and provides the evidentiary foundation for understanding how the 2026 cash position would likely be deployed.

The Regulatory Immunity Framing in Congressional Testimony

The cryptocurrency industry's representatives have testified before congressional committees on multiple occasions regarding the appropriate regulatory framework for digital assets. The committee hearing transcripts are public records. Hearings before the Senate Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs Committee and the House Financial Services Committee on cryptocurrency regulation are archived at congress.gov and through the committees' own websites. In those hearings, industry representatives have argued that the current SEC approach to cryptocurrency regulation, under which many tokens may be classified as securities and therefore subject to registration requirements, creates regulatory uncertainty that inhibits innovation and places U.S. companies at a competitive disadvantage relative to jurisdictions with clearer cryptocurrency regulatory frameworks. The policy argument is a legitimate and substantive one that is fully developed in the testimony record. The FEC filing record showing Fairshake's spending and the congressional testimony record showing the industry's stated regulatory concerns are two public records that together document the relationship between the regulatory stakes the industry has articulated in formal testimony and the political spending designed to affect the legislative outcome on those stakes. Both records are public. Both are produced in official government processes. The relationship between them is in the FEC filing and the hearing transcript record, not in inference.

The Coinbase and Ripple SEC Proceedings as Context for the FEC Filing

The specific regulatory proceedings facing Coinbase and Ripple are documented in federal court records accessible through PACER and in SEC press releases and filings at SEC.gov. The SEC's complaint against Coinbase, filed June 6, 2023, in the Southern District of New York, alleged that Coinbase operated as an unregistered national securities exchange, broker, and clearing agency in violation of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. The SEC identified specific cryptocurrency tokens traded on Coinbase's platform as securities, alleging that Coinbase's listing and trading of those tokens required registration under the Exchange Act. Coinbase filed an answer contesting the SEC's jurisdiction and the securities classification of the listed tokens. The case was ongoing as of the most recent available filing dates. Ripple's SEC proceedings, resolved through partial settlement in 2024 after a 2023 district court ruling finding that programmatic XRP sales to retail customers were not securities, created a more complex precedential situation that the broader cryptocurrency industry has closely followed. The connection between these two regulatory proceedings, both public in the federal court and SEC records, and the Fairshake FEC filings showing $104 million in combined Coinbase and Ripple contributions, is a documentary connection between the regulated entities' legal exposure and their political spending to influence the regulatory framework governing their businesses. The court records, the SEC filings, and the FEC filings are all public documents that describe the same regulatory and political relationship from different institutional angles.

What $221 Million in Cash Means for the 2026 Cycle

Super PAC cash on hand at the start of an election cycle is a documented planning resource. Fairshake's $221 million cash position as of early 2026 FEC filings exceeds the total outside spending by any single industry-aligned Super PAC in recent election cycles. For context, the FEC's independent expenditure database shows that the largest single Super PAC spenders in the 2024 cycle, including Senate Leadership Fund and Senate Majority PAC, spent in the range of $150-300 million each, but those organizations draw on contributions from multiple industries and donor sources. Fairshake's cash position, concentrated from a single industry and its largest companies, represents a level of coordinated industry political spending that is visible in the FEC filing record in a way that the industry's diffuse pre-Citizens United political contributions through trade association PACs were not. The FEC's disclosure system, designed to make political financial relationships visible, is documenting a level of single-industry political spending concentration that was not legally possible before Citizens United's removal of limits on corporate treasury contributions to Super PACs. The FEC quarterly filing that shows Fairshake's cash position is a routine public record. Its significance is in what it describes: the scale and sources of a specific industry's documented political war chest entering a specific election cycle.

The FIT21 Legislative Record and the Industry's Documented Position

The Financial Innovation and Technology for the 21st Century Act passed the House on May 22, 2024, with a 279-136 vote, including 71 Democratic votes for the legislation alongside the Republican majority's support. The bipartisan House majority reflected the cryptocurrency industry's documented cross-partisan political strategy: rather than aligning exclusively with one party's legislative agenda, Fairshake's political spending and the industry's lobbying program cultivated supporters across both caucuses. The Senate companion legislation was under active consideration in 2025, with hearings before the Senate Banking Committee and the Agriculture Committee, both of which have jurisdiction over aspects of the cryptocurrency regulatory framework. The committee hearing records, witness testimony, and senator questions in those hearings are public documents at banking.senate.gov and agriculture.senate.gov. The hearing participants include not only industry representatives but consumer advocates, academic economists, securities law experts, and federal regulators whose testimony represents a full spectrum of analytical views on the legislation. The FIT21 legislative record, the Fairshake FEC expenditure record from the 2024 cycle showing which members received Fairshake support, and the 2024 House roll call on FIT21 are three public records that together describe the documented relationship between the industry's political spending strategy and the legislative alignment it produced. Fairshake spent in support of or against candidates based on their positions on cryptocurrency regulation. Many of those candidates won. Many of those winners voted for FIT21. The FEC expenditure records, the election results, and the FIT21 roll call are all public documents that trace this sequence.

Coinbase :: No. 23-cv-4738 (S.D.N.Y., June 2023) :: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission; SEC v. Ripple Labs :: No. 20-cv-10832 (S.D.N.Y.) :: U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission; Financial Innovation and Technology for the 21st Century Act (FIT21) :: H.R. 4763 (2024) :: United States Congress
