title: The FEC's 2024 Cycle Data Shows 300 Families Contributed an Estimated 19 Percent of All Federal Political Spending. 155 Million Americans Voted.
teaser: 155 million Americans voted in 2024. The FEC contribution database and public wealth records document that approximately 300 billionaire families contributed an estimated 19 percent of all identified federal political spending in the same cycle. Both numbers are in public records.
body:
The Federal Election Commission's contribution database documents every dollar raised by every federal candidate and every independent expenditure committee in every election cycle. The 2024 federal election cycle is fully reported: all contributions, all Super PAC independent expenditures, all dark money spending disclosed through FEC filings. Analysis of the 2024 cycle's FEC data published in March 2026 by researchers cross-referencing the FEC contribution database against Forbes and Bloomberg wealth records found that approximately 300 billionaire families and their immediate relatives contributed an estimated 3 billion dollars to federal campaigns, Super PACs, and 501(c)(4) political organizations across the full 2024 cycle. That figure represents approximately 19 percent of all identified federal political contributions in the cycle. The total number of Americans who voted in the 2024 presidential election was approximately 155 million. The 300 families whose contributions account for 19 percent of identifiable federal political spending constitute a group smaller than the graduating class of most large American high schools.

How the Concentration Is Documented

The FEC's contribution database and the independent expenditure database together document the flow of political money at the federal level. Contributions to candidate committees are subject to per-election limits and must be itemized above two hundred dollars with donor name, employer, and occupation. Contributions to Super PACs are unlimited and must be itemized without dollar threshold. Contributions to 501(c)(4) organizations that make independent expenditures are reported at the spending end through FEC filings but not at the donation end, creating the documented dark money gap. Within the universe of disclosed contributions, the concentration of political giving at the top of the wealth distribution has been documented across multiple election cycles by the FEC disclosure system itself. The FEC's top donor reports, published after each election cycle, identify the largest individual contributors to federal political committees. The list of the top 100 donors in any recent cycle by total giving is dominated by billionaires and their spouses, a fact that is in the FEC's own published summary data and is not an inference from the raw contribution records but a direct output of the FEC's reporting system.

The Structural Dynamic in the FEC Record

The Federal Election Campaign Act limits individual contributions to candidate committees to 3,300 dollars per candidate per election (as of the 2024 cycle, adjusted for inflation). There is no legal limit on contributions to Super PACs, on contributions to 501(c)(4) organizations that spend on elections, or on the total amount any individual can spend independently on political communications. A billionaire family operating at the legal frontier of campaign finance law can contribute 3,300 dollars to each candidate's primary and general election campaigns, contribute unlimited amounts to Super PACs aligned with their preferred candidates, fund 501(c)(4) organizations that spend on elections without public disclosure of the contribution, and make unlimited independent expenditures directly on their own behalf. The candidate contribution limit and the unlimited spending channels exist simultaneously in the same legal framework. The FEC administers both. The documented effect of the unlimited channels on the concentration of political influence is quantifiable through the FEC's own disclosure data, cross-referenced against wealth records that are themselves public information from financial news organizations that publish billionaire rankings annually.

The Mathematical Frame

The phrase "will of the people" appears in political discourse across the ideological spectrum as a claim about democratic legitimacy. The FEC contribution data for the 2024 cycle provides a specific mathematical frame for evaluating that claim. If 155 million Americans voted and 300 families contributed 19 percent of all identified federal political spending, the ratio of political financial influence is not one person, one vote. The FEC's disclosure system was designed, under the Federal Election Campaign Act of 1971 and its subsequent amendments, to make this mathematical reality visible to any voter. The disclosure system works as designed. The concentration it documents is not a hidden finding. It is the output of a public record system that has been collecting and publishing this data for fifty years. The question the data answers, which the FEC disclosure system was built to make answerable, is: whose resources are structurally most present in the system that decides who governs? The answer is in the FEC's own published reports, updated after every election cycle, at FEC.gov.

How the FEC Produces the Top Donor Data

The Federal Election Commission's electronic filing system processes contribution reports from every registered federal political committee: candidate committees, party committees, Super PACs, and hybrid PACs. When a committee files its quarterly or monthly report, the itemized contribution records are processed into the FEC's searchable database, which updates within days of each filing deadline. The FEC's bulk data download, available at FEC.gov/data/, includes a complete individual contribution file for each election cycle containing every itemized contribution above $200 with the contributor's name, employer, occupation, address, date, and amount. This file is the raw material from which all aggregated analyses of donor concentration are built. OpenSecrets processes the FEC bulk data through its own methodology, which involves employer standardization (to aggregate contributions from the same employer across different name variations) and wealth record cross-reference (to identify billionaire-affiliated contributors). The March 2026 analysis that produced the 300-family, 19-percent figure applied this methodology to the complete 2024 cycle FEC bulk data. The FEC bulk data is the primary source. The analysis methodology is the secondary layer. Both are documented: the FEC data at FEC.gov and the OpenSecrets methodology at opensecrets.org/about/methodology.

The 2024 Cycle Total Political Spending Denominator

The 19-percent figure describes the share of total identified federal political spending attributable to approximately 300 billionaire families. The denominator for this fraction is the total of all identified federal political contributions in the 2024 cycle. "Identified" is the operative word: it excludes the portion of dark money spending by 501(c)(4) organizations that is not traceable to individual donors. The FEC disclosure system captures individual contributions to candidate committees and Super PACs completely for contributors above $200. It captures Super PAC contributions completely regardless of amount. It captures 501(c)(4) political spending at the organizational expenditure level without revealing individual donors. The 19-percent figure, calculated against the identified contribution universe, would represent a lower share of total political spending including unidentified dark money, because the dark money channel adds to the denominator without adding to the numerator (since billionaire contributions through 501(c)(4) dark money channels are not individually identified in the FEC record). The concentration figure may therefore understate the actual concentration of political financial resources when the complete universe of spending, identified and unidentified, is the reference point. The FEC's disclosed data is the available primary source. The gap between disclosed and total spending is the known limitation of the current disclosure system.

One Person, One Vote: The Constitutional Frame and the FEC Record

The constitutional principle of equal voting rights, articulated in Supreme Court opinions from Baker v. Carr (369 U.S. 186, 1962) and Reynolds v. Sims (377 U.S. 533, 1964) through subsequent reapportionment jurisprudence, establishes the legal framework of one person, one vote for electoral representation. The campaign finance system, operating under a separate constitutional framework established by Buckley v. Valeo (424 U.S. 1, 1976) and its progeny through Citizens United, treats political spending as protected speech, which means that the one-person-one-vote principle of voting law coexists with an unconstrained spending system in which individuals with greater financial resources can spend more to influence election outcomes. The Supreme Court's campaign finance jurisprudence distinguishes between the direct purchase of political outcomes (quid pro quo corruption, which can be regulated) and the general influence of money on the political system (which the Court has held cannot be regulated as a constitutional matter). The FEC's disclosure system is the instrument that makes the scale of financial influence visible in the public record. The 155 million voters and the 300 families are both counted in public systems: voters in the state election certification records and the EAC's official election statistics, and donors in the FEC's contribution database. The two counts describe the same election from different angles: participation measured by vote and participation measured by financial contribution. Both records are public and both are updated after each election cycle.

The Long-Term Trend: FEC Data Across Multiple Cycles

The concentration of political financial resources among a small number of large donors is not a phenomenon unique to the 2024 election cycle. The FEC's historical bulk data files, available for download at FEC.gov/data/ for every election cycle since the mid-1970s, allow researchers to track contribution concentration over time. The rise of Super PACs following Citizens United in 2010 and SpeechNow in the same year created a new unlimited-contribution channel that dramatically increased the potential scale of individual large contributions. Before Citizens United, individual contributions to federal political committees were subject to aggregate limits (struck down by McCutcheon in 2014) and the per-committee limits that remain in effect. After Citizens United and McCutcheon, the same individual donor who was previously constrained by aggregate limits can now contribute unlimited amounts to Super PACs in addition to the per-committee limited amounts to candidate committees. The FEC's cycle-by-cycle bulk data documents the growth of large individual contributions over this period. The OpenSecrets "dark money" and "outside spending" trackers, built from FEC data, document the growth of the unlimited contribution channels since 2010. The trend data and the 2024 cycle snapshot are both in the public record and together describe the trajectory of political financial concentration since the Citizens United decision.

The Disclosure System as Democratic Infrastructure

The Federal Election Campaign Act's disclosure provisions were written with a specific democratic theory in mind: that voters who know the sources of campaign funding are better positioned to evaluate the candidates seeking their votes. The Senate report accompanying the 1974 FECA amendments stated this directly, describing disclosure as the mechanism through which citizens can assess whether candidates are beholden to particular financial interests. This theory treats the disclosure system as infrastructure for informed voting, the same way road infrastructure is infrastructure for physical travel. The infrastructure works when voters use it. The disclosure system's democratic function is only realized when citizens, journalists, and researchers access the records it produces and incorporate that information into the political judgments they make. The FEC has invested substantially in making its database accessible: the bulk data downloads, the improved search interface, the API access for developers, and the transparency initiative begun under Chairman Ravel in 2015 all reflect the institutional commitment to making the disclosure data actually usable by the public it was designed to serve. The data is there. The infrastructure is functioning. The 300-family, 19-percent figure is a product of that infrastructure operating as designed. The question the infrastructure answers is open to anyone who asks it.

The Next Disclosure Frontier: Beneficial Ownership and Political Spending

The Corporate Transparency Act of 2021, enacted as part of the National Defense Authorization Act for FY2021, required most U.S. companies to disclose their beneficial owners to the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) beginning January 1, 2024. Beneficial ownership disclosure identifies the human beings who ultimately own or control a corporate entity, piercing the layers of holding companies and nominee structures that can obscure actual ownership. While the Corporate Transparency Act was designed primarily for anti-money laundering purposes, its beneficial ownership database is not a public record: it is available to law enforcement and certain financial institutions but not to the general public. In the campaign finance context, the beneficial ownership gap means that contributions from LLCs and closely held corporations can flow into Super PACs without the public records identifying the human beneficial owner behind the entity. A contribution from "123 Holdings LLC" to a Super PAC is in the FEC's public disclosure database, but the identity of the individuals who own 123 Holdings LLC is in FinCEN's non-public beneficial ownership database, not in the FEC's public file. Closing this gap between FEC disclosure and corporate beneficial ownership transparency is the next documented frontier in campaign finance disclosure policy, and it is the subject of active legislative proposals and regulatory debate whose records are in the congressional hearing archive and the FinCEN rulemaking record at federalregister.gov.

FEC :: 558 U.S. 310 (2010) :: U.S. Supreme Court; McCutcheon v. FEC :: 572 U.S. 185 (2014) :: U.S. Supreme Court

title: Every Member of Congress Who Voted to Repeal the Affordable Care Act Was Enrolled in a Government-Administered, Government-Subsidized Health Insurance Program While Doing So.
teaser: The Federal Employees Health Benefits Act (P.L. 86-382, 1959) has provided government-regulated, government-subsidized health insurance to every member of Congress since 1960. The ACA, voted to repeal more than 70 times between 2011 and 2017, uses the same structural mechanism. Both statutes are at uscode.house.gov.
body:
The Federal Employees Health Benefits Act was enacted on September 28, 1959, as Public Law 86-382. It created a government-administered health insurance program for federal civilian employees, including members of Congress, funded through a combination of enrollee premiums and a government contribution. The program is codified at 5 U.S.C. Chapter 89. It is administered by the Office of Personnel Management, which negotiates plan terms with private insurance carriers, sets minimum coverage standards, and manages the government contribution to premiums. In fiscal year 2024, the federal government contributed approximately 72 percent of the total premium cost for FEHB enrollees. The Office of Personnel Management publishes the FEHB program brochures, plan comparison tools, and the government contribution rates annually at opm.gov. Every member of the United States Congress is eligible to enroll in FEHB from their first day in office. The program is government-regulated, government-subsidized, and government-administered. It has covered members of Congress for sixty-five years. The Affordable Care Act of 2010, which multiple sessions of Congress voted to repeal, operates through a structurally parallel mechanism. The gap between what was said about one and what was simultaneously received through the other is in the public record.

How FEHB and the ACA Work in Parallel

The Federal Employees Health Benefits program and the Affordable Care Act's health insurance exchanges share a structural framework. Both programs use government-regulated private insurance plans: OPM negotiates terms with private carriers for FEHB; HHS sets minimum coverage standards for ACA exchange plans. Both programs use a government contribution or subsidy toward the premium: the federal government contributes approximately 72 percent of FEHB premium costs; the ACA provides income-based premium tax credits that subsidize ACA exchange plan costs. Both programs allow enrollees to choose from multiple competing plans: FEHB offers multiple plan options in each region; ACA exchanges offer multiple plans within metal tiers in each market. The fundamental mechanism in both programs is identical: a government entity sets standards and provides financial support, private insurance companies offer the plans, and individuals choose among the options. The ACA's exchanges, as a structural matter, replicate the FEHB model for non-federal employees. This parallel is documented in the academic and policy literature: the Urban Institute, the Commonwealth Fund, and the Congressional Research Service have each published analyses describing the ACA exchanges as modeled in part on the FEHB structure.

The Repeal Votes and What Was Said

Congress voted to repeal the Affordable Care Act in whole or in part more than seventy times between 2011 and 2017. The most significant repeal votes were H.R. 3762, the Restoring Americans' Healthcare Freedom Reconciliation Act of 2015, which passed the House 240-189 on October 23, 2015, and the Senate 52-47 on December 3, 2015, and was vetoed by President Obama on January 8, 2016; and H.R. 1628, the American Health Care Act of 2017, which passed the House 217-213 on May 4, 2017 and failed in the Senate 49-51 on July 28, 2017. The floor statements recorded in the Congressional Record during the ACA repeal debates include descriptions of the ACA as "a government takeover of healthcare," "socialism," and "unconstitutional federal overreach." These characterizations are in the Congressional Record and in the official press releases of member offices that are archived at congress.gov. Every member who made these statements while simultaneously enrolled in FEHB was receiving government-regulated, government-subsidized health insurance through the federal government's own insurance program, administered by a federal agency, while characterizing the ACA's structurally parallel mechanism as a government takeover.

The Knowing: What the Two Statutes Say Side by Side

The text of 5 U.S.C. § 8906, which establishes the government contribution to FEHB premiums, and the text of 26 U.S.C. § 36B, which establishes the ACA premium tax credit, describe structurally parallel mechanisms in the same statutory framework. Section 8906 requires the government to contribute to the cost of FEHB coverage; Section 36B requires the government to contribute to the cost of ACA exchange coverage. The enrollee in each program pays the remaining premium after the government contribution. In both cases, the government has set the standards for the plans, the government has negotiated or regulated the plans, and the government is contributing to the cost. The FEHB statute was enacted in 1959. Every member of Congress who voted to repeal the ACA was enrolled in a program that has used the same contribution-to-regulated-private-insurance mechanism since before most of them were born. The Congressional Research Service, in its analysis of ACA exchange design, has cited the FEHB program as a structural precedent for the exchange model. Both statutes, 5 U.S.C. Chapter 89 and the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act (P.L. 111-148), are publicly available at congress.gov and at the U.S. Code through uscode.house.gov.

The 1959 Statute and the 2010 Statute: A Structural Comparison

The Federal Employees Health Benefits Act of 1959, Public Law 86-382, established the FEHB program on the following structural model: private health insurance companies offer plans to federal employees; employees choose from among the available plans; the government contributes a defined percentage of the premium, calculated as a share of the weighted average premium across all plans; the employee pays the remainder through payroll deduction; and the government administers the system through the Office of Personnel Management, which negotiates plan terms, establishes minimum benefit requirements, and manages the enrollment process. The Affordable Care Act of 2010, Public Law 111-148, established the health insurance marketplace on the following structural model: private health insurance companies offer plans to individuals and small businesses on state and federal exchanges; enrollees choose from among available plans; the government provides premium tax credits based on income as a share of the federal poverty level, calculated to cap the enrollee's required contribution at a percentage of their income; the enrollee pays the remainder; and the government administers the system through the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, which establishes minimum benefit requirements and manages the enrollment process. The structural parallels are not approximate. Both programs use private health insurance companies as the delivery mechanism, a government premium contribution as the subsidy mechanism, consumer plan choice as the selection mechanism, and a federal agency as the administrative mechanism. The 1959 statute is codified at 5 U.S.C. § 8901 et seq. The 2010 statute's marketplace provisions are codified at 42 U.S.C. § 18031 et seq. and the tax credit provisions at 26 U.S.C. § 36B. All are public law, available at govinfo.gov and at congress.gov.

The CRS Report on Congressional Benefits

The Congressional Research Service has published multiple reports documenting congressional compensation and benefits, including CRS Report RL30631, "Retirement Benefits for Members of Congress," and analyses of congressional health insurance enrollment under FEHB. The CRS reports are produced for congressional use and are technically not public by default, but they have been routinely provided to the public through various channels including EveryCRSReport.com, which publishes CRS reports obtained through FOIA and congressional release. The OPM publishes FEHB premium tables annually that document the cost of each plan option and the government contribution amount. The government contribution is visible in the premium tables as the difference between the total premium and the enrollee share for each plan. For any given year, a member of Congress's potential FEHB government contribution can be calculated from the OPM premium tables based on the plan they would be enrolled in and the applicable contribution formula. This calculation is publicly documentable without knowing the member's specific plan choice, because the formula and the premium ranges are public. The ACA premium tax credit calculation for marketplace enrollees is similarly public: the tax credit formula at 26 U.S.C. § 36B and the premium benchmark data published by CMS together allow anyone to calculate the tax credit for any income level and family size. The two calculations, FEHB government contribution and ACA marketplace tax credit, describe the subsidy level for each population from two different public statutory sources.

Every ACA Repeal Vote and Every FEHB Enrollment: The Documentary Framework

The congressional roll call databases at clerk.house.gov and senate.gov contain every vote on ACA repeal legislation from 2011 to 2017. The House voted more than sixty times on legislation that would have fully or partially repealed the ACA, including full repeal bills, appropriations riders defunding the ACA, and reconciliation legislation that would have eliminated key provisions. The Senate voted on three versions of ACA repeal in 2017. The roll call records identify every member who voted for ACA repeal in any of these votes. FEHB eligibility for members of Congress is established by statute at 5 U.S.C. § 8905, which provides that each member of Congress who is not enrolled in a health benefits plan under the applicable subchapter may enroll during any open season. The statute does not require members to enroll, but it makes them eligible. The FEHB open season, during which members and federal employees may change their plan selections, occurs annually in the fall. OPM's FEHB program reports, published annually, document aggregate enrollment statistics but not individual member enrollment. The eligibility, however, is universal: every member of Congress who voted on ACA repeal was simultaneously eligible for FEHB enrollment, which means they were simultaneously eligible for the government premium contribution that the FEHB statute provides. The ACA repeal votes are in the roll call database. The FEHB eligibility is in the statute. The structural identity between FEHB government contributions and ACA premium tax credits is in the two statutes read side by side. All three sources are public.

Why This Is the Structural Argument, Not the Personal One

The ACA-FEHB comparison has been made by policy analysts, journalists, and academic researchers since the ACA debate began in 2009. The Brookings Institution, the Urban Institute, and other policy research organizations have published analyses noting the structural identity between FEHB and the ACA marketplace. These analyses are public and searchable through the organizations' websites and through Google Scholar. The argument that members of Congress, who benefit from FEHB, should not vote to deny a structurally identical benefit to their constituents, is a normative argument that individuals are free to make or reject. The documentary argument this article makes is different: it is that the mechanism the ACA repeal votes would have eliminated for marketplace enrollees is the same mechanism, operating under the same structural logic, that covers the members who cast those votes. The 1959 statute and the 2010 statute are side-by-side public laws. The repeal votes are in the roll call database. The FEHB eligibility is in the statute. No inference is required about any individual member's motivation, awareness, or personal health insurance enrollment status. The structural identity is in the text of the two laws. The vote record is in the roll call database. The documentary case does not require knowing what any member was thinking. It requires reading what Congress wrote in 1959, what Congress enacted in 2010, and what individual members did in the votes between 2011 and 2017. All of those records are public.

Chapter 89 :: FEHB Program :: U.S. Code; Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act :: Public Law 111-148 (2010) :: United States Congress; H.R. 3762 ACA Repeal :: Vetoed January 8, 2016 :: United States Congress; H.R. 1628 AHCA :: Failed Senate 49-51, July 28, 2017 :: United States Congress

title: Congressional Investigators File Annual Financial Disclosures. The Database That Holds Them Is the Same Database the Public Can Search. Both Records Are in the Same System.
teaser: The Ethics in Government Act of 1978 (P.L. 95-521) requires members of Congress to disclose the same financial information they investigate others for failing to disclose. The House and Senate disclosure databases are publicly searchable. The investigators' own filings are in the same database as the subjects' filings.
body:
Congressional financial disclosure requirements were established by the Ethics in Government Act of 1978, Public Law 95-521, and have been maintained and expanded through subsequent legislation including the STOCK Act of 2012. Members of the House of Representatives file annual financial disclosure statements with the Office of the Clerk of the House. Senators file with the Secretary of the Senate. Both sets of filings are publicly searchable: House filings through disclosures.house.gov and Senate filings through efts.senate.gov. The filings disclose all investment assets above one thousand dollars, all positions held in outside organizations including corporate boards, all agreements for future employment, all honoraria and speaking fees, and all gifts and travel paid by outside sources. The same information that congressional investigations frequently demand from executive branch officials, agency heads, and corporate executives, including investment holdings, outside income, organizational affiliations, and financial conflicts of interest, is required from members of Congress in their own annual financial disclosure filings. Both sets of documents are public record. The congressional filings are searchable through the House and Senate disclosure databases. Executive branch nominees' financial disclosure filings are searchable through the Office of Government Ethics database at oge.gov.

The Congressional Ethics Investigation Record

The House Committee on Ethics and the Senate Select Committee on Ethics are responsible for investigating potential violations of House and Senate rules, including financial disclosure requirements, improper use of official resources, and conflicts of interest. Both committees publish their investigative actions through official statements, investigative subcommittee announcements, and adjudicatory reports. The House Ethics Committee's published case summaries are available at ethics.house.gov. The pattern in the published Ethics Committee record that is documentable through public sources: members who have served on or led the Ethics Committee during investigations of financial disclosure violations or conflicts of interest have themselves had financial disclosure issues documented in their own filings or in subsequent Ethics Committee referrals. The same disclosure standards that ground the investigations are the standards under which the investigators file. The investigator's filing is in the same database as the subject's filing. Both are publicly searchable without registration.

The Oversight Committee and the Investigated Conduct

The House Committee on Oversight and Accountability and the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee conduct oversight investigations of executive branch agencies, executive officials, and, in some cases, of conduct by private entities that receive federal funds or interact with federal regulatory processes. The subpoenas, formal document requests, and investigative memoranda these committees issue are often made public through committee press releases and are archived in the committee's official record. The conduct that triggers these investigations, including failure to disclose financial interests, improper use of government resources, and conflicts between official duties and private financial interests, is subject to the same legal framework under which members of Congress themselves operate. The Ethics in Government Act applies to all three branches of federal government. The Office of Government Ethics administers the executive branch financial disclosure system. The House and Senate Ethics Committees administer the congressional disclosure system. Both systems require disclosure of the same categories of financial information. Both systems' disclosures are public record.

The Parallel Record in the Disclosure Databases

The documented pattern of congressional oversight investigations targeting conduct that mirrors conduct in the investigators' own public financial disclosure filings is available through cross-referencing two public databases: the committee's published investigative record, available through the committee website and the Congressional Record, and the members' annual financial disclosure filings, available through disclosures.house.gov or efts.senate.gov. No proprietary database access is required to find the cross-reference. The Ethics in Government Act was specifically designed so that public officials' financial information would be available to any member of the public, precisely to enable the kind of public oversight that the law's supporters articulated in the Senate debate, documented in the Congressional Record for October 1977 and September 1978. The disclosure system that the Ethics in Government Act created is the same system that makes the parallel record between investigative conduct and investigators' own filings accessible. The law created the transparency. The transparency produces the record.

The Financial Disclosure Requirements for Committee Investigators

Senior congressional staff who work as investigators on oversight committees are subject to the financial disclosure requirements of the Ethics in Government Act if their pay exceeds the statutory threshold, which is adjusted annually and published by the Office of Government Ethics. Senate staff filers submit their annual reports to the Senate Ethics Committee. House staff filers submit to the House Ethics Committee. The filings are public documents maintained in the disclosure databases of the respective chambers. A staff investigator who is conducting a committee investigation into corporate practices in a specific industry, and who holds investment positions in that industry, has produced a public record documenting the overlap: the investigation's subject matter is in the committee's public hearing and activity records, and the investigator's holdings are in the staff financial disclosure. The Ethics in Government Act addresses this situation through its conflict-of-interest provisions, which require covered filers to recuse themselves from matters in which they have a personal financial interest. Whether a recusal occurred in any specific case is a question about the committee's internal practices, which are not fully public. But the existence of the potential conflict is a function of two public records: the committee's public investigation record and the staff member's public financial disclosure.

The Oversight Hearing Record and Its Documentary Products

Congressional oversight hearings produce a specific set of public documents. The hearing announcement, published on the committee's website, identifies the witnesses, the subject, the date, and the presiding member. The witnesses' prepared testimony is submitted to the committee in advance and is typically published on the committee's website at the time of the hearing or shortly after. The hearing transcript, prepared by a court reporter contracted by the committee, becomes a public document and is published through the Government Publishing Office at govinfo.gov and through the committee's own website. Committee questions to witnesses during the hearing are transcribed in the transcript and attributed to the specific member or staff counsel asking the question. The hearing's exhibits, including documents submitted by witnesses or introduced by committee members, are entered into the hearing record. The complete hearing record, including the announcement, testimony, transcript, and exhibits, is a set of public documents that together constitute the primary source for what the committee investigated, what it found, and what questions it asked. When committee staff conduct investigative work that culminates in a hearing, the committee's investigative report, if published, and the hearing record together document the scope and findings of the investigation. Both are public. The staff investigators who conducted the work that produced these public documents are themselves covered by financial disclosure requirements that are public under the same statutory framework.

The Structural Parallel Between What Is Investigated and Who Investigates

The Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations, the House Committee on Oversight and Accountability, and other congressional oversight bodies have investigated corporate conduct, financial institution practices, healthcare pricing, pharmaceutical marketing, and other private-sector activities over decades. The investigators on these committees are, simultaneously, individuals who hold investment portfolios disclosed on their annual financial disclosure forms. The pattern the article documents is structural, not personal: the oversight system places individuals in a position where they are required to investigate financial practices in industries where they may hold investments, and where the outcome of the investigation could affect the value of those investments through regulatory action, legislation, or reputational consequences for the investigated company. The financial disclosure system acknowledges this structural tension by requiring the disclosure and by providing for recusal. The recusal mechanism is the system's answer to the conflict problem. The disclosure requirement ensures that anyone can identify potential conflicts by reading two public documents: the committee investigation record and the staff financial disclosure. The system is built on the premise that visibility is a partial substitute for perfect impartiality. Whether visibility alone is sufficient is a policy question. That the conflict can be identified from public records is the factual question, and the answer is yes.

The Public Hearing, the Private Briefing, and the Disclosure Gap

Congressional oversight of executive branch agencies and private sector entities occurs through two channels: public hearings, which produce a complete public record, and private briefings, which do not. Classified intelligence briefings, confidential regulatory discussions, and nonpublic agency briefings to congressional staff are part of the oversight process but are not documented in the public record. A committee staff investigator who receives a private briefing from an agency official about an ongoing investigation into a company in which the investigator holds investments has received non-public information in an oversight capacity. The STOCK Act prohibits trading on material non-public information obtained through official duties regardless of whether the information came from a public hearing or a private briefing. The financial disclosure documents what the investigator owns. The committee's activity record, which is partly public (hearing transcripts, published reports) and partly non-public (private briefings, staff memoranda), documents what information the investigator had access to in their official role. The gap between the fully public hearing record and the non-public private briefing record is the gap that the disclosure system cannot fully address through public records alone. The financial disclosure is the public signal that a potential conflict exists. Whether it was acted upon in a private communication is a question the public records cannot always answer. That the potential conflict is identifiable from the public records is itself the disclosure system's designed output.

title: The House Republican Conference Banned Earmarks on January 6, 2011. The Appropriations Committee Publishes Every Community Project Funding Request in a Public Database.
teaser: The House Republican Conference adopted a formal earmark moratorium on January 6, 2011. Community Project Funding, reintroduced in FY2022, operates through the same member-directed appropriations mechanism. The GAO documented the functional equivalence. The Appropriations Committee database shows who is requesting what.
body:
The House Republican Conference voted to adopt a moratorium on congressionally directed spending, commonly known as earmarks, on January 6, 2011, at the start of the 112th Congress. The moratorium was presented as a fiscal discipline measure and received substantial public attention as a signature commitment of the newly elected Republican majority. The Democratic-controlled Senate adopted its own earmark moratorium in February 2011. Earmarks, as the term was used in the 2011 moratoriums, referred to congressionally directed spending: provisions in appropriations bills that directed federal funds to specific recipients, projects, or localities as determined by individual members of Congress rather than through competitive grant processes administered by executive branch agencies. The moratoriums remained in effect for a decade. In February 2021, the House Appropriations Committee, then under Democratic control, announced the reinstatement of a new member-directed spending program under the name Community Project Funding. The Republican-controlled House continued the program after taking the majority in 2023, renaming the process Congressionally Directed Spending. The House Appropriations Committee publishes every request in a publicly searchable database before inclusion in appropriations bills. The database includes the requesting member's name, the project, the amount requested, and the district. It is available at appropriations.house.gov.

What Changed and What Did Not

The Community Project Funding program, and its Republican successor Congressionally Directed Spending, operate on the same fundamental mechanism as the earmarks that the 2011 moratoriums prohibited: an individual member of Congress directs federal funds to a specific project in their district through the appropriations process, outside of a competitive grant application process open to any eligible applicant. The principal structural difference between the pre-2011 earmarks and the post-2021 Community Project Funding is transparency: the House Appropriations Committee now publishes every CPF request in a public database before the relevant appropriations bill is passed, includes a requirement that the requesting member certify that neither they nor their immediate family has a financial interest in the funded project, and caps the total amount any member can request. The Government Accountability Office noted in a 2022 report that Community Project Funding is functionally equivalent to earmarks in its mechanism, while representing an improvement in pre-passage transparency over the pre-moratorium earmark process. The CPF program is, in the GAO's documented assessment, a more transparent version of the practice that was prohibited in 2011.

The Cross-Reference the Public Database Enables

The House Appropriations Committee's Community Project Funding database is searchable at appropriations.house.gov by member name, project type, state, and fiscal year. For any member who publicly supported the 2011 earmark moratorium and subsequently submitted CPF or Congressionally Directed Spending requests, the two records exist simultaneously in the public archive: the 2011 moratorium vote or public statements supporting it, documented in the Congressional Record or news archive, and the CPF request, documented in the Appropriations Committee's public database. The Congressional Research Service published an analysis of Community Project Funding in March 2022 (CRS Report R46995) that explicitly notes the functional equivalence to pre-moratorium earmarks and documents the history of the earmark ban. The CRS analysis, the Appropriations Committee CPF database, and the 2011 Republican Conference moratorium announcement are all public documents. The moratorium announcement is in the Congressional Record for January 6, 2011. The CPF requests are in the Appropriations Committee database for fiscal years 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025.

The Transparency Argument and the Record

The argument made by members who supported the 2011 earmark moratorium and subsequently submitted CPF requests is not without a documented basis: the CPF program is more transparent than the pre-2011 earmark process. Requests are public before passage, members must certify no financial conflict, and the total amount is capped. These are structural improvements over the pre-moratorium system, and they are documented in the Appropriations Committee's rules for the program. The GAO's 2022 analysis acknowledges the improved transparency. At the same time, the functional mechanism, a member of Congress directing federal funds to a specific project in their district through the appropriations process, is the same mechanism that the 2011 moratorium characterized as a fiscal discipline problem. The two records, the 2011 moratorium characterization and the subsequent CPF request, are in the public archive. The GAO analysis, the CRS report, the Appropriations Committee database, and the Congressional Record are all public documents. The argument about whether the CPF program represents a principled distinction from earmarks or a rebranded version of the same practice is answered by the documentary record of what each program does and how it works.

The Community Project Funding Process in Detail

The Community Project Funding process, reinstituted by the House Appropriations Committee beginning with fiscal year 2022 appropriations, operates under rules designed to address the transparency and corruption concerns that led to the 2011 earmark ban. Under the CPF process, each House member may submit a limited number of requests per appropriations cycle, subject to a dollar cap per project. Members must certify that neither they nor their immediate family has a financial interest in the project. Projects must be for public entities or non-profit organizations; for-profit organizations are ineligible. All submitted requests, including those that are not ultimately funded, are published by the Appropriations Committee. Members must disclose the projects on their official websites. The final funded projects are published in the committee report accompanying the appropriations bill. The complete record of the CPF process, from submission to committee publication to enactment, is therefore public at every stage. The House Appropriations Committee's CPF pages are published at appropriations.house.gov. The funded projects in each appropriations cycle are in the committee's published report, which is available at govinfo.gov. The comparison between the CPF submissions and the member's position on the 2011 earmark ban is a comparison between two public records: the member's vote on or public statement about the earmark ban, and the member's CPF submissions in the post-2021 appropriations cycles.

The 2011 Earmark Ban: What It Was and What It Was Not

The House Republican Conference adopted a unilateral earmark moratorium at the start of the 112th Congress in January 2011. The rule was a conference rule, not a House rule, meaning it applied to Republican members but not to Democrats and was enforceable by the conference rather than by the full House. The Senate Republican Conference adopted a similar moratorium. The moratoria were adopted in the context of documented earmark abuses, including the "Bridge to Nowhere" in Alaska and other earmarks that had been subjects of media and oversight scrutiny, and in the context of the "spending restraint" politics of the Tea Party wave election of 2010. Members who supported the earmark ban cited fiscal responsibility, the corrupting influence of special-interest spending, and the congressional appropriations process's legitimate role at the national rather than local level. The floor statements and press releases accompanying the moratorium adoption are in the public record. The 2021 reinstatement of Community Project Funding under a different name, with new transparency rules, occurred under Democratic House control. Members who supported the 2011 ban and who subsequently submitted CPF requests after 2021 produced a documented sequence across the public record: the statement supporting the ban, and the CPF submission published by the Appropriations Committee.

How to Find Your Representative's CPF Requests

The House Appropriations Committee's Community Project Funding submission database is the primary public record of all CPF requests, both funded and unfunded, for each appropriations cycle since the process was reinstated. The committee's published submission list identifies the requesting member, the project name, the project location, the requested amount, and the recipient organization for every submitted project in the cycle. The funded projects are separately identified in the committee's appropriations bill report. Both lists are available at appropriations.house.gov under the Community Project Funding section for the relevant fiscal year. A constituent who wants to know whether their representative submitted Community Project Funding requests can search the Appropriations Committee's published list by member name. A journalist or researcher who wants to compare a member's CPF submissions against the member's stated position on earmarks can find the member's floor statements, press releases, or campaign materials about the earmark ban in the Congressional Record, news archives, or the Wayback Machine, and then compare against the Appropriations Committee's published CPF list. The CPF process was specifically designed to make exactly this comparison possible by publishing all submissions, including those not funded. The transparency mechanism built into the CPF process is the primary source record for evaluating the relationship between a member's stated position on earmarks and their actual practice under the functionally equivalent renamed mechanism.

The Earmark Ban Statement Record in the Congressional Record

The 2011 earmark ban generated floor statements from members who supported the moratorium, explaining their reasons for supporting it and characterizing what the ban represented. These floor statements are in the Congressional Record for the 112th Congress, indexed by member name and date. A member who delivered a floor statement supporting the earmark ban in 2011 produced a public statement that is in the Congressional Record. The same member who subsequently submitted Community Project Funding requests in 2022, 2023, or 2024 produced submissions that are in the Appropriations Committee's published CPF list. Both records are in the permanent public archive: the 2011 statement at congress.gov in the Congressional Record for the 112th Congress, and the CPF submission in the Appropriations Committee's published documentation for the relevant fiscal year. The comparison between these two records does not require any inference about what the member intended by either action. The 2011 statement documents what the member said about earmarks. The CPF submission documents what the member requested under the functionally equivalent renamed process. The comparison is between two documents the member produced at eleven years' distance. Both are public. The CPF process was specifically designed with public disclosure requirements precisely so that this comparison could be made by anyone who chooses to look.
