seo_meta_title: House Earmark Ban vs Real Spending | CB-000142

title: The Inflation Reduction Act Passed 51-50. The Senate Roll Call and the FEC Energy Sector Contribution Database Use the Same Identifier: the Senator's Name.
teaser: The Inflation Reduction Act, Public Law 117-169, passed 51-50 on August 7, 2022 (Senate Roll Call Vote 325). Every Republican voted no. The FEC energy sector contribution database is publicly searchable by senator name. Both databases were live on the same day. The cross-reference requires arithmetic, not investigation.
body:
The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, signed as Public Law 117-169 on August 16, 2022, included the largest single federal investment in clean energy and climate programs in United States history: approximately 369 billion dollars over ten years in tax credits, grants, and loan programs for clean energy manufacturing, electric vehicles, building efficiency, and methane reduction. The Act passed the Senate 51-50 on August 7, 2022, with Vice President Harris casting the tie-breaking vote, and the House 220-207 on August 12, 2022. Every Republican in both chambers voted against the bill. The Senate roll call record for August 7, 2022 (Roll Call Vote 325) documents each senator's vote. The Federal Election Commission's campaign finance database documents the oil, gas, and coal sector contributions to each senator's campaigns across their election cycles. The two records exist in the same public archive. Neither requires specialized access. The FEC database's industry classification system and the Senate roll call database both use the senator's name and state as identifiers, which are the same identifiers in both systems.

How the FEC Tracks Industry Contributions

The Center for Responsive Politics (OpenSecrets) maintains the most comprehensive public aggregation of FEC contribution data by industry sector. Their methodology assigns each PAC and each itemized individual contributor to an industry category using Standard Industrial Classification codes and their own supplemental classification system. The oil, gas, and coal sector is tracked separately from the broader energy and natural resources category. For each member of Congress, OpenSecrets provides a total of contributions from the oil, gas, and coal sector across all election cycles available in the FEC database. The data is downloadable without registration at opensecrets.org/industries/. The FEC's own database at FEC.gov provides the underlying contribution records by PAC name and individual contributor employer, allowing any researcher to reproduce OpenSecrets' industry categorization from primary source documents. Cross-referencing the Senate Roll Call Vote 325 tally for the Inflation Reduction Act against the OpenSecrets oil, gas, and coal contribution totals for each senator who voted no produces a complete documented record of the vote and the contribution history of the voters.

The Energy Sector PAC Registration Record

Energy sector political action committees are registered with the FEC and identified in the FEC's committee database. The FEC registration database at FEC.gov/data/committees/ identifies each PAC's connected organization. PACs sponsored by oil and gas companies, pipeline operators, coal producers, and energy trade associations including the American Petroleum Institute, the American Gas Association, the National Mining Association, and the Independent Petroleum Association of America are all separately registered and identifiable in the FEC PAC database. Their contribution histories are searchable by recipient at FEC.gov. A senator who voted no on Senate Roll Call Vote 325 and who received contributions from energy sector PACs or from individual contributors employed by energy companies has two records in the public archive: the roll call vote, documented in the Senate roll call database, and the contribution history, documented in the FEC's electronic filing system. The FEC's bulk data download at FEC.gov/data/bulk-downloads/ provides the complete contribution database in machine-readable format for researchers who want to analyze the full universe of energy sector contributions across all election cycles.

What the Combined Record Shows

The combined record of the Inflation Reduction Act vote and the energy sector contribution database does not require editorial analysis to produce a finding. It requires arithmetic and a key-join on the senator's name. Every senator who voted against the largest clean energy investment in United States history had a documented energy sector contribution record available in the same public archive on the same day they cast their vote. The FEC updates contribution data within 48 hours of a filing. The Senate roll call is published the same day as the vote. The OpenSecrets aggregation typically reflects the most recent FEC data within a few days. The record is not a historical artifact that took years to compile. It is a real-time public record that existed simultaneously with the vote. The legislative history of the Inflation Reduction Act, the Senate roll call, and the complete FEC contribution database are all archived at their respective government and public interest databases and are available to any member of the public without subscription, registration, or specialized knowledge.

The FEC Contribution Database and Industry Classification

Individual contributions to federal campaigns documented in the FEC database include the contributor's employer and occupation fields, which are required when the contribution is above $200. OpenSecrets aggregates these individual contribution records by industry sector using a classification system that assigns each employer to an industry category. The energy and natural resources sector in the OpenSecrets classification includes oil and gas companies and their employees, electric utilities, coal mining companies, and alternative energy companies. Contributions to a specific member from individuals whose employer is classified in the oil and gas industry are aggregated in OpenSecrets' candidate profile for that member under "top industries." The FEC's own contribution database allows searching by contributor employer for any committee, so a researcher can verify the OpenSecrets industry totals by querying the FEC's own data. The FEC database is available in bulk download format for researchers and through the FEC.gov search interface for individual queries. For members who sit on committees with jurisdiction over energy policy, the combination of energy industry contributions (from the FEC database) and the committee assignment (from the committee websites and congress.gov) documents the overlap between the financial relationship with the industry and the official responsibility for legislating the industry's regulatory environment.

The Inflation Reduction Act Vote and the Energy Sector Response

The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, enacted as Public Law 117-169 on August 16, 2022, passed the Senate on August 7, 2022, on a 51-50 vote with Vice President Harris casting the tiebreaking vote. The vote was strictly along party lines: all 50 Democratic senators voted in favor and all 50 Republican senators voted against. The roll call for the Senate vote is documented at senate.gov. The IRA included approximately $369 billion in climate and clean energy provisions over ten years, including production and investment tax credits for clean electricity, electric vehicles, hydrogen production, and advanced manufacturing, as well as incentives for domestic clean energy supply chains and methane reduction requirements for oil and gas operations. The methane fee provision in the IRA was the first direct fee on methane emissions from the oil and gas sector at the federal level. The oil and gas industry's formal opposition to the methane provisions, the clean energy tax credits, and the IRA more broadly is documented in testimony before congressional committees, in lobbying disclosure filings at lda.senate.gov, and in public statements by the American Petroleum Institute and other industry organizations. The 51-50 vote record and the FEC contribution data showing pre-vote energy industry contributions to the no-voting senators are both public records that document the financial relationship and the legislative outcome in the same time period.

The FEC Same-Day and Pre-Vote Contribution Window

The FEC's contribution database includes the date of each itemized contribution. Federal campaign finance laws permit contributions to be made at any time, including during active legislative sessions. There is no legal restriction on making contributions to a senator on the day of a floor vote. The FEC disclosure schedule means that contributions made close to a vote may not appear in the public record until the next quarterly reporting deadline, or in the case of contributions made close to an election, within the accelerated pre-election reporting window. The 45-day quarterly reporting cycle means that a contribution made in the week of a floor vote may not be publicly visible until several weeks after the vote. The transparency gap between the transaction date and the public disclosure date is a feature of the current disclosure schedule, not a deficiency in the underlying data. Once disclosed, the contribution date is fixed in the FEC record, and the relationship between the contribution date and the vote date is computable from the public data. OpenSecrets' contribution timeline tools and the FEC's own contribution search by date range allow researchers to identify contributions from specific industry sectors made within specified windows around specific votes. The methodology is documented at opensecrets.org, and the underlying data is verifiable at FEC.gov.

The FEC Roll Call Plus Contribution Cross-Reference: How It Works

The FEC contribution database at FEC.gov/data/receipts/ allows querying by recipient committee, date range, contributor employer, and contributor name. For a senator who voted on the Inflation Reduction Act on August 7, 2022, the relevant query is: what contributions from fossil fuel industry-affiliated individuals and PACs did this senator receive in the contribution window before the vote? The query returns itemized contributions where the contributor's employer is identified as an oil and gas company, a coal mining company, an electric utility, or an industry trade association such as the American Petroleum Institute. The employer field is a text field entered by the contributor's campaign, so industry identification requires standardizing employer names across variations. OpenSecrets performs this standardization in producing its industry contribution totals, and documents its methodology at opensecrets.org. The FEC's own bulk data allows independent replication of the analysis using the raw contribution records. The Senate roll call for the IRA, recorded on August 7, 2022, is at senate.gov under the 117th Congress roll call records. The vote shows 51-50 with the Vice President casting the tiebreaking vote. The contribution data and the vote record together document the financial relationship between the fossil fuel industry and the senators who voted no, in the same time period and through the same public data systems.
verified_source: Inflation Reduction Act :: Public Law 117-169 (August 16, 2022) :: United States Congress; Senate Roll Call Vote 325 :: August 7, 2022 :: U.S. Senate; OpenSecrets Industry Contribution Database :: Center for Responsive Politics :: opensecrets.org; FEC Committee and Receipts Database :: Federal Election Commission :: FEC.gov
seo_meta_title: Inflation Act Vote Tied to Energy | CB-000141

title: The CBO Scores Every Bill. The Congressional Record Has Every Floor Speech. The C-SPAN Archive Has the Video. All Three Are Searchable.
teaser: The CBO publishes deficit scores for every major bill. The Congressional Record archives every floor statement. The C-SPAN archive goes back to 1979. Placing a member's deficit rhetoric alongside the CBO score for the legislation they voted for requires three public databases and no specialized access.
body:
The Congressional Budget Office is a nonpartisan federal agency that produces economic and budget analyses for Congress. It was established by the Congressional Budget Act of 1974, Public Law 93-344, to provide Congress with independent economic and budget analysis separate from the Office of Management and Budget, which is part of the executive branch. The CBO scores every major piece of legislation: it calculates the ten-year cost or savings of a bill relative to the baseline, and it produces long-term budget projections. These scores are public documents, published at cbo.gov, and they are the official numbers used in congressional budget debates. The Congressional Record is the official verbatim transcript of all proceedings on the floor of the House and Senate. It is published daily when Congress is in session and is searchable at congress.gov. Cross-referencing a member's CBO-scored votes with their floor statements from the Congressional Record over time produces a specific kind of documentary comparison: the gap between what a member says about the federal debt in their floor statements and what the CBO determined was the fiscal impact of the legislation they voted for in the same or adjacent sessions.

The Deficit Record by Party and Administration

The CBO publishes a historical budget data table each year showing the federal deficit or surplus for every fiscal year since 1965. The table shows receipts, outlays, and the resulting surplus or deficit as a share of GDP. The Congressional Record contains floor statements by members on the subject of the federal debt and deficit going back to 1873 in digitized form and to 1995 in full-text searchable form through congress.gov. The C-SPAN Congressional Chronicle archives floor speech video from 1987 forward and allows keyword searches by member. Together, these three archives produce a searchable record of who said what about deficits, when they said it, what party controlled the presidency at the time, and what the actual CBO-documented deficit trajectory was during the same period. The documented pattern in the combined record is that floor speeches warning about unsustainable deficits and government spending increase in frequency and intensity during administrations of the opposing party. The CBO historical data table documents the deficit numbers. The floor speech record documents the rhetoric. Both are in public archives that are searchable by the same date and member identifiers.

The CBO Scores on Specific Legislation

The CBO produces a cost estimate for every bill that is considered by a full committee or by the House or Senate. These estimates are published at cbo.gov within days of the bill's consideration and remain archived indefinitely. For any member whose floor statements warn about deficit spending, the CBO cost estimates for the legislation that member voted for provide the documentary comparison. The Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, Public Law 115-97, received a CBO score before passage. The CBO estimated the Act would increase the deficit by approximately 1.46 trillion dollars over ten years on a conventional basis. The bill passed the Senate 51-48 on December 20, 2017. Every senator who voted yes had access to the CBO score before the vote. The CBO score is a public document. The Senate roll call record is a public document. The Congressional Record contains the floor statements made by senators on deficit spending during the same legislative session. All three are searchable through the same federal archive at congress.gov and cbo.gov.

The C-SPAN Archive as Primary Source

C-SPAN began recording and archiving House floor proceedings in March 1979 and Senate floor proceedings in June 1986. The C-SPAN Video Library at c-span.org/congress/ provides a searchable archive of congressional floor proceedings, committee hearings, press conferences, and campaign events. The Congressional Chronicle tool within the library allows searches by member name, keyword, and date range. Floor statements on specific topics are retrievable by member across multiple Congresses, making it possible to document how a member's stated position on a topic changed over time or across administrations. The archive is a primary source: the footage is the unedited record of what was said, on the record, in the chamber. The CBO cost estimates, the Senate roll call records, and the C-SPAN floor speech archive together constitute a three-source primary source chain for documenting the gap between fiscal rhetoric and fiscal votes. Each source is independently maintained by a different public institution. Each is publicly accessible without subscription or registration.

The CBO Score as a Legislative Primary Source

The Congressional Budget Office score for a piece of legislation is produced before the vote, transmitted to the requesting committee or the leadership of the relevant chamber, and published on the CBO's website at cbo.gov. The score documents the projected budgetary impact over a ten-year window, divided by fiscal year, and identifies the policy mechanisms driving the budgetary change. For revenue legislation, the score shows the projected increase or decrease in federal revenues by provision. For spending legislation, the score shows the projected increase or decrease in mandatory or discretionary outlays. The CBO's scoring reflects economic assumptions about growth, inflation, interest rates, and behavioral responses to policy changes, all of which are documented in the CBO's annual economic assumptions report and in the specific score's footnotes and technical appendix. The score is a public document the moment it is transmitted to Congress. It is available at cbo.gov in the legislation's score search and in the letters archive. A member of Congress who votes for legislation whose CBO score projects a specific deficit impact, and who simultaneously makes floor statements citing the national debt as a fiscal emergency, has produced both records in the same session of Congress. The CBO score is at cbo.gov. The floor statement is in the Congressional Record. The vote is in the roll call database. All three are public documents generated within days of each other during the legislative process.

The C-SPAN Archive as a Primary Source

C-SPAN has recorded and archived congressional floor proceedings, committee hearings, press conferences, and other Washington events since 1979. The archive at c-spanvideo.org is searchable by date, keyword, and member name. C-SPAN footage provides the audio and video record of what was said on the floor, at committee hearings, and at press conferences, supplementing the written transcript in the Congressional Record. Floor statements about the national debt, deficit, or fiscal irresponsibility are searchable in the C-SPAN archive by keyword. A search for a specific member's name combined with terms like "national debt," "deficit," or "fiscal crisis" returns footage of that member's public statements on those topics over the years the member has been in office. The C-SPAN archive's coverage predates most members' current terms and in many cases captures statements made during prior campaigns, earlier terms, or prior roles in state government. A member who has publicly cited the national debt as a fiscal emergency in multiple C-SPAN-archived appearances, and whose CBO-scored voting record shows a net positive deficit impact over the same period, has produced a documentary record across two public archives: the C-SPAN video archive and the CBO score library. The combination is the three-archive chain: CBO score, C-SPAN footage, Congressional Record transcript, all describing the same member's fiscal position from different documentary angles.

The Deficit Floor and How the CBO Documents It

The federal deficit in any given year is the difference between total federal revenues and total federal outlays. The CBO publishes the official estimate of the current-year deficit in its Budget and Economic Outlook, updated twice annually. The historical deficit data going back to 1965 is published in the CBO's historical budget tables, available at cbo.gov/publication/51118. The historical data shows the deficit in dollar terms and as a percentage of GDP for each fiscal year. Congress's contribution to each year's deficit is a function of the legislation it has enacted: the revenue laws that determine what taxes are collected, the appropriations bills that determine discretionary spending, and the mandatory program laws that determine the spending on Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid. The CBO's tally of legislation's deficit impact is in its record of enacted legislation scores, available at cbo.gov. For any session of Congress, the record of what legislation was enacted and what the CBO projected each piece of legislation would add to or subtract from the deficit is complete and public. A member who expresses concern about the deficit in floor statements and then votes for legislation the CBO projects will increase the deficit has produced a documented and traceable record. The CBO score shows the projected impact of their yes vote. The Congressional Record shows what they said about the deficit. The historical budget tables show where the deficit is. All three are at cbo.gov or at congress.gov.

Searching the CBO Score Library by Member's Key Votes

The CBO's letter database at cbo.gov/cost-estimates is searchable by bill number, title, date, and committee. For any major piece of legislation, searching the relevant bill number returns the CBO's score and all related analytical products. The CBO's estimate letters are dated documents that become permanent records in the score library. A researcher who wants to understand the deficit impact of a specific member's voting record can identify the CBO scores for the major legislation the member voted for or against, sum the projected deficit impacts weighted by the member's vote direction, and produce a documented estimate of the cumulative fiscal impact of that member's legislative choices over a defined period. This methodology is used by fiscal watchdog organizations including the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget, which publishes analyses of enacted legislation's deficit impact. The CRFB's analyses draw on CBO scores and are public at crfb.org. The underlying CBO scores are at cbo.gov. The roll call votes are at clerk.house.gov and senate.gov. The Congressional Record's floor statements on deficit and debt are searchable by member name and keyword through congress.gov's Congressional Record search function. The complete primary source chain for evaluating any member's documented fiscal record is available through these four public systems, all accessible without subscription or specialized access.
verified_source: Congressional Budget Act of 1974 :: Public Law 93-344 :: United States Congress; CBO Historical Budget Data :: Congressional Budget Office (annual) :: cbo.gov; CBO Cost Estimate: Tax Cuts and Jobs Act :: Congressional Budget Office (November 2017) :: cbo.gov/publication/53311; C-SPAN Congressional Archive :: C-SPAN (1979-present) :: c-span.org
seo_meta_title: Congressional Records Fully Searchable | CB-000140

title: The Senate Intelligence Committee Listed Twenty Specific Claims CIA Officials Made About the Interrogation Program. Then Listed the CIA's Own Documents That Contradicted Each One.
teaser: The Senate Intelligence Committee's 525-page report, released December 9, 2014, identifies twenty specific representations CIA officials made to Congress and the public. For each one, it cites CIA's own internal documents. The gap between the two is the documented finding. The report is publicly available.
body:
The Senate Select Committee on Intelligence conducted a study of the Central Intelligence Agency's Detention and Interrogation Program beginning in 2009. The study took five years and reviewed more than 6.3 million pages of CIA documents. The final report runs 6,700 pages. On December 9, 2014, the committee released a 525-page executive summary of the report, which had been negotiated between the committee and the CIA and White House for several years before release. The executive summary contains a structured finding that has received less public attention than the report's documentation of the interrogation techniques themselves: a list of twenty specific representations that CIA officials made to Congress, the White House, the Department of Justice, and the public about the program that the committee's review of CIA's own internal documents found to be inaccurate or unsupported. Each of the twenty representations is identified in the executive summary with a specific heading. Each is followed by documentary evidence from CIA's own internal records showing what the agency's own contemporaneous documents said. The gap between what was represented and what the internal documents showed is a primary source finding, not an editorial judgment.

The Twenty Representations and Their Primary Source Contradictions

The twenty representations documented in the executive summary of the Senate Intelligence Committee report cover the following categories: whether enhanced interrogation techniques produced unique and otherwise unavailable intelligence; whether enhanced interrogation techniques were necessary to obtain cooperation from detainees; whether the CIA had been truthful in its briefings to congressional oversight committees; whether the program had been reviewed and approved by the Department of Justice's Office of Legal Counsel on the basis of accurate information; and whether the program operated according to the guidelines the CIA had described to its oversight bodies. For each of the twenty representations, the committee cited specific internal CIA documents. These internal documents include cables from overseas detention sites, internal memoranda from CIA leadership, and records of briefings. The specific documents cited in the footnotes of the 525-page executive summary are identified by CIA document number, date, and author where that information was not redacted. The Senate Intelligence Committee report is the first government document to formally catalog the gap between the public representations and the internal records on a structured, representation-by-representation basis.

The Congressional Oversight Record

The CIA provided briefings to the congressional intelligence committees regarding the detention and interrogation program beginning in 2002. The briefings are documented in CIA records and in the committee's independent review. The 525-page executive summary documents specific instances where the content of CIA briefings to committee members differed from what CIA's own internal records reflected about the program's operation and effectiveness. The committee's finding, at page 445 of the executive summary, states that the CIA "provided inaccurate information" to congressional oversight bodies "on numerous occasions." The word "numerous" refers to the twenty specific representations documented in the preceding sections. The congressional oversight committees' role, under Article I of the Constitution and the National Security Act of 1947, is to provide oversight of intelligence agencies on behalf of Congress and the public. The oversight record, and the documented gap between what the oversight committees were told and what the agency's own records showed, are both in the 525-page document that is publicly available through the Senate Intelligence Committee's website and the Government Publishing Office.

The Document's Availability and What It Contains

The 525-page executive summary of the Senate Intelligence Committee's Study of the CIA's Detention and Interrogation Program is publicly available through the Senate Intelligence Committee at intelligence.senate.gov and through the Government Publishing Office at gpo.gov. The document was declassified with redactions following a two-year negotiation between the committee and the executive branch. The redactions, which appear as black bars throughout the text, cover specific operational details, names of CIA personnel, and certain country names. The twenty structured findings about the accuracy of CIA representations are not redacted. The committee's methodology, which was to compare the representations against CIA's own contemporaneous internal documents, is described in the preface. The report identifies itself as a primary source review: it does not evaluate the interrogation techniques against external ethical or legal standards in the findings section. It compares what was said publicly against what the agency's own records showed contemporaneously. That comparison, available in a public government document since December 9, 2014, is the record.

The Structure of the Senate Intelligence Committee's Documentary Review

The Senate Select Committee on Intelligence's investigation into the CIA's detention and interrogation program was based primarily on a review of the CIA's own internal documents: cables, emails, memoranda, operational reports, and inspector general records. The committee reviewed more than 6.3 million pages of CIA records under a document review agreement that was ultimately contested by the CIA, resulting in a referral by the committee to the Justice Department for investigation of potential unauthorized access to committee computers by CIA personnel. The 6.3 million pages of CIA records are classified. The executive summary released in December 2014, at 525 pages, represents the committee's findings derived from those classified records. The executive summary is a public document. The underlying classified records are not public. The committee's findings, presented in the executive summary, include 20 specific representations that the committee found the CIA had made to Congress and to the executive branch that were inaccurate or incomplete based on the committee's review of the CIA's own documents. Each finding cites the specific CIA document, identified by a classified document number, that the committee found contradicted the CIA's representation. The reader cannot independently verify the underlying classified documents. But the committee's process, its evidentiary basis, and the CIA's formal response are all in the public record. The CIA released a 136-page response, also publicly available, disputing many of the committee's conclusions. The committee published a point-by-point rebuttal to the CIA's response.

The Twenty Inaccurate Representations: Structure and Primary Source Base

The executive summary organizes the committee's findings into a set of numbered key findings, with the findings about inaccurate representations concentrated in the sections addressing the CIA's briefings of the congressional intelligence committees, the White House, and the Department of Justice Office of Legal Counsel. Each representation finding follows the same documentary structure: the committee identifies a specific communication from the CIA to a specific recipient on a specific date, states the substance of what the CIA communicated, and then cites the CIA's own records showing what the CIA knew about the program's status, effectiveness, and legal compliance at the time of the communication. The gap between what the CIA communicated and what the CIA's own records showed it knew is the documented basis for the committee's inaccuracy finding. The executive summary is 525 pages. The 20 inaccuracy findings are distributed across those pages. The full text is available through the Senate Intelligence Committee's website, at the Government Publishing Office's govinfo.gov, and through multiple news organizations' document archives. The CIA's response document is also publicly available. Both documents describe the same historical events using different evidentiary interpretations. Both are primary sources for understanding what the committee found and how the CIA responded.

The Legal Framework: Congressional Oversight Authority

Congress's authority to oversee intelligence community activities derives from the National Security Act of 1947, the Intelligence Oversight Act of 1980, and the congressional oversight provisions of the Intelligence Reform and Terrorism Prevention Act of 2004. The Intelligence Oversight Act requires the President to keep the intelligence committees "fully and currently informed" of intelligence activities, including significant anticipated activities before they are initiated. The CIA's obligation to brief the intelligence committees on significant covert action programs is therefore a statutory requirement, not a discretionary practice. The committee's finding that the CIA provided inaccurate information to congressional oversight bodies is a finding about compliance with this statutory obligation. The legal framework for congressional intelligence oversight is public: the National Security Act and its amendments are at 50 U.S.C. § 3091 et seq. The committee's interpretation of that framework's requirements is in the executive summary. The CIA's interpretation of the same framework is in its response document. The statutory text, the committee's finding, and the CIA's response are each public documents that together describe the legal and documentary dispute about what the CIA's oversight obligations required and whether they were met. The underlying classified evidence that determines which interpretation is factually correct remains classified.

The CIA Response and the Committee Rebuttal: A Three-Document Record

The public record on the Senate Intelligence Committee's torture report is a three-document set: the committee's 525-page executive summary, the CIA's 136-page formal response, and the committee's formal rebuttal to the CIA response. All three documents are public and available through the Senate Intelligence Committee's website, through the Government Publishing Office, and through news organizations' document archives. The three-document set is the complete public record of the factual dispute between the committee's findings and the CIA's characterization of those findings. Reading all three documents in sequence produces a picture of the evidentiary dispute: where the committee and the CIA agree on what happened, where they disagree on what the CIA's own documents show, and where they disagree on the legal or policy interpretation of agreed-upon facts. The CIA's response, formatted as a point-by-point rebuttal of the committee's key findings, identifies specific findings it contests and provides its alternative account. The committee's rebuttal identifies where it believes the CIA's response is itself inaccurate or incomplete. The three-document set does not resolve every factual dispute, because the underlying classified evidence that would definitively resolve contested points remains classified. The public record is the three documents. They represent the full scope of what can be evaluated without security clearance. The primary source for each is the document itself, not summaries of it.

title: The STOCK Act Requires Members to Report Stock Trades Within 30 Days. The Disclosure Database Shows Which Committees They Serve On. Both Are Public.
teaser: Public Law 112-105, signed April 4, 2012, requires congressional stock trade disclosures within 30-45 days. The House Financial Disclosure database and committee assignment records are both public. Cross-referencing them is what the STOCK Act was designed to allow voters to do.
body:
The Stop Trading on Congressional Knowledge Act was signed by President Obama on April 4, 2012, as Public Law 112-105. The Act clarifies that members of Congress and congressional staff are subject to insider trading prohibitions under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and requires members to report stock transactions above one thousand dollars within 30 to 45 days of the transaction. Before the STOCK Act, members were required to disclose investment holdings annually in financial disclosure filings but were not required to report individual transactions within any particular timeframe. The STOCK Act also required the reports to be filed electronically and made publicly searchable. The House Financial Disclosure database at disclosures.house.gov and the Senate's electronic filing system now provide searchable access to all member stock transaction reports. Business Insider used these databases in a 2021 investigation to identify which members had failed to comply with the STOCK Act's 30-to-45-day reporting requirement and which members were trading stocks in industries directly regulated by their committee assignments.

What Business Insider Found in the Public Record

The Business Insider investigation, published in September 2021, matched the House and Senate financial disclosure databases against the committee assignment records published by the House and Senate. Committee assignments are public record: the House and Senate publish the complete committee roster for each Congress on their official websites and in the Congressional Record. For each member, the investigation identified which committees they served on, what industries those committees regulated, and whether the member had disclosed stock trades in companies operating in those regulated industries. The investigation found that more than 50 members had traded stock in industries directly overseen by their committees during the preceding two years. The trades were legal. The STOCK Act prohibits insider trading, defined as trading on material nonpublic information. It does not prohibit members from trading in regulated industries; it requires only that such trades be disclosed within 30 to 45 days. The disclosure requirement is what makes the record. The record is the database that Business Insider used, and that any member of the public can access at disclosures.house.gov.

The Specific Committee Overlap Pattern

The committee-to-industry regulatory relationship that makes the overlap documentable is itself a public record. The Congressional Research Service publishes the jurisdictions of each House and Senate committee in its Committee Guide, which identifies which statutes, industries, and regulatory programs fall within each committee's oversight authority. The House Energy and Commerce Committee has jurisdiction over the pharmaceutical and medical device industries, telecommunications, and energy. The House Financial Services Committee has jurisdiction over banking, securities regulation, and insurance. The House Armed Services Committee has jurisdiction over defense procurement and military contracting. Cross-referencing these published committee jurisdictions against the STOCK Act transaction disclosures of the members who serve on those committees, available through disclosures.house.gov, produces the documented overlap. The Congressional Research Service Guide, the committee rosters, and the STOCK Act database are all public documents that the STOCK Act itself was designed to make cross-referenceable by the public. The Act's sponsors cited this cross-referencing capability as one of the bill's primary accountability mechanisms during the Senate floor debate, recorded in the Congressional Record.

Reporting Violations in the Public Record

The STOCK Act specifies a civil penalty of two hundred dollars for failure to file the transaction report within the required timeframe. Business Insider's investigation documented that 75 members had been assessed or should have been assessed the penalty for late filing. The disclosure records that document each late filing are the same records available at disclosures.house.gov and through the Senate's disclosure system. A late filing shows a transaction date and a filing date. The gap between the two dates, compared against the 30-to-45-day requirement, identifies whether the filing was timely. No investigative tool is required to make this determination. The transaction disclosure records are public, the requirement is in the statute at P.L. 112-105, and the arithmetic of the gap is the documented violation. The 75 members who were found to have late filings are in those databases. The Committee on Ethics is responsible for enforcement. Its public records of STOCK Act enforcement actions are available through the House Ethics Committee's published case records, which constitute a separate layer of the documentary record.

What the Periodic Transaction Report Requires

The STOCK Act's periodic transaction report, filed on a standardized form, requires the member to report each transaction in a covered investment with a value above $1,000 within 45 days of the transaction date. The report must include the name of the asset, the type of transaction (purchase, sale, or exchange), the transaction date, the approximate value range (using standardized value bands from the disclosure instructions), and whether the transaction was made by the member, their spouse, or a dependent child. The 45-day filing window and the report's public availability create a near-real-time public record of members' investment activity in specific securities. The House and Senate maintain separate databases for periodic transaction reports. The House database at disclosures.house.gov and the Senate database at efts.senate.gov allow searches by member name and date range. The Washington Post, the New York Times, Unusual Whales, and other organizations maintain compiled databases of congressional stock trades built from the periodic transaction reports and updated regularly. These secondary databases aggregate the FEC's raw disclosure data into formats that facilitate searching and pattern analysis. The primary source is always the periodic transaction report itself, which is a public document filed with the clerk of the relevant chamber and published on the disclosure database.

The Committee Hearing Schedule and What It Reveals

Congressional committee hearings are publicly scheduled through the committee's official schedule, published on the committee's website and through the congress.gov hearing schedule. Committee hearings are announced in advance, typically several days before they occur, with the witnesses, the topic, and the committee members' roles documented in the official announcement. When a committee schedules a hearing on a topic with potential market impact, such as a hearing on drug pricing that features pharmaceutical executives as witnesses, a hearing on financial regulation that features banking regulators, or a hearing on technology antitrust that features tech company executives, the hearing announcement is a public document that market participants outside Congress receive simultaneously with committee members. The committee members, however, have additional non-public information: the staff briefings that prepare members for the hearing, the witness testimonies submitted before the hearing (some of which are not publicly disclosed in advance), and the committee's own internal research and analysis. The STOCK Act's prohibition on trading on material non-public information is designed to address this informational asymmetry. The hearing schedule and the periodic transaction reports, read together, document whether transactions in the securities of companies appearing before the committee occurred in proximity to the hearing date. The hearing schedule is public. The transaction report is public. The comparison is mechanical.

The Pattern Across Multiple Sectors and Committees

Academic research published in peer-reviewed journals has examined the question of whether congressional stock trades outperform the market and whether there is evidence of trading on material non-public information. Studies published in the Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis, the American Economic Review, and other peer-reviewed journals have examined congressional trading returns relative to market benchmarks in the pre-STOCK Act period and have found statistically significant outperformance in some analyses. Post-STOCK Act studies have produced more mixed results. The academic literature on congressional stock trading is publicly available through SSRN at ssrn.com and through university library databases. The periodic transaction reports that form the empirical basis for this research are public documents. The research methodology, which compares transaction dates against committee hearings, legislative actions, and regulatory decisions in the relevant sectors, is documented in the published papers. The combination of public transaction data, public committee records, and public legislative activity produces the raw data for the analysis. The analytical question of whether the observed trading patterns reflect information advantages or coincidence is the subject of ongoing academic debate. The documentary question of whether the trades occurred, when they occurred, and what the member's committee responsibilities were on the relevant dates is answered by the public records.

The 45-Day Clock and What the Late Filing Record Shows

The STOCK Act's 45-day filing requirement for periodic transaction reports has produced its own documentary record: the record of late filings and the fines assessed for them. The House and Senate ethics offices maintain records of STOCK Act violations and the fines collected, and these records are published periodically in ethics committee reports and press releases. Organizations tracking STOCK Act compliance, including Unusual Whales, the Campaign Legal Center, and news organizations, have compiled databases of late STOCK Act filers from the public disclosure records. The $200 fine for late STOCK Act reports, which has been widely criticized as insufficient deterrent, is itself a documented policy choice: Congress set the fine level in the statute, and Congress has not increased it despite documented widespread non-compliance. A member who filed a STOCK Act periodic transaction report late, paid the $200 fine, and sits on a committee with jurisdiction over the traded security, has produced three simultaneous public records: the late filing in the disclosure database (with the original transaction date and the filing date, from which the lateness is calculable), the ethics office record of the fine, and the committee assignment in the committee records. All three records are public. The late filing record is particularly relevant because the 45-day window was designed to provide timely public disclosure of members' trading activity during active legislative periods, and a late filing during a period of committee activity reduces the transparency value of the disclosure precisely when it is most relevant.

title: The DISCLOSE Act Has Been Blocked Since 2010. The 501(c)(4) Architecture It Would Have Closed Is in the Internal Revenue Code.
teaser: 26 U.S.C. § 501(c)(4) allows social welfare organizations to spend up to 49% of activity on politics without disclosing donors. The DISCLOSE Act would have required disclosure above $10,000. The Senate cloture vote failed 57-41 on July 27, 2010. Dark money spending totaled $750 million in the 2020 cycle. All in the public record.
body:
Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(4) designates organizations described as "civic leagues or organizations not organized for profit but operated exclusively for the promotion of social welfare." The IRS interpretation, formalized in Treasury Regulation 1.501(c)(4)-1(a)(2)(ii), allows 501(c)(4) organizations to engage in political activity as long as political activity is not the organization's "primary purpose." The IRS has interpreted "primary purpose" as a facts-and-circumstances determination, generally treating less than 50 percent of activities being political as consistent with 501(c)(4) status. A 501(c)(4) organization is not required to publicly disclose its donors on its Form 990, the annual return that tax-exempt organizations file with the IRS. The Form 990 is publicly available; the Schedule B, which lists donors, is not publicly disclosed for 501(c)(4)s. This is the specific legal architecture of "dark money": a 501(c)(4) organization can raise unlimited funds from undisclosed donors, spend up to 49 percent of those funds on political activity, and report that activity to the FEC under its organizational name without ever disclosing the identities of the people who funded it.

What the DISCLOSE Act Would Have Changed

The Democracy Is Strengthened by Casting Light On Spending in Elections Act, first introduced as H.R. 5175 on June 16, 2010, five months after Citizens United, would have required any organization spending more than 10,000 dollars in an election to disclose donors who contributed more than 10,000 dollars for political purposes. The bill covered 501(c)(4) organizations, 501(c)(6) trade associations, and 527 political organizations that were not already required to disclose under FECA. If enacted, it would have closed the primary donor disclosure gap in post-Citizens United campaign finance. The bill passed the House 219-206 on June 24, 2010. The Senate voted on cloture on July 27, 2010. The cloture vote failed 57-41. It failed again in subsequent sessions. The legislative history of each failed cloture vote is in the Senate roll call record. The dark money spending in the election cycles immediately following each failed cloture vote is in the FEC independent expenditure database, which does not show the underlying donors but does show the 501(c)(4) organization names that made the expenditures.

The FEC Report and the Source Organization

When a 501(c)(4) organization makes an independent expenditure in a federal election, it reports that expenditure to the FEC on Form 5 (Report of Independent Expenditures Made and Contributions Received) or through Form 24 within 24 hours before an election. The FEC filing identifies the 501(c)(4) organization by name and employer identification number, the candidate supported or opposed, the amount, and the type of communication. The filing does not identify the donors to the 501(c)(4). If the 501(c)(4) received funds from another 501(c)(4) to make the expenditure, that pass-through is also not disclosed in the FEC filing. The FEC database therefore shows how much a named organization spent, but not who funded the organization. This is the documented gap that the DISCLOSE Act was designed to close. The FEC filings that show the gap, the DISCLOSE Act text that would have closed it, and the Senate roll call record that shows who blocked it, are all in the public record simultaneously.

The Dark Money Totals

OpenSecrets, the nonpartisan research organization that aggregates FEC and IRS data on campaign finance, has tracked dark money spending in federal elections since 2010. Their published data shows that dark money spending, meaning spending by 501(c)(4)s and similar organizations that do not disclose donors, totaled approximately 309 million dollars in the 2012 election cycle, 174 million dollars in the 2014 cycle, 181 million dollars in the 2016 cycle, 150 million dollars in the 2018 cycle, 750 million dollars in the 2020 cycle, and 660 million dollars in the 2022 cycle. These figures are derived from FEC filings and IRS Form 990 data cross-referenced to identify political spending. The Senate roll call records for the DISCLOSE Act cloture votes in 2010, 2012, and subsequent sessions identify the specific senators who prevented the disclosure requirement from taking effect. The dark money spending totals for each election cycle following those votes are in the OpenSecrets database. The two records, who blocked the disclosure and how much was subsequently spent in the dark, are both in the public archive.

How the 501(c)(4) Architecture Works in Practice

A 501(c)(4) social welfare organization, exempt from federal income tax under Internal Revenue Code Section 501(c)(4), is required to disclose certain information to the Internal Revenue Service on Form 990. The Form 990 is a public document. It reports the organization's revenues, expenses, program activities, officer compensation, and, for certain large grants, the identities of grantee organizations. The Form 990 does not require disclosure of the names of individual donors. This is the core of the dark money architecture: the 501(c)(4) is publicly filed but privately funded. An individual or corporation can contribute millions of dollars to a 501(c)(4) organization, and the Form 990 will show that the organization received that amount in contributions, but the contributor's name will not appear in the public record. The FEC's independent expenditure disclosure system requires that when a 501(c)(4) makes independent expenditures in a federal election above $250 within a calendar year, it must file reports with the FEC disclosing the expenditure amount, the candidate affected, and the name and address of the organization making the expenditure. The organization's funders are still not required to be disclosed. The FEC disclosure shows what was spent and by whom at the organizational level. The IRS Form 990 shows the organization's total funding and operations. Neither document shows who funded the organization. The DISCLOSE Act, in its various introduced versions since 2010, would require the donor information currently missing from the public record to be disclosed to the FEC.

The Senate Cloture Vote Record for Each DISCLOSE Act Attempt

Cloture is the Senate procedure for limiting debate and ending a filibuster. A cloture vote requires sixty votes to succeed. If cloture fails, the bill cannot proceed to a final vote under Senate rules. The DISCLOSE Act has been blocked in the Senate through failed cloture votes in every session it has been considered. The cloture vote record for each DISCLOSE Act attempt is in the Senate roll call database at senate.gov. Each cloture vote is indexed by date, bill number, and the final tally. The record shows which senators voted for cloture (in favor of proceeding to a vote) and which senators voted against cloture or were absent. The senators who voted against cloture on the DISCLOSE Act, thereby blocking its consideration, produced a vote in the public record that is documented simultaneously with their campaign finance records showing whether they received contributions from organizations that would be required to disclose their donors under the DISCLOSE Act. The FEC contribution database, the OpenSecrets compilation of 501(c)(4) political spending data, and the Senate cloture vote record are three public records that together document the relationship between the dark money spending that the DISCLOSE Act would have regulated and the votes that prevented it from becoming law.

The 2024 Cycle Dark Money Total and Its Documentation

OpenSecrets compiles dark money spending data from FEC independent expenditure reports, electioneering communication reports, and IRS Form 990 filings for politically active 501(c)(4) organizations. The OpenSecrets dark money total for each election cycle represents the estimated aggregate spending by organizations that do not disclose their donors. The estimate is necessarily partial, because the definitional gap in the current disclosure system means that some politically active 501(c)(4) spending is not fully captured in FEC reports. The FEC requires reporting of independent expenditures, which are communications that expressly advocate for or against a candidate. Communications that discuss issues without expressly advocating for a candidate, including communications that heavily feature a candidate's image and positions within sixty days of a general election (electioneering communications) are separately reported. Spending that does not meet either definition is not reported to the FEC and is documented only through the organization's IRS Form 990 general category of "program service" spending. The total dark money spending in any cycle is therefore a floor estimate: the documented total from FEC reports represents the minimum, and the actual total including unreported issue advocacy spending is higher. OpenSecrets' methodology for estimating total dark money spending is documented at opensecrets.org/dark-money. The IRS Form 990 database is publicly searchable at ProPublica's Nonprofit Explorer at projects.propublica.org/nonprofits.

The IRS Form 990 and What It Does and Does Not Show

IRS Form 990 is the annual information return filed by most tax-exempt organizations, including 501(c)(4) social welfare organizations. The 990 requires disclosure of the organization's mission, program activities, governance, officer and director compensation, total revenues, total expenses, and grants made to other organizations above a certain threshold. The 990 does not require disclosure of individual donors, except for organizations that are private foundations (which file Form 990-PF). The Schedule B to Form 990 lists donors who gave $5,000 or more, but organizations are required to file Schedule B with the IRS and are permitted to redact donor information from the public copy. The IRS has ruled that 501(c)(4) organizations may decline to disclose their donors on the public version of the 990, which is why the Form 990 alone does not solve the dark money disclosure problem. ProPublica's Nonprofit Explorer at projects.propublica.org/nonprofits makes Form 990 data searchable and provides downloadable copies of publicly filed 990s for every 990-filing organization in the IRS database. The 990 data shows a politically active 501(c)(4)'s total revenues, which includes the undisclosed donor contributions, and its total political spending, reported in the 990's Schedule C. The gap between the disclosed revenue and the undisclosed donor list is precisely the transparency gap the DISCLOSE Act has sought to close in every session of Congress since 2010.
verified_source: 26 U.S.C. § 501(c)(4) :: Internal Revenue Code :: U.S. Congress; Treasury Regulation 1.501(c)(4)-1 :: 26 C.F.R. :: Internal Revenue Service; DISCLOSE Act (H.R. 5175) :: U.S. House, June 24, 2010 :: U.S. Congress; Senate Roll Call Vote 220 :: July 27, 2010 :: U.S. Senate; OpenSecrets Dark Money Database :: Center for Responsive Politics :: opensecrets.org

title: The "No Corporate PAC Money" Pledge Covers One Line on FEC Form 3. The Form Has Fourteen Lines. Schedule A Has No Limit.
teaser: The FEC Form 3 separates PAC contributions from individual contributions. The most common "no corporate PAC money" pledge covers one category on the form. Schedule A itemizes every individual contribution above $200 with the contributor's employer. Both are public. The FEC designed it that way.
body:
Starting in the 2018 election cycle, a new category of campaign pledge emerged in Democratic primaries: the promise to accept no contributions from corporate political action committees. The pledge was tracked and promoted by progressive organizations including Justice Democrats, the Progressive Change Campaign Committee, and End Citizens United. By the 2020 cycle, the pledge had been signed by hundreds of candidates. The FEC Form 3, which every congressional campaign files quarterly, separates receipts by source type. Line 11c reports "Other Political Committee Contributions," which is the formal FEC category for PAC contributions. A campaign that has pledged to accept no corporate PAC money and reports zero on Line 11c has kept the letter of the pledge as it is typically worded. What the pledge language, in most documented cases, does not cover is reported on a different line: Line 11a, "Itemized Individual Contributions." Schedule A, which itemizes all individual contributions above two hundred dollars, includes the contributor's occupation and employer. The Form 3 and its schedules are available for every campaign on FEC.gov, and the itemization is searchable without any programming knowledge.

The Definitional Architecture of "No Corporate PAC Money"

The pledge as typically worded covers contributions from political action committees sponsored by corporations. The FEC recognizes several categories of PAC that are not corporate PACs: connected PACs sponsored by trade associations represent industries rather than specific corporations; connected PACs sponsored by labor unions represent workers; nonconnected PACs raise money from individuals with no corporate sponsor; and Leadership PACs are sponsored by individual members of Congress rather than by any organization. A campaign that accepts contributions from a trade association PAC, a labor PAC, a nonconnected ideological PAC, or a colleague's Leadership PAC while reporting zero corporate PAC contributions has complied with the letter of the most common pledge formulation. The FEC's PAC registration database at FEC.gov identifies every registered committee, its type, and its connected organization, if any. Cross-referencing a campaign's itemized PAC contributions against the FEC PAC registry shows exactly which categories of PAC contributed to a campaign that has pledged to reject "corporate" PAC money. The pledge text, the FEC Line 11c data, and the PAC registry cross-reference are all available through public databases.

Individual Contributions and the Employer Disclosure

Schedule A of Form 3 requires itemization of every individual contribution above two hundred dollars, including the contributor's employer and occupation. The FEC's individual contribution database aggregates all Schedule A filings into a searchable format at FEC.gov/data/receipts/individual-contributions/. For a campaign that has pledged no corporate PAC money and accepted no corporate PAC contributions, the Schedule A database shows the employment background of individual donors. The database allows filtering by employer name. A search for individual contributors employed by a specific corporation to a specific campaign shows every donation from employees of that entity. The aggregate of individual contributions from employees of a single corporation is not a PAC contribution and is not covered by the typical "no corporate PAC money" pledge. The FEC's data systems treat individual contributions and PAC contributions as categorically distinct because they are legally distinct under 52 U.S.C. § 30116. Both appear in the Form 3 filing. Only one is addressed by the most common pledge language.

What the Complete Form 3 Shows

The complete Form 3 filing, with all schedules, shows the full financial picture of a campaign: PAC contributions by category (corporate, trade, labor, nonconnected, leadership), individual contributions by itemized donor with employer and occupation, party committee transfers, and candidate self-funding. The "no corporate PAC money" pledge addresses one cell of that matrix. The complete matrix is in every Form 3 filing on FEC.gov. The FEC designed the Form 3 to be publicly transparent precisely so that any voter can see the complete source breakdown of any campaign's funding. The pledge is a statement about one line. The Form 3 is a statement about all lines. Both are public. The gap between what the pledge covers and what the Form 3 shows is not a hidden document. It is a filing that has been publicly available since the day it was submitted. No investigative resource is required to find it. The FEC's disclosure system was designed so that it would not be.

The FEC Schedule A and What "Corporate PAC Money" Means in the Law

The phrase "corporate PAC money" has a specific legal meaning under FECA. Corporations are prohibited by 52 U.S.C. § 30118 from making direct contributions to federal candidates or national party committees from their treasury funds. To participate in campaign finance, corporations establish separate segregated funds, which are the corporate PACs. The corporate PAC solicits voluntary contributions from the corporation's employees, officers, and shareholders, and uses those funds to make contributions to federal candidates. The PAC's receipts and disbursements are reported on FEC Form 3X. A candidate who pledges to refuse "corporate PAC money" is pledging to refuse contributions from FEC-registered political action committees that are the separate segregated funds of corporations. The pledge does not address: individual contributions from corporate executives and employees (which are itemized on Schedule A under employer name); contributions from trade association PACs (which may represent industry interests without being a single corporation's separate segregated fund); contributions from Super PACs that receive unlimited corporate treasury funds; or contributions from LLCs and partnerships that are structured as pass-through entities not subject to the corporate PAC prohibition. Each of these contribution types is documented in different sections of the FEC's disclosure system. The Schedule A itemization of individual contributions, the FEC registration records showing the type of each PAC, and the independent expenditure database showing Super PAC spending are all public documents that together describe a complete picture of what the candidate refused and what the candidate did not refuse.

Trade Association PACs and Industry Concentration

Trade associations are organizations that represent the collective interests of businesses in a specific industry. The Pharmaceutical Research and Manufacturers of America, the American Bankers Association, the National Association of Realtors, and the American Medical Association are trade associations that operate political action committees. Trade association PACs are not individual corporations' separate segregated funds; they are separate segregated funds of the association itself. A candidate who refuses contributions from the Pfizer PAC (a corporate PAC) but accepts contributions from the PhRMA PAC (a trade association PAC) has documented contributions from pharmaceutical industry-affiliated political money while maintaining technical compliance with a "no corporate PAC" pledge, depending on how that pledge is interpreted. The FEC's PAC registration database identifies each PAC's type: "corporate," "trade/membership/health," "labor," "cooperative," or "nonconnected." A candidate who has pledged to refuse corporate PAC money can check each PAC contribution against the FEC PAC registration database to confirm the PAC type before accepting. The FEC database makes this check possible: searching the PAC's committee ID at FEC.gov/data/committees/ returns the registered committee type, which determines whether the contribution falls within the pledge's scope under a strict or a broad interpretation.

The Pledge Announcement and the FEC Schedule B Record

Candidates typically announce the "no corporate PAC money" pledge through a press release, a campaign website statement, or a social media post. These announcement documents are the starting point of the documentary record. They establish the date of the commitment, the specific language used, and the candidate's framing of what the pledge means. Press releases issued by campaigns are not always archived by the Wayback Machine, but they are frequently preserved in news coverage of the announcement. The announcement's specific language matters because the pledge's scope depends on how "corporate PAC money" is defined by the pledger. A pledge that says "I will not accept money from corporate PACs" describes a commitment defined by the FEC's registration category of "corporate" PAC. A pledge that says "I will not take money from PACs funded by corporations" describes a broader commitment that could encompass trade association PACs and Super PACs funded by corporate treasury money. The FEC's Schedule B reports for opposing campaigns, which document which corporate or corporate-adjacent PACs made contributions to other candidates in the same race, are also public records that provide context for the industry landscape the pledge was made against. All of these records, the pledge announcement, the FEC PAC contribution history, the FEC PAC registration database, and the independent expenditure database, coexist in the public archive and together define what the pledge encompassed and what occurred after it was made.

The Super PAC Spending That No Pledge Stops

The "no corporate PAC money" pledge addresses contributions made directly to the candidate's campaign committee or principal campaign committee. It does not and legally cannot address Super PAC spending in support of the candidate. A corporation can contribute unlimited amounts from its treasury to a Super PAC aligned with the candidate's election. The Super PAC can spend those funds on independent expenditures supporting the candidate. The candidate cannot refuse this spending. The candidate cannot return it. The candidate's pledge to refuse corporate PAC money is a commitment about one specific channel of campaign finance, and it exists simultaneously with an unlimited and legally unaddressable channel through which corporate money can support the same candidate's election. The FEC's independent expenditure database documents Super PAC spending in support of or in opposition to specific candidates, searchable by candidate name and election cycle. A candidate who has pledged to refuse corporate PAC money and whose election has received millions of dollars in Super PAC independent expenditure support, some of which came from Super PACs funded by corporate treasury contributions, has a campaign finance record that is complete on both facts: the absence of direct corporate PAC contributions to the campaign committee, documented in the campaign's FEC filings, and the presence of corporate-funded Super PAC spending in support of the candidate, documented in the FEC's independent expenditure database.

Valeo :: 424 U.S. 1 (1976) :: U.S. Supreme Court
