title: The Contract with America Promised a Term Limits Vote. The Vote Happened in 1995. The FEC Candidacy Database Documents What Happened to the Pledge-Signers After That.
teaser: House Joint Resolution 73, March 29, 1995: 227 in favor, 204 opposed, failed to reach two-thirds. The Contract with America's term limits promise was to bring a vote, not to pass one. U.S. Term Limits tracks what each pledge-signer did afterward. The FEC candidacy database documents the filings.
body:
The Contract with America was introduced by the House Republican Conference on September 27, 1994, six weeks before the midterm elections. It was signed by 367 Republican candidates on the Capitol steps. One of its ten provisions was a pledge to bring a constitutional amendment for congressional term limits to the floor of the House for a vote. The vote occurred on March 29, 1995, as House Joint Resolution 73. The amendment required a two-thirds supermajority, or 290 votes in a 435-member House, to pass. It received 227 votes in favor and 204 opposed. Four separate versions of the term limits amendment were voted on the same day, each establishing a different limit on House and Senate terms. None of the four reached two-thirds. The Contract with America's promise, as written, was to bring the amendment to a vote. The vote happened. The Contract with America did not promise to pass the amendment, only to vote on it. This distinction is in the text of the Contract, which is in the public record. What happened after 1995, over the next three decades, is also in the public record.

What U.S. Term Limits Documents

U.S. Term Limits is a nonprofit advocacy organization that has tracked term limits pledges since its founding in 1992. Its pledge program asks candidates to sign a specific written commitment to limit their own service to a defined number of terms: three terms in the House (six years) or two terms in the Senate (twelve years) in the standard pledge, though members have signed pledges specifying different limits. U.S. Term Limits maintains a public database of pledge signers and, separately, a documented list of pledge violations, which the organization calls its Honor Roll and Hall of Shame respectively. The pledge is a personal commitment document. It is not legally enforceable. It is, however, a public record: pledge signers are named in the organization's press releases, congressional campaign materials, and the pledge database itself. Cross-referencing the pledge database against the FEC candidacy database, which records every election in which a candidate has filed, produces the documented list of members who signed a pledge to a specific term limit and then filed for election beyond that limit. Both databases are public. The cross-reference produces a list that U.S. Term Limits itself maintains and publishes.

The FEC Candidacy Database and What It Shows

The FEC's candidacy database at FEC.gov/data/candidates/ records every individual who has filed a Statement of Candidacy (FEC Form 2) for a federal office. The database shows every election cycle in which a candidate filed, the office sought, the state, and the party. For any candidate who has filed multiple times, the database shows the complete chronological history of their federal candidacy filings. The database covers all federal elections from 1979 to the present. A candidate who signed a term limits pledge specifying a maximum of three terms in the House and then filed for a fourth term produced two records: the pledge document, which is in U.S. Term Limits' public archive, and the FEC Form 2 filing, which is in the FEC candidacy database. Neither requires interpretation. The pledge specifies a number. The FEC database shows how many times the candidate filed. The arithmetic is the documentary cross-reference.

The 1994 Class and What Followed

The 1994 midterm elections produced the largest shift in House seats since 1948. Fifty-four seats flipped from Democratic to Republican, producing the first Republican House majority since 1954. Seventy-three Republican freshmen were elected, many of them having signed the Contract with America and individual term limits pledges. The class of 1994 became the most documented cohort in U.S. Term Limits' tracking history. The 1994 class members' individual term limits pledges are documented in press releases, campaign materials, and the U.S. Term Limits archive from that cycle. The FEC candidacy database shows how many subsequent elections each member of the 1994 class filed for. The thirty-year documentary record, available through FEC.gov and the U.S. Term Limits archive, shows how the pledge language, the term specifications, the subsequent FEC filings, and the documented violations are distributed across the class. The record does not require editorial judgment to read. It requires the 1994 pledge text and the FEC candidacy history for the same individual, placed in the same frame.

How U.S. Term Limits Documents Its Pledge Program

U.S. Term Limits is a non-profit advocacy organization founded in 1992 that campaigns for constitutional and legislative term limits on members of Congress. The organization maintains a pledge program in which candidates for federal office sign a written commitment to serve no more than a specified number of terms: three terms in the House (six years) and two terms in the Senate (twelve years). Signed pledges are photographed and maintained in U.S. Term Limits' public archive. The organization's website, termlimits.com, maintains a publicly searchable database of pledge signers by state and congressional district. The database shows the member's name, the office for which the pledge was signed, the year of signing, and the member's current status with respect to the pledge. Members who have exceeded the pledged number of terms are identified in the database. U.S. Term Limits has tracked pledge compliance since the organization began collecting pledges and has publicly named members who exceeded their pledge commitment. The FEC's candidacy database at FEC.gov/data/candidates/ documents every election cycle in which a candidate filed a Form 2 (Statement of Candidacy) for federal office. The combination of the U.S. Term Limits pledge archive and the FEC candidacy database produces a complete record: the pledge specified a maximum number of terms, and the FEC database shows how many elections the pledger participated in as a candidate. The arithmetic is the documentary cross-reference.

The Constitutional Term Limits Litigation Record

State-level term limits for federal officeholders were struck down by the Supreme Court in U.S. Term Limits, Inc. v. Thornton (514 U.S. 779, 1995), which held that states cannot impose qualifications for federal office beyond those specified in the Constitution (age, citizenship, and residence). The Thornton decision means that the only legally enforceable term limits for members of Congress would require a constitutional amendment. The proposed term limits constitutional amendment has been introduced in multiple sessions of Congress and has not received the two-thirds vote in both chambers required for a constitutional amendment. The roll call record for votes on term limits constitutional amendment proposals is in the clerk's database. Members who signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, voted against the constitutional amendment that would have made term limits legally enforceable, and then served beyond their pledge commitment, produced three records: the pledge, the vote against the amendment, and the FEC candidacy database showing the additional terms. The vote against the constitutional amendment is not necessarily inconsistent with the pledge, since a voluntary pledge can be honored without a constitutional mandate. The inconsistency documented by the pledge archive and the FEC candidacy database is between the voluntary commitment and the voluntary breach of that commitment. The constitutional amendment vote adds context about the legal framework in which the voluntary pledge operates.

The Term Limits Pledge and Campaign Documentation

Candidates who sign the U.S. Term Limits pledge typically publicize the commitment during their campaigns. Campaign websites, news coverage of the campaign, and debate transcripts frequently reference the term limits commitment as a qualification the candidate is offering to voters. These campaign-era references to the pledge are archived in local news databases, in the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine, and in opposition research archives maintained by political parties and advocacy organizations. When a candidate who publicized a term limits pledge during their initial campaign subsequently runs for re-election in excess of the pledged number of terms, the campaign-era documentation of the pledge commitment and the FEC Form 2 filing for the subsequent campaign both exist in the public record. Local news coverage of the subsequent campaign may or may not address the pledge breach, depending on whether the issue is raised by opponents or journalists. The pledge archive at termlimits.com documents the commitment. The FEC candidacy database documents the filing history. The campaign-era news coverage documents the public commitment made to voters. All three are public records accessible through different channels: termlimits.com, FEC.gov, and news archives.

What Pledge Compliance Looks Like in the FEC Record

A member of Congress who honored a three-term House pledge would have filed three and only three FEC Form 2 statements of candidacy for House elections and then retired from the House at the end of the third term. The FEC candidacy database at FEC.gov/data/candidates/ shows the complete filing history for every candidate. For any member who signed the U.S. Term Limits pledge, the FEC database provides the ground truth on compliance: the number of separate election cycles for which the member filed as a candidate is the number of terms they sought. A member who signed a three-term pledge and filed Form 2 for a fourth term election produced a fourth entry in the FEC candidacy database that documents the breach. The database entry shows the filing date, the election cycle, the state, the district, and the party. The U.S. Term Limits pledge document shows the commitment. The FEC database shows the subsequent candidacy history. The arithmetic difference between the pledged number of terms and the number of Form 2 filings in the FEC database is the documented scope of the breach. U.S. Term Limits' public database at termlimits.com independently tracks this comparison and identifies members who have exceeded their pledges. Both the third-party tracking and the FEC primary source are public and independently verifiable.

title: You Cannot Legally Refuse a Super PAC. The Campaign Finance Law That Created Super PACs Also Makes It Illegal to Coordinate Stopping Them.
teaser: Citizens United v. FEC was decided 5-4 on January 21, 2010. The coordination prohibition at 11 C.F.R. § 109.20 makes it illegal to coordinate with a Super PAC, including to ask it to stop spending on your behalf. The condemnation and the benefit are both in the public record because the law requires it.
body:
Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission was decided by the United States Supreme Court on January 21, 2010, with a vote of 5 to 4. The decision held that the First Amendment prohibits the government from restricting independent political expenditures by corporations, associations, and labor unions. It created the legal foundation for the independent expenditure-only committees now commonly known as Super PACs. In the sixteen months following the decision, more than 80 Super PACs registered with the FEC. By the 2012 election cycle, they had spent more than 600 million dollars in federal races. The congressional response was immediate and bipartisan in its rhetoric: hundreds of members of Congress, in press releases, floor statements, and campaign announcements, condemned the decision as a threat to democratic integrity. The FEC records from those same election cycles document, in line-item detail, exactly how much Super PAC spending was directed in support of the members doing the condemning. Both records exist simultaneously and are available through the same federal disclosure system.

The Legal Architecture Most People Do Not Know

The feature of campaign finance law that makes the parallel record possible is rarely explained in coverage of Super PAC spending: it is illegal for a candidate to coordinate with a Super PAC that supports them. The coordination prohibition is codified at 11 C.F.R. § 109.20 and 11 C.F.R. § 109.21. "Coordination" is defined to include discussions of advertising strategy, media placement, polling data, or any other electioneering activity. If a candidate's campaign coordinates with a Super PAC, the Super PAC's expenditure becomes an in-kind contribution to the campaign, subject to contribution limits, making the expenditure a violation. This creates a specific documented legal reality: a candidate who publicly condemns a Super PAC spending money on their behalf cannot legally contact that Super PAC to ask it to stop. The legal mechanism that prevents illegal coordination also prevents voluntary refusal. A candidate can publicly disavow the spending. They cannot legally coordinate the disavowal with the Super PAC's decision-making. The condemnation and the benefit exist in the same documented record because the law that created the benefit also makes the benefit legally irrefusable through coordination.

What the FEC Form 24 Documents

Super PAC independent expenditures are reported to the FEC on Form 24, the 24-Hour Notice of Independent Expenditures, which must be filed within 24 hours of any independent expenditure exceeding 1,000 dollars made within 20 days before an election. The form identifies the committee making the expenditure, the candidate the expenditure supports or opposes, the amount, the type of communication, and the date. The FEC's independent expenditure database at FEC.gov aggregates all Form 24 filings and allows queries by candidate, election cycle, and committee. For any senator or representative who made public statements condemning Citizens United and Super PAC spending, the FEC independent expenditure database provides the documented record of Super PAC activity on their behalf. The two databases, the public statements archive and the FEC Form 24 filings, describe the same election from different angles. The FEC designed the Form 24 to be filed within 24 hours precisely so that voters could know, in real time, who was spending to influence an election. The real-time disclosure works. The record it produces is complete.

The DISCLOSE Act and What Was Not Passed

Congress attempted to respond to Citizens United with the Democracy Is Strengthened by Casting Light On Spending in Elections Act, introduced as H.R. 5175 in the House and S. 3628 in the Senate in June 2010, five months after the Citizens United decision. The Act would have required Super PACs and politically active 501(c)(4) organizations to disclose donors who contributed more than 10,000 dollars for political purposes. The Senate voted on cloture on July 27, 2010. The cloture vote failed 57-41, three votes short of the 60-vote threshold required to proceed to a final vote. Every senator who voted no on the cloture motion in July 2010 is in the Senate roll call record for that vote. The FEC's independent expenditure database shows what Super PACs spent in support of those senators in subsequent election cycles. The DISCLOSE Act, the cloture vote, and the independent expenditure filings are all available through congressional and FEC databases, and together they produce a specific documentary record of the gap between the stated position on Super PAC transparency and the documented record of Super PAC benefit.

Why the Candidate Cannot Control What the Super PAC Says

The independence requirement for Super PAC spending is not merely a legal technicality. Under FEC regulations implementing Citizens United and SpeechNow.org v. FEC (599 F.3d 686, D.C. Cir. 2010), a Super PAC that coordinates its spending with a candidate's campaign is treated as making a contribution to the campaign, which converts the unlimited spending into a contribution subject to the FECA limits. Coordination is defined under FEC regulations at 11 C.F.R. § 109.20-21, and includes sharing strategic information about campaign plans, message, targeting, or media plans. A Super PAC that asks the campaign what message to run and follows the answer has coordinated. A Super PAC that follows the candidate's public statements, public media schedules, and public policy positions without direct communication with the campaign has not coordinated under the FEC definition. The practical consequence of the non-coordination requirement is that the candidate and the Super PAC may be advancing incompatible messages, using incompatible targeting strategies, or spending on incompatible media markets, and the candidate has no legal mechanism to correct the Super PAC without creating a coordination problem. The candidate can disavow a Super PAC's message publicly. That public disavowal becomes a public record. The Super PAC can continue running the message the candidate disavowed. That continued spending is documented in the FEC's independent expenditure database. Both records coexist in the public archive.

The FEC Advisory Opinion Record on Coordination

The Federal Election Commission issues advisory opinions interpreting FECA and FEC regulations in response to requests from campaigns, committees, and other parties seeking guidance on specific contemplated activities. Advisory opinions are binding on the requesting party and provide general guidance on the activity described. The FEC's advisory opinion database, searchable at FEC.gov/legal/advisory-opinions/, contains hundreds of opinions on coordination questions. The opinions define the boundaries of permissible communication between campaigns and Super PACs, including the "firewall" arrangements that campaigns and Super PACs use to document their independence, the permissible use of public information by Super PACs without triggering coordination, and the specific categories of communication that constitute coordination. Reading the FEC's advisory opinions on coordination produces a precise understanding of what the candidate can and cannot legally do when a Super PAC begins spending on their behalf. The candidate cannot tell the Super PAC to stop. The candidate cannot tell the Super PAC what to say. The candidate can speak publicly about their positions, and the Super PAC can independently observe those public positions and craft its messaging accordingly, provided that no private communication occurs. The advisory opinion record documents exactly where the legal line falls and why candidates who claim independence from Super PACs spending on their behalf are describing a legal reality, not a political choice.

The Super PAC Disclosure Timeline and Strategic Implications

Super PAC independent expenditures above $10,000 made within twenty days of an election must be reported to the FEC within 48 hours. Expenditures made more than twenty days before an election are reported in the regular quarterly filing cycle. The 48-hour reporting requirement for late expenditures means that the FEC's independent expenditure database is updated in near-real time during the final weeks of an election campaign. The database documents the Super PAC's spending by candidate supported or opposed, the amount spent, the nature of the communication (television, radio, digital, mail, phone), and the target election. The candidate whose race is being spent on receives no advance notice of the spending under the non-coordination requirement; the spending may appear in the FEC database before the candidate's campaign team is aware of it through public channels. The FEC's independent expenditure database is publicly accessible at FEC.gov/data/independent-expenditures/ and is updated daily from 48-hour reports. A candidate who claims to oppose Super PAC spending in their race and whose race is the subject of millions of dollars in Super PAC independent expenditures has both claims in the public record: the candidate's public statement, and the FEC database documenting the spending they cannot stop. The FEC record makes the candidate's legal position visible in real time.

The Non-Coordination Safe Harbor and Its Limits

The FEC's non-coordination safe harbor for Super PACs rests on a specific legal architecture. A Super PAC that relies only on the candidate's public statements, publicly available voter files, public polling data, and other publicly available information when making its spending decisions has not coordinated with the campaign in the legally relevant sense. The candidate's campaign website, public speeches, social media posts, and news coverage are all publicly available sources that a Super PAC may use without triggering the coordination rule. The FEC's advisory opinions on coordination document the specific communications that cross the legal line: a campaign staff member who provides targeting data to a Super PAC, a campaign that shares its media buying schedule with a Super PAC, or a candidate who attends a Super PAC fundraising event with knowledge of what the Super PAC plans to do. The safe harbor is real, but it is narrow, and the FEC's enforcement record shows that coordination investigations are triggered by specific communications, not by the observation that the Super PAC's messaging is consistent with the campaign's. The candidate who says they have no control over a Super PAC spending on their behalf is describing a legal reality that the FEC's coordination rules create. The candidate who says the Super PAC's message does not represent them has produced a public disavowal that is in the record alongside the Super PAC's continued spending.

FEC :: 558 U.S. 310 (2010) :: U.S. Supreme Court; FEC Coordination Rules :: 11 C.F.R. §§ 109.20-109.21 :: Federal Election Commission; FEC Form 24 :: 52 U.S.C. § 30104(g) :: Federal Election Commission; DISCLOSE Act Cloture Vote :: Senate Roll Call Vote 220 (July 27, 2010) :: U.S. Senate

title: The FEC Form 3 Is Public. The "Fighting Special Interests" Campaign and the PAC Funding Line Are on the Same Federal Database.
teaser: The Federal Election Commission's Form 3 reports the exact dollar amount every congressional campaign raised from political action committees. The database is public, searchable, and requires no specialized access. The campaign announcement and the Form 3 describe the same campaign from different angles.
body:
The Federal Election Commission maintains a public database of every campaign finance filing in every federal election going back to 1980. The database is searchable at FEC.gov without registration, without a subscription, and without any specialized software. Every congressional campaign is required to file Form 3, the Statement of Receipts and Disbursements, which breaks down all money raised by source type: individual contributions, political action committee contributions, party committee transfers, and candidate self-funding. The line labeled "Total Contributions from Political Committees" shows the exact dollar amount a campaign received from PACs. Dividing that number by Total Receipts gives the percentage of a campaign's funding that came from political action committees. This number is available for every House and Senate race in every election cycle. It is not hidden. It requires no special access. It is the most direct documentary record of where campaign money actually comes from, and it is updated with every quarterly filing, every monthly filing, and every 48-hour and 24-hour pre-election report.

The "Fighting Special Interests" Campaign and What Form 3 Shows

The phrase "fighting special interests" has appeared in thousands of congressional campaign announcements and advertising scripts. It is among the most consistently used formulations in campaign messaging across both parties, across decades, and across every region of the country. The phrase is not defined by campaign finance law. "Special interests" is an informal category, not an FEC reporting category. Political action committees, however, are an FEC reporting category, and their contributions appear on a specific numbered line in every Form 3 filing. A campaign that uses "fighting special interests" as a central message and simultaneously reports 60 percent of total receipts from PAC contributions has produced two records: the campaign message, which is in the campaign's own advertising archive, and the FEC Form 3, which is in the public disclosure database. Neither record is secret. They are simply rarely placed side by side. The FEC's data download portal allows anyone to pull complete contribution records for any candidate in any cycle and calculate the PAC funding percentage without any programming knowledge required.

How the Form 3 Database Works

The FEC's online query system at FEC.gov allows candidate-level searches that return itemized receipts by source type. For Senate races, Form 3 is filed with the Secretary of the Senate and transmitted to the FEC. For House races, it is filed directly with the FEC. The database allows filtering by election cycle, state, and candidate name. Once a candidate's filing history is pulled, the summary pages show total PAC contributions and total individual contributions separately. The itemized PAC contributions include the name of each contributing PAC, the amount, and the date. The PAC name typically identifies the sponsoring entity: a corporation, a trade association, a labor union, or an ideological organization. A PAC named "XYZ Corporation Political Action Committee" identifies its sponsor directly. A PAC named "Committee for American Progress in Manufacturing" may require a cross-reference to the FEC's organization registration database, which is also public, to identify the sponsoring entity. Both databases are on the same website. The cross-reference takes minutes.

What the Record Produces

When the FEC contribution database and a campaign's public messaging archive are placed in the same frame, the record is specific. The campaign announcement is the edited version of the financial story. The Form 3 is the unedited version. The gap between the two is not a matter of investigative discovery. It is a matter of reading two documents that have always been publicly available and have always described the same campaign from different angles. The FEC's Electronic Filing System has made every quarterly report searchable since 2001. Pre-2001 filings are available in scanned form. There is no federal election in the past twenty-five years for which this cross-reference is not possible. The FEC designed the public disclosure system precisely so that voters could perform this cross-reference. The design works. The question the disclosure system was built to answer, in every election cycle, for every candidate, is: who is paying for this campaign? The Form 3 answers it by line item.

What FEC Form 3 Actually Reports

FEC Form 3 is the official report of receipts and disbursements for an authorized committee, which is the technical name for a candidate's principal campaign committee. The form reports total receipts, total disbursements, and cash on hand for the reporting period, and it itemizes individual contributions above $200 with the contributor's name, address, employer, occupation, and contribution date. The itemization threshold means that contributions below $200 are aggregated into an "unitemized receipts" total without individual contributor disclosure. The form also reports transfers from other committees, loans from the candidate, loans from banks, and debts owed. Every candidate for federal office who raises or spends more than $5,000 must file Form 3 and must do so on the FEC's disclosure schedule: monthly or quarterly during election years, with pre-election and post-election accelerated reports, and semi-annually in non-election years. The FEC has made its electronic filing system mandatory for all committees above a certain threshold and provides the raw data in bulk download format at FEC.gov/data. The Form 3 data is searchable by candidate name, committee ID, date range, and contributor information. The complete filing history for any federal candidate since 1994 (when electronic filing became widespread) is in the FEC's electronic filing database. Paper filings from earlier years are available through the FEC's archived records.

The "Fighting Special Interests" Claim and the Schedule A Record

Schedule A of FEC Form 3 is the itemized receipt schedule. It lists every individual contribution above $200 with the contributor's employer and occupation. The employer and occupation fields, required under the Federal Election Campaign Act, make it possible to identify contributions from employees of specific industries, trade associations, and lobbying firms without requiring the contributor to self-identify as a "special interest" representative. A contribution from an individual whose employer is listed as "Goldman Sachs" and whose occupation is listed as "Managing Director" is an itemized individual contribution from a financial industry professional. A contribution from an individual whose employer is listed as "Pharmaceutical Research and Manufacturers of America" and whose occupation is listed as "Vice President, Government Affairs" is an itemized individual contribution from a pharmaceutical industry lobbying organization employee. Neither contributor is required by the FEC to use the phrase "special interest" in their disclosure. The employer and occupation fields provide the factual basis for characterizing the contributor's industry affiliation. The candidate who claims to be fighting special interests while receiving Schedule A contributions from the employees of those interests has produced both the claim, in campaign materials and public statements, and the Schedule A record, in FEC filings. Both are public and both describe the same campaign finance reality from different angles.

The Revolving Door Between Campaigns and Lobbying

Federal lobbying disclosure under the Lobbying Disclosure Act of 1995 documents the clients, issues, and contacts of registered lobbyists. The LDA database at lda.senate.gov is searchable by lobbyist name, client name, and issue area. Former congressional staffers and former members of Congress who become registered lobbyists are subject to statutory cooling-off periods: two years for former members, one year for senior staff, before they may lobby their former chambers. After the cooling-off period, their lobbying activity is documented in the LDA database. The campaign finance record and the lobbying record can be cross-referenced: a lobbyist whose LDA filings show representation of a specific industry, and whose LDA registration shows prior employment in a congressional office, provides a documented connection between the former congressional role and the subsequent lobbying representation. The candidate who cites fighting special interests while their former staff are registered lobbyists for those interests, and while their campaign receives contributions from those former staff members, has a documented record across three public databases: the FEC campaign finance database, the LDA lobbying database, and the public employment records of the congressional office. All three are public and cross-referenceable by name.

The FEC's Bulk Data and What Researchers Can Build From It

The FEC publishes its complete contribution and expenditure database in bulk download format at FEC.gov/data/. The bulk data files are updated weekly and cover all federal election cycles from 1979 to the present. A researcher who downloads the individual contribution file for a specific election cycle has a dataset containing every itemized individual contribution to every federal candidate and committee in that cycle, with the contributor's name, employer, occupation, amount, date, and the recipient committee. This dataset allows aggregation by employer, by industry (using the OpenSecrets industry classification applied to employer names), by contribution size, and by any other field in the data. The FEC's bulk data is the foundation for all the secondary databases and analyses that aggregated campaign finance research uses. OpenSecrets, the Campaign Finance Institute, the Center for Responsive Politics, and academic researchers all draw on FEC bulk data as the primary source. A candidate who claims to fight special interests and whose FEC filings show itemized contributions from employees of lobbying firms, trade associations, and regulated industries is documented in the FEC's own bulk data. The analysis is reproducible by any researcher who downloads the bulk data files from FEC.gov. The methodology is the same one the FEC uses to produce its own summary reports. The raw data is public. The methodology is documented. The findings are reproducible.

Valeo :: 424 U.S. 1 (1976) :: U.S. Supreme Court

title: The National Debt Is $34 Trillion. The Defense Budget Is $886 Billion. The BRAC Process That Would Reduce It Has Been Refused Since 2005.
teaser: The CBO projects federal debt at 166 percent of GDP by 2054. The DoD has requested BRAC authority in every defense bill since 2013. Congress has refused every time. USASpending.gov documents the contracts. The Congressional Record documents the fiscal discipline speeches. Both are in the same archive.
body:
The national debt of the United States crossed 34 trillion dollars in January 2024. The Congressional Budget Office, in its Long-Term Budget Outlook published in March 2024, projected that the debt would reach 166 percent of gross domestic product by 2054 under current law. The CBO is a nonpartisan agency of Congress that produces the official budget projections used in congressional budget deliberations. The same Congress that receives the CBO projections also votes on the annual National Defense Authorization Act and the defense appropriations bill that together set the defense budget. The defense budget for fiscal year 2024, set by the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2024 signed as Public Law 118-31 on December 22, 2023, authorized 886 billion dollars in national defense spending. The CBO Long-Term Budget Outlook and the NDAA authorization are both products of the same congressional process. The floor statements warning about the national debt and the roll call votes authorizing the defense budget that is the single largest discretionary line item in the federal ledger are in the same Congressional Record.

What the Base Realignment Record Shows

The Base Realignment and Closure process, established by the Defense Base Closure and Realignment Act of 1990, Public Law 101-510, creates an independent commission to recommend consolidation of military installations as a means of reducing defense overhead costs. The Department of Defense has formally estimated that excess base capacity costs the federal government approximately 19 to 25 percent above efficient levels of infrastructure maintenance. Congress authorized BRAC rounds in 1991, 1993, 1995, and 2005. No BRAC round has been authorized since 2005, despite repeated requests from the Secretary of Defense under both Republican and Democratic administrations. The 2013, 2015, 2017, 2019, 2021, and 2023 NDAA debates each included formal DoD requests for new BRAC authority and congressional refusals. The pattern of refusal crosses party lines and is geographically explained by a consistent feature of the congressional record: every military installation has a congressional district, and every congressional district with a military installation has a member of Congress whose constituents are economically dependent on it. The floor statements opposing BRAC, preserved in the Congressional Record, frequently appear alongside floor statements warning about government spending and the national debt, from the same members, in the same sessions.

USASpending and the Defense Contract Record

USASpending.gov documents defense contracts by recipient, program, congressional district, and fiscal year. The Department of Defense's own database, the Defense Contract Action Data System, provides additional granularity. Together, these databases document defense contract spending at the congressional district level for every contract above the micro-purchase threshold. The documented pattern, consistent across decades of congressional appropriations, is that defense spending functions as a form of institutionalized federal investment that is insulated from the fiscal conservatism arguments applied to other categories of federal spending. Members who vote against the SNAP reauthorization on grounds of fiscal discipline, who oppose Medicaid expansion as an unaffordable federal commitment, and who invoke the national debt in floor statements opposing domestic discretionary spending, consistently support defense appropriations at or above the DoD request level and consistently oppose any BRAC process that would reduce the defense infrastructure in their districts. USASpending.gov documents the contracts. The congressional roll calls document the votes. The Congressional Record documents the floor statements on fiscal discipline. All three records are public and cross-referenceable.

The CBO Record of What Drives the Debt

The Congressional Budget Office has published analyses of the primary drivers of the long-term federal debt in multiple reports. The CBO's 2024 Long-Term Budget Outlook identifies the three primary drivers of projected debt growth as mandatory spending growth (primarily Social Security and Medicare driven by demographic aging), net interest payments on the existing debt, and the interaction between revenue levels and spending levels under current law. Discretionary spending, which includes defense, is projected to decline as a share of GDP under current law. The CBO analysis does not identify defense spending as the primary driver of long-term debt growth. It does document that discretionary spending decisions, made annually through the appropriations process, affect the short-term deficit trajectory. The NDAA debates and appropriations negotiations each year produce a documented record of who argues for fiscal discipline at the level of principle and who argues for specific spending increases at the level of their district's contracts and installations. The CBO budget analyses and the NDAA roll call records are public documents. They do not need to be cross-referenced by inference. They are cross-referenced by the members themselves, who receive the CBO projections, vote on the NDAA, and make floor statements on the national debt, all in the same session of Congress.

The BRAC Request History and the Congressional Response Record

The Department of Defense's formal requests for new Base Realignment and Closure authority are documented in the annual budget submission process. The DoD's budget justification documents, submitted to Congress each February with the President's budget request, contain the rationale for BRAC authority and the DoD's estimates of excess capacity and potential savings. These documents are public and available at comptroller.defense.gov under the annual budget materials. The Armed Services Committee's markup records, hearing transcripts, and NDAA conference reports document Congress's response to each BRAC request: the specific language declining to authorize BRAC, the reasons cited in committee debate, and the alternative approaches, if any, offered by the committee. The committee hearing record for NDAA debates is public and searchable at congress.gov. Members who sit on the Armed Services Committee and who represent districts with military installations have participated in the committee debates on BRAC authority and have cast votes in committee and on the floor on NDAA provisions that exclude BRAC authorization. The financial nexus between military base employment and regional economies is documented in the DoD's own economic impact studies, published for each major installation, and in the Bureau of Economic Analysis's local area economic data at bea.gov. The relationship between a committee member's district-level economic interest in maintaining a military installation and the committee's vote against BRAC authorization is traceable through these public records.

The Deficit Reduction and Defense Spending Arithmetic

The Congressional Budget Office publishes an annual Budget and Economic Outlook and periodic updates that include projections of defense and non-defense discretionary spending. The CBO's ten-year projections show that under current law, defense discretionary spending is projected to remain flat or decline modestly as a share of GDP, while mandatory spending on Medicare and Social Security grows as a share of GDP due to demographic aging. The CBO projections are public at cbo.gov and are updated after major legislation. The NDAA debate each year produces floor statements from members who advocate for defense spending increases and from members who advocate for deficit reduction. When the same member advocates for deficit reduction in the context of domestic discretionary spending and advocates for defense spending increases in the context of the NDAA, both positions are in the Congressional Record in the same session of Congress. The CBO projects the deficit impact of both the spending increase and the spending reduction independently. The fiscal arithmetic of the simultaneous positions is not an inference. The CBO scores each policy choice. The Congressional Record contains each floor statement. The arithmetic connecting them is in the CBO's published scorekeeping reports and baseline projections.

The Selective Fiscal Discipline Frame and Its Documentary Signature

The phrase "fiscal discipline" appears in the Congressional Record hundreds of times in each session of Congress. The term is applied to proposed spending on domestic programs, foreign aid, nutrition assistance, housing, and education. It is applied less frequently to defense spending and almost never to existing defense contracts and installations in the fiscal discipline advocate's own district. The pattern of selective application of fiscal discipline arguments is documented by the combination of two public records: the Congressional Record's index of members' floor statements mentioning fiscal discipline, deficit, or national debt, and the USASpending database showing defense contract spending in those same members' districts. The pattern is not subtle when the records are compared directly. A member who cites fiscal discipline as the basis for opposing a $50 million domestic program and who represents a district receiving $2 billion in annual defense contracts has produced both records in the public archive. The floor statement is indexed in the Congressional Record. The defense contract data is in USASpending. The comparison is between what the member said about fiscal discipline and what the member has accepted in federal spending, measured in the same dollar units, in the same fiscal year.

The Defense Contract and District Economy Link

The DoD's annual economic impact reports for major military installations estimate the total economic contribution of each installation to the surrounding regional economy, including direct employment, indirect employment through defense contractor supply chains, and induced employment from the spending of military and civilian workers. These economic impact reports are prepared by the DoD and by installation commanders and are public documents. They provide the financial context for understanding congressional resistance to BRAC: a large military installation that employs thousands of military and civilian personnel and supports thousands more indirect jobs represents a substantial share of the regional economy in many congressional districts. The economic impact report quantifies this contribution in dollar terms. The USASpending database documents the defense contract spending in the district's economy beyond the installation's direct payroll. The combination of the installation economic impact report and the USASpending defense contract data describes the total defense-funded economic activity in a congressional district. When a member who represents a district with this level of defense economic activity votes against BRAC authorization while citing fiscal discipline in the context of other spending, the economic impact data provides the context for understanding the incentive structure that the fiscal discipline argument is being applied against. The economic impact reports are public. The USASpending data is public. The vote record is public. All three are available without specialized access.

title: The SBA Released 525 Billion Dollars in PPP Loan Data. The Congressional Financial Disclosures and the Roll Call Records Are in the Same Public Archive.
teaser: The CARES Act passed 419-6. The SBA released the complete PPP loan dataset on December 1, 2020, after a federal court order. Congressional financial disclosures are public record. The cross-reference between the two databases is a function of public record access, not inference.
body:
The Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act, Public Law 116-136, was signed on March 27, 2020. The legislation created the Paycheck Protection Program with an initial appropriation of 349 billion dollars, subsequently expanded to 659 billion dollars through the Paycheck Protection Program and Health Care Enhancement Act. The program provided forgivable loans to small businesses to cover payroll costs during the pandemic closure period. The Small Business Administration administered the program. On December 1, 2020, under court order following litigation brought by news organizations, the SBA released the complete PPP loan dataset, including recipient names, loan amounts, and lender information, for the approximately 5.2 million loans totaling 525 billion dollars disbursed through the program. The dataset is public record. Separately, the congressional voting record on pandemic relief legislation is public record through the congressional roll call databases. Together, the two databases produce a specific documentary record when cross-referenced.

What the SBA Data Shows

The SBA PPP loan dataset, available through the SBA's own data portal and through the Treasury Department's reporting portal established under the CARES Act's transparency requirements, identifies loan recipients by name, NAICS code, congressional district, loan amount, and forgiveness status. Businesses linked to members of Congress or their immediate family members are identifiable in the dataset through cross-reference with congressional financial disclosure filings, which are public record searchable through the House Financial Disclosure database and the Senate Financial Disclosure database. Multiple documented cases exist in the public record. Representative Roger Williams of Texas, who chairs the House Small Business Committee, reported in congressional financial disclosures that his dealerships received between 1 million and 2.17 million dollars in PPP loans. Williams voted for the CARES Act but subsequently voted against several subsequent pandemic relief measures. Representative Vicky Hartzler of Missouri reported that her family farm received a PPP loan. Senator Kelly Loeffler of Georgia, before her 2021 Senate runoff loss, reported ownership stakes in entities that received PPP funds. The financial disclosure and the SBA records are cross-referenceable through the public databases without any proprietary data access.

The Votes Against Expansion

The CARES Act passed the House 419-6 and the Senate 96-0 on March 25 and 27, 2020 respectively. The subsequent COVID relief legislation produced more contested voting records. The HEROES Act, which passed the House in May 2020 with additional pandemic relief provisions, passed 208-199 with near-party-line opposition from Republicans. The Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2021, which included 900 billion dollars in additional COVID relief, passed 359-53 in the House and 92-6 in the Senate in December 2020. The American Rescue Plan Act, Public Law 117-2, which provided the third major round of COVID relief, passed the House 219-212 and the Senate 50-49 in March 2021 on party-line votes, with no Republican support in either chamber. The documented pattern in the cross-referenced records is that several members who received or whose associated entities received PPP loans from the first round of relief were among those who opposed subsequent rounds of relief for other businesses. The SBA database and the roll call records are both public and searchable.

The Transparency Litigation Record

The SBA initially refused to release the names of PPP loan recipients, arguing that the information was proprietary business data protected from FOIA disclosure. A coalition of news organizations, including the Washington Post, the New York Times, and ProPublica, filed FOIA lawsuits seeking the disclosure. U.S. District Court Judge James Boasberg ruled on November 5, 2020, that the SBA was required to release full loan recipient data, finding that the public interest in transparency for federal spending outweighed the claimed privacy interests. The SBA released the complete dataset on December 1, 2020. The litigation record, the court ruling, and the released dataset are all public record. The dataset's release was itself a documented exercise in government transparency: the federal government created a program, distributed 525 billion dollars from it, initially declined to say publicly who received the money, was ordered by a federal court to disclose the recipients, and then released a dataset that allowed any member of the public to cross-reference the recipients against any other public record. The congressional financial disclosure filings that allow that cross-reference are updated annually and are searchable through the congressional disclosure databases maintained by the Clerk of the House and the Secretary of the Senate.

The Court-Ordered SBA Disclosure and What It Produced

The Small Business Administration initially resisted disclosing the names of PPP loan recipients, arguing that the loan data was confidential business information protected from FOIA disclosure under Exemption 4 of the Freedom of Information Act. News organizations and advocacy groups filed FOIA requests and, after initial denials, pursued litigation in federal court. The SBA agreed to release recipient data following the federal court order. The data released in July 2020 covered loans of $150,000 or more by disclosing recipient name, business address, loan amount range, lender, jobs retained, business type, and the congressional district. Subsequently, more complete loan-level data covering all PPP loans was released. The ProPublica Paycheck Protection Program Loan Database and the Small Business Administration's own PPP data portal both compile the released data in searchable form. The SBA's data portal is at sba.gov. The ProPublica database includes data from both the initial partial release and the subsequent more complete releases. The released data shows the loan amount, the recipient entity name, and the congressional district for every PPP loan above the disclosure threshold. Cross-referencing this data against financial disclosures and campaign finance records for members of Congress is a function of the three public databases: SBA loan data, OGE or congressional financial disclosures, and FEC contribution records.

The Small Business Definition and Who Qualified for PPP

The Paycheck Protection Program was authorized under the CARES Act (Public Law 116-136, March 27, 2020) for small businesses, non-profits, sole proprietors, independent contractors, and self-employed individuals. The size standard for most industries was 500 employees or fewer, though some industries used different SBA size standards. Large publicly traded companies, hedge funds, and private equity firms were initially excluded or discouraged, though some received loans before the exclusions were clarified. The program's design reflected Congress's intent to reach small businesses and their employees through a mechanism that used existing bank relationships and SBA-approved lenders to process loans quickly. The documented recipients of PPP loans include entities that are not small businesses in the conventional sense: large restaurant chains, hotel groups, sports organizations, law firms, and members of Congress or their business entities. The SBA's published eligibility criteria and the actual distribution of loans, as documented in the SBA's own loan data, are both public records. The eligibility criteria are in the CARES Act at Section 1102 and in SBA's implementing regulations. The actual distribution is in the SBA's released loan data. The gap between the program's stated small business focus and the documented distribution of funds is a function of the program design, not a failure of the disclosure system.

The Small Business Advocacy and PPP Recipient Record

Members of Congress who publicly advocated for the PPP as a lifeline for small businesses during floor debate and in press statements, and whose own business entities or family business entities subsequently received PPP loans, produced a documented sequence: the floor statements and press releases in the public record, and the loan recipient data in the SBA database. The floor statements and press releases are archived on member websites and in news archives. The SBA loan data is at sba.gov and in the ProPublica database. The financial disclosures that members file annually with the House or Senate ethics offices document business interests and partnership income, which in some cases overlaps with the business entities that appear in the SBA loan data. Reading the financial disclosure, the SBA loan database, and the member's public statements about PPP in sequence produces a complete account of what the member said about the program, what entities associated with the member received under the program, and what those entities' financial relationships to the member are under the disclosure system. The CARES Act authorization, the SBA loan data, and the financial disclosure system are all public records maintained by federal agencies and available without subscription at sba.gov, the House disclosure system, and efts.senate.gov.

The ProPublica PPP Database and Independent Verification

ProPublica's Paycheck Protection Program Loan Database, available at projects.propublica.org/coronavirus/bailouts, compiles SBA PPP loan data from the multiple disclosure releases that followed the court-ordered disclosure. The database is searchable by borrower name, city, state, loan range, lender, and NAICS industry code. ProPublica's compilation of the SBA data has been used by reporters, government oversight bodies, and academic researchers to identify patterns in PPP loan distribution, including loans to members of Congress, their families, and their business associates. The primary source for all searches in the ProPublica database is the SBA's own released data. ProPublica documents its data sourcing and methodology at the database's information page, allowing researchers to verify results against the original SBA data at sba.gov. The combination of the SBA primary data and the ProPublica secondary compilation provides two independent routes to the same loan records, allowing cross-verification of any specific loan finding. A specific PPP loan identified in the ProPublica database can be verified in the SBA's own data using the loan number or borrower name. The court-ordered disclosure that produced the data, the SBA's data release, and the ProPublica compilation are all documented events in the public record. The loan data itself is the primary source. The database is the search tool.

SBA :: U.S. District Court, D.D.C. (November 2020) :: Federal Judiciary; House Financial Disclosure Database :: Clerk of the U.S. House of Representatives :: disclosures.house.gov; American Rescue Plan Act :: Public Law 117-2 (2021) :: United States Congress

title: The Federal Government Has Paid $425 Billion in Farm Subsidies Since 1995. The Distribution Is in the USDA Record.
teaser: The EWG Farm Subsidy Database, built from USDA records, documents $425 billion in payments from 1995 to 2021. The top 10 percent of recipients received 78 percent. The distribution, the recipient list, and the voting records of the legislators who defend the program are all in the public record.
body:
The federal government has paid 425 billion dollars in farm subsidies between 1995 and 2021, documented in the Environmental Working Group Farm Subsidy Database, the most comprehensive public record of federal agricultural payments available outside the USDA itself. The payments flow through programs including the Agricultural Risk Coverage program, the Price Loss Coverage program, the Conservation Reserve Program, and direct commodity support for corn, soybeans, wheat, cotton, and dairy. The distribution of those payments is documented in the same database: the top 10 percent of recipients received 78 percent of all subsidy payments over the period. The bottom 80 percent of recipients received an average of less than 9,000 dollars each across the entire twenty-six-year span. The recipients in the top decile are not, in the aggregate, the small family farms most frequently invoked in the political defense of agricultural support programs. They are large commercial operations, agricultural corporations, and in a number of documented cases, entities whose principals have simultaneously advocated for reducing government dependency in other program areas. Both the subsidy payments and the public statements are in verifiable records.

What the EWG Database Documents

The Environmental Working Group Farm Subsidy Database draws on USDA payment records obtained through the Freedom of Information Act and compiles them into a searchable public database covering payments from 1995 through the most recent available fiscal year. The database allows searches by recipient name, state, county, and program type. It documents the total amount each recipient has received across the full period of available records. The USDA's own data, cross-referenced against the EWG compilation, shows that federal farm subsidies disproportionately benefit large-scale commodity operations. A 2019 analysis by the Government Accountability Office, reviewing USDA Farm Service Agency records, found that 62 percent of farms received no federal subsidy payments and that the distribution of payments was heavily concentrated among operations with gross cash farm income above 1 million dollars annually. The same GAO analysis found that the eligibility rules, particularly the actively engaged in farming standard, had been interpreted broadly enough to allow payments to flow to landowners with minimal operational involvement in the farming activity generating the payment.

The Legislative Record Alongside the Payment Record

The congressional voting record on agricultural appropriations, farm bill reauthorizations, and food assistance programs provides the comparative record. The Farm Bill, reauthorized approximately every five years, governs both agricultural commodity support programs and the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program. The two programs have been linked in the same legislative vehicle since 1973, creating a documented political dynamic in which urban legislators support commodity programs in exchange for rural legislator support of food assistance, and vice versa. The 2018 Farm Bill, Public Law 115-334, passed the House 369-47 and the Senate 87-13. Floor statements in the Congressional Record show that multiple legislators who voted for the 2018 Farm Bill's commodity support provisions had in the same or adjacent congressional sessions voted against expansions of SNAP, Medicaid, and housing assistance on grounds that such programs created dependency on federal support. The commodity support programs and the food assistance programs that were simultaneously defended and opposed by the same members are both documented in the same Farm Bill they voted for.

The Documented Recipients in the Public Record

The EWG database and USDA payment records document individual recipients by name where those records are subject to FOIA disclosure. Court decisions, including a 2012 ruling in a case brought by the Kansas City Star and other news organizations, have affirmed the public's right to access individual payment records. The database shows documented payments to agricultural operations connected to individuals who have made public statements opposing government subsidy programs in general while supporting the continuation of agricultural support programs specifically. The pattern is not unique to one party, one region, or one commodity sector. It is documented across corn country in the Midwest, cotton country in the South, and dairy operations in the Northeast and Upper Midwest. The USDA payment records and the Congressional Record statements exist in the same public archive. No cross-referencing tool is required to find them simultaneously. The EWG database provides the payment record. The Congressional Record provides the floor statements. Together they constitute a complete documentary record of the gap between the position and the receipt.

The EWG Farm Subsidy Database and Its Methodology

The Environmental Working Group's farm subsidy database at ewg.org/farm-subsidy draws from USDA records obtained through Freedom of Information Act requests and published federal data. The database documents subsidy payments by recipient name, farm program, payment amount, year, and county. It covers all major USDA commodity support programs including direct payments (discontinued in 2014), counter-cyclical payments, price loss coverage, agricultural risk coverage, conservation program payments, and disaster assistance. The EWG database is one of the most comprehensive public tools for researching farm subsidy distribution and has been used by journalists, researchers, and the government's own oversight bodies. The USDA Economic Research Service and the Farm Service Agency also publish subsidy data through their own systems. The FSA publishes county-level data on program participation and payment amounts. The USDA's payment data, combined with property records at the county level (which document land ownership), allows researchers to trace subsidy payments to their ultimate beneficiaries, including members of Congress, their families, and major agricultural corporations. The Government Accountability Office has published analyses of farm subsidy distribution and the concentration of payments among larger operations, available through gao.gov. The documentary standard for farm subsidy research is the same as for any other federal spending program: who received the payment, how much, under what program, and in which year. All of these fields are in the USDA's payment data.

The Farm Bill Debate and the Subsidy Recipient Overlap

The farm bill is a multi-year omnibus agricultural policy bill reauthorized approximately every five years. It covers commodity support programs, nutrition programs including SNAP, conservation programs, rural development, crop insurance, and trade promotion. The commodity support programs, which account for the subsidy payments documented in the EWG database, are the subject of ongoing policy debate about payment concentration, subsidy limits, and the appropriate role of the federal government in agricultural markets. Members of Congress who receive farm subsidies through their agricultural operations, or whose family members receive subsidies, and who also vote on the farm bill and participate in the House and Senate Agriculture Committees that draft the legislation, are in a position where their private financial interests and their official legislative responsibilities overlap in a documented and publicly visible way. The Agriculture Committee membership is at agriculture.house.gov and agriculture.senate.gov. The members' financial disclosures, which cover farm operations and agricultural income above disclosure thresholds, are at disclosures.house.gov and efts.senate.gov. The EWG database covers subsidy payments by recipient name. These three public records, read together, document the overlap between subsidy recipients, subsidy legislators, and subsidy oversight officials.

The Subsidy and Free Market Argument Combination

The policy argument that agricultural markets should operate on free market principles, without federal price supports, is a legitimate economic position. It has been advanced in various forms by economists, policy analysts, and some members of Congress over multiple decades. The Congressional Record contains numerous floor statements from members arguing that federal agricultural subsidies distort markets, create dependency, and represent an inappropriate federal role in private economic activity. The EWG database and the USDA payment records document which members of Congress, or their families or farm operations, receive the subsidies that those same floor arguments characterize as market distortions. The documentary cross-reference is not between an abstract policy position and a personal financial interest. It is between a documented floor statement about agricultural subsidy policy and a documented USDA payment record showing the member or their family as a payment recipient. Both records are public. The floor statement is in the Congressional Record. The payment record is in the USDA database and the EWG compilation. The cross-reference is the record of what the member said about the policy and what the member received under the policy. The record is complete on both points.

How Farm Program Eligibility and Payment History Connect to Legislative Positions

USDA's Farm Service Agency administers the commodity support programs that generate most farm subsidy payments. Participation in FSA programs requires enrollment by the farm operator, documentation of the farm's commodity production history, and annual certification of planted acres and yields. The FSA maintains records of each participating farm operation's program history, including payment amounts by year and program. These records are the underlying data source for the EWG farm subsidy database and for USDA's own published data. When a member of Congress or their family member is listed as a payment recipient in the FSA records, the listing reflects enrollment in the FSA program on behalf of a qualifying farm operation. The farm operation's program participation is a documented fact in the FSA record. The member's or family member's connection to the operation may be documented in property records, corporate filings, or the member's financial disclosure if the operation's income or assets exceed the disclosure threshold. The USDA farm subsidy data and the member's financial disclosure, read together, produce the documentary link between the farm program participation and the member's official position. Both records are public. Both are searchable. The EWG database makes the connection searchable by name at ewg.org/farm-subsidy. The underlying USDA data is the primary source.

title: The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act Passed 228-206. The Members Who Voted No Did Not Stop the Grant Announcements.
teaser: House Roll Call 369, November 5, 2021: 228 for, 206 against. Thirteen Republicans who voted yes lost committee assignments or faced primary challenges. USASpending.gov documents where the money went. The vote record and the grant database, placed together, produce a specific result.
body:
The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, formally enacted as Public Law 117-58 on November 15, 2021, appropriated 1.2 trillion dollars for transportation, broadband, water systems, and energy infrastructure, including 550 billion dollars in new federal investment above baseline highway funding. The House passage vote, Roll Call 369, recorded on November 5, 2021, produced a final count of 228 in favor and 206 opposed. Six Democrats voted no. Thirteen Republicans voted yes, and those thirteen faced immediate retaliation from Republican leadership, including removal from committee assignments and formal primary challenges funded by aligned political organizations. The 201 Republicans who voted no faced no equivalent institutional consequence. What followed in the months and years after enactment is a matter of public record: USASpending.gov tracks every dollar disbursed under the legislation, and congressional offices track every announcement of a funded project in their districts. Both databases are public. The pattern they produce when cross-referenced with Roll Call 369 is the subject of this article.

What USASpending.gov Documents

USASpending.gov is the federal government's official source for data on federal spending. The database tracks grants, contracts, direct payments, and loans by recipient, purpose, state, county, and congressional district. Under the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, USASpending.gov documents every disbursement against the legislation's forty-one titled programs, including the BEAD broadband program, the Carbon Reduction Program, the Rebuilding American Infrastructure with Sustainability and Equity grants, the Bridge Investment Program, and the Safe Streets and Roads for All program. The database is updated continuously and is searchable by congressional district. A query of IIJA disbursements by congressional district cross-referenced against Roll Call 369 produces a dataset in which no-voting members' districts appear as recipients of infrastructure funding at rates proportional to their district's demographic and infrastructure characteristics, independent of the member's vote. The legislation did not restrict grants to districts whose members voted yes. The money follows the formula and the competitive grant criteria, not the vote. This is documented in the program rules, published in the Federal Register.

The Documented Communications Pattern

The Government Accountability Office reviewed IIJA implementation in multiple reports beginning in 2022. Separately, the Washington Post documented in June 2022 that at least thirteen House Republicans who voted against the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act had sent press releases or attended public ceremonies for IIJA-funded projects in their districts within six months of the bill's enactment. The Post's documentation relied on congressional press releases, which are published by congressional offices and archived at their official websites, and on federal grant announcement records, which are public through USASpending.gov and agency press offices. The thirteen Republicans documented in the June 2022 reporting did not include all no-voting members who subsequently communicated positively about infrastructure funding in their districts. The documented thirteen represent only those instances where the timeline between the vote and the announcement was short enough to draw a direct documentary connection from public sources available at the time of reporting.

The Thirteen Who Voted Yes

The record also documents what happened to the thirteen Republicans who voted yes on Roll Call 369. Representatives Don Bacon (NE), Brian Fitzpatrick (PA), Andrew Garbarino (NY), Anthony Gonzalez (OH), John Katko (NY), Adam Kinzinger (IL), Nicole Malliotakis (NY), David McKinley (WV), Tom Reed (NY), Chris Smith (NJ), Fred Upton (MI), Jeff Van Drew (NJ), and Don Young (AK) voted yes. Kevin McCarthy, then House Minority Leader, called the vote a mistake and stated publicly that the members had "let Democrats off the hook." David McKinley of West Virginia, whose state received among the highest per-capita IIJA allocations in the country, lost his Republican primary in 2022 to a Trump-endorsed opponent. The primary challenge was directly linked to his IIJA vote. The infrastructure funding continued to flow to West Virginia regardless of the primary outcome. Both records, the primary challenge documentation in FEC filings and the West Virginia IIJA disbursement data in USASpending.gov, are public.

The USASpending Documentation Standard

USASpending.gov is the official source for federal spending data, established pursuant to the Federal Funding Accountability and Transparency Act of 2006, Public Law 109-282, and updated by the DATA Act of 2014, Public Law 113-101. Every federal contract, grant, loan, and direct payment above the reporting threshold is documented at USASpending.gov with the recipient's name, the award amount, the place of performance, the program, the awarding agency, and the congressional district. The DATA Act required federal agencies to adopt common data standards so that spending information across agencies can be aggregated, searched, and compared. For Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act awards, the relevant federal agencies upload spending records to USASpending as awards are made. Each infrastructure award appears in the database with a place-of-performance congressional district code, which identifies the congressional district where the project is located. This district code can be cross-referenced against the roll call vote record for the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. The roll call for the House vote is at clerk.house.gov under Roll Call 369, recorded on November 5, 2021. The Senate roll call was recorded on August 10, 2021. Both are indexed by member name, state, and district. The cross-reference of USASpending district codes against the roll call district identifiers is a mechanical process, not an analytical one. The database produces the comparison when queried by district.

The Washington Post Credit-Claiming Study and What It Documents

The Washington Post's reporting on members of Congress who voted against the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act and subsequently attended ribbon-cutting events or issued press releases about funded projects is itself a documentary product: the reporters compared official members' press releases and event announcements against the roll call vote record. Press releases issued by congressional offices through the House and Senate communication offices are public documents. They are distributed to local media, posted on official member websites, and archived. Many are entered into the Congressional Record by members who insert them as extensions of remarks, making them part of the permanent congressional archive. The ribbon-cutting events documented by local news coverage are in news archives searchable by publication and date. The combination of the vote record, the press release record, and the local news archive produces a complete documentary chain from the vote against the bill to the public appearance at a project funded by the bill. The Washington Post study systematically compared these three record types. The methodology is in the published reporting. The underlying data, the vote record and the USASpending award data, are independently verifiable through government databases. The news coverage and press releases are independently verifiable through news archives and the Wayback Machine.

The Formula Grant Distribution and Congressional Representation

The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act distributed funding through multiple mechanisms. Formula grants, which are the largest category, distribute funds to states based on statutory formulas that account for factors including population, lane-miles of federal-aid highways, vehicle miles traveled, and bridge deck area. The formula distribution means that every state receives funding, regardless of how its representatives voted on the bill. A state whose entire congressional delegation voted against the Infrastructure Act still receives formula grant funding through its state transportation agency. The state transportation agencies, not the individual members of Congress, administer most formula grant spending. The federal-aid highway funding flows from the Federal Highway Administration to state departments of transportation, which then select and fund specific projects. The projects funded from federal-aid highway dollars are documented in FHWA's project-level database and in USASpending. The fact that a member of Congress did not vote for the legislation that funds a project in their district does not prevent the funding from reaching the district. The formula grants flow based on the statutory formula, not on the voting record of individual members. The documented tension is between the member's stated opposition to the legislation and their subsequent public association with the projects it funds.

The Press Release Archive and Local News Record

Congressional offices issue press releases about federal investments in their districts through the House and Senate communication offices. These press releases are public documents distributed to local media and posted on official member websites. Local news organizations, including newspapers, television stations, and digital news outlets, publish stories about federal infrastructure projects in their coverage areas. The combination of the official press release, the local news story, and the vote record creates a three-source documentary chain that is reproducible for any member who appeared at an infrastructure funding event after voting against the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. The Wayback Machine at web.archive.org archives member websites at irregular intervals; press releases posted on official sites in 2022 and 2023 may be accessible through archived snapshots even if the current site does not maintain a searchable archive. Local news archives, often accessible through newspaper websites or through database services at public libraries, contain the contemporaneous coverage of project announcements and ribbon-cutting events. The roll call vote record at clerk.house.gov is permanent and searchable by date and member name. Together, these three sources constitute the public documentary chain for evaluating the relationship between the vote and the subsequent public association with the project's benefits.
