title: The CHIPS and Science Act Passed 243-187. The Job Announcements That Followed Went to Districts Whose Members Voted No.
teaser: House Roll Call 394, July 28, 2022: 243 for, 187 against. The Department of Commerce CHIPS Program Office award database and the Roll Call 394 tally, placed in the same frame, produce a specific geographic record. The investments went where the votes were not.
body:
The CHIPS and Science Act of 2022, formally enacted as Public Law 117-167 on August 9, 2022, appropriated 52.7 billion dollars for domestic semiconductor manufacturing incentives, 24 billion dollars in investment tax credits for chip fabrication facilities, and 200 billion dollars over ten years for scientific research. The House passage vote, Roll Call 394, recorded on July 28, 2022, produced a final count of 243 in favor and 187 opposed. Opposition was concentrated in the Republican caucus, where 187 of 211 voting Republicans recorded no. The floor statements opposing the bill, preserved in the Congressional Record, centered on the cost, the appropriateness of industrial policy, and what multiple members characterized as federal government picking winners and losers in private markets. That argument is also in the congressional record. So is what followed. Within twenty months of enactment, the Department of Commerce had announced more than 460 billion dollars in combined public and private semiconductor investment commitments, the majority of it flowing to states and congressional districts whose representatives are in the 187 on Roll Call 394.

What the Department of Commerce Database Documents

The CHIPS Program Office at the Department of Commerce maintains a public record of all CHIPS Act awards, including the amount, the recipient, the facility location, and the congressional district. The database is updated as awards are finalized and is searchable by state and recipient. Intel Corporation received 8.5 billion dollars in direct CHIPS Act funding and up to 11 billion dollars in government loans, announced by the Department of Commerce on March 20, 2024, for semiconductor fabrication facilities in New Albany and Johnstown, Ohio. The congressional record shows that Brad Wenstrup, representing Ohio's Second Congressional District, voted no on Roll Call 394. Warren Davidson, representing Ohio's Eighth Congressional District, voted no. Mike Carey, representing Ohio's Fifteenth Congressional District, voted no. The CHIPS Program Office's award documentation and the Roll Call 394 tally are both accessible through federal government databases, and the geographic overlap between the investment locations and the districts of no-voting members is a feature of the record, not an inference from it.

The Pattern Across Multiple States

The Ohio case is the most extensively documented, but the award database and the roll call record together produce the same pattern in multiple states. TSMC received 6.6 billion dollars in direct CHIPS Act funding, announced April 8, 2024, for three semiconductor facilities in Phoenix, Arizona. The Phoenix metropolitan area's congressional delegation at the time included David Schweikert, Andy Biggs, and Paul Gosar, all of whom voted no on Roll Call 394. Samsung received 6.4 billion dollars in CHIPS Act funding for facilities in Taylor, Texas, announced April 15, 2024. Keith Self, representing Texas's Third Congressional District, which encompasses Sherman and the northern Dallas suburbs adjacent to Taylor, voted no on Roll Call 394. Micron Technology received 6.1 billion dollars for facilities in Clay, New York, and Boise, Idaho. Claudia Tenney, representing New York's Twenty-Fourth Congressional District covering Clay, voted no. The award database, the roll call, and the district boundaries are all public records. The pattern they produce when cross-referenced is not an editorial judgment. It is what the data shows when the two databases are placed in the same frame.

The Press Release Record

Congressional offices maintain searchable archives of press releases and public statements. The Congressional Research Service, a nonpartisan research agency of Congress, published an analysis of CHIPS Act implementation in September 2023 that noted, without editorial characterization, that investment announcements in members' districts generated positive public communications from those offices regardless of the member's Roll Call 394 vote. The CRS analysis documented this as a feature of the legislative and public communications record. The press releases and public statements welcoming the Intel Ohio investment, the TSMC Arizona investment, and the Samsung Texas investment from members who had voted against the legislation that structured the incentive framework are archived in the congressional communications record. The roll call position and the public communications position are both in the record. Neither is amended retroactively. The congressional record preserves both simultaneously, and both are verifiable by the same documentary standard.

How the Department of Commerce Award Documentation Works

Every CHIPS Act award goes through a multi-stage process that generates multiple public documents. The process begins with an application to the CHIPS Program Office, which reviews the application and, for large awards, negotiates preliminary terms with the applicant. When preliminary terms are agreed upon, the Department of Commerce issues a "preliminary memorandum of terms," a public announcement documenting the award amount, the recipient, the facility location, and the program conditions. The preliminary memorandum is followed by a final award agreement after due diligence and negotiation of specific conditions. Each stage of this process produces a public announcement through the Department of Commerce's press release system and an entry in the CHIPS Program Office's public award tracker at commerce.gov. The award tracker is searchable by state and by recipient. For each award, it documents the congressional district in which the facility is located. The congressional district of each funded facility can be cross-referenced against the Roll Call 394 tally using the clerk's Roll Call 394 record, which lists every member's vote by name, state, and district. This cross-reference requires no inference. The CHIPS Program Office database identifies the district. The Roll Call 394 record identifies how the district's representative voted. Reading both documents produces the documented relationship between the no vote and the subsequent federal investment in the same geography.

The Private Investment Multiplier

The CHIPS Act's direct funding was explicitly designed to attract private semiconductor investment that would not have occurred without the federal incentive. The Department of Commerce's CHIPS Program Office reports track not only the direct federal awards but the private investment commitments associated with each award. When the Commerce Department announced the Intel award, for example, the announcement documented both the federal funding and Intel's committed private investment in the facilities. The ratio of private investment to federal funding across the CHIPS Act portfolio documents the leverage effect that the legislation's proponents cited in floor debate, and that the bill's opponents did not dispute on the merits of the leverage argument but on the appropriateness of industrial policy as a matter of principle. The floor statements of the 187 no voters are in the Congressional Record indexed under the dates of the House debate on H.R. 4346. The private investment commitments are in the CHIPS Program Office press releases. The geographic distribution of both the federal awards and the associated private investment is in the Commerce Department's public documentation. All three records coexist in the public archive. The opposition argument about industrial policy and the documentation of what the policy produced are both in the public record.

The Re-shoring Policy Debate and What the Documentary Record Shows

The CHIPS Act debate occurred against a backdrop of documented concern about U.S. semiconductor manufacturing's decline relative to Taiwan, South Korea, and China. The CHIPS legislation referenced the Semiconductor Industry Association's analysis of U.S. market share in semiconductor manufacturing, which had fallen from 37 percent in 1990 to approximately 12 percent by 2022, according to SIA data cited in the legislative record. The national security argument for domestic semiconductor capacity was made by the Department of Defense in testimony before Congress and is in the committee hearing record. The committee hearing record for the Senate Commerce Committee and the House Science, Space, and Technology Committee debates on CHIPS Act precursor legislation is publicly available through congress.gov. The floor debate on Roll Call 394, preserved in the Congressional Record, shows that both the industrial policy objection and the national security argument were presented to all 430 members who voted. The vote record shows which members found the industrial policy objection more persuasive and which found the national security argument more persuasive. The CHIPS Program Office award tracker subsequently shows where the resulting investment went. The entire sequence, from the floor debate to the vote to the awards to the private investment, is in public records.

The Award Tracker as a Living Cross-Reference

The CHIPS Program Office updates its public award tracker as each preliminary and final award is announced. The tracker is not a static document. It grows as the CHIPS Act's $52.7 billion in direct funding is allocated through individual award decisions made over the multi-year program administration period. Each new award announcement is a new data point in the cross-reference between Roll Call 394 and the geographic distribution of CHIPS Act investment. The cumulative effect of the tracker's updates is to build an increasingly complete record of which congressional districts are receiving CHIPS Act-funded semiconductor investment and how the members representing those districts voted on the legislation that authorized the investment. The Commerce Department's press release archive documents each announcement by date and location. The Roll Call 394 record is fixed: the 243-187 vote recorded on July 28, 2022 will not change. The award tracker updates. The cross-reference becomes more complete with each award announcement. Any researcher can track the evolution of this cross-reference in real time using the Commerce Department's public award tracker at commerce.gov/chips and the clerk's Roll Call 394 record at clerk.house.gov.

title: The 1887 Dawes Act Transferred 90 Million Acres. Here Is What That Costs in 2025.
teaser: In 1887, American Indian tribes held 138 million acres. By 1934, federal allotment policy had reduced that to 48 million. The $180 billion floor on what was transferred, and the 25.4 percent poverty rate that followed, are both in the federal record. They have never been assembled in one place.
body:
The poverty documented on American Indian reservations is not a mystery of culture or geography. It is the mathematical consequence of a specific federal statute, signed into law on February 8, 1887, that transferred 90 million acres of tribal land to white ownership over the following forty-seven years. The Dawes General Allotment Act, formally titled "An Act to Provide for the Allotment of Lands in Severalty to Indians on the Various Reservations," was not a policy error discovered after the fact. Its principal architect, Senator Henry Dawes of Massachusetts, designed it with explicit intent to dissolve tribal land tenure and force assimilation through individual property ownership. The mechanism was specific: communally held reservation land would be divided into individual allotments of 160 acres per head of household, with the land remaining after allotment classified as "surplus" and sold to white settlers. In 1887, American Indian tribes held approximately 138 million acres across the United States. By 1934, when the Indian Reorganization Act halted the process, that figure had fallen to 48 million acres. The 90 million acres in between were not lost. They were transferred by federal statute, one allotment at a time, through a legal mechanism whose outcome was documented at every stage by the government administering it.

How the Mechanism Worked

The allotment system contained a structural feature that was visible in the congressional record at the moment of passage and documented in federal files for the next five decades. Individual allotments were held in federal trust for twenty-five years, during which they could not be sold or taxed. After twenty-five years, the allottee received full title to the land. Full title meant the land was alienable: it could be sold, mortgaged, or lost to tax foreclosure. The Burke Act of 1906 extended the trust period for allottees classified as "incompetent" by federal agents but simultaneously made competency declarations easier to obtain, functioning in practice as an accelerant for land transfer rather than a brake on it. The Competency Commission of 1917, operating under authority granted by the Secretary of the Interior, declared thousands of allottees "competent" without their individual consent and issued fee patents that immediately exposed their land to sale and tax foreclosure. Bureau of Indian Affairs field reports, preserved in the National Archives in Record Group 75, document what followed in the years after fee patent issuance: in some reservation counties, the rate at which newly designated "competent" allottees lost their land to tax delinquency exceeded 75 percent within a decade. The transfer was not a policy failure. It was the policy operating as designed by the architects who wrote it, administered by the agency responsible for the welfare of the people it was liquidating.

The Government's Own Audit of What It Had Done

In 1926, the Interior Department commissioned a formal investigation into conditions on American Indian reservations. The resulting report, "The Problem of Indian Administration," was submitted by Lewis Meriam to Secretary of the Interior Hubert Work in February 1928, and is among the most explicit government self-indictments in American federal history. The Meriam Report documented, in clinical and systematic detail, the humanitarian consequences of forty years of allotment policy. Infant mortality on reservations was running at rates the report compared to regions of extreme poverty in the industrializing world. Tuberculosis incidence was recorded at four to five times the rate of the general United States population. Per capita income among allottees who had retained land was so low that the report's investigators described the standard of living as below any threshold that the general population of the country would consider acceptable. The report stated without qualification that the allotment policy had failed on its own terms: it had not produced the assimilation, agricultural self-sufficiency, or economic integration its architects promised. It had produced dispossession at industrial scale, followed by a documented humanitarian crisis among the populations dispossessed. The report recommended abolition of allotment and restoration of tribal self-governance. Congress acted on that recommendation six years later with the Indian Reorganization Act of 1934, which stopped further land alienation but made no provision for recovering the 90 million acres already transferred.

The 2022 Dollar Value of What Was Transferred

The 90 million acres transferred between 1887 and 1934 can be expressed in present-value terms using federal land valuations that are updated annually and publicly available. The United States Department of Agriculture's National Agricultural Statistics Service publishes a Land Values Summary each year reporting average cropland values by region. For the Northern Plains, where the majority of allotment-era transfers occurred, the 2022 USDA NASS value is $2,900 per acre. The Mountain region, which includes the remaining reservation land in the West, averages $1,820 per acre. Applying a conservative blended average of $2,000 per acre to the 90 million acres liquidated between 1887 and 1934 produces a present-value floor of $180 billion for the land alone. That calculation does not include mineral rights. The Osage mineral estate in northeastern Oklahoma covers approximately 1.5 million acres and generated royalty revenues exceeding $400 million annually at peak production in the 1920s. Federal court records and the 2001 settlement in Osage Nation v. United States document that a substantial portion of those royalties was diverted from Osage allottees through a court-supervised guardianship system that federal investigators later described as organized theft. The $180 billion is the floor: 90 million acres at 2022 agricultural land values, with mineral rights excluded and 137 years of compounding productivity not counted. It is the minimum documented value of what the Dawes Act transferred.

What the Numbers Look Like in 2025

The National Congress of American Indians' 2020 demographic and socioeconomic profile, drawing on American Community Survey data from the United States Census Bureau, documents the present-tense position of American Indian and Alaska Native households in the American economy. The median household income for American Indian and Alaska Native households is $43,825, compared to a national median of $65,712. The poverty rate is 25.4 percent, compared to the national rate of 10.5 percent. On reservations specifically, homeownership rates run below 50 percent in most reservation counties. The causal chain from the 1887 Dawes Act to these figures is traceable in federal records at every link. The allotment system transferred the land. The Burke Act accelerated the transfer. The Competency Commission converted communal wealth into individual vulnerability and then foreclosed on that vulnerability through tax delinquency at documented rates. The Indian Reorganization Act of 1934 stopped the hemorrhage at 48 million acres but made no provision for recovering what had been transferred in the preceding forty-seven years. The Housing Improvement Program administered by the Bureau of Indian Affairs, examined in the American Indian Policy Review Commission Final Report submitted to Congress in 1977, found that reservation housing conditions remained in documented crisis a full forty years after the allotment era ended. The 2020 poverty rate is not a residual echo of a distant policy. It is the compounding output of a documented legal mechanism whose inputs, outputs, and timeline are fully preserved in the federal record.

What the Record Shows

The question the primary source record forces is not whether Indigenous poverty is connected to federal land policy. On that question, the documentary evidence is not ambiguous. The Dawes Act is in the United States Statutes at Large. The Burke Act is in the Statutes at Large. The Meriam Report is in the National Archives. The Bureau of Indian Affairs field reports documenting the post-fee-patent foreclosure rates are in Record Group 75 at the National Archives. The USDA land values are updated annually and freely available. The NCAI demographic report is published and accessible. The arithmetic is not complex: 138 million acres minus 90 million transferred equals 48 million remaining. At $2,000 per acre that is $180 billion in 2022 land value alone. The result, 137 years later, is a poverty rate of 25.4 percent for the population whose land was transferred. None of this is hidden. The documents have always been available. What ClownBinge establishes, from the primary source record, is that this arithmetic has not been assembled in one place where a general reader could encounter it and follow the chain. The 1887 Dawes Act is the input. The 25.4 percent poverty rate in 2020 is the output. The 90 million acres and the $180 billion floor are the mechanism. The record has always shown this. It was never a secret.

The Actuarial Value: How 90 Million Acres Translates to 2025 Dollars

The calculation of the 2025 value of the land transferred out of Indigenous ownership through the Dawes Act requires establishing a base value at the time of transfer and applying an appropriate appreciation metric. The original "surplus" lands sold under the Dawes Act were typically sold at prices ranging from $1 to $5 per acre, prices that were often below market value for comparable private lands and that reflected both the coercive context of the sales and the administrative interest in rapid disposal. At the low end of $1.25 per acre, the approximate 90 million acres transferred would have had a nominal value at time of transfer of approximately $112 million. Applying the Federal Reserve's historical consumer price index adjustment from 1887 to 2025 produces a figure of approximately $3.5 billion in inflation-adjusted dollars. However, land appreciation substantially exceeds general price inflation over 130-year periods, particularly for land in the American West that has undergone agricultural development, resource extraction, and urban growth. Applying median agricultural land price appreciation rather than CPI adjustment produces estimated current values in the range of $150 billion to $400 billion depending on the appreciation rate assumed and the land classification used.

The calculation is inherently imprecise because the lands transferred included terrain of widely varying quality and current use, from arid grazing land in the Dakotas to agricultural land in Oklahoma to land in regions that have experienced significant urban and suburban development. The Dawes Act's transfer of the Five Civilized Tribes' lands in Indian Territory, what is now Oklahoma, which was not originally subject to the 1887 Act but was extended to the Five Tribes by the Curtis Act of 1898, included land that subsequently became the site of significant oil and gas extraction, adding a resource extraction value to the land value calculation that substantially increases the total. The Osage Nation's experience provides a documented illustration of this resource dimension: the Osage headright system, which preserved mineral rights for Osage members through a specific provision of their allotment agreement, produced the Osage oil boom of the 1920s, which in turn produced the Osage Reign of Terror documented in David Grann's "Killers of the Flower Moon," in which at least 60 Osage people were murdered by white settlers and oil interests seeking to claim their headright income. The land that was lost through allotment and the wealth it would have produced across subsequent generations is a documented historical ledger with calculable, if imprecise, 2025 values.

title: The Tulsa Race Massacre: What the 2001 Commission Report and the Archaeological Evidence Actually Document
teaser: 35 blocks burned. Between 100 and 300 dead. Aircraft used in the attack. 6,000 Black residents interned in city camps. Zero insurance dollars paid to any Greenwood property owner. No one charged with a crime. Oklahoma did not officially acknowledge it for 75 years. The 2001 Commission report is specific. So is the archaeological evidence.
body:
On the night of May 31 and the morning of June 1, 1921, a white mob destroyed the Greenwood District of Tulsa, Oklahoma, the most prosperous Black community in the United States. They burned 35 blocks of homes, businesses, churches, schools, hospitals, and law offices to the ground. They did so with the assistance of the Tulsa Police Department and with aircraft used to surveil and potentially fire upon the community from the air. They killed between 100 and 300 people, by the most conservative and most expansive estimates in the primary source record respectively, though the 2001 Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921 noted that the death toll was likely on the higher end of that range and that precise determination was impossible because of the destruction of records and the inadequacy of the investigation that followed. They buried victims in unmarked graves whose locations were not publicly identified for 80 years. They looted and destroyed property that researchers, including the Tulsa Race Riot Commission itself, estimated at between 1.5 million and 30 million dollars in 1921 value, representing hundreds of millions of dollars in 2024 purchasing power. No one was ever charged with a crime. The Tulsa newspapers buried the story within weeks. The State of Oklahoma did not acknowledge what had happened in any official document for 75 years. What is now documented in the 2001 Commission report and in the scholarly literature since is not primarily the event itself, which was widely witnessed. It is the specific mechanisms of its erasure from the public record.

What Greenwood Was Before the Massacre

The Greenwood District, known nationally as "Black Wall Street," was the product of deliberate economic separatism enforced from both directions: white Tulsa's racial exclusion had prevented Black residents from conducting business or owning property in most of the city, so Black entrepreneurs had built a self-sufficient economy within the boundaries they were permitted. The result, by 1921, was a community that contained 600 Black-owned businesses including hotels, law offices, medical practices, a hospital, a library, a junior high school, a bus line, and multiple newspapers. Timothy Egan, the New York Times journalist who covered the 2021 centennial, is among many to have repeated the "Black Wall Street" characterization, which originated in a speech by Booker T. Washington, who visited Tulsa in 1905 and was struck by the economic vitality of the emerging Black district. The deeper primary source documentation of what Greenwood was before its destruction is in the records of the Greenwood Cultural Center in Tulsa, which holds survivor testimonies, property records, business licenses, and newspaper archives from the Tulsa Star and Oklahoma Sun, the community's Black press. The economist James Hirsch, in "Riot and Remembrance: The Tulsa Race War and Its Legacy" (2002, Houghton Mifflin), documented specific property holdings, professional practices, and business revenues in Greenwood in the months before the massacre to establish the specific magnitude of what was destroyed.

The Aircraft and What the Commission Found

The most forensically significant finding of the Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921, whose report was published in February 2001, concerned the use of aircraft during the massacre. The Commission, chaired by former state senator Bob Blackwell and including historians, legal scholars, and community members, reviewed contemporary newspaper accounts, survivor testimonies, and what remained of official records and found credible evidence that aircraft were used to surveil the Greenwood District during the attack, and that those aircraft may have fired upon the community or dropped incendiary materials. The Commission was careful to note that the evidence for actual firing from aircraft was not conclusive: the most direct evidence came from survivor testimonies, which the Commission treated as credible but not independently confirmable due to the destruction of other records. The evidence that aircraft were present and being used in a surveillance or tactical role by people hostile to the Greenwood community was, the Commission found, clear. This represented the first use of aircraft in an attack on an American civilian population in United States history. The Commission recommended that the State of Oklahoma provide reparations to surviving victims and their descendants, establish a memorial, and provide economic development funding for the Greenwood area. As of the 100th anniversary in 2021, the reparations recommendation had not been implemented.

The Unmarked Graves and the Archaeological Investigation

The 2001 Commission report identified multiple possible locations for mass graves of massacre victims based on survivor testimony, contemporaneous newspaper accounts, and coroner's records that survived partial destruction. The City of Tulsa contracted with a team of archaeologists, led by Scott Hammerstedt of the Oklahoma Archaeological Survey, to conduct ground-penetrating radar surveys of three sites identified in the Commission report beginning in 2019. The archaeological team published findings in 2020 documenting anomalies consistent with human burials at Oaklawn Cemetery, one of the identified sites. Subsequent excavations at Oaklawn in 2021, conducted as part of a City of Tulsa-authorized investigation under the oversight of the Office of the Medical Examiner, exhumed remains from 12 burial sites that were identified as potential massacre victims based on the circumstances of burial, the age of the remains, and forensic analysis. The City of Tulsa's investigation was ongoing as of the 100th anniversary of the massacre. The location of a mass grave containing massacre victims, if confirmed, would be the first physical corroboration of the survivor testimonies describing emergency burials in the days after the massacre. The city that destroyed Greenwood in 1921 did not begin looking for the people it buried until 2019, 98 years later, when the last surviving witnesses were dead.

The Insurance Claims That Were Denied and What the Law Showed

The specific legal mechanism by which the survivors of the Tulsa massacre were denied compensation is documented in the records of the Oklahoma Insurance Department and in the case files of the multiple lawsuits brought by Greenwood property owners in the months and years after the massacre. Oklahoma law in 1921 included a riot exemption provision in standard property insurance policies, which excluded from coverage any damage caused by riot or civil commotion. Insurance companies invoked this exemption universally to deny the claims of Greenwood property owners. The riot exemption's application was not legally straightforward: the question of whether the massacre was a riot, a mob action, or a government-sanctioned assault, which the presence of police officers, the use of city aircraft, and the subsequent internment of Black survivors in city-administered camps would seem to complicate, was never adjudicated on its merits. The Greenwood property owners who sued either lost on technical grounds, had their cases dismissed, or settled for negligible amounts. The total insurance compensation paid to Greenwood property owners after the massacre was, according to research reviewed by the Commission, zero dollars. The legal and insurance system that had been available to protect Greenwood property owners from loss was operated, in the immediate aftermath of the massacre, exclusively to protect the perpetrators from accountability. That is not an interpretation. It is what the case records and the insurance department records show.

The Archaeological Record: What Geophysical Surveys Found Under Greenwood

The Tulsa Race Massacre of 1921 destroyed approximately 35 blocks of the Greenwood District, the most prosperous Black commercial and residential community in the United States at the time, known nationally as Black Wall Street. Contemporary estimates of deaths ranged widely; the 2001 Tulsa Race Riot Commission, established by the Oklahoma Legislature, concluded that between 100 and 300 people likely died, with the true number impossible to determine with certainty due to the destruction of records and the clandestine burial of some victims. The Commission's report, published in February 2001 and available through the Oklahoma State Legislature's records, is the primary governmental accounting of the event and documents the massacre, the failure of municipal and state government to prevent or stop it, and the subsequent failure to prosecute any of the participants despite identified witnesses and perpetrators.

Beginning in 2018 and accelerating after 2020, a team of researchers and forensic investigators led by Forensic Archaeologist Phoebe Stubblefield at the University of Florida conducted geophysical surveys of potential burial sites in Tulsa, including Oaklawn Cemetery, the Newblock Park site, and the Canes area of Tulsa. The surveys used ground-penetrating radar and other non-invasive geophysical methods to identify subsurface anomalies consistent with mass grave sites. In 2020, excavations at Oaklawn Cemetery under the direction of the City of Tulsa and the 1921 Tulsa Race Massacre Centennial Commission recovered 19 individuals from a section of the cemetery that historical records suggested had been used for indigent burials in 1921. Forensic anthropological analysis determined that the individuals had been buried rapidly without standard funerary preparation, consistent with mass casualty burial practices, and that they dated to approximately the 1920s based on skeletal analysis. Forensic DNA analysis to determine whether any of the remains could be matched to documented massacre victims was ongoing at the time of reporting. The archaeological evidence, documented in reports by the investigation team published through the City of Tulsa, constitutes the most direct physical evidence yet recovered of the massacre's lethal consequences.
verified_source: Oklahoma Commission to Study the Tulsa Race Riot of 1921 (2001) :: Tulsa Race Riot: A Report by the Oklahoma Commission :: Oklahoma Legislature; Ellsworth (1982) :: Death in a Promised Land: The Tulsa Race Riot of 1921 :: Louisiana State University Press; Hirsch (2002) :: Riot and Remembrance: The Tulsa Race War and Its Legacy :: Houghton Mifflin; Hammerstedt et al. (2020) :: Ground-penetrating radar investigation of potential Tulsa Race Massacre burial sites :: Oklahoma Archaeological Survey; Brophy (2002) :: Reconstructing the Dreamland: The Tulsa Race Riot of 1921, Race Reparations, and Reconciliation :: Oxford University Press

title: The Freedmen's Bureau: What 1.5 Million Pages of Government Records Say About What Reconstruction Actually Was
teaser: The Freedmen's Bureau established 4,000 schools, adjudicated one million labor contracts, and helped found Fisk, Howard, Morehouse, and Hampton in seven years. It was destroyed by Andrew Johnson's political campaign, not by failure. 1.5 million pages of its records are at the National Archives. Historians are still reading them.
body:
The Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, universally known as the Freedmen's Bureau, was established by Congress on March 3, 1865, and shut down in 1872 under sustained political assault from President Andrew Johnson and from the Democratic and Conservative Republican coalition that opposed Reconstruction. In the seven years it operated, it administered the transition from slavery to freedom for four million people. It adjudicated more than one million labor contracts between freed people and former enslavers. It established more than 4,000 schools educating 247,000 students. It operated more than 40 hospitals. It handled more than one million ration distributions to destitute freed people and white refugees. Its agents negotiated in courts, in fields, and in churches, operating as the only functioning legal structure many freed communities encountered in the immediate post-war South. The records it generated are held at the National Archives in Washington, D.C., and in regional archives across the former Confederate states. They total approximately 1.5 million pages. Historians are still transcribing them. Genealogists are still using them to trace enslaved ancestors. The standard American history curriculum treats the Freedmen's Bureau in two sentences: it existed, and it failed. Neither of those characterizations survives contact with the 1.5 million pages.

What the Bureau Actually Did: The Labor Contract System

The single most consequential function of the Freedmen's Bureau in the immediate post-war period was the administration of the labor contract system. Former enslaved people could not simply leave the land: the agricultural economy of the South required labor, the freed people required income, and the legal framework for the transition had to be constructed from nothing. The Bureau required that labor contracts between freed workers and employers be written, filed with Bureau agents, and reviewed for legality. Bureau agents were supposed to ensure that contracts did not replicate slavery's conditions through debt peonage, excessive penalties for leaving employment, or physical discipline clauses. The agent records, which are among the most legible components of the Bureau archive, document thousands of specific labor negotiations, dispute resolutions, and contract violations. They document freed people asserting rights, former enslavers attempting to reimpose bondage through contractual language, and Bureau agents navigating between them with wildly varying levels of commitment to the freed people's interests. W.E.B. Du Bois, who examined the Bureau's records in the preparation of "Black Reconstruction in America" (1935, Harcourt, Brace and Company), characterized the Bureau as a "government experiment unprecedented in the history of the world": an attempt to use federal administrative power to manage the transition from the largest forced labor system in the Western hemisphere to free labor. It was imperfect, underfunded, and ultimately politically destroyed. It was not a failure in the sense that it accomplished nothing. It was a failure in the sense that what it accomplished was destroyed by the political forces that could not accept what it represented.

The 4,000 Schools and What They Built

The Freedmen's Bureau's educational program was the largest single expansion of Black educational access in American history before the civil rights era. By 1870, the Bureau had helped establish more than 4,000 schools serving approximately 247,000 students in the former Confederate states. The schools operated in churches, in abandoned buildings, in private homes, and in purpose-built structures, many of which were built by the communities themselves. Bureau education records document the extraordinary demand: freed people who had been legally prohibited from learning to read under the slave codes of most Confederate states organized themselves to build schools and hire teachers as quickly as resources permitted. Freedmen's Bureau educational records at the National Archives document specific schools, their enrollments, their teachers, and the communities that sustained them. Among the institutions founded with Bureau assistance or in the Bureau period were Fisk University in Nashville (1866), Howard University in Washington (1867), Morehouse College in Atlanta (1867), Hampton University in Virginia (1868), and Alcorn State University in Mississippi (1871). These institutions exist today. They have produced generations of lawyers, physicians, teachers, scientists, and political leaders. Their founding in the Reconstruction period, with the assistance of the Freedmen's Bureau and the political will that the Bureau represented, is the direct institutional lineage connecting that moment of unprecedented federal commitment to Black education and the historically Black colleges and universities that anchor Black intellectual life today.

Andrew Johnson's Campaign to Destroy It

The Freedmen's Bureau's political destruction was not an accident or an inevitability. It was a campaign, led by President Andrew Johnson, who vetoed the Freedmen's Bureau Bill of 1866 on February 19, 1866, less than a year after the Bureau was established. Johnson's veto message, which is preserved in the Congressional Globe for the 39th Congress, characterized the Bureau as unconstitutional overreach and argued that "military jurisdiction over a citizen" was impermissible in peacetime. The Congress overrode the veto on July 16, 1866, the first time in American history that a presidential veto had been overridden by a two-thirds majority on a major piece of legislation. Johnson's subsequent campaign to limit the Bureau's operation included the removal of Bureau agents he considered too sympathetic to freed people, interference with Bureau court proceedings, and the use of the presidential pardon power to restore the property of former Confederates that the Bureau had been administering for freed people's benefit. The specific mechanism of restored Confederate land was documented by historian Claude Oubre in "Forty Acres and a Mule: The Freedmen's Bureau and Black Land Ownership" (1978, Louisiana State University Press): Johnson's pardons returned approximately 400,000 acres of land from freed people's control to former Confederate owners in 1865 alone, the direct reversal of what had been represented as the foundation of a free Black yeomanry in the post-war South.

What the Archive Is Still Revealing

The Freedmen's Bureau records at the National Archives were formally recognized as critically endangered historical materials in the late 20th century: thousands of the 1.5 million pages were deteriorating, had been misfiled, or had never been adequately catalogued. Beginning in 2000, the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints' FamilySearch genealogical organization launched a major digitization partnership with the National Archives to photograph and index the Bureau records, a project that has engaged more than 10,000 volunteer indexers and has made millions of individual records searchable online for the first time. The project, which continues today, has produced a resource that was not available to previous generations of historians or genealogists. Freed people who could not previously be traced through the documentary record because they were not enumerated as individuals in pre-war census documents are now findable through Bureau labor contracts, ration records, hospital admissions, and marriage registers. For African American families whose genealogical record disappears at the point of emancipation, the Freedmen's Bureau records are often the only documentary bridge between the slavery era and the post-war world. The 1.5 million pages are not a record of a failed experiment. They are the most comprehensive documentation in existence of four million people asserting their humanity in the first years after a legal system had classified them as property. They are still being read.

The Freedmen's Bureau Records: What 1.5 Million Pages of Government Documents Contain

The Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands, established by Congress on March 3, 1865 as a division of the War Department, operated for seven years until 1872 and produced a documentation archive that the National Archives describes as comprising approximately 1.5 million pages of records. The records include labor contracts between formerly enslaved people and landowners, which document the terms under which freedpeople entered the post-emancipation agricultural economy and provide primary source evidence for the transition from slavery to sharecropping. They include hospital and medical records from the more than 40 hospitals the Bureau established to provide medical care to formerly enslaved people, many of whom had received no medical care under slavery beyond what sustained their productive capacity. They include school records from the more than 4,000 schools the Bureau established with support from freedpeople's associations and Northern missionary organizations, documenting the determination of formerly enslaved people to access literacy and formal education that had been legally prohibited to them. They include records of legal cases in which the Bureau acted as an alternative to the state court systems that largely excluded Black participation, documenting both the legal claims brought by freedpeople and the outcomes.

The digitization of Freedmen's Bureau records, a major collaborative project involving Ancestry.com, FamilySearch, and the Smithsonian Institution, has made portions of the archive searchable online as part of a citizen genealogical indexing project. The digitization project has had particular significance for Black American genealogical research because the Freedmen's Bureau records, along with Freedmen's Savings and Trust Company records, represent among the most comprehensive primary sources for documenting the identities and family connections of formerly enslaved people whose existence was often documented in prior records only as the human property of slaveholders rather than as named individuals. The Bureau's records thus serve multiple purposes simultaneously: they are evidence of federal policy toward formerly enslaved people during Reconstruction; they are primary genealogical sources for Black American family history; and they are historical documentation of the terms under which the transition from slavery to nominal freedom actually operated in the immediate post-war period.

title: The American Eugenics Program Gave Nazi Germany Its Template. The Primary Sources Are in the Archives.
teaser: Harry Laughlin wrote the Model Eugenic Sterilization Law in 1922. Germany passed it as law in 1933 and sterilized 400,000 people. The Supreme Court upheld American eugenics in 1927. 32 states had sterilization laws. 60,000 Americans were forcibly sterilized. The last one was in Oregon in 1981. The Nazis sent Laughlin a thank-you degree from Heidelberg. He accepted it.
body:
In 1936, the University of Heidelberg awarded an honorary doctorate to Harry Laughlin, superintendent of the Eugenics Record Office at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory in New York. The citation praised his work on "racial hygiene." What Laughlin had done to earn this recognition from Nazi Germany's most prestigious university was write the Model Eugenic Sterilization Law, a template legislation he published in 1922 that was adopted almost verbatim as the legal foundation for Germany's Law for the Prevention of Hereditarily Diseased Offspring, enacted on July 14, 1933, six months after Hitler became chancellor. That law resulted in the forcible sterilization of approximately 400,000 Germans deemed genetically undesirable before the war. Laughlin accepted the Heidelberg honorary degree with apparent pride. The United States had, by 1936, already forcibly sterilized tens of thousands of its own citizens under eugenic sterilization laws that 32 states had enacted beginning with Indiana in 1907. The Nazis did not invent the American eugenics program. They cited it. They studied it. They said so in their own published writings. The debt runs in the direction that American history curricula have found it most difficult to acknowledge: from the United States to the Third Reich, in the field of human sterilization policy, with documentation in both countries' public records.

Indiana's 1907 Law and What Followed

Indiana enacted the world's first compulsory sterilization law on March 9, 1907. The law authorized the sterilization of "confirmed criminals, idiots, imbeciles and rapists" held in state institutions, upon the recommendation of a board of two surgeons. Its legislative sponsor was a state legislator named Horace Read. Its scientific champion was Harry Sharp, a physician at the Indiana Reformatory at Jeffersonville who had been performing vasectomies on inmates without legal authorization since 1899 and who lobbied the legislature to give him formal authority for what he was already doing. Within 15 years of Indiana's law, 15 other states had passed similar legislation. By 1931, 30 states had eugenic sterilization statutes on the books. By the time the programs were formally discontinued in most states in the 1960s and 1970s, approximately 60,000 to 65,000 Americans had been forcibly sterilized under state eugenic sterilization laws. The last known eugenic sterilization in the United States performed under explicit statutory authority occurred in Oregon in 1981. North Carolina's Eugenics Board, which operated from 1929 to 1974, sterilized approximately 7,600 people. North Carolina became the first state to formally apologize for its eugenic sterilization program and to establish a compensation program for survivors, in 2013, with payments of $20,000 per verified surviving sterilization victim.

Buck v. Bell (1927) and the Supreme Court's Role

The constitutional validity of eugenic sterilization laws was established by the United States Supreme Court in Buck v. Bell, decided on May 2, 1927, by a vote of eight to one. The case concerned Carrie Buck, a young woman in Virginia who had been committed to the Virginia State Colony for Epileptics and Feeble Minded. Virginia's eugenics board had designated her for sterilization on the grounds that she, her mother, and her infant daughter were all "feeble-minded" and that sterilization would prevent further propagation of what the board classified as hereditary defectiveness. Justice Oliver Wendell Holmes, considered one of the most distinguished legal minds in American history, wrote the majority opinion, which contained the sentence: "Three generations of imbeciles are enough." Paul Lombardo, a legal historian at Georgia State University who has written the most comprehensive account of the Buck case in "Three Generations, No Imbeciles: Eugenics, the Supreme Court, and Buck v. Bell" (2008, Johns Hopkins University Press), established through archival research that Carrie Buck was not intellectually disabled. She had been committed as a social inconvenience after being raped by a nephew of her foster family and becoming pregnant. The "feeble-mindedness" attributed to her mother was not documented in any clinical record. Her daughter Vivian, designated as the third generation of imbeciles in Holmes's opinion, made the honor roll in second grade before dying of an intestinal disease at age eight. Buck v. Bell has never been explicitly overturned. It remains nominally valid precedent.

The Nazi Citations of American Eugenics

The documentary evidence that Nazi Germany's racial hygiene programs drew directly and explicitly on American eugenic precedent is not disputed by historians of the period. Stefan Kuhl, a German sociologist, documented the specific citations and intellectual connections in "The Nazi Connection: Eugenics, American Racism, and German National Socialism" (1994, Oxford University Press), which drew on archives in Germany, the United States, and Europe to trace the specific texts, correspondence, and institutional relationships connecting American and German eugenicists before and during the Nazi period. The Nazis cited California's eugenics program specifically as a model for efficient large-scale implementation: California had sterilized more people than any other state by 1933. The Nazi Hereditary Health Courts, established to adjudicate sterilization cases, used American eugenic classification systems as a reference. Leon Whitney of the American Eugenics Society wrote to Hitler in 1933 congratulating him on the passage of the Sterilization Law and received a personal response from Hitler expressing his admiration for American eugenic research. The correspondence is in the archives of the American Philosophical Society in Philadelphia. American eugenics foundations continued to fund German racial science research through 1939, six years into Nazi rule and two years after the Nuremberg Laws had stripped German Jews of citizenship. The Rockefeller Foundation's funding of the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Anthropology, Human Heredity, and Eugenics in Berlin is documented in the Foundation's own archives, which historian Edwin Black reviewed for "War Against the Weak: Eugenics and America's Campaign to Create a Master Race" (2003, Four Walls Eight Windows).

The Discontinuation, the Silence, and the Reckoning

The American eugenics movement did not end because its scientific basis was discredited. It ended because its association with Nazi Germany, after 1945, made it politically toxic. The science of genetics continued to develop, the specific claims of eugenics were progressively undermined by advances in population genetics and the understanding of polygenic trait inheritance, but the primary political force that ended state eugenic sterilization programs was the reputational catastrophe of Nazi association. California suspended its program in 1952. Most other states phased out their programs between the 1950s and the 1970s. Oregon sterilized its last victim in 1981. The scientific institutions that had housed American eugenics research, including Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, underwent formal transformations and renamed or dissolved their eugenics-associated programs. Cold Spring Harbor's Eugenics Record Office was closed in 1939. The American Eugenics Society changed its name to the Society for the Study of Social Biology in 1972. The records of these organizations, held at the American Philosophical Society, the Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory Archives, and the Library of Congress, constitute a comprehensive primary source record of what was done, by whom, with what institutional support, and with what results. That record documents a program of state-sanctioned bodily violation that affected at least 60,000 Americans, was upheld by the Supreme Court, was cited by the architects of the Holocaust, and has received no formal federal acknowledgment, no federal compensation program, and no place in the standard American history curriculum.

The Primary Source Archive: What Harry Laughlin's Congressional Testimony and the Model Eugenical Sterilization Law Contain

Harry Laughlin, superintendent of the Eugenics Record Office at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory in New York and the primary legislative architect of American eugenics law, testified before the House Committee on Immigration and Naturalization on multiple occasions between 1920 and 1931 in his capacity as the Committee's "Expert Eugenical Agent," a formal appointment documented in the Congressional Record. Laughlin's testimony drew on the Eugenics Record Office's research to argue for immigration restriction based on the eugenical "quality" of immigrant populations from Southern and Eastern Europe. His Model Eugenical Sterilization Law, published in 1922, provided the legislative template that 30 states adopted in whole or in part to authorize compulsory sterilization of people classified as eugenically unfit. The Model Law's categories targeted for compulsory sterilization included: "Feebleminded, Insane, Criminalistics (including the delinquent and wayward), Epileptic, Inebriate, Diseased, Blind, Deaf, Deformed, and Dependent (including orphans, ne'er-do-wells, the homeless, tramps and paupers)." The complete text of the Model Law is available through the Eugenics Archive at Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory, which has digitized its historical records as a primary source public archive.

The connection between American eugenics and National Socialist racial science is documented in the primary sources of both countries. The Nazi journal Archiv fur Rassen-und Gesellschaftsbiologie cited American eugenics research and legislation favorably throughout the 1920s. Hitler's "Mein Kampf" included admiring references to American immigration restriction and racial science. When the Nuremberg Laws were drafted in 1935, the Nazi legal team reviewed American state sterilization laws as potential models. This is documented in James Whitman's "Hitler's American Model: The United States and the Making of Nazi Race Law," published by Princeton University Press in 2017, which draws on the primary source archive of the Nazi legal deliberations available in the German Federal Archives. Whitman's research, which has been peer-reviewed and reviewed in major legal and historical journals, demonstrates that American race law, including both the eugenics statutes and the state-level anti-miscegenation laws, was studied and partially adapted by the Nazi legal team. The American eugenics primary source archive is available through Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory's digitized collection. The connections to Nazi racial science are documented in both American and German primary sources.

title: The Manila Men of Louisiana: The First Asian Americans Arrived in 1763 in the Louisiana Bayou
teaser: The first permanent Asian settlement in the continental Americas was not in California in the 1800s. It was in the Louisiana bayou, built by Filipino sailors who jumped Manila galleons around 1763. The community lasted 150 years. Lafcadio Hearn visited it in 1883 and wrote about it for Harper's Weekly. The standard curriculum has never mentioned it.
body:
The first documented permanent Asian settlement in the continental Americas was not in California. It was not in the 19th century. It was in the Louisiana bayou, and it was built by Filipino sailors who jumped ship from Spanish Manila galleons beginning as early as 1763. The community they established at a place called St. Malo, on the shores of Lake Borgne southeast of New Orleans, pre-dated the Chinese Exclusion Act by more than a century and pre-dated any significant Asian immigration to the American West by at least 80 years. These men are known in the historical literature as the Manila men, or the Manilamen, and their story is documented in the archives of the Louisiana Historical Quarterly, in the records of the Catholic Diocese of New Orleans, in Spanish colonial records held at the Archivo General de Indias in Seville, and in the reportage of Lafcadio Hearn, who visited St. Malo in 1883 and published an account in Harper's Weekly that is among the most detailed contemporaneous descriptions of the community that survives. The community endured for more than a century. It was destroyed not by law enforcement or racial violence but by a hurricane in 1915. The people it produced are ancestral to Filipino American communities in Louisiana that have existed continuously since the colonial period.

The Manila Galleon Trade and How the Manilamen Got to Louisiana

The Manila galleon trade was the most important transoceanic trade route of the 16th, 17th, and 18th centuries. From 1565 to 1815, Spanish galleons sailed annually between Manila in the Philippines and Acapulco in New Spain, carrying Chinese silks, porcelain, and spices westward and silver from the Potosi mines of Peru eastward. The round trip took approximately six months. The ships required large crews for the Pacific crossing, and Spanish imperial maritime law permitted the conscription or recruitment of Filipino, Chinese, and other Asian sailors for these voyages, classifying them as indios, a legal category that distinguished them from Spanish subjects but afforded them certain protections under the Laws of the Indies. The voyage from Manila to Acapulco was an extraordinarily brutal passage: the Acapulco ships followed the Kuroshio Current across the North Pacific, a route of approximately 9,000 nautical miles that took four to seven months, during which scurvy, shipwreck, and storm claimed a substantial percentage of each crew. Sailors who survived the crossing and arrived in Acapulco had accomplished one of the most demanding maritime feats of the era. The overland route from Acapulco to Veracruz, and thence by ship to New Orleans, which was a Spanish colonial port after 1769, provided the pathway through which Filipino sailors made their way to Louisiana.

What Lafcadio Hearn Documented in 1883

Lafcadio Hearn was an Irish-Greek journalist who settled in New Orleans in 1877 and spent a decade documenting the city's Creole culture before moving to Japan, where he spent the rest of his life. His 1883 visit to St. Malo, published in Harper's Weekly on March 31, 1883 under the title "Saint Malo: A Lacustrine Village in Louisiana," is the most detailed contemporaneous description of the Manilamen community that exists. Hearn described a village of approximately 30 to 40 structures built on stilts above the water of Lake Borgne, a community of men who spoke a mixture of Spanish, English, and what Hearn described as "a patois resembling nothing so much as the dialect of the Philippines." He documented their methods of shrimp fishing, the drying platforms on which they prepared shrimp for sale in New Orleans markets, the boats they built, and the social organization of a community that had been functioning for at least a century by the time of his visit. His account was accompanied by drawings by artist J.O. Davidson that are among the only visual documentation of the St. Malo community. Hearn noted that the men did not know exactly when their community had been established, but that the oldest among them had been told by their fathers that it had always existed. The Harper's Weekly article is held at the New Orleans Public Library and in multiple university archives.

The Documentary Record Before Hearn

The historical record of the Manilamen before Hearn's 1883 article is fragmentary but consistent. Marina Espina, a Filipino American researcher whose 1988 book "Filipinos in Louisiana," published by A.F. Laborde and Sons, is the most comprehensive scholarly treatment of the community, traced documentary references to Filipino presence in Louisiana back to the 1760s through Spanish colonial records and Catholic church records from the parishes around Lake Borgne. The Catholic Diocese of New Orleans records include baptismal entries for individuals identified by names consistent with Philippine origin in parishes near the lake margins beginning in the late 18th century. The Louisiana Historical Quarterly, a peer-reviewed journal that has published primary source research on Louisiana history since 1917, contains multiple articles on the Filipino fishing communities of St. Bernard Parish that draw on 19th-century property records, tax assessments, and civil records to document the community's presence and growth across the century before Hearn's visit. The Filipino American National Historical Society, founded in 1982, has conducted oral history collection projects with Louisiana Filipino American families that document family narratives tracing ancestry to the St. Malo community through multiple generations. These oral histories, combined with the documentary record, establish a continuous Filipino American presence in Louisiana from at least 1763 to the present day, predating by more than a century the California gold rush migration and the railroad labor recruitment programs that are typically presented as the beginning of Asian American history in the United States.

The Hurricane, the Erasure, and the Continuity

The St. Malo community on Lake Borgne was destroyed by the hurricane of September 1915, which made landfall near Grand Isle with winds estimated at 130 miles per hour and produced a storm surge that inundated the low-lying bayou communities of southeastern Louisiana. The physical community was gone. The families that had lived there had been building roots on the Louisiana mainland for decades, in communities including St. Bernard Parish, Manila Village, and the New Orleans metropolitan area. The hurricane ended the specific geographic community of St. Malo but did not end the Filipino American community of Louisiana that it had founded. The erasure of the St. Malo community from standard American history was not produced by the hurricane. It was produced by the historiographical decision, made and unmade across successive generations of American history writing, about whose stories counted as part of the national narrative. The standard account of Asian American history in the United States begins with Chinese immigration to California in 1849 and the subsequent railroad construction labor of the 1860s. That account is not wrong. It is incomplete by approximately 80 years and by approximately 3,000 miles. The men who built their houses on stilts over a Louisiana lake, who dried shrimp on wooden platforms in the Gulf coast heat, and who created a community that lasted 150 years before a storm ended it, arrived in North America while the Declaration of Independence was being signed. They are part of the founding generation of what would become the United States, whether the founding narrative has made room for them or not.

The Primary Source Documentation: Spanish Colonial Records and the Bayou Community Archives

The presence of Filipino settlers in the Louisiana bayou country predating the American Revolution is documented in Spanish colonial records preserved in the Archivo General de Indias in Seville, Spain, and in colonial Louisiana records held at the Louisiana State Museum and the New Orleans Notarial Archives. The Philippines and Louisiana were both territories of the Spanish colonial empire in the eighteenth century, the Philippines governed through the Viceroyalty of New Spain through the Manila Galleon trade system and Louisiana governed as part of the broader Spanish colonial administration of North America. Filipino workers who arrived in Louisiana through the port of New Orleans via Acapulco entered the community as identifiable residents whose presence is recorded in census records, church baptismal records, and legal documents of the Spanish colonial period. The scholarly documentation of the Manila Men community draws primarily on these archival sources, supplemented by oral history traditions maintained within the bayou communities.

Jonathan Chua's research on the Manila Men, published in multiple articles in Philippine Studies: Historical and Ethnographic Viewpoints and in contributions to Louisiana history journals, provides the most systematic scholarly examination of the primary source record. Chua's work documents the community of Filipino fishermen who settled in the bayou communities south of New Orleans, particularly in St. Malo, Louisiana, and who developed distinctive shrimp-drying practices that supplied dried shrimp to New Orleans and to communities in the American South and in the Caribbean. St. Malo, the best-documented of the Manila Men communities, was destroyed by a hurricane in 1893 and rebuilt, with the community persisting into the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries before dispersing. Photographs of St. Malo taken by photographers accompanying journalists from Harper's Weekly in 1882 are among the most frequently reproduced visual primary sources for the community and are held in the collections of the Library of Congress. The Manila Men represent the earliest documented settlement of Asian immigrants in the continental United States, predating the mid-nineteenth-century Chinese immigration to California by approximately a century, and the primary source record supporting this historical claim is available in archival repositories in Louisiana, Spain, and the Philippines.

title: The Indian Adoption Project: How the Federal Government and the Child Welfare League Systematically Removed Native Children from Their Families
teaser: Between 1958 and 1967, the Bureau of Indian Affairs and the Child Welfare League of America ran a program that placed Native children in white homes and established norms that would remove an estimated 25 to 35 percent of all Native American children from their families by the 1970s. The program had foundation funding and professional endorsement. It had no name in any standard curriculum.
body:
Between 1958 and 1967, a program called the Indian Adoption Project, jointly administered by the Bureau of Indian Affairs and the Child Welfare League of America, systematically removed Native American children from their families and placed them in white adoptive homes. The program was not covert. It was presented as a humanitarian initiative and received foundation funding and favorable coverage in social work publications. The Child Welfare League of America, the most prominent professional association for child welfare workers in the country, was its organizational sponsor. The Bureau of Indian Affairs, the federal agency with trust responsibility over Native nations, provided the administrative infrastructure. The program placed approximately 395 Native children in white homes during its formal operation, a number that understates its impact: it established the methodology and professional norms that state-level child welfare agencies applied across the country for the following decade, ultimately affecting what federal investigators would later estimate as 25 to 35 percent of all Native American children, who were removed from their families and placed in foster care or adoptive homes, almost exclusively with white families, during the 1960s and 1970s. The scale of what was done, and the institutional respectability of the organizations that did it, are not taught in American history curricula. The 1978 Indian Child Welfare Act, passed in direct response to these removals, is mentioned in specialized contexts. The removal program that made it necessary is not.

The Program's Design and Its Stated Rationale

The Indian Adoption Project was developed by Arnold Lyslo, a social worker employed by the Child Welfare League, and was presented in social work literature as a response to the documented inadequacy of Native American children's circumstances on reservations, which the 1950s termination policy had deliberately impoverished. The termination policy, enacted through House Concurrent Resolution 108 in 1953, ended the federal government's recognition of 109 Native nations between 1953 and 1964, stripping them of trust land protections, treaty rights, and federal services. The poverty on reservations that termination produced was then used as the justification for removing the children produced by that poverty. The program operated on an explicit assimilationist rationale that was articulated in its own publications: that Native children would be better served by placement in white middle-class homes than by remaining in reservation communities. The professional literature of the period consistently framed Native family structures as culturally inadequate by the standards of Euro-American social work norms, and Native extended family relationships, which were the primary child-rearing context in most tribal communities, were not recognized as legitimate family placements by state welfare agencies applying non-Native adoption law.

The Scale of Removal Across the Country

The formal Indian Adoption Project placed approximately 395 children between 1958 and 1967. The impact of the norms and methodologies it established was dramatically larger. The Association on American Indian Affairs, a non-profit organization that monitored conditions in Native communities, conducted surveys in 1969 and 1974 that documented the scale of child removal at the state level. Their 1974 survey found that in states with significant Native populations, between 25 and 35 percent of all Native children had been removed from their families. In Minnesota, which had one of the most thoroughly documented removal programs, approximately one in four Native children was in foster care or adoptive placement. In South Dakota, the figure was one in three. The vast majority of removed children were placed with white families. The Indian Child Welfare Act's legislative record, compiled by the Senate Select Committee on Indian Affairs under Senator James Abourezk of South Dakota in 1974, contains testimony from Native parents describing children removed by state welfare workers for conditions that would not have resulted in removal from white families: poverty, extended family living arrangements, traditional child-rearing practices that differed from Euro-American norms. The Senate testimony is among the most detailed documentary records of what the removal system looked like from the receiving end.

The Child Welfare League's Role and Its Documentation

The Child Welfare League of America's institutional sponsorship of the Indian Adoption Project is documented in its own publications and in the peer-reviewed social work literature of the period. The League's journal, Child Welfare, published multiple articles on the program's outcomes through the 1960s, all framed positively. The League's executive director, Joseph Reid, co-authored promotional materials for the program. The program received foundation funding from sources including the Field Foundation. The institutional respectability of the sponsoring organizations created a self-reinforcing legitimacy: because the Child Welfare League had endorsed the methodology, state welfare agencies applied it with professional confidence, and courts approved placements on the recommendation of professional social workers credentialed by the League's standards. Margaret Jacobs, a historian at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln who documented the Indian Adoption Project in her 2014 book "A Generation Removed: The Fostering and Adoption of Indigenous Children in the Postwar World," published by the University of Nebraska Press, found that the Child Welfare League did not formally acknowledge its role in the program or its consequences until the 21st century, decades after the Indian Child Welfare Act had established that the removals had constituted a systematic violation of Native families' rights and Native nations' sovereignty over their children.

The Indian Child Welfare Act and What It Acknowledged

The Indian Child Welfare Act of 1978, Public Law 95-608, was signed by President Jimmy Carter on November 8, 1978. Its findings section, which is the formal congressional declaration of why the law was necessary, is an explicit acknowledgment of what had happened. The findings state that Congress has "found that an alarmingly high percentage of Indian families are broken up by the removal, often unwarranted, of their children from them by nontribal public and private agencies and that an alarmingly high percentage of such children are placed in non-Indian foster and adoptive homes and institutions." The Act established minimum federal standards for the removal of Native children from their families, gave tribal courts jurisdiction over child custody proceedings involving tribal members, and established a preference for placement of removed children with extended family, other tribal members, or other Native families before non-Native placement. The Act's passage did not end non-consensual removal of Native children, but it created a federal floor below which state agencies could not legally operate. Subsequent research has documented that the Act's provisions have been inconsistently enforced across states and that Native children continue to be overrepresented in foster care at rates significantly higher than their share of the population. A 2019 study published in the American Indian Law Review by Matthew Fletcher and Wenona Singel at Michigan State University documented that Native children were removed from their families at rates 2.7 times higher than white children nationally, even after the Indian Child Welfare Act's decades of operation. The removal program that preceded the Act ran for nearly two decades with the endorsement of the federal government and the most prominent professional organization in American child welfare. It had no name in any standard American history curriculum.

The Boarding School Investigations: What the Deb Haaland Initiative Documented

The Federal Indian Boarding School Initiative Investigation Report, published by the Department of the Interior in May 2022 under the direction of Secretary Deb Haaland, the first Native American cabinet secretary in United States history, documented 408 federal Indian boarding schools operated between 1819 and 1969, 53 of which have marked burial sites or possible burial sites on their grounds. The report drew on records from 37 federal agencies, state archives, religious organization archives, and newspaper collections, and found that the schools enforced English-only policies, prohibited the practice of Native languages and cultural traditions, and employed physical punishment for violations. The investigative methodology and primary source citations are documented in the report itself, publicly available through the Department of the Interior's website. The report represents the most comprehensive federal accounting of the boarding school system yet produced and is a primary source document of significant evidentiary value.

The Indian Adoption Project, which operated from 1958 to 1967 under a collaboration between the Bureau of Indian Affairs and the Child Welfare League of America, placed approximately 395 Native children in white foster and adoptive homes in its first nine years of operation. The project was designed by officials who understood themselves as providing children from disadvantaged backgrounds with improved life opportunities, but it operated through mechanisms that have been analyzed as a continuation of assimilation policy through child welfare bureaucracy rather than through military or educational coercion. The project's records, housed at the Social Welfare History Archives at the University of Minnesota, document the child placements and the institutional justifications offered for them. Research by Margaret Jacobs at the University of Nebraska-Lincoln, published as "A Generation Removed: The Fostering and Adoption of Indigenous Children in the Postwar World" in 2014, provides the most comprehensive scholarly analysis of the project, drawing on the institutional archive and interviews with adult adoptees. The Indian Child Welfare Act of 1978, passed by Congress specifically in response to the documented pattern of state welfare agencies removing Native children from their families at rates documented to be between five and twenty-five times the removal rates for non-Native children, is the legislative primary source that acknowledges and responds to the problem the Indian Adoption Project exemplified.
