title: Do Republicans Support White DEI? Introducing WDEI: White Discrimination, Exclusion, and Ignorance
teaser: The January 2025 executive order banning DEI ran to 3,600 words. It did not mention legacy admissions. It did not mention grandfather clauses. It did not mention the documented racial wealth gap created by federal housing policy. If the argument is about merit, the omission is the argument. This is Part Two of the ClownBinge DEI investigation. Part One documented who was actually appointed. Part Two asks the question that follows: if not DEI, then what exactly is the system they are preserving?
body:
This is Part Two of the ClownBinge DEI investigation. Part One: The Attack on DEI Was a Ruse documented the credential gap between Obama-era and Trump-era appointees, department by department. This article builds directly from those findings.

The January 2025 executive order banning federal DEI programs ran to 3,600 words. It did not mention legacy admissions. It did not mention grandfather clauses. It did not address the Federal Housing Administration's documented history of racially explicit mortgage discrimination. It did not propose any remedy for the racial wealth gap its own government helped construct. The order was titled "Ending Illegal Discrimination and Restoring Merit-Based Opportunity." Merit-based opportunity, in its telling, begins today. Whatever happened before today is not the order's concern.

That gap is the argument. This article names it.

What Part One Found

The first investigation compared credentials. Department of Energy: a Nobel Prize in Physics and an MIT theoretical physicist on one side; an animal science graduate who forgot the agency existed on the other. Department of Education: a Harvard sociologist and a triple-degree holder from Harvard, Yale, and Columbia on one side; a B.A. in Business Administration who had never attended public school on the other. The full record is in Part One. The pattern across every department is the same. The administration that called DEI a threat to merit produced the demonstrably less credentialed appointees. The argument that this was about qualifications does not survive the comparison.

What Part One did not name is the system the replacement appointments represent. This article does.

WDEI: A Definition

WDEI stands for White Discrimination, Exclusion, and Ignorance. It is not an epithet. It is a description of three documented institutional practices that, taken together, constitute a functioning preferential system for white Americans that has operated continuously in American public life, that has never been targeted by an executive order, and that the anti-DEI movement has shown no interest in dismantling.

Each component has a scholarly and evidentiary record.

W: The White Affirmative Action Infrastructure

The GI Bill of 1944 is the largest affirmative action program in American history. Ira Katznelson, in the most comprehensive scholarly account of the period, documented that Black veterans were systematically excluded from its housing loans, university access, and small business provisions through a combination of segregated institutions and racially discriminatory administration. The result was a federal transfer of wealth, education, and homeownership to white families specifically. Not through private bias alone. Through program design.

Legacy admissions at elite universities are a direct heir to this system. Arcidiacono, Kinsler, and Ransom, using Harvard's own litigation-disclosed admissions data, documented that legacy status provided an admissions preference equivalent to a 45-point increase in the probability of admission, independently of academic credentials. Legacy applicants were overwhelmingly white. The executive orders that ended race-conscious admissions programs at federal contractors did not mention legacy preferences. A system that explicitly transfers advantage across generations by bloodline, disproportionately to one racial group, was preserved in its entirety. That is the W.

D: Documented Discrimination

The discrimination component of WDEI is the most extensively audited phenomenon in labor economics. Bertrand and Mullainathan submitted 5,000 identical resumes to 1,300 employers, varying only the racial connotation of the applicant's name. Resumes with white-sounding names received 50 percent more callbacks than identical resumes with Black-sounding names. Identical qualifications. The only variable was the name. The researchers found that a higher-quality resume improved outcomes more for white applicants than for Black applicants, meaning discrimination also reduced the return on merit itself.

The effect persists when applicants know about it and attempt to correct for it. Kang and colleagues, publishing in Administrative Science Quarterly, found that Black applicants who removed all racial signals from their resumes received 25.5 percent callback rates, compared to 10 percent for unaltered resumes. Identical qualifications, again. Companies with explicit diversity commitments showed no significant difference in their callback rates. The discrimination preceded DEI. It survived it. It is documented to persist now. That is the D.

E: The Architecture of Exclusion

Exclusion requires no active hostility. It requires only that the existing system continue operating. Cheryl Harris, in what became one of the most cited articles in American legal scholarship, published in the Harvard Law Review, argued that whiteness in the United States has functioned historically as a form of property, conferring enforceable rights of access and expectation. The legal refusal to account for racial identity, she documented, does not produce neutrality. It preserves whatever racial advantage had already been accumulated and treats it as the neutral baseline.

The exclusion visible in Part One's appointment data is a contemporary expression of this. The departments whose leadership shifted from career-domain experts to political and personal loyalists did not produce demographically random results. The pattern is consistent with what Harris described: a system that is not explicitly organized by race nonetheless produces racially consistent outcomes when it operates from a baseline that was itself racially constructed. That is the E.

I: The Ignorance That Preserves the System

Ignorance, in WDEI, refers to a specific and documented phenomenon: the systematic underestimation by white Americans of the racial disparities that WDEI produces and maintains. Kraus and colleagues, publishing in Perspectives on Psychological Science, found that white Americans systematically underestimated the Black-white wealth gap, believing it to be roughly half its documented size. The misperception was not random. It was directional and consistent: the problem always appeared smaller than it was. The researchers found that this systematic underestimation made it easier to oppose corrective policies because the condition being corrected had been rendered, perceptually, as nearly nonexistent.

Eduardo Bonilla-Silva has documented the ideological infrastructure that converts this ignorance into policy. Colorblind racism, as he characterizes it, does not require racial hostility. It requires only a vocabulary of neutrality: merit, individual responsibility, equal opportunity, colorblindness itself. Each principle, applied to a structurally unequal baseline, reproduces the inequality it claims not to see. The executive order that abolished DEI while leaving legacy admissions intact is a nearly textbook application of what Bonilla-Silva describes: abstract liberalism invoked to oppose the correction while preserving the condition. That is the I.

The Rhetorical Question, Answered

The question in the title of this article is rhetorical. The record answers it. If you preserve legacy admissions, you are running a preferential system for the children of people who attended institutions that were, within living memory, explicitly racially segregated. If you preserve the geographic wealth patterns created by racially explicit federal mortgage discrimination, you are the beneficiary of a government DEI program that operated from 1934 to 1968 with legal authority and federal funding. If you oppose the correction of a documented 50 percent hiring callback gap while leaving unchanged the institutional structures that produce it, you have not ended DEI. You have selected which DEI to protect.

WDEI is not an accusation that any individual is conscious of this, or intends it, or would endorse it if it were named plainly. Kraus's research suggests most would not recognize the magnitude of the disparity they are defending. Bonilla-Silva's framework suggests the ideological vocabulary of merit and colorblindness makes the system self-concealing. Harris's legal analysis suggests the baseline itself has been constructed so that whiteness reads as neutral and any correction reads as preference.

The neologism is offered not as an epithet but as a mirror. If the programs you eliminate are the ones that correct for a racial hierarchy, and the programs you preserve are the ones that perpetuate it, then merit is not the principle at work. A name for the actual principle is useful. This one fits the documented record. The scientific record on what evolution actually selects for, and why genetic diversity is not optional, is covered in a separate ClownBinge investigation.

White DEI as Documented Practice: The Historical and Statistical Record

The concept of White DEI, defined as Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion practices that specifically benefit white Americans through preferential access, is not a rhetorical inversion. It describes documented historical and contemporary practices that the primary source record shows were explicitly designed to advantage white Americans in access to housing, education, employment, and political representation. The GI Bill's differential administration, documented through VA mortgage guarantee records and Hilary Herbold's research in the Journal of Blacks in Higher Education, functioned as a white-exclusive DEI program in practice, producing homeownership, education subsidies, and economic mobility for white veterans at rates that were dramatically higher than for Black veterans with identical service records. This is not an allegation. It is documented in the VA's own administrative records and in the research literature.

Legacy admissions at selective universities represent a contemporary practice documented to function as an affirmative preference for white and wealthy applicants. The Harvard University admissions trial records, produced through the litigation in Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard that ultimately reached the Supreme Court, documented that legacy admissions preference produced acceptance rates for legacy applicants that were approximately 34 percent, compared to approximately 14 percent for non-legacy applicants with similar academic credentials. The demographic composition of legacy applicants at elite universities, which overwhelmingly reflects the white and economically privileged composition of the generations of graduates from which legacy applicants descend, means that legacy preferences function as a race-correlated admissions preference that benefits white applicants at measurably higher rates than non-white applicants. The Supreme Court's 2023 ruling in Students for Fair Admissions eliminated race-conscious admissions for diversity purposes while leaving legacy admissions, donor preferences, and athletic recruitment preferences intact, a selective application of colorblindness doctrine whose differential effect on the racial composition of the admitted class is a documented and predictable consequence of the ruling's scope.

title: The Negativity Is Killing You: The Case for Retiring Hatred and Unnecessary Stress
teaser: Your body does not know the difference between the driver who cut you off and a predator. It responds to both with the same cascade of cortisol, inflammation, and cardiovascular activation. The research across three decades is unambiguous: chronic hatred, sustained anger, and unnecessary stress are physiological events with measurable, cumulative, lethal consequences. The case for retiring them is not moral. It is biological.
body:
Your body does not know you are right. It does not know that your anger is justified, that your contempt is earned, or that the person you cannot stop thinking about deserves every ounce of hostility you feel toward them. What your body knows is that you are under threat. And it responds the same way it has responded to threats for two hundred thousand years: cortisol, inflammation, elevated heart rate, suppressed immunity, accelerated cellular aging. The moral correctness of your grievance is irrelevant to the biology. The damage accumulates regardless.

What Cortisol Is Doing Right Now

Cortisol is a stress hormone. Its job, in the acute sense, is to prepare you to respond to a threat: elevate blood glucose, sharpen focus, mobilize energy. That function is adaptive and necessary. The problem is that the human stress response did not evolve for the kind of threat most people in 2026 actually face: the news, the commute, the coworker, the political landscape, the sustained low-grade hostility of navigating a world that regularly produces outrage.

Dallman and colleagues at UCSF, publishing in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, documented the mechanism: chronic stress drives cortisol-mediated visceral fat accumulation. Not general weight gain. Specifically abdominal fat, which is metabolically distinct: more inflammatory, more directly linked to cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes, and uniquely responsive to cortisol stimulation.

Separate research by Elissa Epel and colleagues, published in Psychosomatic Medicine, confirmed that stress-induced cortisol reactivity predicts greater abdominal fat independently of overall body weight. The research controlled for body mass index. You can be eating the same diet as your lower-stress counterpart and accumulating more dangerous fat because of the chronic psychological threat state your body is running.

Anger Is a Cardiac Event

The research on anger and cardiovascular risk is not ambiguous. Mittleman and colleagues at Harvard Medical School, publishing in Circulation, found that episodes of anger elevated the relative risk of myocardial infarction onset by 2.3 times in the two hours following the anger episode. The effect was dose-dependent. Anger activates the sympathetic nervous system, which produces acute hemodynamic stress, increased platelet aggregation, and potential coronary artery spasm. Your heart does not interpret your anger as righteous. It interprets it as a cardiovascular emergency.

The acute risk is compounded by the chronic one. Chida and Steptoe published a meta-analysis of 44 prospective studies in the Journal of the American College of Cardiology. The analysis found that sustained anger and hostility were associated with a 19 percent increased risk of coronary heart disease in initially healthy people, and a 24 percent increased risk of poor prognosis in those who already had it. The association held after controlling for smoking, blood pressure, and cholesterol. Hostility is an independent cardiovascular risk factor.

Your Immune System Is Watching

Segerstrom and Miller conducted the most comprehensive meta-analysis of psychological stress and immune function in the scientific literature. Their review of 293 studies and more than 18,000 participants found that chronic stressors, including sustained interpersonal conflict and unresolved hostility, reliably suppress both cellular and humoral immunity while simultaneously raising systemic inflammation markers. Natural killer cell activity decreases. T-cell proliferation decreases. The longer the stress persists, the more global the suppression becomes. Sustained hatred is not just emotionally exhausting. It is immunologically expensive.

You Are Aging Yourself in Real Time

Telomeres are the protective caps at the ends of chromosomes. Their length is a marker of cellular age. Shorter telomeres are associated with earlier onset of age-related disease, diminished tissue repair, and earlier death. They shorten naturally with age. They also shorten in response to chronic psychological stress.

Epel and Nobel laureate Elizabeth Blackburn, publishing in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences, found that chronically stressed women carried telomere lengths equivalent to nine to seventeen years of additional cellular aging compared to lower-stress controls. The most chronically stressed participants had chromosomes that looked, at the cellular level, like those of women a decade and a half older. Chronic stress does not just feel like aging. It produces it at a measurable, accelerated rate.

The Hidden Tax: Weathering

For Black Americans, the chronic stress equation carries an additional documented variable. Arline Geronimus at the University of Michigan has spent decades researching what she calls the weathering hypothesis: the idea that the chronic psychosocial stress of racism produces accelerated biological aging. Her research, published in the American Journal of Public Health, found that Black Americans in their 40s carry biological wear markers comparable to white Americans in their 60s. The gap widens with age. The chronic stress of navigating racism is not merely a psychological burden. It is a documented physiological tax.

Bruce McEwen, in the New England Journal of Medicine, established allostatic load as the conceptual framework for understanding how this accumulates. It is not any single anger episode but the sustained, unresolved activation of the stress response over years that produces the most severe consequences. The system was designed for acute threats followed by recovery. It was not designed for a daily, unrelenting stream of psychological threats with no physiological resolution.

What Retiring It Actually Does

The forgiveness research is as rigorous as the hostility research, and it points in the measurable opposite direction. Lawler and colleagues, publishing in the Journal of Behavioral Medicine, found that forgiveness was associated with significant reductions in blood pressure, heart rate, and cortisol levels, independent of simply feeling better about the situation. Forgiving does not require believing the other person was right. It requires releasing the physiological activation of sustained hostility. The argument is not moral. It is cardiovascular.

Barbara Fredrickson demonstrated in controlled laboratory research that positive emotional states produce a measurable cardiovascular undoing effect, actively reversing the physiological consequences of negative activation. Positive emotions are not simply the absence of negative ones. They produce distinct physiological effects that counteract the damage from sustained stress. Cultivating them is not wishful thinking. It is cardiovascular rehabilitation.

The Case, Stated Plainly

Hatred is expensive. Sustained anger is cardiovascular debt. Chronic contempt is an immune suppressor, a fat storage accelerator, a telomere shredder, and a biological aging mechanism. The research does not allow for a comfortable interpretation in which the person sustaining hostility is only harming the target of that hostility. They are also, and with documented physiological precision, harming themselves.

The case for retiring unnecessary hatred and stress is not that the things you are angry about are not real. Many of them are real, documented, and worth fighting. The case is that chronic, unresolved, ruminative hostility with no productive outlet is a physiological state that your body cannot sustain without consequence. The anger that produces action is different from the anger that just sits in the body, activating cortisol, elevating blood pressure, suppressing immunity, and shortening your telomeres while the person you are angry at sleeps soundly through the night.

Retire what is killing you. Not for the person who caused it. For the body that has to carry it.

Chronic Stress and Telomere Attrition: The Molecular Biology of Accelerated Aging

Telomeres are the protective caps at the ends of chromosomes, composed of repetitive TTAGGG nucleotide sequences bound by specialized proteins. Each time a cell divides, telomeres shorten by approximately 50 to 200 base pairs, because DNA polymerase cannot fully replicate the terminal ends of linear chromosomes. When telomeres shorten to a critical length, cells enter a senescent state or undergo apoptosis. Telomere length is therefore a proxy measure of cellular aging and remaining replicative capacity. The enzyme telomerase can partially reverse telomere shortening by adding TTAGGG sequences to chromosome ends, but its activity varies across cell types and decreases with age. Elizabeth Blackburn, Carol Greider, and Jack Szostak received the 2009 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for the discovery of telomerase and the mechanisms of chromosomal protection by telomeres.

The connection between psychological stress and telomere attrition was established in a landmark 2004 paper by Epel, Blackburn, Lin, Dhabhar, Adler, Morrow, and Cawthon published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences. The study compared telomere length in women who were caregivers for chronically ill or disabled children, a model of chronic psychological stress, to women with healthy children serving as controls. The caregivers showed significantly shorter telomere length and lower telomerase activity than controls, even after controlling for chronological age. The magnitude of the difference was equivalent to approximately 10 years of additional biological aging. Critically, among the caregiver group, those who rated their stress as highest showed the shortest telomeres, establishing a dose-response relationship between perceived psychological stress and telomere attrition. Subsequent meta-analyses including a 2012 review by Shalev and colleagues in PLoS ONE pooling data from 27 studies have consistently confirmed the association between perceived stress and shorter telomere length in circulating leukocytes.

The Rumination Research: What Sustained Negative Thinking Does to Physiology

Rumination, defined in the clinical psychology literature as the tendency to repetitively and passively focus on symptoms of distress and the possible causes and consequences of those symptoms rather than engaging in problem-solving or distraction, was identified by Susan Nolen-Hoeksema at Yale University as a key mechanism in the onset and maintenance of depression. Nolen-Hoeksema's response styles theory, developed through longitudinal studies beginning in the late 1980s, documented that ruminative response style in the face of depressed mood predicted both the severity and the duration of depressive episodes, while distraction and problem-solving response styles predicted faster recovery. A 2019 meta-analysis by Mor and Winquist in Psychological Bulletin, pooling data from 43 studies, confirmed that rumination is associated with increased severity of depression, anxiety, and negative affect across diverse samples.

The physiological consequences of rumination operate through the same stress-response pathways as acute threat. A 2005 study by Brosschot, Gerin, and Thayer published in the Journal of Psychosomatic Research proposed the perseverative cognition hypothesis: that the health effects of stress are mediated not only by the stress exposure itself but by the cognitive representation of that stress, which prolongs the physiological activation associated with the stressor beyond its actual duration. People who ruminate on stressful events continue to show elevated cortisol, elevated cardiovascular reactivity, and suppressed immune function hours and days after the stressor has resolved. A 2013 study by Zoccola and Dickerson at Brown University and UC Santa Barbara respectively, published in Psychosomatic Medicine, found that ruminators showed elevated cortisol for a significantly longer duration after a laboratory social evaluative stressor than non-ruminators with identical objective exposure to the stressor. The body cannot distinguish between a real and a replayed threat. Every replay of the threatening event activates the same physiological response as the original event, compounding the biological cost of the initial stressor with each repetition.

title: Do Unto Others: The Case for No More Economic Wars. Our Children's Children Will Hate Us for Them.
teaser: America spends $8 trillion bombing the majority of humanity and then wonders why the world does not trust it. The Global South is not a foreign policy abstraction. It is six billion people who are keeping receipts. Their children are growing up. Ours will pay the bill. This is the intergenerational math of economic warfare, and it does not resolve in America's favor.
body:
America teaches its children to say sorry when they hurt someone. It does not teach them that it bombed Iraq for oil infrastructure contracts, that it froze Libya's sovereign wealth fund while the country descended into slave markets, or that it overthrew Iran's democracy in 1953 to protect British petroleum interests. Those children grow up anyway. So do the children in Baghdad and Benghazi and Tehran. The difference is that the latter group knows exactly what happened to them. And they are not going to forget.

The Majority Face of Humanity

The Global South is not a diplomatic euphemism. It is a demographic fact. Approximately 6.6 billion of the world's 8 billion people live in the Global South. That is more than 80 percent of humanity. Every population projection through 2100 shows that share growing, not shrinking. When America wages economic warfare against Iraq, Iran, Libya, Venezuela, or Yemen, it is not conducting a targeted operation against a rogue government. It is conducting it against the demographic majority of the human race.

The people most directly harmed by American military and economic interventions are not the governments those interventions claim to target. The governments have bunkers. The people have neighborhoods. UNICEF's documented record of grave violations against children in conflict zones that the United States has been militarily involved in spans decades and countries: Iraq, Afghanistan, Yemen, Syria, Libya. The children in those records did not vote for their governments. They were not combatants. They were in the way of an economic calculation made in Washington, and the consequences of that calculation are documented in their developmental outcomes, their displacement records, and their mortality statistics.

The Bill Our Children Will Pay

The Brown University Costs of War Project has been tracking the full financial cost of American post-9/11 military engagements since 2011. Their 2023 report produced a figure that the American political conversation has not seriously reckoned with. The total cost of post-9/11 American wars, including direct military spending, veterans' disability and healthcare costs projected through 2050, homeland security expenditures, and interest on war-related debt, is estimated at approximately $8 trillion. Most of this has not yet been paid. The wars were financed through deficit spending. The interest is compounding. The veterans' care obligations extend for decades. American children who were not born on September 12, 2001 will spend their working lives paying for decisions made before they existed.

This is the domestic intergenerational account. It runs alongside the foreign one. The children who will inherit the debt are the same generation who will inherit the foreign policy consequences of creating the conditions that produced the next cycle of conflict. The bill arrives on both sides of the ledger at the same time, and the people who approved the original expenditure will be gone when it comes due.

We Bombed Them So They Could Not Compete

The honest reading of the pattern documented in the declassified record, across Iran, Iraq, and Libya, is not that America was defending democracy or fighting terrorism. The pattern, consistent across the documented record, is competitive suppression. Countries that nationalize their resources, price their oil outside the dollar system, build independent financial institutions, or attempt to create regional economic frameworks that exclude American mediation are the countries that receive American military attention. The sequence is not coincidental. It is operational. This has been documented in CB-000076 of this publication with primary sources.

The Iraqi economy in 2003 was not a threat to American security. It was a potential competitor for regional economic influence, sitting on the second-largest proven oil reserves on earth, and it had just announced it would denominate its oil sales in euros. The Libyan economy in 2011 was not committing genocide requiring NATO intervention under the Responsibility to Protect doctrine. It was building a gold-backed pan-African currency that would have given fourteen African nations monetary independence from French Treasury control. The United Nations Conference on Trade and Development's review of evidence on sanctions and conflict finds what the record suggests: military and economic intervention in resource-rich developing nations consistently produces developmental regression that outlasts the conflict by decades. Countries that were positioned to develop autonomous economic capacity do not develop it. The resources remain accessible to external extraction. The competition does not materialize.

Robert Pape's research on the strategic logic of political violence adds the mechanism that connects competitive suppression to its consequence. Pape's comprehensive analysis of every documented suicide terrorism campaign between 1980 and 2003 finds that the primary strategic driver is not religious extremism but foreign military occupation. Ninety-five percent of documented suicide attacks were directed against democratic states with troops in the attacker's homeland. The terrorism America has spent $8 trillion fighting is, by the most rigorous empirical analysis of its own strategic logic, a response to the military presence that costs $8 trillion to maintain. The cycle is self-financing, self-perpetuating, and leaves the bill to the next generation.

What They Were Becoming

The countries that were destabilized or destroyed were not failed states before the interventions. Iraq had universities and a professional class. Libya had the highest Human Development Index on the African continent and no external debt. Iran in 1953 had a functioning parliament and an elected prime minister. These were not primitive economies requiring Western management. They were developing economies with the resource base to become serious regional powers.

The Global South they were part of is now developing that regional power anyway, through the countries that were not bombed. The IMF's 2024 data shows that emerging market and developing economies now account for approximately 60 percent of global GDP measured by purchasing power parity, up from 40 percent in 2000. The BRICS grouping, which expanded in 2024 to include Saudi Arabia, the UAE, Iran, Ethiopia, and Egypt, represents approximately 37 percent of global GDP. The Global South is not waiting for permission to develop. It is developing. The countries that were not bombed are doing what the countries that were bombed were prevented from doing.

The intergenerational hate has two directions. American children will inherit the debt and the blowback. The children of the Global South will inherit the memory of who bombed their parents and why. Neither group chose their inheritance. Both are getting it.

They Will Hate Us Too, and They Will Be Right

Research on intergenerational trauma documents that the psychological and physiological consequences of violence do not resolve within a single generation. Peer-reviewed research on epigenetic transmission of trauma documents measurable biological differences in the children of people who experienced severe collective violence, differences in stress hormone regulation and gene expression that persist across generations without direct exposure to the original events. The children growing up in the aftermath of the Iraq War, the Libyan intervention, and decades of Iranian sanctions are not neutrally absorbing what happened to their parents and grandparents. They are inheriting it at a cellular level.

The indignation those children feel toward the United States is not irrational. It is not the product of propaganda or religious extremism or cultural dysfunction. It is the logical response of people who can read the declassified record, who know what the Downing Street Memo says, what the CIA did in 1953, who watched their country’s sovereign wealth fund get frozen and their cities reduced to militias. They understand that the stated justifications were not the actual justifications. Their indignation is documented, rational, and proportionate to the documented harm.

This is the paradox that American foreign policy has never honestly confronted. America teaches its children the Golden Rule in the first grade. It applies that principle to every domestic relationship its children will have. It does not apply it to the children in the countries it bombs. And the children in those countries know it. They have the same access to the historical record that anyone does. They are not confused about what happened. They are simply on the other side of it, and they will be on the other side of it for generations, with compounding interest, because trauma compounds the same way debt does.

The Case for No More Economic Wars

The case does not rest on sentiment. It rests on arithmetic. The United States military is the world's single largest institutional consumer of fossil fuels. The same countries targeted by American military intervention for their oil resources are the countries most vulnerable to the climate consequences of burning that oil. The Global South did not industrialize on fossil fuels. It is inheriting the climate consequences of the industrialization of countries that did, and it is also being bombed by the largest single military consumer of the fuel driving those consequences. The ledger is not balanced in any direction.

The Global South is 80 percent of humanity. It is where virtually all population growth will occur. It is where the majority of global GDP growth is already happening. It is where the children are who will determine whether the twenty-second century is one in which the United States has partners or enemies. Every economic war that suppresses a developing nation's autonomous growth, every intervention that installs a compliant government over a democratic one, every sanction that impoverishes a civilian population to pressure a government, is a deposit into an account that the next generation of Americans will have to reckon with.

Do unto others. The principle is not complicated. The application of it to foreign policy does not require a new doctrine or a new administration or a new law. It requires the acknowledgment that the children in Baghdad and Benghazi and Tehran are the same as the children in Baltimore and Boston and Baton Rouge. None of them chose what was done in their name or to their country, and all of them will live with the consequences for the rest of their lives.

America's children will hate it for the debt. The world's children will hate it for the bombs. Both groups will have the receipts. Both groups will be right.

title: The Countries America Bombs Are Overwhelmingly Non-White. Their Resources Are Almost Always the Point. The Evidence Is in the Record.
teaser: Iraq had the world's second-largest proven oil reserves. Libya had the largest in Africa and was building a gold-backed currency to break Western financial control of the continent. Iran nationalized its oil in 1953 and had a functioning democracy until the CIA ended it. The pattern across every American war of choice is not a theory. It is a documented fact that the declassified record confirms.
body:
There is a version of American foreign policy that is taught in schools: a superpower defending freedom, spreading democracy, keeping the peace. Then there is the version in the declassified record. The two versions do not resemble each other. What the documents show, across seven decades of military intervention, is a pattern so consistent it stopped being a coincidence sometime around the third or fourth coup. The countries America destabilizes are overwhelmingly majority non-white. Their crime, in case after documented case, is controlling resources the West wants and making independent decisions about who gets them.

The Pattern You Are Not Supposed to Name

William Blum spent decades cataloguing U.S. military and CIA interventions since World War II. His tally, documented in primary government sources, congressional records, and declassified intelligence files, runs to more than fifty countries. The overwhelming majority of those fifty countries are in Africa, Latin America, Asia, and the Middle East. The overwhelming majority of their populations are people of color. This is not a coincidence that requires statistical proof. It is visible on a map.

John Perkins, a former economic consultant who worked for firms advising the U.S. government on development financing, described the playbook in 2004. The sequence is consistent: first, economic pressure through development loans designed to create debt dependency. If the government refuses, covert destabilization. If that fails, military force. The countries that move through all three stages tend to have one thing in common. They said no.

What they said no to varies by case. In Iran in 1953, they said no to British ownership of Iranian oil. In Libya in 2011, Muammar Gaddafi was in the process of saying no to the French-controlled monetary system that governed fourteen African nations. In Iraq in 2003, Saddam Hussein had announced in 2000 that Iraq would begin selling its oil in euros rather than dollars. Each of these decisions triggered a response from the West. The response was never proportionate to any stated security threat. It was proportionate to the economic threat.

Iran: The Coup That Started Everything

Mohammad Mosaddegh was elected Prime Minister of Iran in 1951. He was a lawyer and a democrat who had spent his career arguing that Iranians should own Iranian oil. The Anglo-Persian Oil Company, the British firm that would later become BP, controlled Iranian petroleum extraction and kept the overwhelming share of profits. Mosaddegh nationalized the oil fields. The British government, working with the Eisenhower administration, decided he had to go.

In 2013, the CIA declassified its own internal history of the operation, written by agency historian Donald Wilber. The document confirms that Operation Ajax was planned and executed to reverse Iran's nationalization of its own oil fields. The democratically elected government was replaced by Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, who ruled as an autocrat until the 1979 revolution that the United States has spent every year since trying to reverse.

The hostage crisis, the sanctions, the nuclear standoff, the proxy conflicts across the region: all of it flows directly from the decision in 1953 to overthrow a man whose crime was wanting Iranians to own Iranian oil. When Mosaddegh addressed the United Nations Security Council in 1951, he said that his country sought only to administer its own resources for its own people. The Security Council took no action. The CIA took plenty.

Iran today sits on the world's fourth-largest proven oil reserves and the second-largest natural gas reserves. The United States has maintained a sanctions regime against it for more than forty years. The Iran nuclear deal negotiated in 2015 suspended that regime in exchange for verified limits on uranium enrichment. Iran complied. The International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed compliance repeatedly. In 2018, the United States withdrew from the agreement unilaterally. If the nuclear program had been the actual concern, withdrawal from a working agreement would not be the logical response.

Iraq: The Intelligence That Was Fixed Around the Policy

On July 23, 2002, the head of British foreign intelligence, Richard Dearlove, returned from a meeting in Washington and briefed senior members of the Blair government. His assessment was recorded in a classified memorandum. The memorandum was leaked to The Sunday Times in 2005 and became known as the Downing Street Memo.

The memo reads, in part: 'Military action was now seen as inevitable. Bush wanted to remove Saddam, through military action, justified by the conjunction of terrorism and WMD. But the intelligence and facts were being fixed around the policy.' This memo was written eight months before the March 2003 invasion. The decision had already been made. The intelligence was being assembled to justify it.

The WMD did not exist. Every weapons inspector, every post-invasion search, every commission of inquiry confirmed this after the fact. What existed in Iraq was the second-largest proven oil reserve on earth, an economy that had announced in 2000 it would denominate its oil sales in euros rather than dollars, and a government that had spent the 1990s under UN sanctions that had killed an estimated 500,000 Iraqi children according to UNICEF's own reporting.

The human cost of the 2003 invasion was documented by a peer-reviewed study published in The Lancet in 2006. The study, conducted by researchers at Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, estimated 654,965 excess Iraqi deaths in the three years following the invasion. The study used the same epidemiological methodology used to estimate mortality in Darfur and the Democratic Republic of Congo without controversy. The administration disputed the number. No peer-reviewed counter-study has produced a substantially lower estimate.

In the months following the invasion, Halliburton, the defense and energy corporation formerly led by Vice President Dick Cheney, received no-bid contracts worth an estimated $39.5 billion to manage Iraqi oil infrastructure and provide military logistics support. The Treasury Department froze Iraqi oil revenues and transferred them to the Development Fund for Iraq, administered initially by the Coalition Provisional Authority under American control. The oil fields were not nationalized by the invading force in any legal sense. They were simply managed by it.

Libya: The Man Who Threatened the Dollar

In 2010, the United Nations Development Programme ranked Libya 53rd in its Human Development Index, the highest ranking of any African nation. Gaddafi's government provided free healthcare, free education through university, free housing for citizens, and maintained the country with no external debt. Libya held a sovereign wealth fund of more than $32 billion. The country had the largest proven oil reserves on the African continent.

In early 2011, Gaddafi was in active discussions with other African heads of state about replacing the CFA franc, the currency used by fourteen former French colonies whose monetary policy was controlled by the French Treasury, with a gold-backed pan-African dinar for oil transactions. The CFA franc arrangement required those fourteen nations to deposit 50 percent of their foreign reserves in the French Treasury as a condition of independence, a colonial financial arrangement that had persisted since 1945.

A memorandum from presidential adviser Sidney Blumenthal to Secretary of State Hillary Clinton, released through a FOIA request, stated explicitly that Gaddafi's gold-backed currency plan was among the primary motivations for France's aggressive push for NATO intervention. Gaddafi held an estimated 143 tons of gold reserves. The email was written seven months before the NATO bombing campaign that ended his government and his life.

The humanitarian justification for the Libya intervention was the Responsibility to Protect doctrine, the argument that the international community has an obligation to intervene when a government is attacking its own civilians. The intervention was authorized by the UN Security Council under Resolution 1973. Within weeks, what had been authorized as a no-fly zone had become active NATO airstrikes supporting rebel ground forces. Gaddafi was captured and killed in October 2011.

What followed in Libya was not democracy. The sovereign wealth fund was frozen and largely dispersed. The country fractured into competing militias and rival governments. Open slave markets for sub-Saharan African migrants were documented and reported by CNN in 2017. The country that had the highest Human Development Index on the African continent became a failed state. The oil continued to flow.

The Petrodollar: Why the Currency of Oil Is the Currency of Control

To understand why Iraq's announcement that it would sell oil in euros and Gaddafi's gold dinar plan both preceded Western military intervention, it is necessary to understand the petrodollar system. In 1974, Henry Kissinger negotiated an agreement with Saudi Arabia in which the kingdom agreed to price and sell all of its oil exclusively in U.S. dollars, in exchange for American military protection. OPEC adopted this framework. The result is that every nation on earth that imports oil must hold U.S. dollar reserves to do so, creating permanent global demand for American currency and underwriting the dollar's status as the world's reserve currency.

This system is not incidental to American economic power. It is foundational to it. It is why the U.S. can run persistent trade deficits that would bankrupt any other nation. It is why American debt is treated as the global safe haven asset. And it is why any oil-producing nation that announces it will sell its oil in another currency is not making an economic decision. It is making a geopolitical statement that the United States has consistently treated as an act of war.

Iraq switched to euros in 2000. The invasion came in 2003. Libya was building a gold alternative in 2011. The bombing came in 2011. Iran has been attempting to sell oil in non-dollar currencies for decades. The sanctions and the threat of military force have been constant. Venezuela, which has the world's largest proven oil reserves, elected a government that attempted to price its oil outside the dollar system. The United States has supported coup attempts, economic sanctions, and opposition funding there for twenty years.

The Geography of Who Gets Bombed

Chalmers Johnson, a former CIA consultant and political scientist, spent the final decade of his career documenting what he called "blowback": the unintended consequences of covert American interventions that return to harm American interests and people. Johnson documents that the United States maintains what functions as a global empire through a network of more than 800 military bases in approximately 80 countries, concentrated overwhelmingly in non-white-majority nations in Africa, Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America. The nations in which the U.S. maintains no military presence and has never conducted regime change operations are almost exclusively wealthy, white-majority nations in Western Europe.

The scholar Walter Rodney argued in 1972 that the underdevelopment of Africa was not a natural condition or a failure of African capacity. It was the direct result of a deliberate and sustained extraction of African resources and labor by European powers who required African underdevelopment as the basis of their own development. Rodney's analysis, written fifty years ago, precisely describes the operational logic of what happened in Libya, Iraq, and Iran. The pattern is not a relic of colonialism. It is colonialism's continuation by other means: regime change instead of direct administration, sanctions instead of trade monopolies, IMF structural adjustment instead of chartered companies.

The question of race is not incidental to this analysis. It is structural. The nations that the United States has invaded, bombed, sanctioned, or destabilized since 1945 are overwhelmingly majority non-white. The nations whose right to nuclear weapons is treated as legitimate, the nations that receive U.S. military aid rather than U.S. military force, are overwhelmingly majority white. Saudi Arabia beheads more people per year than Iran and has no elections. It is an American ally. The analysis that separates the racial geography of American military intervention from the racial geography of American domestic policy misses the point that they are expressions of the same system.

The Evidence Is in the Record

None of this requires a theory. The CIA has declassified its own history of the 1953 Iran coup. The Downing Street Memo is a primary document in the British National Archives. The Clinton State Department emails on Libya's gold dinar are available through the State Department's own FOIA portal. The Lancet study is peer-reviewed and cited in the medical literature. The petrodollar agreement is documented in the academic economic history of the Kissinger era. Halliburton's contracts are in the Congressional Record.

What has not been declassified, what has not been litigated, what has not been the subject of a presidential commission, is the cumulative moral accounting of what these interventions cost the people who lived through them. The 654,000 Iraqis. The Libyans sold in slave markets in the ruins of the country with the highest Human Development Index on their continent. The Iranians who have lived under crushing economic sanctions for forty years for the crime of having had a revolution in response to a coup that America organized. The Iranians who had a democracy in 1953 and have not been permitted to have one since.

America has never called any of this what it is. It calls it national security. It calls it humanitarian intervention. It calls it the protection of democracy. The democracies it protects are almost never the ones where the people are majority non-white and poor and happen to be sitting on resources the West requires. Those democracies it ends.

The record is not hidden. It is declassified, peer-reviewed, FOIA-released, and sitting in the congressional archives. The question is not whether the pattern exists. It is why, given that the evidence is this clear, the country continues to act as though it does not.

title: Racism Is Never OK: The Difficult Conversations We Must All Have With Our Families. Accountability Matters.
teaser: The hardest conversation you will ever have is the one at your own dinner table. The research shows it is also among the most important ones you will ever have. Across every racial community in America, people are finding the courage to hold the people they love accountable for the harm they cause. This is what that looks like, and why it matters more than most of us have been told.
body:
You know the moment. Someone at the table says something. It might be your grandmother describing the new neighbors. It might be your uncle at Thanksgiving, your father on the drive home from church, your mother on the phone. The word or the assumption hangs in the air and you feel it land in your chest, and for a fraction of a second you are deciding: let it go, or say something. This article is about the people who said something. And about what the research shows happens when they do.

The Dinner Table Is Not a Neutral Space

Beverly Daniel Tatum, psychologist and former president of Spelman College, has spent her career studying how racial identity forms in American families. Her foundational work, first published in 1997 and updated in 2017, documents something that parents across racial communities often resist acknowledging: silence about race in family settings does not produce racially neutral children. It produces children who have absorbed whatever racial attitudes surrounded them, without any framework to examine or question what they have taken in.

Tatum's research is not an indictment of families. It is an invitation to them. The family table is where most of what we believe about the world is first handed to us, and where, if we are fortunate, it can also first be examined. The people who push back at that table are not being disloyal to their families. They are taking the family's own values seriously enough to test them against the world.

Research on racial socialization published in Developmental Psychology by Diane Hughes and colleagues across multiple institutions shows a consistent pattern. Families who actively discuss race and racism with their children, who name prejudice when they encounter it and model responses to it, produce children with stronger psychological resilience, clearer values, and a greater capacity for empathy across group lines. These conversations are not comfortable. They are, the research shows, among the most consequential parenting choices a family can make.

This Happens in Every Community

The image most commonly associated with family accountability conversations about race is a young white person pushing back at an older white relative. That image is real. It is also incomplete. Across every racial and ethnic community in America, people are holding members of their own families accountable for bias, and the shape of those conversations varies in ways that the mainstream conversation about racism rarely makes room to acknowledge.

Eduardo Bonilla-Silva's work on the architecture of racial prejudice in America documents something important: racial prejudice is not the exclusive property of any single group. It is a structural feature of American life that shapes attitudes across communities, often in ways that are not consciously recognized or named. Black families navigate anti-immigrant bias directed at Latino neighbors. Latino families navigate anti-Black prejudice passed down through generations. Asian American families navigate prejudice toward both Black and Latino communities. Indigenous communities navigate pressures from multiple directions simultaneously. The family accountability conversation is not a white family phenomenon. It is an American phenomenon.

Research by Luis Fraga and colleagues, published as part of the Latino National Political Survey, documents the documented tension in some Latino communities around anti-Black attitudes, and the equally documented efforts within those communities by family members, clergy, and organizers who challenge it directly. These internal community conversations, family members challenging family members across the kitchen table on the dignity of Black neighbors, are part of the same tradition as the white college student confronting a grandparent at Thanksgiving. The stakes are different. The love is the same.

The Science of Speaking Up

Gordon Allport's contact hypothesis, first articulated in 1954 and subsequently tested in hundreds of studies across six decades, established that meaningful contact between members of different groups reduces prejudice under the right conditions. A 2006 meta-analysis by Thomas Pettigrew and Linda Tropp of 515 studies involving more than 250,000 participants confirmed it: face-to-face contact with members of other groups reduces prejudice, and the effect is strongest when that contact is personal and sustained.

The family relationship is the most personal and sustained contact most people ever have. When a family member learns that someone they love has a close friendship with, a meaningful relationship with, or deep respect for members of a group they have been dismissing, that knowledge changes them. Not always immediately. Not always completely. But the research is consistent: personal connection is the most powerful prejudice-reduction mechanism that exists. And the family member who says something at the dinner table is creating exactly that kind of personal, sustained contact with the truth.

John Dovidio and Samuel Gaertner's research on what they call "aversive racism" adds another dimension. Most contemporary prejudice operates not through explicit hostility but through unconscious bias held by people who genuinely believe themselves to be unprejudiced. Relationship-based challenges, the kind that happen at the family table, are more effective at shifting this kind of bias than public confrontation. The family member who says something is, the research indicates, using the most effective tool available.

Calling In, Not Just Calling Out

Loretta Ross, a veteran human rights activist and scholar at Smith College, has spent years articulating the difference between "calling out" and "calling in." Calling out, she argues, treats the person who causes harm as an opponent to be defeated. Calling in treats them as someone whose growth matters to you, someone you are willing to stay in relationship with through the discomfort of change. Ross, who has worked with former white supremacists on deradicalization, argues that the most durable change happens through sustained relationship. The family dinner table, uncomfortable as it is, is exactly where that sustained relationship exists.

Calling in does not mean softening the message. It does not mean accepting the premise that the comment was not harmful, or laughing it off to preserve the peace. It means delivering the truth in a way that keeps the relationship intact, because the relationship is the vehicle for the change. It sounds like: "I love you and I need you to hear me on this." It sounds like: "That is not who our family is going to be." It sounds like: "I am telling you this because what you just said matters and so do you."

It does not always go well the first time. The research on behavior change is consistent on this point: most meaningful shifts happen over multiple conversations, not one. The person at the table who says something is rarely the person who delivers a single devastating argument and watches a worldview transform in real time. They are the person who plants something, returns to it, and trusts that love and time will do what a single dinner conversation cannot.

What It Actually Looks Like Across Communities

In Black families navigating anti-immigrant bias, the conversation often centers on solidarity and shared history. The argument that connects: Black Americans and Latino immigrants share a documented history of being used as economic scapegoats by political systems that benefit from their division. The conversation that shifts attitudes most effectively, the research and the organizers agree, is not a political argument. It is a personal one: the family member who says, I know a family that came here the same way your grandfather came here, and they deserve the same dignity.

In white families navigating the gap between an older generation's assumptions and a younger generation's experience, the most documented path forward is consistent, patient, relationship-based truth-telling. Not a single explosion at Thanksgiving, but a pattern of small corrections, redirections, and conversations over time. The young white person who says, gently and consistently, "That word is not okay in this house," is doing the same work as the Black family member who says, "We do not talk about our neighbors that way." The form varies. The commitment is identical.

In Latino and Asian American families navigating anti-Black prejudice inherited from cultures and histories that have their own complicated relationships with race, the conversation often starts with naming what was never named. Colorism. The specific way some communities were taught to distance themselves from Blackness as a survival strategy in a country that punished it. The family member who opens that conversation is not accusing their parents of malice. They are offering them the context their parents were never given.

The Aha Moment Is This

James Baldwin wrote in 1962 that not everything that is faced can be changed, but nothing can be changed until it is faced. He was writing about the country. He was also, without knowing it, writing about every kitchen table in America where someone is about to take a breath and say something difficult to someone they love.

The aha moment in this conversation is not a dramatic one. It does not arrive in a single argument or a single dinner. It arrives, if it arrives, quietly: the next time your relative meets the neighbors they dismissed and treats them differently. The next time they catch themselves before saying the thing they used to say freely. The next time they tell you, maybe years later, that they have been thinking about what you said.

The research on this is clear: people who report the most significant shifts in their racial attitudes most often identify a trusted family member as the catalyst. Not a political argument. Not a viral moment. Someone who loved them enough to tell them the truth.

That is what accountability in a family looks like. It is not punishment. It is not rejection. It is the decision, made in love, that the people at your table deserve better from you than silence, and that you deserve better from them than the person that silence lets them remain. The most radical thing you can do in a family is refuse to pretend. The most loving thing you can do is stay at the table anyway, and keep talking, because the alternative is leaving them exactly where they are.

Racism is never okay. Not at the table, not in the car, not on the phone, not in any community, not from any direction. And the people who say so, clearly and with love, to the people who matter most to them, are doing some of the most important work this country has. They are just doing it somewhere the cameras do not reach.

title: A Country Built on Stolen Land, Stolen Labor, and Stolen Lives Would Like You to Know It Built Itself.
teaser: The argument that Black, Indigenous, and Mexican people are lazy was invented by the same civilization that crossed an ocean to kidnap African people because it refused to do its own work. Edward Baptist documented what that labor actually produced. The Federal Reserve has the current numbers. The gap between those two data points is not an accident. It is a policy outcome.
body:
Let us begin with the logic. The argument that Black Americans, Indigenous people, and Mexican laborers are inherently lazy has been a cornerstone of American political rhetoric for two centuries. It is also an argument made by a civilization that crossed thousands of miles of ocean to kidnap millions of African people, transport them in conditions deliberately designed to break the body and the mind, and force them to work without wages for generations, because it did not want to do the work itself. The laziness argument has always required its proponents not to examine who, specifically, was avoiding labor. The documented economic record does not share that reluctance.

What the Cotton Economy Actually Was

In 2014, historian Edward Baptist published The Half Has Never Been Told: Slavery and the Making of American Capitalism. The book is not a moral argument. It is an economic analysis. Baptist spent years in the primary sources, including plantation ledgers, account books, slave narratives, and commodity records, documenting what the American economy was built on and how it operated. Baptist documents that cotton, produced almost entirely by enslaved people, grew from 1.5 million pounds in 1790 to 2.3 billion pounds by 1860, and by that year represented more than half of all American exports.

The financial infrastructure of the United States, including its banks, its insurance industry, and its credit markets, was built around the cotton economy. Northern textile mills processed Southern cotton. Wall Street financed the plantations. Liverpool bought the product. The entire Atlantic economy of the nineteenth century ran on the labor of people who were owned. Baptist argues, with primary source evidence, that this was not a regional peculiarity of the antebellum South. It was the American economy. The two were not distinct things.

He also documents what he calls the "pushing system." Enslavers did not simply put people in fields and collect the yield. They created a systematic regime of data management and torture, recording each enslaved person's daily cotton output and using the whip to ensure that output increased, year over year, with the precision of a modern productivity metric. Cotton productivity per enslaved worker increased by roughly 400 percent between 1800 and 1860, a rate Baptist argues cannot be explained by technological change and must be attributed to the systematic use of violence as economic optimization. This is the labor that built the American economy. The people performing it were described, in the same era, as lazy.

The Numbers the Census Kept

The 1860 United States Census documented the total value of enslaved people as property at approximately 3.5 billion dollars, representing roughly 40 percent of all measurable American wealth at the time. The capital value of enslaved people in 1860 exceeded the total combined value of all factories, railroads, and banks in the United States. Economists applying modern valuation methodologies to this figure have estimated its present-day equivalent at between ten and fourteen trillion dollars. That is not the value of what enslaved labor produced. That is the value of the people themselves as financial instruments: collateral on loans, assets on balance sheets, and inheritable property passed between generations of white families who built their wealth through ownership of human beings.

The people who were that wealth, listed in census schedules not as persons but as numbers beside their enslavers' names, were building nothing for themselves. They were building it for people who would later tell their descendants that their ancestors had built it alone.

The Land Was Not Theirs Either

The story of American self-construction also requires a specific understanding of the land on which that construction occurred. The Homestead Act of 1862 is commonly cited as evidence of American industriousness: 270 million acres of "free" land distributed to settlers who would work it and build lives on it. The 270 million acres were not empty. They belonged to Indigenous nations whose displacement had been accomplished through centuries of broken treaties, military campaigns, forced removal, and deliberate starvation. The Homestead Act did not create free land. It redistributed stolen land to people whose descendants would later describe themselves as self-made.

The agricultural wealth of the American Midwest and West, the farmland, the cattle ranges, and the mineral rights, was built on a foundation of Indigenous dispossession so complete that it has largely ceased to register as a transaction. It registers instead as inheritance. As something that was simply there, waiting to be claimed by people with the industriousness to claim it. The people from whom it was taken are described, in the same tradition, as having done nothing with it.

After the War: The Promise Made and Unmade

When the Civil War ended, the federal government made a specific promise to the four million people it had just nominally freed. Special Field Order No. 15, issued by General William T. Sherman in January 1865 and endorsed by Secretary of War Edwin Stanton, redistributed 400,000 acres of confiscated Confederate coastal land to formerly enslaved families: roughly 40 acres per family, with the loan of Army mules. The Freedmen's Bureau was established to administer the transition. President Andrew Johnson rescinded the order within months of taking office after Lincoln's assassination. The land was returned to its former Confederate owners. The 40,000 Black families who had begun settling it were evicted.

What followed was sharecropping, a system in which formerly enslaved people farmed land owned by their former enslavers, paid for tools, seed, and housing through the yield of their labor, and were kept in debt by accounting systems they were frequently not permitted to audit. The legal architecture of sharecropping reproduced the economic conditions of slavery without the name. It was enforced by vagrancy laws, convict leasing, and the documented violence of Reconstruction-era terrorism. The wealth that formerly enslaved people produced in the sharecropping system flowed, as it had before, upward and outward to white landowners and the financial systems that served them.

The GI Bill They Didn't Get

The Servicemen's Readjustment Act of 1944, known as the GI Bill, is among the most celebrated pieces of social legislation in American history. It financed the college education of a generation, provided low-cost home mortgages, and created the postwar American middle class. It is frequently cited as evidence of what government investment can accomplish. What is cited less frequently is its administration. Research published in the Industrial and Labor Relations Review documented that Black veterans were systematically excluded from the GI Bill's most wealth-building benefits: universities refused their applications, and the Federal Housing Administration refused to guarantee mortgages in the neighborhoods where Black veterans lived.

The FHA's redlining system, documented in the Home Owners' Loan Corporation residential security maps held by the National Archives, color-coded neighborhoods by their perceived investment risk. Neighborhoods with significant Black populations were rated hazardous regardless of residents' economic status, making them ineligible for FHA-backed mortgages, the primary mechanism through which the postwar housing boom built generational white wealth. Black families who had served in the same war, survived the same campaigns, and returned to the same country were excluded from the specific government program that turned service into wealth. The white families who accessed it built equity. Their children inherited that equity. The gap compounded.

The Federal Reserve Has the Current Numbers

The Survey of Consumer Finances, published by the Federal Reserve Board in 2023, documents the current state of the wealth gap with the precision of a financial instrument. The median net worth of white families in 2022 was $285,000. The median net worth of Black families was $44,900, a ratio of roughly six to one. The gap has not meaningfully closed since the passage of the Civil Rights Act in 1964.

This is not a gap produced by individual choices about savings rates. It is the compounded result of the specific policy sequence documented above. That sequence includes unpaid labor for generations, promises of land redistribution rescinded within months, exclusion from the specific federal programs that converted the postwar economy into generational white wealth, and the ongoing effects of discriminatory credit and housing markets whose redlined maps are still visible in neighborhood property values today. Economists Darrick Hamilton and William Darity Jr. have argued that the racial wealth gap is not a cultural problem with an individual solution; it is a policy outcome with a policy origin, and the primary source record of those policies is not in dispute.

On the Laziness of the People Who Built This

The "lazy" argument has a specific political function. It converts a documented history of forced labor, legal exclusion, and compounding dispossession into a character flaw. It locates the cause of the wealth gap in the people who were denied wealth, rather than in the specific mechanisms of denial. It asks the recipient of a six-to-one wealth advantage, an advantage built on enslaved labor, stolen land, broken promises, and discriminatory federal policy, to believe that the advantage reflects merit and the disadvantage reflects effort.

The argument was made about enslaved people who worked from before sunrise to after sunset under the threat of physical violence, producing billions of dollars in economic value for people who owned them. It was made about Indigenous people whose land was seized because they were deemed to be wasting it. It has been made about Mexican and Mexican-American laborers who built the railroads of the American Southwest, harvested the crops of California's agricultural economy under the bracero program, and have been essential to American agricultural production for a century, while being told, in the same breath, that they contribute nothing and take everything.

The documented record does not support the argument. The documented record shows a civilization that went to another continent to steal human beings for their labor and built its economy on that labor for two and a half centuries. That same civilization broke its promises of restitution within months of a war fought over those stolen human beings, excluded the descendants of that labor from the specific federal programs that built the white middle class, and then described the resulting wealth gap as a reflection of work ethic.

The primary sources are in the National Archives. Edward Baptist spent years in them. The Federal Reserve publishes the current numbers annually. The gap between what was built and who received credit for building it is not a matter of interpretation. It is a matter of arithmetic. And the arithmetic has never been on the side of the argument that erected it.
