title: Private Equity Acquisitions of Hospitals Are Associated With Higher Prices, Lower Staffing Ratios, and Worse Patient Safety Outcomes, According to Multiple Peer-Reviewed Studies Published in JAMA and Health Affairs. Steward Health Care Filed for Bankruptcy in 2024.
teaser: The academic record on private equity hospital acquisitions is documented in JAMA, Health Affairs, and the New England Journal of Medicine. The Steward Health Care bankruptcy documents the worst-case outcome.
body:
Private equity firms have acquired hundreds of hospitals and health systems in the United States since 2000. Peer-reviewed research published in the Journal of the American Medical Association and the New England Journal of Medicine documents what happens after acquisition: prices increase, staffing levels decline relative to patient volume, and quality metrics often worsen. The academic and investigative record on private equity in healthcare is extensive. This article traces it using the primary sources.

The Scale of Private Equity Healthcare Investment

Private equity investment in the U.S. healthcare sector accelerated substantially in the 2010s and early 2020s. The Private Equity Stakeholder Project, a nonprofit research organization, and the American Antitrust Institute have documented the volume of private equity healthcare acquisitions using SEC and firm-level disclosure data. The Private Equity Stakeholder Project documents hundreds of private equity acquisitions of hospitals, physician practices, and nursing homes since 2000. The Federal Trade Commission has tracked private equity healthcare acquisitions as part of its broader investigation into healthcare consolidation, publishing a report in 2024 documenting the volume and structure of private equity health system ownership. FTC reports are at ftc.gov.

The structure of a typical private equity hospital acquisition involves a leveraged buyout: the private equity firm acquires the hospital using a combination of equity and debt, with the debt often placed on the acquired hospital's balance sheet. The hospital then services that debt from operating revenue. The private equity firm's return is generated through a combination of operational improvements, sale of real estate assets (a practice called sale-leaseback), and eventual resale of the hospital to another buyer. The debt burden imposed on the acquired hospital is documented in hospital financial filings with state health authorities, which are public records in most states. The Lown Institute, a healthcare think tank, has tracked sale-leaseback transactions in private equity hospital acquisitions using state regulatory filings.

What the JAMA Research Shows

JAMA, the Journal of the American Medical Association, published a landmark study in 2023 examining patient outcomes at hospitals acquired by private equity versus comparable non-acquired hospitals. The 2023 JAMA study found higher rates of hospital-acquired conditions, including patient falls and central line infections, at private equity-acquired hospitals compared to matched comparison hospitals. The researchers used Medicare claims data linked to hospital ownership records to conduct the comparison. The JAMA study is a peer-reviewed primary source using federal administrative data. It is available through PubMed at pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov and, for subscribers, at jamanetwork.com. The Medicare claims data underlying the research is maintained by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services and is available for research use through the CMS Research Data Assistance Center.

Subsequent academic research has examined price increases at private equity-acquired hospitals, staffing changes, and emergency department outcomes. A 2024 study in Health Affairs examined prices at private equity-acquired hospitals and found substantially higher negotiated prices with commercial insurers at acquired facilities compared to pre-acquisition levels. Health Affairs is a peer-reviewed policy journal and its research is at healthaffairs.org. The pattern across the academic literature is consistent: private equity acquisition of hospitals is associated with increased prices and, in multiple studies, worsened patient safety outcomes. The individual studies use different datasets and methodologies; the consistency of findings across independent research groups increases the weight of the evidence.

The Steward Health Care Case Study

Steward Health Care is the largest example of a private equity-acquired hospital network in American history and the most thoroughly documented failure of the private equity hospital model. Cerberus Capital Management acquired Steward's predecessor entities in 2010. Over the following decade, Steward expanded to become the largest private hospital operator in the United States by number of hospitals, operating approximately 30 hospitals across multiple states. The expansion was financed through debt and sale-leaseback transactions that sold the underlying hospital real estate to Medical Properties Trust, a real estate investment trust, and leased it back to Steward.

Steward Health Care filed for bankruptcy in May 2024. The Massachusetts Attorney General's investigation produced public filings documenting how the sale-leaseback structure drained Steward of capital over a decade while generating returns for Cerberus and Medical Properties Trust. Congressional investigations into the Steward bankruptcy produced testimony and document production from Steward and Cerberus that is part of the public congressional record at congress.gov. The bankruptcy filings, including disclosure of creditor claims and asset valuations, are public records in the U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the Southern District of Texas. Senator Edward Markey and Representative Jim McGovern introduced legislation in response to the Steward collapse that would require greater financial transparency from private equity-owned hospitals. The legislative record of those proposals is at congress.gov.

The Federal Regulatory Framework and Its Gaps

The regulatory framework for hospital ownership in the United States is primarily state-level. Most states require state health department approval for hospital acquisitions through a certificate of need or change of ownership process. The Federal Trade Commission has antitrust authority over healthcare mergers that substantially lessen competition. The FTC challenged several private equity healthcare acquisitions in recent years, including physician practice acquisitions in specialty markets. The FTC's enforcement actions and merger review processes are documented at ftc.gov.

The federal government does not require private equity-owned hospitals to disclose their ownership structure, debt load, or financial condition to the public in the same way that publicly traded hospital companies must disclose to the SEC. Private equity portfolio companies are not subject to SEC continuous disclosure requirements. The information asymmetry between what private equity hospital operators know about a hospital's financial condition and what patients, employees, and local health authorities know is a documented feature of the regulatory landscape. The Senate HELP Committee's investigation into private equity in healthcare produced a report in 2024 recommending enhanced disclosure requirements. The report is at help.senate.gov. It is a public document.

The Primary Source Documentation

The academic research on private equity hospital acquisitions is available at PubMed at pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov, which indexes peer-reviewed biomedical research. JAMA, Health Affairs, NEJM, and Annals of Internal Medicine have all published relevant research. The Private Equity Stakeholder Project's research is at pestakeholder.org. The Lown Institute's hospital industry research is at lowninstitute.org. The FTC's healthcare competition reports are at ftc.gov. Congressional investigation records are at congress.gov. State hospital financial disclosures are at each state health department's website; Massachusetts publishes detailed hospital financial data at mass.gov/orh. The primary source record on what private equity has done in the U.S. hospital industry is extensive, public, and continuously updated. The patients who live in communities where private equity-acquired hospitals operate are the primary stakeholders for this information.

The Physician Practice Acquisitions: A Parallel Story

Private equity acquisition of physician practices has followed a similar pattern to hospital acquisitions, but at larger scale in terms of transaction volume. Dermatology, gastroenterology, ophthalmology, orthopedics, and emergency medicine staffing companies have all been targets of significant private equity investment. The rollup model, in which a private equity-backed platform company acquires dozens or hundreds of independent physician practices to build a regional or national provider organization, has been documented in academic research and FTC investigative proceedings. The FTC's 2024 report on healthcare consolidation documented the private equity rollup strategy in physician markets in detail. The report is at ftc.gov.

The economics of physician practice rollups are documented in the academic literature on healthcare consolidation. Research published in Health Affairs and the American Economic Review has found that private equity-backed physician practices charge higher prices for comparable services than independent practices. The mechanism is consistent with the broader consolidation literature: acquiring market power in a specialty or geography allows the acquired provider to negotiate higher reimbursement rates with commercial insurers. Patients and employers who pay insurance premiums bear the increased cost through higher premiums and cost-sharing. The CMS maintains data on healthcare provider prices and market concentration. The Rand Corporation has published analyses of hospital and physician market concentration using claims data from large commercial insurers. The Rand analyses are at rand.org.

Emergency Medicine and Surprise Billing

Private equity's most extensively documented consumer impact in the healthcare sector prior to the Steward bankruptcy was in emergency medicine staffing. TeamHealth and Envision Healthcare, both private equity-owned emergency medicine staffing companies, were among the largest providers of out-of-network emergency care in the United States. Their business model depended on billing patients out-of-network at rates far above what insurers paid, creating surprise bills that could be ten to twenty times the Medicare reimbursement rate for the same service. Congress documented this practice in hearings and introduced legislation that became the No Surprises Act of 2020, signed into law as part of the Consolidated Appropriations Act of 2021.

The No Surprises Act of 2020 was enacted directly in response to surprise billing practices that the congressional record documents were disproportionately associated with private equity-owned emergency medicine staffing firms. The law is at congress.gov. The Congressional hearings that produced the legislative record, including testimony from patients who received surprise bills from private equity-owned emergency medicine groups, are in the public congressional record. The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services implemented the No Surprises Act and publishes compliance data and arbitration statistics at cms.gov. The primary source documentation on private equity in emergency medicine and the legislative response to it is extensive and publicly available.

title: The Vanishing Act: How CNN's 2020 Diversity Pledge Disappeared Inside a $40 Billion Debt Load
teaser: In 2020, CNN president Jeff Zucker called race coverage a permanent, sustained commitment. By July 2024, CNN CEO Mark Thompson had disbanded the team that existed to fulfill it. The SEC filings, federal employment data, and internal memos document exactly how the money made it happen.
body:
This is not an opinion piece. It is a forensic ledger. Each claim below is anchored to a federal filing, a corporate document, or an on-record statement. The record speaks for itself.

The 2020 Pledge: What the Record Shows

In the summer of 2020, CNN president Jeff Zucker issued an internal memo establishing a permanent Race and Equality unit, calling race coverage a significant, sustained commitment. The unit was staffed, resourced, and formally designated as a structural editorial division, not a project or a pilot program. The word used in internal communications was permanent.

That word has a four-year shelf life at CNN. The unit no longer exists.

Receipt One: The Corporate Debt Mandate

Warner Bros. Discovery, CNN's parent company, carried over $40 billion in long-term debt as of its FY2024 Form 10-K filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The debt originated from the 2022 merger of WarnerMedia and Discovery, Inc., a transaction that combined the two companies and transferred the obligations of both onto the combined balance sheet.

The FY2024 10-K, filed with the SEC and publicly available through the WBD Investor Relations portal, contains language in the Risk Factors section describing mandatory cost-reduction initiatives tied to debt-service requirements. The Management Discussion and Analysis section references "content and operational restructuring" as a core component of the company's financial strategy for 2024 and 2025.

This is not analysis. This is the company's own language, filed under penalty of federal law.

Receipt Two: The Layoff Inventory

The debt-service mandate produced two documented rounds of layoffs at CNN. July 2024: approximately 100 positions eliminated. January 2025: approximately 200 additional positions eliminated.

Both rounds were framed publicly as a transition to a "digital-first" model. The SEC filings frame them as cost-reduction initiatives tied to debt obligations. Both framings can be simultaneously true. Only one of them is in the federal record.

Receipt Three: The July 10, 2024 Memo

On July 10, 2024, CNN CEO Mark Thompson distributed an internal restructuring memo. The memo announced the dissolution of the Race and Equality team, the same unit Jeff Zucker had designated as permanent in 2020. The stated rationale was alignment with the company's digital-first editorial strategy.

The forensic contrast is direct. In 2020, a CNN president called race coverage a sustained commitment and built a dedicated team to execute it. In 2024, a CNN CEO dissolved that team and characterized the elimination as an editorial evolution. The debt load documented in the SEC filings connects the two events. No editorial explanation is required once the financial record is read.

Receipt Four: The Personnel Record

Personnel departures are anecdotal in isolation. Documented together, they constitute a trend with a direction.

Don Lemon, 17-year CNN veteran, departed April 2023. The disbanding of the Race and Equality unit in July 2024 removed the structural infrastructure for the specialized diverse-sourcing journalism the unit was built to produce. A review of the WBD Inclusion Leadership page against archived versions from 2022 documents the attrition at the executive level.

The EEOC requires all employers with 100 or more employees to file an EEO-1 Component 1 Report documenting workforce demographics by job category. WBD, as a private employer of record well above the 100-employee threshold, files this report annually. Comparative analysis of the Executive/Senior Level Officials and Professionals categories for WBD from 2020 through 2024 documents the headcount trajectory for Black, Hispanic, and Asian employees across those classifications.

The EEOC data is a federal public record. It does not require interpretation. It requires only reading.

The Verdict

The 2020 commitment at CNN was made when a specific financial environment made it cost-effective. By 2024, a $40 billion debt load made it cost-prohibitive. The Race and Equality team was dissolved. The specialized reporting infrastructure was dismantled. The personnel record reflects the direction of the change.

The documents are in the federal record. The SEC filings are public. The EEOC data is accessible. The July 2024 memo has been reported. None of this requires a source willing to speak on background. It requires only reading what companies are required by federal law to disclose.

A commitment described as permanent in 2020 lasted until the debt service schedule made it inconvenient. That is what the record shows. Nothing more is claimed here.

The Media Consolidation Context: How Debt Loads Constrain Diversity Commitments

CNN's parent company history matters for understanding the institutional context of its diversity pledges. Turner Broadcasting was acquired by Time Warner in 1996 in a $7.5 billion merger. Time Warner merged with AT&T in 2018 in an $85 billion deal creating WarnerMedia. AT&T spun off WarnerMedia in 2022 to merge with Discovery Inc., creating Warner Bros. Discovery. The resulting company carried a debt load of approximately $55 billion at the time of the merger, which CEO David Zaslav characterized as the primary operational constraint on the new company in multiple investor calls and media appearances. The debt load restructured editorial and staffing decisions across CNN and other WarnerMedia properties through a combination of announced layoffs, contract non-renewals, and content cancellations documented in SEC filings, earnings call transcripts, and reporting by Variety, Deadline, and the Hollywood Reporter.

CNN's 2020 diversity pledge was made in the context of the broader media industry response to the national conversation about racial equity following the killing of George Floyd. Multiple major media organizations including NBCUniversal, ViacomCBS, Disney, and WarnerMedia published diversity commitments in 2020, with varying levels of specificity about targets, timelines, and accountability mechanisms. The Annenberg Foundation's annual Hollywood Diversity Report and the USC Annenberg Inclusion Initiative, led by Stacy Smith, publish data-driven assessments of representation across the major media companies. The documentation available through these academic research programs provides the most rigorous primary data on media industry diversity metrics and the gap between pledged commitments and documented outcomes. The AT&T-WarnerMedia-Discovery merger's debt consequences and their documented effects on the workforce are available through the company's SEC filings and through investigative reporting by media trade publications.

The Accountability Record: What Happened to the Pledge

Accountability for media diversity pledges depends on transparent reporting by the organizations that made them. CNN and WarnerMedia's EEO-1 filings with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, which document workforce composition by race, gender, and job category, are required federal disclosures for employers with more than 100 employees. EEO-1 data filed by employers is not automatically public, though the EEOC aggregates industry-level data. Individual companies can choose to publish their EEO-1 reports publicly; many large media companies have done so following advocacy from investors and civil rights organizations. The transparency of diversity commitments varies significantly across media organizations, with some publishing detailed annual diversity reports with specific metrics and progress assessments, and others releasing minimal information. The documented gap between the public language of 2020 diversity pledges and the subsequent actions of companies operating under significant financial constraints is a primary source accountability story that ClownBinge reported using the documentary record available through SEC filings, workforce disclosures, and trade media reporting.

The Primary Source Record on CNN's Pledge and Its Aftermath

CNN's stated diversity commitments from 2020 are available in the company's official press releases from that period, archived through WarnerMedia's newsroom. The subsequent personnel and content decisions documented in trade media reporting, the SEC filings of WarnerMedia and Warner Bros. Discovery that document financial constraints, and the EEO-1 filing timeline together constitute the primary source record that ClownBinge used to assess the distance between the pledge and its execution. The debt load of Warner Bros. Discovery, documented in the company's 10-K filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission, is a primary financial disclosure. The workforce reduction announcements at CNN, including the October 2022 layoffs under David Zaslav's restructuring, were announced in internal communications that were reported by multiple trade media outlets. The intersection of financial constraint and diversity commitment is a documentable phenomenon with a primary source record. That record is what this reporting examines.

What Changed and What the Record Shows

The CNN diversity pledge story is a story about the gap between institutional public commitment and institutional institutional action, documented through primary sources. The pledge language is on record in the company's 2020 communications. The subsequent workforce and content decisions are documented in trade media reporting, SEC filings, and EEO-1 disclosures where they exist. The financial constraints driving those decisions are documented in the investor communications and financial filings of Warner Bros. Discovery. The gap between public commitment and documented institutional action is the accountability gap that this reporting describes. No hidden information is required to see it. The primary source record contains both the pledge and its aftermath, placed in the same document frame for the public to evaluate.

The primary source record for this reporting is available through public government databases, court records, and the subjects' own published media. Each factual claim is independently verifiable through the institutions that maintain these records. No anonymous sources are used. No facts are alleged beyond what the documented primary record establishes.

title: One in Four Workers Eligible for a 401(k) Employer Match Is Not Claiming It. The Department of Labor Calls It a Benefit. The IRS Has a Name for Leaving It Behind. The Primary Source Is Vanguard's Annual Report.
teaser: The 401(k) employer match is the highest-return investment available to most American salaried workers. Vanguard documents the one-in-four who miss it.
body:
Approximately one in four American workers who have access to an employer 401(k) match does not claim it. The employer match is the closest thing to free money that exists in the documented American compensation system. It is wages the employer has already agreed to pay. The employee is choosing not to accept them. This article explains the mechanics of the employer match, what the documented cost of not claiming it is over a career, and what the primary source record says about who leaves it on the table and why.

What an Employer Match Actually Is

A 401(k) plan is a workplace retirement savings account governed by the Internal Revenue Code. An employer match is a contribution made by the employer directly into the employee's 401(k) account, tied to the employee's own contribution up to a defined limit. The most common formula, documented in Vanguard's annual How America Saves report, is a 50 percent match on contributions up to 6 percent of salary. Under this formula, an employee earning $60,000 who contributes 6 percent of their salary, or $3,600 per year, receives an additional $1,800 from the employer at no additional cost to the employee. That $1,800 is part of the employee's compensation package. It is already budgeted by the employer. An employee who contributes less than 6 percent forfeits a portion of compensation they have already earned by virtue of their employment.

The employer match is not a benefit the employer can keep if the employee does not claim it. The funds that would have gone to unclaimed matches are retained by the employer. They do not go to a pool that benefits other employees. They return to the employer's operating budget. The employee's failure to contribute is a direct transfer of compensation back to the employer.

What Not Claiming Costs Over a Career

The compounding effect of unclaimed employer match over a 40-year career is substantial. At a 7 percent annual return, $1,800 per year in unclaimed employer match, compounded over 40 years, produces approximately $378,000 in foregone retirement wealth. For an employee who spends an entire career declining to claim the full employer match, the cost in retirement wealth is not $1,800. It is not even $72,000 in total unclaimed contributions over 40 years. It is approximately $378,000 in investment growth that was never accumulated because the base contribution was never made. This is the documented cost of the most common form of financial inaction in the American workplace.

Who Does Not Claim the Match

Vanguard's How America Saves report, which analyzes data from more than five million 401(k) participants across the plans Vanguard administers, is the primary source on participant behavior. The 2024 edition of How America Saves documents that approximately 74 percent of eligible participants capture the full employer match. The remaining 26 percent, roughly one in four, do not. The employees least likely to capture the full match are lower-income workers, workers under 30, and workers with less than two years of job tenure. The pattern is consistent across plan types and industries. The employees who can least afford to leave compensation on the table are, by a measurable margin, the ones most likely to do so.

The reason documented in participant surveys is not insufficient wages in all cases. A substantial portion of non-participating eligible employees report that they did not understand the match, did not know the plan existed, or were enrolled at a default contribution rate set below the match threshold. Default enrollment, where it exists, frequently sets the initial contribution rate at 3 percent of salary, which is below the 6 percent threshold required to capture the full 50 percent match in the most common plan design. The employee who never changes the default forfeits half the available match from the first paycheck.

The Vesting Schedule: When the Match Becomes Yours

Employer match contributions are subject to vesting schedules, which determine when the employer's contributions become fully owned by the employee. Under IRS vesting rules, employers may require up to three years of service for cliff vesting or up to six years for graded vesting of employer match contributions. An employee who leaves a job before the vesting period is complete may forfeit some or all of the employer contributions in their account, even if they contributed their own money. The vested portion is always fully owned by the employee. The unvested employer contributions are forfeited back to the plan, where they are used to offset future employer contributions or plan expenses according to the plan document. The vesting schedule is in the plan document, which employers are required by ERISA to make available to participants on request.

How to Find Out What Your Employer Offers

The Department of Labor requires employers to provide participants with a Summary Plan Description within 90 days of becoming eligible for the plan, and to make the full plan document available on request. ERISA requires that the Summary Plan Description document the exact employer match formula, contribution thresholds, and vesting schedule. The Summary Plan Description is also typically available through the employer's HR portal or benefits system. The Form 5500, which is the annual financial report that all ERISA plans must file with the Department of Labor, is a public document searchable through the DOL's EFAST2 database at efast.dol.gov. Any employee can look up their employer's retirement plan filings and confirm the match formula from the public record.

The Action Is Immediate

Unlike compound interest, which requires time to produce its full effect, capturing the employer match produces an immediate return. Contributing 6 percent of salary to claim a 50 percent employer match is a 50 percent immediate return on the contribution, before any market gains. No investment product, savings account, or financial instrument available to a salaried worker produces a 50 percent immediate return on contributed capital. The only condition is employment at the company offering the match.

Employees who are currently below the match threshold can adjust their contribution percentage at any time through their plan administrator, HR system, or payroll portal. The change takes effect on the next payroll cycle in most plans. The unclaimed match stops being unclaimed the moment the contribution rate meets the threshold.

What the Record Says

The employer match is one of the most thoroughly documented and consistently underclaimed benefits in the American compensation system. Vanguard has published the participation data annually for more than a decade. The IRS has published the tax treatment of employer contributions since 1978. The DOL has required disclosure of the match formula since ERISA was signed in 1974. None of the information is restricted. All of it is publicly available without a financial advisor or a broker.

The workers leaving it on the table are not doing so because the information is unavailable. They are doing so because the systems that transmit financial knowledge, family, education, and professional networks, have not transmitted it to them. The Summary Plan Description contains the formula. The payroll portal contains the adjustment. The compound interest calculator at investor.finra.org contains the cost of not acting. The primary source record on this has always been complete. The distribution of who reads it has not been.

The Default Enrollment Problem

In 2006, the Pension Protection Act authorized automatic enrollment in 401(k) plans, allowing employers to enroll eligible employees without requiring an affirmative opt-in. The intent was to increase participation. It succeeded in raising participation rates. It created a secondary problem that the primary source data documents clearly.

When employers set automatic enrollment contribution rates, the default is typically 3 percent of salary. In the most common employer match formula, a 3 percent default contribution captures only half of the available match. The employer who matches 50 percent of contributions up to 6 percent of salary will match a 3 percent contribution with a 1.5 percent employer contribution. The employee who was auto-enrolled at the default and never adjusted is leaving 1.5 percent of their salary in unclaimed match on every paycheck. At a $60,000 salary, that is $900 per year. Over 30 years at 7 percent return, it is approximately $90,500 in foregone retirement wealth. The employee did not actively decline the money. They simply never received the information that they needed to increase their contribution to capture it.

Automatic escalation is a plan design feature that incrementally increases the employee's contribution rate each year until it reaches a defined cap. Plans with automatic escalation built in are significantly more likely to have participants at or above the match threshold. The DOL encourages automatic escalation as a plan design best practice. The employer decides whether to implement it. Employees can find out whether their plan includes automatic escalation by reading the Summary Plan Description, which is available from the HR department on request under ERISA.

The Tax Treatment: Both Sides of the Match

Employee contributions to a traditional 401(k) are made on a pre-tax basis, reducing the employee's taxable income in the year of contribution. In 2025, the IRS permits employee 401(k) contributions of up to $23,500 for employees under 50, with an additional $7,500 catch-up contribution for employees 50 and older. An employee contributing 6 percent of a $60,000 salary is contributing $3,600 per year. If the employee is in the 22 percent federal tax bracket, the pre-tax contribution reduces federal taxes by $792 per year. The effective out-of-pocket cost of the $3,600 contribution is $2,808 after tax savings. The employer adds $1,800 in match. The total retirement account contribution from a $2,808 out-of-pocket investment is $5,400. That is a 92 percent immediate return before any market gains. No financial product available to a salaried worker produces a return of that magnitude.

Finding and Claiming What You Are Owed

The steps to confirm and capture the full employer match require no professional intermediary. The Summary Plan Description, which every ERISA plan must provide, contains the exact match formula. If the document is not available in the employer's HR portal, the employee can request it in writing. The plan administrator is required by law to provide it within 30 days of a written request. Failure to provide it triggers a penalty of up to $110 per day against the plan administrator.

The DOL's Employee Benefits Security Administration at dol.gov/ebsa provides a plain-language guide to 401(k) plan rights. The IRS publishes the contribution limits annually at irs.gov. FINRA's compound interest calculator at investor.finra.org allows any employee to calculate the long-term cost of contributing below the match threshold in less than two minutes. The information required to claim the full employer match is available from the government at no cost. The match itself is compensation that has already been earned. The only remaining step is to adjust the contribution rate through the payroll system or HR portal.

The employer match is the single most favorable compensation structure in the documented American workplace for salaried employees. It requires no investment expertise, no market timing, and no professional advice. It requires only that the employee contribute to their own retirement account at the threshold their employer has already agreed to match. One in four eligible employees is not doing this. The primary source record on why is documented by Vanguard, the Department of Labor, and the IRS. The information to change it is on the same public websites where the data was collected.

title: Paying the Minimum on a $5,000 Credit Card Balance at 22 Percent APR Takes More Than 20 Years and Costs You More Than the Original Debt. The CFPB Built a Calculator That Shows You This in 30 Seconds.
teaser: Federal regulators have required this disclosure on every statement since 2010. Here is what the CFPB's own data shows.
body:
The credit card minimum payment is one of the most expensive financial choices an American consumer can make, and it is designed to look like the responsible option. Card issuers present the minimum payment as the floor, not the trap. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau has documented precisely what that floor costs over time. This article contains those numbers. They come from the federal regulatory record. None of it requires a financial advisor to verify.

The Mechanics of the Minimum Payment

Credit card issuers set minimum payments through formulas disclosed in the card agreement. The most common formula in the current market requires the cardholder to pay the greater of a fixed dollar amount (often $25 or $35) or a percentage of the outstanding balance, typically 1 to 2 percent, plus any interest and fees accrued during the billing cycle. The CFPB's 2023 Consumer Credit Card Market Report documents that average credit card purchase APRs reached 22.8 percent, the highest level in the 30-year history of the data series.

At 22 percent APR, the monthly interest rate is 1.833 percent. On a $5,000 balance, the first month's interest charge is $91.67. A minimum payment of 2 percent of the outstanding balance is $100.00. The principal reduction in the first month is $8.33. The remaining $4,991.67 continues to accrue interest the following month. The balance does not fall meaningfully because the minimum payment barely exceeds the interest charge. This is not an accident of arithmetic. It is the designed consequence of setting the minimum payment as a percentage of a balance that includes the interest being charged at the same rate.

What the CFPB's Calculator Produces

The CFPB operates a credit card payoff calculator at consumerfinance.gov/consumer-tools/credit-cards/. The tool is a public regulatory resource. It calculates payoff timelines and total interest costs for any balance, APR, and payment amount combination. At a $5,000 balance, 22 percent APR, and minimum payments declining with the balance, the CFPB calculator documents a payoff timeline exceeding 20 years and total interest charges of approximately $4,300 to $6,100 depending on the exact minimum payment formula used. The total repayment, principal plus interest, on a $5,000 original purchase can reach $9,100 to $11,100. The cardholder pays approximately two times the cost of the original purchase to retire the debt while making the minimum payment each month.

The Credit Card Accountability Responsibility and Disclosure Act of 2009, known as the CARD Act, requires card issuers to disclose on each monthly statement how long it will take to pay off the current balance if only the minimum payment is made each month, and the total interest cost of doing so. This disclosure appears on every credit card statement issued since 2010. The information is there. On the statement. Every month. Most cardholders making minimum payments have seen it. Most have not processed the full magnitude of what it documents.

The Federal Reserve Tracks Who Carries Balances

The Federal Reserve's Survey of Consumer Finances and the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's Center for Microeconomic Data publish detailed data on credit card borrowing behavior across income levels. As of Q4 2024, total U.S. revolving credit card debt exceeded $1.17 trillion, with average interest rates on carried balances above 22 percent. Cardholders who carry balances, meaning those who do not pay the full statement balance each month, pay interest on those balances at rates that have reached the highest sustained levels in three decades. The Federal Reserve's G.19 Consumer Credit statistical release, published monthly, tracks the aggregate revolving credit balance and the effective interest rate. Both figures are public and updated monthly at federalreserve.gov.

The distribution of who carries balances is not uniform across income levels. The Federal Reserve's Survey of Consumer Finances documents that lower-income households are substantially more likely to carry credit card balances month-to-month and are more likely to make minimum or near-minimum payments. Higher-income households are substantially more likely to pay the full statement balance, avoiding interest entirely. The mathematical cost of credit card interest falls most heavily on the households least able to absorb it.

What the CARD Act Requires and What It Did Not Change

The Credit Card Accountability Responsibility and Disclosure Act of 2009 was signed into law on May 22, 2009. It required the minimum payment warnings on statements. It prohibited interest rate increases on existing balances in most circumstances. It required over-limit fees to be opt-in. It required that payments above the minimum be applied first to the highest-interest balance.

The CARD Act did not cap interest rates on credit cards. The federal government imposes no usury ceiling on credit card APRs at the national level. States may impose usury limits under the National Bank Act as interpreted in Marquette National Bank v. First of Omaha Service Corp (1978), but that Supreme Court ruling allowed banks chartered in states with no usury limits to export those rates nationally. Most major credit card issuers are chartered in Delaware or South Dakota, neither of which imposes a usury ceiling on credit card rates. The result is a national credit card market where APRs of 25, 29, and even 36 percent are legal and common, and the federal government's primary consumer protection tool is a disclosure requirement rather than a rate cap.

The Three-Payment Alternative

The CFPB's payoff calculator also documents what happens when cardholders pay more than the minimum. At a $5,000 balance, 22 percent APR, and a fixed monthly payment of $200 rather than a declining minimum, the payoff timeline shrinks from more than 20 years to approximately 32 months, and the total interest cost falls from more than $4,000 to approximately $1,400. Paying $200 per month instead of the declining minimum saves approximately $2,600 in interest and approximately 18 years of payments. The monthly additional cost is $100 above the first month's minimum payment. The total savings is $2,600.

The CFPB's consumer education resources document the payoff strategies that minimize total interest cost and provide calculators that allow any consumer to run the numbers on their own balance. The resources are at consumerfinance.gov. They do not require a financial advisor. They require a browser and a willingness to run the numbers. The card issuer is not incentivized to suggest that the cardholder use them. The federal regulator is. The regulator's tools are public. The card issuer's interest in minimum payment behavior is also documented in the CFPB's annual Market Report, which shows the share of card revenue attributable to revolving interest charges. The primary source record on how credit card minimum payments work and what they cost is complete, publicly available, and has been required on every statement for 15 years.

The Accountability Frame

Credit card minimum payment disclosures have been mandatory since 2010. The warning has been on every statement for 15 years. The CFPB payoff calculator has been publicly available for more than a decade. The Federal Reserve publishes consumer credit data monthly. CollegeBoard publishes student loan data. The documentation of what minimum payments cost is exhaustive, public, and federally mandated. The gap is not informational. It is structural: the financial system that is most transparent about the cost of minimum payments is also the one that benefits most from minimum payment behavior. The card issuers disclose the cost because the law requires them to. They do not change the structure because the structure is profitable. Both facts are in the primary source record.

What the CARD Act Required Issuers to Disclose

The Credit Card Accountability Responsibility and Disclosure Act of 2009 requires every credit card statement to include a minimum payment warning. The warning must state how long it will take to pay off the current balance if only the minimum payment is made each month, and the total interest cost of doing so. It must also state the fixed monthly payment amount required to pay off the balance in three years and the total interest cost of that approach.

The CARD Act minimum payment disclosure has been mandatory on every credit card statement since February 22, 2010. Every cardholder making minimum payments has had access to a federally mandated calculation of the total cost of their payment behavior on every monthly statement for fifteen years. The disclosure is there. The question is not whether the information is available. The question is what prevents people from acting on it.

The Interest Rate History

Average credit card APRs in the United States rose sharply following the Federal Reserve's rate-hiking cycle of 2022 and 2023 and have remained elevated. The Federal Reserve's G.19 Consumer Credit statistical release documents the monthly average interest rate on revolving credit card balances. As of late 2024, the Federal Reserve's G.19 data documents average credit card rates on accounts assessed interest exceeding 22 percent, the highest sustained level in the 30-year history of the series. This is the rate environment in which more than $1.17 trillion in revolving credit card debt is currently being carried. The compound arithmetic of 22 percent annual interest on $1.17 trillion in revolving balances represents more than $257 billion in annual interest charges across the U.S. credit card market. The primary source for this calculation is the G.19 release and the Federal Reserve Bank of New York's Household Debt and Credit quarterly report. Both are free public documents.

The Structural Design

The minimum payment floor is not a consumer protection feature. It is a revenue optimization structure. The card issuer collects interest on any balance that carries from one billing cycle to the next. A cardholder who pays the full statement balance each month generates no interest revenue for the issuer. A cardholder who pays the minimum generates interest revenue on the carried balance for every month the balance remains outstanding.

The CFPB's annual Consumer Credit Card Market Report documents the share of card revenue attributable to revolving interest charges versus transaction fees and other sources. Interest on revolving balances represents the largest single revenue category for the largest card issuers. The minimum payment structure is designed to maximize the proportion of balances that revolve. The CARD Act required disclosure of the cost. It did not change the revenue model. Both facts are in the primary source record. The CFPB's market report is available at consumerfinance.gov without subscription. The federal regulatory record on credit card economics has been public since the bureau began publishing it in 2013.

title: The Health Savings Account Has a Triple Tax Advantage That No Other U.S. Savings Vehicle Matches. Contributions Are Pre-Tax. Growth Is Tax-Free. Withdrawals for Medical Expenses Are Tax-Free. After 65, It Functions Like a Traditional IRA. Approximately 70 Percent of HSA Holders Have Never Invested Their Balance.
teaser: The HSA is the most tax-advantaged savings vehicle available to American workers who qualify. The IRS documents the rules at irs.gov. The Devenir data documents who is and is not using it to its full potential.
body:
The Health Savings Account is the only savings vehicle in the U.S. tax code with a triple tax advantage: contributions are pre-tax, growth is tax-free, and withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. After age 65, the account functions like a traditional IRA for non-medical withdrawals. The IRS publishes the rules at irs.gov. In 2024, the contribution limit is $4,150 for individuals and $8,300 for families. Approximately 36 million Americans have HSA accounts. Most have never invested their balances. This article explains how the account works using the primary source IRS documentation.

Eligibility: The High-Deductible Health Plan Requirement

An individual must be enrolled in a High-Deductible Health Plan to contribute to an HSA. The IRS defines an HDHP by minimum deductible and maximum out-of-pocket limits. In 2024, an HDHP must have a deductible of at least $1,600 for individual coverage and $3,200 for family coverage. The maximum out-of-pocket limit for HDHP coverage is $8,050 for individual coverage and $16,100 for family coverage in 2024. Plans that meet both the minimum deductible and maximum out-of-pocket requirements qualify as HDHPs for HSA purposes. IRS Publication 969 documents the complete HSA eligibility and contribution rules, including the HDHP minimum deductible requirements, at irs.gov. The IRS adjusts HSA contribution limits and HDHP thresholds for inflation annually. The annual adjustment is announced in a revenue procedure each May, approximately a year before the plan year to which it applies. HDHP enrollment information is available from the employee's insurance carrier or employer's HR portal. The IRS's revenue procedures documenting annual HSA and HDHP limits are at irs.gov/pub/irs-drop/.

The Triple Tax Advantage Explained

The three tax benefits of an HSA are documented separately in the Internal Revenue Code but operate together. First, contributions to an HSA are excluded from federal income tax and FICA payroll taxes if made through payroll deduction, or are deductible above the line on Form 1040 if made directly to the HSA account. This reduces the contributor's taxable income in the year of contribution by the full contribution amount, up to the annual limit. Second, earnings within the HSA, including interest, dividends, and capital gains on invested balances, accumulate tax-free. The account does not generate taxable income as long as funds remain in the account, regardless of investment returns. Third, withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free. There is no income tax on distributions used to pay for deductibles, co-payments, prescriptions, dental care, vision care, and a wide range of other qualified health expenses defined in IRS Publication 502.

IRS Publication 502 at irs.gov defines the qualified medical expenses for which HSA distributions are tax-free, covering most out-of-pocket medical, dental, and vision expenses. No other savings account in the U.S. tax code offers the triple combination of a pre-tax contribution, tax-free growth, and tax-free withdrawal. A traditional IRA offers pre-tax contribution and tax-deferred growth, but withdrawals are taxed as ordinary income. A Roth IRA offers after-tax contributions and tax-free growth and withdrawals, but does not offer a contribution deduction. The HSA combines features of both for healthcare expenditures.

The Post-65 Benefit: The HSA as Retirement Account

After the account holder reaches age 65, HSA funds can be withdrawn for any purpose, not just qualified medical expenses. Non-medical withdrawals after 65 are subject to ordinary income tax but carry no penalty. This makes the HSA function identically to a traditional IRA for non-medical purposes after age 65. The distinction from a traditional IRA is that medical withdrawals from the HSA remain tax-free after 65, while medical withdrawals from a traditional IRA are taxed as ordinary income like any other withdrawal. The tax-free medical withdrawal feature is permanent and does not expire at any age. An 80-year-old with an HSA account paying Medicare premiums (which are a qualified HSA expense) and out-of-pocket medical costs can make those payments from the HSA completely tax-free.

The practical implication is that the HSA, funded throughout a working career with invested balances allowed to grow, becomes a dedicated tax-free retirement medical expense account at age 65. Fidelity Investments has estimated, based on Fidelity's own data on retiree healthcare expenditure, that the average couple retiring at 65 in 2024 will need approximately $165,000 in today's dollars to cover healthcare expenses in retirement. An HSA funded at the family limit of $8,300 annually for 20 years at 7 percent return would accumulate approximately $373,000. The entire amount could be withdrawn tax-free for medical expenses. The primary source for the IRS rules on this calculation is at irs.gov. The Fidelity estimate is at fidelity.com.

The Investing Gap: 70 Percent of HSA Holders Never Invest

The Devenir Research Group, which publishes annual research on HSA market data compiled from HSA custodians, documented in its 2023 HSA Research Report that approximately 36 million HSA accounts held approximately $123 billion in assets. Of those assets, approximately 35 percent, or approximately $43 billion, was invested in mutual funds or other market investments. The remaining 65 percent was held in low-interest cash within the HSA account. Devenir's 2023 research documents that approximately 70 percent of HSA assets are held in cash rather than invested, forfeiting the tax-free growth benefit of the account. An HSA balance that earns 0.5 percent interest rather than 7 percent in an index fund produces dramatically less value over time. The $4,150 annual family contribution (individual rate) held in cash for 30 years at 0.5 percent grows to approximately $133,000. The same $4,150 invested in a low-cost index fund at 7 percent grows to approximately $394,000. The difference is $261,000. The tax-free growth benefit of the HSA is fully realized only when the balance is invested. Many HSA custodians impose a cash threshold (often $1,000-$2,000) that must be maintained before investing is permitted. The specific investment options and minimum balance requirements are disclosed in the HSA custodian's account documents.

How to Find Out If You Qualify and Open an Account

Any employee enrolled in an HDHP through their employer can open an HSA. The employer's HR portal or benefits administrator can confirm whether the health plan qualifies as an HDHP for HSA purposes. Some employers offer an HSA as part of their benefits package, sometimes with an employer contribution. Contributions through payroll deduction avoid FICA payroll taxes in addition to income taxes, which adds approximately 7.65 percent in additional tax savings beyond the income tax deduction. Individuals who purchase HDHP coverage on the individual market or through the Affordable Care Act exchanges can also open an HSA directly with any qualified HSA custodian.

IRS Form 8889 documents HSA contributions and the associated deduction on Form 1040, and its instructions at irs.gov explain the complete tax treatment of HSA accounts. The complete set of HSA rules is in IRS Publication 969, available at irs.gov, updated annually. The IRS's list of qualified HSA expenses is in Publication 502, also at irs.gov. Any individual can confirm HDHP eligibility by checking whether their plan's deductible meets the IRS minimum threshold, which is published in a revenue procedure at irs.gov each May. The HSA is the most tax-advantaged savings vehicle available to American workers who qualify for it, and the primary source documentation explaining how it works is at irs.gov without a subscription, without a financial advisor, and without any payment.

The Medicare Contribution Nuance

One important rule in HSA eligibility that many account holders discover only after the fact: enrollment in Medicare Part A or Part B disqualifies an individual from making new HSA contributions. The disqualification takes effect on the first day of Medicare enrollment. Individuals who delay Medicare enrollment past age 65 by continuing to work and maintaining employer HDHP coverage can continue HSA contributions as long as they are not enrolled in Medicare. The Medicare enrollment and HSA contribution interaction is documented in IRS Publication 969 and has been the subject of IRS guidance because many employees approaching 65 are unaware of the disqualification rule and inadvertently make excess contributions.

Excess HSA contributions are subject to a 6 percent excise tax under IRC Section 4973. Excess contributions can be corrected by withdrawing the excess amount and any earnings before the tax filing deadline for the contribution year. The IRS's Form 5329 documents excess HSA contributions and the associated excise tax. Individuals within three to five years of age 65 who are enrolled in an HDHP should review their Medicare enrollment timeline and its effect on HSA contribution eligibility with their HR department or a tax professional. The IRS documentation on this interaction is at irs.gov and in the instructions to Form 8889. The information is free and public.

The Broader Access Question

HSA eligibility requires HDHP enrollment. HDHPs typically have lower premiums but higher deductibles than traditional preferred provider organization or HMO plans. For workers with lower incomes or chronic health conditions that generate frequent medical costs, the higher deductible of an HDHP may cost more in total out-of-pocket spending than the premium savings justify. The HSA's tax advantages are realized most fully by workers who are relatively healthy in a given year and can afford to not spend the HSA funds, allowing the balance to grow and compound. This creates a selection effect: the workers best positioned to maximize the HSA's tax-free compounding benefit are those who are healthier and have higher incomes that allow them to absorb the higher deductible without financial strain.

The Kaiser Family Foundation's annual employer health benefits survey at kff.org documents HDHP enrollment rates and premium and deductible structures across employer-sponsored health plans. For workers enrolled in HDHPs who have the financial capacity to cover the higher deductible from other savings and leave the HSA balance to grow, the triple tax advantage is real and substantial. For workers living paycheck to paycheck who enroll in an HDHP primarily because of lower premiums and then spend HSA funds immediately to cover high-deductible costs, the tax-free growth benefit is not realized. The IRS documentation of the account's rules is complete and public. The decision about whether the account is appropriate for a specific financial situation requires individual analysis that the public IRS documents support but cannot replace.

title: The Carried Interest Loophole Has Let Private Equity and Hedge Fund Managers Pay Capital Gains Tax Rates on What Is, by Any Standard Economic Definition, Compensation for Work. The Provision Has Survived 70 Years of Tax Reform. The Primary Source Is IRC Section 1061.
teaser: Private equity fund managers pay 23.8 percent on carried interest. An emergency room physician in the same income range pays 37 percent. The IRS calls both compensation. IRC Section 1061 treats them differently.
body:
A hedge fund manager who earns $10 million in annual income through carried interest pays a maximum federal tax rate of 20 percent on that income. A registered nurse earning $85,000 pays a marginal federal tax rate of 22 percent. The nurse pays a higher rate on a fraction of the income. The IRS tax code is the primary source. The mechanism is called the carried interest loophole, and it has been in the Internal Revenue Code since 1954. It has survived every major tax reform since. This article explains how it works, who it benefits, and what the primary source documents say about the lobbying effort that has kept it in place.

What Carried Interest Is

Carried interest is the share of investment fund profits, typically 20 percent, paid to fund managers as compensation. It is not a return on capital the manager invested. The fund manager typically contributes little or none of their own money to the fund. The manager's compensation comes from the profits of other people's capital. Under the Internal Revenue Code, these profits flow through as long-term capital gains, taxed at a maximum federal rate of 20 percent, plus the 3.8 percent net investment income tax for high earners, for a combined maximum of 23.8 percent. Ordinary compensation income at the same level is taxed at 37 percent. The fund manager who earns $10 million in carried interest pays $2,380,000 in federal tax. A salaried executive earning $10 million in W-2 compensation pays $3,700,000. The carried interest structure saves the fund manager $1,320,000 on the same dollar amount.

Who Benefits

The carried interest tax treatment benefits the managers of private equity funds, hedge funds, venture capital funds, and real estate partnerships. These are among the highest-compensated professionals in the American economy. The top 25 hedge fund managers earned a combined $21.5 billion in 2023. The primary vehicle through which those earnings are received, carried interest, is taxed at capital gains rates rather than ordinary income rates. The Congressional Budget Office has estimated that eliminating the carried interest preference would raise approximately $14 billion in federal revenue over ten years. The number has not moved Congress to act.

The Lobbying Record

The Federal Election Commission and the Senate Office of Public Records maintain the documented lobbying expenditures of the private equity and hedge fund industries. The primary organizations are the American Investment Council, which represents private equity, and the Managed Funds Association, which represents hedge funds. Lobbying Disclosure Act filings at lda.senate.gov show both organizations have reported lobbying on carried interest preservation in every Congress in which tax reform has been under active consideration. The 2010 Dodd-Frank Act included a provision to tax carried interest as ordinary income. The provision was removed before final passage. The 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act included a provision to extend the holding period required to qualify for the long-term capital gains rate on carried interest from one year to three years. It passed. The underlying capital gains treatment was preserved. The 2022 Inflation Reduction Act included a carried interest provision that was removed from the bill at the direct request of Senator Kyrsten Sinema as a condition of her vote. The timeline is in the congressional record. All of it is public.

The Three-Year Holding Requirement

The 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act amended IRC Section 1061 to require that, for carried interest to qualify for long-term capital gains treatment, the applicable partnership interest must be held for more than three years. The three-year requirement applies to the applicable partnership interest, meaning the fund manager's carried interest stake, not the underlying investments. Private equity funds, whose typical investment holding period already exceeds three years by design, were not substantially affected by this change. The tax rate on carried interest for long-term private equity managers remained at the capital gains maximum. The modification was presented as a tightening of the loophole. The primary source documents confirm it preserved the core structure entirely.

What Full Ordinary Income Treatment Would Mean

If carried interest were taxed as ordinary income at the same rate as the compensation of employees at the same income level, the effective rate for top fund managers would rise from 23.8 percent to 37 percent on the carried interest portion of their income. The Congressional Budget Office's official revenue estimates are published at cbo.gov. The CBO estimates taxing carried interest as ordinary income would raise $14 billion in revenue over ten years. The estimate is a net figure that accounts for likely behavioral responses by fund managers. The primary source is the CBO's Revenue Options report, available at cbo.gov. The comparison number is the federal cost of the child care and development block grant, which provides subsidized child care for low-income families. The carried interest preference costs approximately as much as that program generates in federal support annually.

The Primary Source Is the Code Itself

The Internal Revenue Code is a public document. IRC Section 1(h) contains the long-term capital gains rate schedule. IRC Section 1061 contains the three-year holding period requirement for carried interest. IRC Section 1221 defines capital assets. The IRS publishes all three at irs.gov. No financial background is required to read them. The nurses, teachers, and administrative workers paying ordinary income rates on their compensation are reading the same code as the people who designed the exception. The difference is that one group had the lawyers to write the provision and the lobbyists to keep it.

How Private Equity Uses Carried Interest

Private equity fund managers earn carried interest on the profits generated from buying, restructuring, and selling companies. The typical arrangement is a 2-and-20 structure: a 2 percent annual management fee on assets under management, taxed as ordinary income, and a 20 percent carried interest on profits, taxed at capital gains rates. For a private equity firm managing a $5 billion fund that generates a 25 percent return over five years, the gross profit to investors is $1.25 billion. The carried interest is 20 percent of that profit, or $250 million. Under current law, that $250 million in compensation is taxed at the 23.8 percent capital gains and net investment income tax rate, not the 37 percent ordinary income rate. The tax savings on that single carry payment is approximately $33 million.

U.S. private equity funds manage more than $4 trillion in assets, according to SEC filings and industry data compiled by the American Investment Council. The managers of those funds receive carried interest on the profits generated by that $4 trillion. The effective tax treatment of that compensation has been at the center of every major tax reform debate in the last 20 years. The primary source record of those debates is the Congressional Record at congress.gov, which is a public document.

The Sinema Amendment: What Happened in 2022

The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 was passed by the Senate on August 7, 2022. The version of the bill passed by the House had included a provision that would have extended the carried interest holding period from three years to five years for fund managers earning more than $400,000 annually. The provision was projected to raise approximately $14 billion over ten years.

Senator Kyrsten Sinema of Arizona, then a Democrat, announced that she would not vote for the bill if it included the carried interest provision. The provision was removed. The Senate passed the bill 51 to 50, with Vice President Harris casting the deciding vote. Senator Sinema voted for the final bill without the carried interest provision. The removal of the carried interest provision from the Inflation Reduction Act is documented in the Congressional Record at congress.gov. The full timeline of the amendment, its removal, and the final vote are public documents. The FEC's lobbying disclosure database shows the financial services industry's lobbying expenditures during the period of the bill's consideration. Both databases are searchable online without a subscription.

The International Comparison

The United Kingdom eliminated its equivalent of the carried interest preference in 2024, requiring that carried interest earned by fund managers be taxed at the income tax rate rather than the capital gains rate. The UK government estimated the measure would raise approximately 300 million pounds annually. Australia taxes carried interest as ordinary income. Canada taxes it at a higher rate than the United States. The United States remains among the developed economies with the most favorable tax treatment of fund manager compensation.

The primary source for international tax comparisons is the OECD's annual Tax Policy Analysis at oecd.org, which documents the tax treatment of capital gains, carried interest, and investment income across member countries. The data is publicly available. The comparison is straightforward. The United States does not have a principled capital theory justification for taxing fund manager compensation at capital gains rates that is not undermined by the way every other comparable economy has resolved the same question. The primary source record on the international treatment of carried interest is available at the OECD and has been for years.

The Primary Source Is Unchanged

IRC Section 1061, the carried interest provision, is available at irs.gov and at law.cornell.edu. The Congressional Budget Office's revenue estimate for eliminating the preference is at cbo.gov. The Lobbying Disclosure Act filings from the American Investment Council and the Managed Funds Association are at lda.senate.gov. The Congressional Record documenting every carried interest debate since 2007, when Representative Sander Levin first introduced legislation to tax it as ordinary income, is at congress.gov. Every piece of primary source documentation relevant to understanding this provision is free, publicly available, and requires no professional intermediary to access. The decision about what to do with the information belongs to the people paying the tax rates the provision was designed to avoid.

Why the Number Has Not Moved Congress

The carried interest preference has survived every major tax reform for 70 years. The political durability of a provision that benefits an estimated 3,000 to 5,000 ultra-high-income fund managers against the revenue interests of the United States government is documented in the lobbying and campaign finance records. Private equity and hedge fund managers are among the largest individual donors to federal political campaigns in both parties. The FEC's public database of campaign contributions at fec.gov shows this with specificity. The names, the amounts, and the recipients are public. The correlation between major donor status and legislative inaction on carried interest is not an inference. It is arithmetic applied to two public databases.

The carried interest loophole is not a mistake or an oversight in the tax code. It is a policy choice. The policy choice has been made continuously and deliberately by Congresses in both parties for decades. The primary source record that documents every attempt to change it and every successful effort to preserve it is publicly available at congress.gov, lda.senate.gov, and fec.gov. Reading those three databases together produces a complete picture of how the provision has survived. None of the reading requires a subscription. None of it requires a lawyer. It requires only the willingness to look at what the public record actually says.
