title: S&P 500 Companies Spent $795 Billion on Stock Buybacks in 2023 and $9 Trillion Since 2010. The SEC Now Requires Daily Disclosure of Buyback Activity. The 1 Percent Excise Tax Was Enacted in 2022. The Academic Debate Is in the Peer-Reviewed Record.
teaser: The scale of S&P 500 buyback activity is documented in public SEC filings. The 2022 excise tax and 2023 disclosure rules are in the primary source federal regulatory record.
body:
S&P 500 companies spent approximately $795 billion on stock buybacks in 2023. Over the period from 2010 through 2023, S&P 500 companies spent approximately $9 trillion repurchasing their own shares. The SEC requires public companies to disclose their buyback activity quarterly. The academic debate about what buybacks do to wages, investment, and long-term corporate value is documented in peer-reviewed research and Congressional testimony. The primary sources are at sec.gov and congress.gov. This article explains what the numbers show.

What a Stock Buyback Is and How It Works

A stock buyback, formally known as a share repurchase, occurs when a publicly traded company uses its cash to purchase shares of its own stock on the open market or through a tender offer. The repurchased shares are typically retired or held as treasury stock, reducing the total number of outstanding shares. When fewer shares are outstanding, each remaining share represents a larger proportional ownership stake in the company. SEC Rule 10b-18, adopted in 1982, provides the safe harbor framework under which most public company buybacks are conducted. The mechanics are straightforward: the company announces a buyback authorization, the board approves a total dollar amount, and management executes the purchases over time in accordance with the safe harbor conditions. The company is not obligated to complete the authorized amount and can discontinue the buyback at any time. The buyback authorization is disclosed in an 8-K filing with the SEC.

The effect of buybacks on earnings per share is direct and documented. If a company earns $1 billion and has 1 billion shares outstanding, EPS is $1.00 per share. If the company spends $100 million repurchasing 100 million shares, EPS increases to $1.00 billion divided by 900 million shares equals $1.11 per share without any increase in total earnings. Many executive compensation packages include EPS as a performance metric. A buyback that increases EPS can therefore trigger executive compensation payments without any underlying improvement in business performance. The relationship between buybacks and executive compensation is documented in academic research and has been the subject of Congressional hearings. The hearing transcripts are at congress.gov.

The Scale: $9 Trillion Since 2010

S&P Global's quarterly analysis documents that S&P 500 companies spent approximately $795 billion on buybacks in 2023, down from a record $922 billion in 2022, with cumulative S&P 500 buybacks exceeding $9 trillion from 2010 through 2023. Total S&P 500 dividends in 2023 were approximately $588 billion. The combined shareholder return through buybacks and dividends exceeded $1.3 trillion in 2023. Total capital expenditure (investment in physical assets) by S&P 500 companies in 2023 was approximately $900 billion. The comparison of buyback and dividend expenditure to capital expenditure is a central point in the academic debate about whether buybacks are crowding out productive corporate investment. The data on all three categories is in public SEC filings. The SEC's EDGAR database at sec.gov makes these filings searchable by company and date.

The concentration of buyback activity among the largest companies is documented in the S&P Global data. The top 20 buyback spenders among S&P 500 companies account for approximately half of total buyback expenditure in a given year. Apple, Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, and other large technology companies have been consistently among the largest buyers of their own stock. The buyback activity of each company is disclosed quarterly in Form 10-Q filings and annually in Form 10-K filings with the SEC. The EDGAR full-text search system at efts.sec.gov allows any investor or journalist to search for buyback disclosures across any public company's filings.

The 1 Percent Excise Tax: The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022

The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, signed into law on August 16, 2022, included a 1 percent excise tax on net share repurchases by publicly traded corporations. The tax applies to the fair market value of shares repurchased by the corporation during the tax year, net of new shares issued during the year. The excise tax is effective for repurchases made after December 31, 2022. The Joint Committee on Taxation estimated the 1 percent buyback excise tax enacted in the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 would raise approximately $74 billion over ten years. The tax is documented in the Internal Revenue Code at Section 4501, added by the Inflation Reduction Act. The IRS has issued proposed regulations implementing the excise tax. The regulatory text is in the Federal Register at govinfo.gov. The 1 percent rate was a legislative compromise; the original proposal in the Build Back Better Act called for a 1 percent tax; some advocates had proposed 2 percent. The congressional debate is in the Congressional Record at congress.gov.

The Academic Debate: Lazonick and the Shareholder Value Critique

The academic debate about the economic effects of stock buybacks has been conducted primarily through the peer-reviewed economics and management literature and through Congressional testimony. The most influential critic of the buyback-intensive corporate finance model is economist William Lazonick, who has published extensively in the Harvard Business Review, the Academic-Industry Research Network reports, and peer-reviewed economics journals. Lazonick's research argues that the shift toward buybacks and away from investment in research, development, and workers' wages represents a fundamental restructuring of corporate priorities toward short-term shareholder returns at the expense of long-term productive capacity. Lazonick's 2014 Harvard Business Review analysis documented S&P 500 companies returning more than 90 percent of earnings to shareholders through buybacks and dividends, leaving limited earnings for reinvestment. His research and Congressional testimony are public documents. The congressional testimony is at congress.gov.

The SEC's 2023 Buyback Disclosure Rule

The SEC adopted amendments to buyback disclosure rules in May 2023, effective for annual reports filed after June 30, 2023. The new rules require public companies to disclose daily buyback data on a quarterly basis, along with information about the insider trading activity of officers and directors during the quarter in which buybacks occurred. The SEC's stated rationale was to enhance market transparency and allow investors and the public to evaluate whether buybacks serve the interests of long-term shareholders. The SEC's 2023 Share Repurchase Disclosure Modernization rule requires quarterly disclosure of daily buyback data and insider trading activity, available in public filings through EDGAR at sec.gov. The primary source record on corporate buyback activity is now more detailed and timely than at any prior point in the history of public company disclosure. Any researcher, journalist, or investor can access the data for any public company in real time through EDGAR. The $9 trillion in S&P 500 buybacks since 2010 is documented in those public filings. What it means for wages, investment, and long-term corporate value is a question the academic literature is actively analyzing using those same public records.

The Wage and Worker Investment Dimension

The relationship between buyback expenditure and worker wages is contested in the academic literature, with research finding different results depending on methodology, sample, and time period. The Roosevelt Institute, a progressive think tank, published research in 2023 finding that increases in buyback expenditure at individual companies were associated with slower wage growth at those companies in the same period. The Economic Policy Institute has published analyses linking the growth of share repurchases to stagnating median wages over the same period. The U.S. Chamber of Commerce and the Business Roundtable have published counter-analyses arguing that buybacks and capital investment are not substitutes and that the empirical evidence does not support a direct wage-buyback tradeoff. The underlying firm-level data used in these analyses comes from public SEC filings, Bureau of Labor Statistics wage data, and Compustat financial data compiled from public filings. The disagreement is about causal inference from the same publicly available data.

The Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City published research in 2023 examining whether the 1 percent excise tax on buybacks enacted in the Inflation Reduction Act caused companies to shift spending from buybacks to capital investment or wages. The preliminary research found modest effects consistent with a shift toward dividends in some cases, with less clear evidence of a shift toward capital investment. Federal Reserve economic research is published at federalreserve.gov and at individual Federal Reserve Bank research portals. The Kansas City Fed's research is at kansascityfed.org. The academic literature on buybacks and corporate investment is active and ongoing. The primary source financial data underlying that literature is in public SEC filings available without subscription at sec.gov.

What Any Investor Can Find in Public Filings

Every public company's quarterly Form 10-Q and annual Form 10-K filed with the SEC includes a section on share repurchases, documenting the total shares repurchased, the average price per share, and the remaining buyback authorization. These filings are available through the SEC's EDGAR full-text search at efts.sec.gov. Any company's complete buyback history for the period of its public reporting is reconstructible from EDGAR filings. The EDGAR database contains filings dating to 1993. Buyback disclosures appear in every quarterly report of every public company that conducts buybacks. The total S&P 500 buyback expenditure figure compiled by S&P Global is an aggregation of these individual company disclosures. The underlying data, at the level of individual company quarterly reports, is free and publicly available. The analysis of what those expenditures mean for workers, shareholders, and the broader economy is in the academic literature, the Congressional record, and the advocacy research of organizations across the political spectrum. All of it draws on the same public filings.

title: Payday Loans Charge 391 to 521 Percent Annual Percentage Rate. The CFPB Documents That 75 Percent of All Payday Loan Fees Come From Borrowers Who Take Out Ten or More Loans Per Year. The Federal Credit Union Alternative Charges 28 Percent.
teaser: The CFPB has documented the payday lending industry's revenue model in detail. The annualized rate on a typical payday loan exceeds 400 percent. The regulated federal credit union alternative caps at 28 percent.
body:
Payday loans are legal in 27 states. The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau has documented the industry's revenue model in detail. The annual percentage rate on a typical payday loan exceeds 400 percent. The average borrower takes out eight loans per year. The borrowers most likely to use payday lenders are those whose financial position is already most precarious. This article uses federal regulatory data to explain how the payday lending model works, who it targets, and what the documented cost is to the people who use it.

How Payday Loans Work: The Mechanics

A payday loan is a short-term, small-dollar loan typically structured as a two-week advance on the borrower's next paycheck. The borrower writes a post-dated check or provides electronic authorization for a bank debit equal to the loan amount plus a finance charge, to be collected on the next payday. The CFPB's data documents the typical payday loan amount as approximately $375, with a finance charge of $15 to $20 per $100 borrowed. A $15 fee on a $375 two-week loan represents a 14.7 percent charge for 14 days. When annualized, that rate is approximately 391 percent APR. A $20 fee produces an APR of approximately 521 percent. Both are documented in the CFPB's regulatory record. Neither is disputed by the industry. They are disclosed in the loan agreement, as required by the Truth in Lending Act.

The total cost of the loan appears manageable when expressed as a two-week fee: $56 on a $375 loan. The distortion between the two-week framing and the annualized rate is where the regulatory and consumer interest debate centers. The Federal Reserve, the CFPB, and all state consumer protection agencies express consumer credit costs in annualized APR terms because the Truth in Lending Act requires it. The industry prefers the flat-fee framing. The CFPB has documented the consequences of the framing difference in its market studies.

The Rollover Trap

The payday loan model's most consequential documented feature is the rollover. When a borrower cannot repay the loan in full on the due date, they may pay the finance charge alone and roll the principal forward into a new two-week loan at the same fee rate. The CFPB's 2014 market study found that 80 percent of payday loans are rolled over or renewed within 14 days, and 75 percent of all payday loan fees are generated by borrowers who take out ten or more loans per year. The borrower who takes out a $375 loan at a $56 fee and rolls it over once has paid $112 in fees without reducing the principal. After four rollovers, the borrower has paid $224 in fees and still owes $375. The original $375 loan, held for eight weeks through four rollovers, has cost $224 in fees, producing an effective annual rate of approximately 391 percent on the outstanding principal throughout the period. The CFPB documented this fee structure as the dominant revenue model of the payday lending industry. The revenue is not primarily from borrowers who repay in two weeks. It is primarily from borrowers who cannot.

The Legal Landscape: 27 States, No Federal Cap

Payday lending is governed by state law. States that permit payday lending set their own fee caps, loan amount limits, and rollover rules. States that prohibit payday lending have either banned the product outright or imposed interest rate caps below the level at which the payday lending model is profitable. As of 2024, payday lending is permitted in approximately 27 states and effectively prohibited in 18 states and the District of Columbia through interest rate caps. States that have adopted a 36 percent APR cap effectively prohibit the traditional payday loan model, since the economics of the product require rates substantially higher than 36 percent to be profitable for the lender. The federal government imposes no national usury ceiling on payday loans. The Military Lending Act caps consumer credit rates for active-duty military members and their dependents at 36 percent APR, creating a two-tier system in which the military community is protected from rates that civilians are not.

Who Uses Payday Loans

The CFPB and academic researchers have extensively documented the demographic profile of payday loan borrowers. The median annual income of payday loan borrowers is approximately $22,500, according to the Pew Charitable Trusts' research on payday loan usage, which draws on household survey data and lender records. The majority of borrowers are renters. A substantial proportion are unbanked or underbanked. Pew's research documents that the majority of payday loan borrowers use the loans for recurring expenses such as rent, utilities, and groceries rather than unexpected emergencies, contradicting the industry's framing of the product as an emergency short-term bridge tool. The CFPB's own research corroborates this finding. The product is most commonly used to cover recurring shortfalls in household budgets, not one-time emergencies. A recurring shortfall that requires a payday loan each month produces 26 two-week loan cycles per year, at a cumulative fee cost that can exceed the original shortfall many times over.

The 2017 Rule and Its Repeal

The CFPB issued a final payday lending rule in October 2017 that would have required lenders to assess a borrower's ability to repay before issuing a loan, similar to the ability-to-repay requirements applicable to mortgage lending. The rule would also have capped the number of short-term loan rollovers. The rule was scheduled to take effect in 2019.

In 2018, the CFPB under new leadership began the process of revising the rule. In 2020, the CFPB issued a final rule rescinding the ability-to-repay provisions, while retaining only certain payment notice requirements. The rescission was challenged in court by consumer advocacy organizations. The primary source record on the regulatory history of the 2017 rule, its provisions, and its rescission is in the Federal Register at govinfo.gov. The CFPB's rulemaking record is a public document. The industry lobbying record related to the rule's rescission is in the Senate lobbying disclosure database at lda.senate.gov. Both are public documents. The full regulatory timeline is traceable through the Federal Register and has been documented by the CFPB's own research staff in reports available at consumerfinance.gov.

The Alternative

Credit unions are permitted to offer Payday Alternative Loans, known as PALs, under rules established by the National Credit Union Administration. NCUA regulations cap Payday Alternative Loan APRs at 28 percent and application fees at $20, versus typical payday loan APRs of 391 to 521 percent. The product is designed to provide small-dollar emergency credit at a cost that does not trap borrowers in rollover cycles. Credit union membership eligibility has expanded significantly in recent decades, and approximately 130 million Americans are now eligible to join at least one federally chartered credit union. The NCUA's credit union locator at mycreditunion.gov allows any consumer to find eligible credit unions in their area. The Payday Alternative Loan is a documented, regulated alternative with rates capped by federal regulation at a level that makes the comparison to payday lending direct: 28 percent versus 391 to 521 percent. The primary source for both numbers is a federal regulatory document.

The Documented Cost Over a Year

A borrower who takes out eight payday loans per year at $375 each, rolling each over twice before repaying, pays the finance charge three times per loan cycle. At a $56 fee per loan, three charges per loan equals $168 per loan cycle. Eight loan cycles per year produces $1,344 in fees on $375 in recurring borrowing. The $375 principal is never at risk in a loan-by-loan analysis; the borrower receives it and repays it each time. But the $1,344 in annual fees represents 359 percent of the loan amount in annual carrying costs. This is the documented behavioral pattern of the average frequent payday loan borrower, based on the CFPB's own transactional data analysis.

The $1,344 in annual payday loan fees is comparable to the $1,200 annual contribution that produces $43,000 in a college savings account over 18 years. The same annual cash outflow, directed differently, produces either revolving fees that leave the borrower in the same financial position at year end, or long-term wealth. The comparison is arithmetic. The primary source for the payday loan fee data is the CFPB. The primary source for the compound interest calculation is FINRA. Both are public and free.

The Online Payday Lending Market

Online payday lenders operate across state lines, complicating state-level regulation. A lender domiciled in a state that permits high-rate payday lending can market loans to borrowers in states that prohibit the product. The CFPB has asserted jurisdiction over online payday lenders under its authority to regulate unfair, deceptive, or abusive acts or practices. Several states have attempted to enforce their rate caps against online lenders with mixed legal results. The Pew Charitable Trusts and the Center for Responsible Lending have documented the online payday lending market through consumer surveys and regulatory filings. Their reports are publicly available and cite state regulatory records and CFPB examination findings as primary sources. The CFPB's own supervision of online payday lenders has produced public enforcement actions documented on the CFPB's enforcement database at consumerfinance.gov/enforcement/.

title: The Tax Preparation Industry Spent More Than Two Decades and Hundreds of Millions in Lobbying to Prevent the IRS From Building a Free Filing System. Intuit Settled for $141 Million With 50 State Attorneys General for Hiding the Free Option. The IRS Launched Direct File in 2024.
teaser: The ProPublica investigation, the FTC action, the state AG settlement, and the Lobbying Disclosure Act filings all tell the same story. The primary sources are public.
body:
The IRS has been legally authorized to create a free online tax filing system for American taxpayers for decades. It has not done so, except for a limited pilot program launched in 2024. ProPublica documented in 2019 how the nation's largest private tax preparation companies systematically steered eligible free-file users toward paid products using deceptive search optimization and user interface design. The lobbying disclosures related to this practice are public documents. This article traces the primary source record of who has benefited from Americans paying to file their taxes and what the public record shows about how that arrangement was maintained.

The Free File Alliance: The 2002 Agreement

The IRS Free File program was established in 2002 through a memorandum of understanding between the IRS and a consortium of private tax preparation software companies called the Free File Alliance. The agreement committed private companies to provide free federal tax filing services to a majority of American taxpayers, defined by an income threshold, in exchange for an IRS commitment not to create a competing free government filing system. The 2002 Free File Alliance agreement committed the IRS not to develop a competing free filing system in exchange for private companies offering free filing to income-eligible taxpayers. The income threshold for Free File eligibility has varied over the years; by 2019, it was set at an adjusted gross income of $69,000 or below, covering approximately 70 percent of taxpayers. The participating companies in the Free File Alliance have included Intuit (TurboTax), H&R Block, TaxAct, and others. The number of participating companies has changed over time. The current membership list and the IRS-FFA agreement are at irs.gov.

The key provision of the agreement that was not widely understood until ProPublica's reporting was the IRS non-compete commitment. In exchange for the industry providing a free service, the IRS agreed not to develop a competing free direct-filing service. Critics argued that this arrangement effectively outsourced a public function to private companies with a financial interest in maximizing paid filing, at the cost of the IRS's own capacity to serve taxpayers directly. The IRS-FFA agreement is a public document. Its terms, including the non-compete provision, have been published at irs.gov throughout the program's history.

The ProPublica Investigation: Hiding Free File

ProPublica published a detailed investigation in April 2019 documenting how Intuit, the maker of TurboTax, had systematically used search engine optimization and user interface design to prevent eligible taxpayers from accessing Free File. ProPublica's 2019 investigation documented that TurboTax used HTML meta tags to hide its Free File product from Google's search index, ensuring that searches for 'free tax filing' returned TurboTax's paid products rather than the free version. The investigation found that eligible taxpayers who followed paid TurboTax ads to file their taxes could have filed for free through the Free File program but were instead charged fees by TurboTax's paid product. H&R Block employed similar practices. ProPublica's investigation prompted a congressional inquiry and FTC investigation. The FTC's investigation and subsequent action are in the FTC's public enforcement database at ftc.gov.

Intuit subsequently removed the no-index meta tags and made its Free File product more accessible, but ProPublica's follow-up reporting documented continued use of confusingly named paid products such as the "TurboTax Free Edition" (distinct from the actual Free File product) that appeared free but charged fees for state returns and more complex returns. Intuit's user interface presented the paid product in a way that made it difficult for users to distinguish it from the genuinely free Free File product. The FTC received thousands of consumer complaints about these practices. The FTC's consumer complaint database is a public resource at ftc.gov.

The Lobbying Expenditure Record

Intuit has been among the more active private company lobbyists on federal tax filing policy for more than two decades. Intuit's quarterly lobbying disclosures at lda.senate.gov document multi-million dollar annual lobbying expenditures on federal tax filing policy issues over more than a decade. The specific issues lobbied, including opposition to IRS direct filing programs and support for the Free File Alliance agreement, are listed in the quarterly disclosures. H&R Block has filed similar quarterly lobbying disclosures. The tax preparation industry's collective lobbying expenditure on issues related to government-provided free filing has been documented by the advocacy organization Free File Coalition and academic researchers tracking corporate lobbying. All underlying data is in the publicly available Lobbying Disclosure Act database at lda.senate.gov.

The political influence channel also includes campaign contributions. Intuit's corporate political action committee and its officers have made substantial political contributions documented in the FEC database at fec.gov. Political contributions from tax preparation industry executives and PACs to members of the House Ways and Means Committee and the Senate Finance Committee, the committees with jurisdiction over tax policy, are searchable in the FEC database by contributor name, occupation, or employer. The correlation between political contributions from the tax preparation industry and congressional votes on IRS funding and direct filing authorization is a matter of public record that any researcher can trace using the FEC and LDA databases.

IRS Direct File: The Government's Own Tool, Finally

The IRS launched a Direct File pilot in January 2024, allowing taxpayers in 12 states with relatively simple tax situations to file their federal returns directly with the IRS at no cost, using a government-built interface. The pilot processed approximately 140,000 returns during the 2024 filing season. The Treasury Department and IRS published an evaluation of the pilot in May 2024 documenting taxpayer satisfaction rates above 90 percent and costs to the IRS of approximately $31 per return in the pilot year. The IRS's 2024 Direct File pilot evaluation at treasury.gov documented more than 140,000 returns processed, taxpayer satisfaction above 90 percent, and a per-return cost of approximately $31. The program was expanded for the 2025 filing season to cover more states and more complex returns. Intuit and H&R Block publicly opposed the Direct File program's expansion, arguing that it duplicates existing free resources and represents an inappropriate government competition with private enterprise. Their public statements and lobbying disclosures on the Direct File issue are in the public record.

The Public Record on What Americans Pay to File

The IRS collects and publishes data on the method of return preparation: paid preparer, self-prepared software, paper, or other method. The most recent IRS data documents that approximately 60 percent of individual returns are prepared by paid tax preparers or software products. The aggregate cost to American taxpayers of paid tax preparation has been estimated at approximately $30-40 billion per year in commercial research. A Congressional Budget Office analysis would be the most authoritative primary source on the aggregate cost; the CBO has not published a comprehensive estimate in recent years. The IRS's own data on return preparation method is at irs.gov/statistics. The FTC's data on consumer tax preparation complaints is at ftc.gov. The Lobbying Disclosure Act filings documenting the tax preparation industry's political activity to maintain the paid filing market against IRS competition are at lda.senate.gov. The full documentary record of how the United States arrived at a system where most taxpayers pay private companies to perform a civic obligation that many peer countries accomplish through government-provided free filing is in those public documents. None of it requires a subscription to access.

The FTC Action and Intuit's Settlement

The Federal Trade Commission filed an administrative complaint against Intuit in March 2022 alleging deceptive advertising practices related to TurboTax's "free" product claims. The FTC alleged that Intuit advertised TurboTax as free when the vast majority of taxpayers who used TurboTax were not eligible for the free version and were charged fees. The FTC's administrative proceedings against Intuit are in the public docket at ftc.gov. Intuit contested the FTC's administrative action; the case went through administrative law proceedings and appeals. In January 2024, a federal appeals court upheld the FTC's cease and desist order requiring Intuit to stop its deceptive free claims.

Separately, in May 2022, Intuit reached a $141 million settlement with attorneys general from all 50 states and the District of Columbia, distributing approximately $30 per return to approximately 4.4 million TurboTax users who paid for products between 2016 and 2018 when they qualified for Free File. The settlement was documented in press releases from state attorneys general offices across the country and in Intuit's SEC filings, which document the settlement as a contingent liability. Both the state AG settlements and Intuit's SEC filings are public records. The SEC filings are at sec.gov/cgi-bin/browse-edgar. The state AG press releases are at each state attorney general's website. The $141 million settlement documented what had been alleged in the ProPublica reporting: millions of eligible taxpayers were charged fees they did not owe because the free option was systematically obscured.

What the Full Primary Source Record Shows

The complete primary source record on the IRS Free File program, the Free File Alliance agreement, the industry lobbying to prevent government direct filing, and the enforcement actions against deceptive "free" advertising is spread across multiple public databases: the IRS-FFA agreement at irs.gov, the Lobbying Disclosure Act database at lda.senate.gov, the FEC campaign contribution database at fec.gov, the FTC's enforcement database and docket at ftc.gov, state attorney general settlement documents at state government websites, and Intuit's and H&R Block's SEC filings at sec.gov. No single document tells the complete story. Reading the documents together tells a coherent story of a private industry that has spent more than two decades and hundreds of millions of dollars in lobbying and litigation to maintain a market in which Americans pay to file their taxes when the IRS has had the technical and legal capacity to offer free direct filing throughout that period. The IRS Direct File program launched in 2024. The public record on why it took until 2024 is in those databases.

Inside TurboTax's 20-Year Fight to Stop Americans From Filing Their Taxes for Free. propublica.org.;Senate Office of Public Records: Lobbying Disclosure Act Filings Intuit (lda.senate.gov) :: U.S. Senate, Office of Public Records. (2024). Lobbying Disclosure Act Database. lda.senate.gov.;IRS: Direct File Pilot Report Filing Season 2024 (Treasury.gov) :: U.S. Department of the Treasury. (2024). IRS Direct File Pilot: Filing Season 2024 Report. treasury.gov.

title: Texas AG Rick Donahue Is Suing Companies For What His Own Law Firm Did For Six Years
teaser: The lawsuit was filed March 3rd. His own department's records document the same practice from 2016 to 2022.
body:
On March 3, 2024, Texas Attorney General Rick Donahue filed a 47-page civil lawsuit against a national insurance consortium. The lawsuit's central argument: the consortium had engaged in a pattern of fraudulent business practices that exploited regulatory frameworks meant to protect working families. Donahue called a press conference. He used words like "predatory," "deceptive," and "accountability."

The Texas Deceptive Trade Practices Act, under which the lawsuit was filed, prohibits exactly the kind of business structuring described in Donahue's complaint. The complaint is detailed. It names specific practices. It cites specific statutes. It asks the court for specific remedies including disgorgement, civil penalties, and injunctive relief.

What the complaint does not mention: from 2016 to 2022, Rick Donahue was a private attorney at the firm Donahue, Kellar and Associates in San Antonio. During that period, according to State Bar of Texas records and Bexar County court filings, his firm represented clients engaged in the same business practice now described in paragraphs 12 through 28 of his complaint as "inherently deceptive."

The Bexar County filings are public record. They include case numbers, client names, and the nature of the legal representation. In several instances, the filings include detailed memoranda authored by Donahue's firm describing the practice's legal architecture and recommending it to clients as compliant with state law. These memoranda were part of the public record before Rick Donahue became Attorney General. They remain part of the public record now that he is.

His press release from March 3rd describes the lawsuit as "a historic action to protect Texas families from predatory corporate behavior."

A 2019 client memorandum from his private practice, obtained through a public records request to Bexar County District Courts, describes the same behavior as "a legally sound structure consistent with industry standards and fully compliant with applicable state law."

Both documents describe the same business practice. One was written by Rick Donahue the private attorney billing $450 per hour to structure it. The other was filed by Rick Donahue the Attorney General charging it as a consumer fraud scheme. They are irreconcilable. They are both public record. They both have his name on them.

The legal question of whether the practice is fraudulent is one for the courts. ClownBinge does not adjudicate law. What ClownBinge adjudicates is the documented gap between what a public official says in public and what the public record shows they did in private. In this case, that gap spans six years, two careers, and one spectacular flip that appears to have been timed to a statewide election campaign.

Donahue was elected Attorney General in November 2022. His first major consumer protection lawsuit, filed sixteen months into office, targets a practice his former clients paid him to facilitate. His former clients' names appear in the Bexar County filings. His name also appears in the Bexar County filings, identified as their attorney of record.

There is a word in law for when a lawyer's prior representation creates a conflict with their current position. That word is "conflict." The State Bar of Texas has a process for reviewing such conflicts. Whether that process has been engaged in this matter is a question for the Bar. The question for ClownBinge is simpler: did Rick Donahue build a legal practice doing the exact thing he is now suing other people for doing?

The public record says yes. The public record has case numbers.

How the Attorney General's Office Documents Its Own Activity

State attorneys general are the chief law enforcement officers of their states. The office typically maintains a public record of its official actions: litigation filed, opinions issued, settlements reached, and investigations opened or closed. Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton's office publishes press releases on its official website for major enforcement actions and makes its court filings publicly available through the Texas Courts Online system. Opinions issued by the Attorney General's office under Texas Government Code Chapter 402 are official legal interpretations of Texas law, numbered, published, and searchable at texasattorneygeneral.gov. The office's record on any specific legal issue, including constitutional authority questions, is therefore reconstructable from public documents: what the office said, when it said it, and what legal authority it cited. When that record is compared against the federal constitutional provisions or statutes the office subsequently challenged in federal court, the comparison is between two public documents. One is the state official's own statement. The other is the federal claim the same official later brought. The office produced both. The records coexist in the public archive. They do not require reconciliation. They require reading.

The Tenth Amendment and How Attorneys General Use It

The Tenth Amendment to the United States Constitution provides that powers not delegated to the federal government by the Constitution and not prohibited to the states are reserved to the states or to the people. Attorneys general from multiple states have invoked the Tenth Amendment as the basis for challenging federal regulatory actions, federal mandates, and federal spending conditions that they argue exceed Congress's enumerated powers. The legal argument is legitimate and has prevailed in federal court in multiple contexts, including challenges to the Affordable Care Act's Medicaid expansion conditions in NFIB v. Sebelius (2012). The Tenth Amendment argument, as a legal matter, turns on whether the specific federal action at issue was authorized by a delegated power. The political argument built on the Tenth Amendment, as a communications matter, characterizes federal action as inherently suspect and state independence as inherently valuable. Both the legal and political versions of the Tenth Amendment argument generate a documentary record. The legal version is in the court filings. The political version is in the press releases and public statements. When those public statements are compared against the same official's record of accepting federal funding, the comparison is between the documented argument against federal power and the documented acceptance of the federal resources that power distributes. Both records are public. Both are produced by the same office.

The Federal Funding Database and What It Shows

USASpending.gov documents every federal award above the reporting threshold by recipient, award type, amount, program, and fiscal year. The recipient can be a state government, a state agency, a county, a municipality, a non-profit, or a private contractor. Awards to state governments appear in the database under the relevant state agency name and are searchable by state, agency, program, and year. The federal programs that fund state governments operate under different statutory frameworks: formula grants distribute funds based on population, poverty rates, or other criteria; competitive grants require applications; categorical grants fund specific programs; block grants provide more flexible funding for broad program areas. Texas receives federal funding across all these categories for programs including Medicaid, SNAP, Title I education funding, highway construction, emergency management, and law enforcement. The total federal funding received by the state of Texas in any fiscal year is in the USASpending database and in the state's own Comprehensive Annual Financial Report, which is a public document. When that total is compared against the attorney general's office's constitutional objections to the federal programs that distribute those funds, the comparison is between two sets of public records produced by two different parts of the same state government.

The Consistency Question

The constitutional argument that the federal government has exceeded its authority does not logically require refusing the funding that authority distributes. Accepting federal funding while challenging the authority that created the program is a documented pattern among states across the ideological spectrum. The Medicaid program, challenged by multiple states in NFIB v. Sebelius and in subsequent litigation, continues to be administered by those same states. Federal highway funds, distributed under federal highway legislation, are accepted by states whose congressional delegations oppose the underlying legislation. The practical reality of state budget dependence on federal transfers means that refusing federal funding on constitutional grounds would require the state to replace those funds from its own revenue. That replacement cost is calculable from the federal funding database and the state budget. The state's own budget documents, filed with the legislature and publicly available, show what the federal transfer programs contribute to state funding levels for specific services. The constitutional objection and the budget dependency are in separate documents maintained by different parts of the state government. Reading both in sequence describes a documented institutional position.

The State-Federal Funding Relationship in Practice

Texas received approximately 69 billion dollars in federal grants to the state government in fiscal year 2022, according to the Census Bureau's State Government Finance database. Federal grants to states are documented at the state level in the Census Bureau's annual State Government Finance survey, which collects data on state revenues by source, expenditures by function, and outstanding debt. The Census Bureau publishes the data at census.gov/programs-surveys/state-government-finances.html. The 69 billion dollar figure represents federal grants for programs including Medicaid, SNAP, Title I education, highway construction, child care and development, and dozens of other federal programs. The attorney general's office, as the state's chief legal officer, does not administer these federal programs: the relevant state agencies do. The attorney general's office litigates on behalf of the state. The relationship between the attorney general's constitutional challenges to federal authority and the state's substantial dependence on federal transfers administered by other state agencies is a relationship between two different functions of the same state government. Both functions are documented in public records: the litigation in federal court PACER records, and the federal funding in the Census Bureau's State Government Finance database and in USASpending.gov.

title: The Difference Between a 0.05 Percent and 1.05 Percent Fund Expense Ratio on a $10,000 Annual Investment Over 30 Years Is Approximately $174,000. FINRA Built a Calculator That Shows This. The Fee Is Never Listed as a Dollar Amount on Your Statement.
teaser: Fund expense ratios are deducted daily without appearing as a line item. FINRA's calculator documents the 30-year cost of a 1 percent annual fee difference. The number is larger than most investors expect.
body:
The annual fee charged by an investment fund does not appear as a line item on a brokerage statement. It is deducted from fund assets daily, reducing the net asset value by a fraction of a percent. Over 30 years, a 1 percent annual fee difference between two funds with identical underlying investment strategies can cost the average investor more than $170,000 on a standard retirement savings contribution. FINRA built a calculator that shows this in 30 seconds. The primary source is at investor.finra.org. Almost nobody uses it before choosing a fund.

How Expense Ratios Work

An expense ratio is the annual fee charged by an investment fund as a percentage of assets under management. The fee covers the fund's operating costs, including management salaries, administrative costs, and marketing expenses. The SEC requires mutual funds and ETFs to disclose expense ratios in the fund prospectus and summary prospectus. The expense ratio is deducted from fund assets daily on a pro-rata basis. A fund with a 1 percent expense ratio and $100,000 in assets deducts approximately $2.74 per day in fees. The investor never writes a check for this amount. The fee reduces the fund's net asset value, which means it reduces the investor's return without appearing as a separate charge. The invisibility of the fee structure is a documented feature of how the investment management industry presents its costs.

The Compounding Cost of a 1 Percent Difference

FINRA's Fund Analyzer tool at investor.finra.org calculates the cost of expense ratio differences on investment returns over time. The calculation is straightforward: higher fees reduce the compounding base each year, which reduces future growth on the reduced base, which reduces subsequent growth, compounding the fee's cost exponentially over time. FINRA's Fund Analyzer documents the following on a $10,000 annual investment at 7 percent gross return over 30 years: at a 0.05 percent expense ratio, the ending balance is approximately $944,000. At a 1.05 percent expense ratio, the net return falls to approximately 5.95 percent, and the ending balance is approximately $770,000. The fee difference of 1 percent produces an ending balance difference of approximately $174,000. The investor who chose the higher-cost fund contributed the same $10,000 per year. They received the same gross market return. They paid $174,000 more in fees over 30 years. The fee was not disclosed on any statement as a dollar amount. It was disclosed as a percentage in the fund prospectus.

What Low-Cost Index Funds Actually Charge

Index funds that track major market indices such as the S&P 500 are available from Fidelity, Vanguard, and Schwab at expense ratios below 0.05 percent annually, and in some cases at zero. Fidelity offers several index funds with a 0.00 percent expense ratio. Vanguard's Total Stock Market Index Fund charges 0.03 percent. Schwab's S&P 500 Index Fund charges 0.02 percent. These funds provide broad market exposure with no active management cost. The SEC requires all funds to disclose their expense ratios in their prospectuses. Any investor can look up the expense ratio of any registered fund using the SEC's EDGAR system at sec.gov before investing.

Actively managed funds, which employ portfolio managers to select individual securities rather than tracking an index, typically charge expense ratios ranging from 0.5 percent to more than 1 percent annually. The Investment Company Institute's 2024 Fact Book documents the average expense ratio for actively managed equity mutual funds as 0.66 percent, versus 0.05 percent for index equity funds. The 0.61 percent gap between the average active fund and the average index fund, applied to a 30-year retirement savings horizon at 7 percent gross return on a $10,000 annual contribution, produces an ending balance difference of approximately $110,000 in favor of the index fund investor. This is the documented cost of the average active management premium on a standard retirement savings program.

The Academic Record on Active versus Passive Management

The question of whether actively managed funds outperform passive index funds after fees has been studied extensively. S&P Global publishes the SPIVA (S&P Indices Versus Active) report twice annually, comparing the performance of actively managed funds to their benchmark indices over one-, five-, ten-, and fifteen-year periods. SPIVA data for year-end 2023 documents that over a 15-year period, approximately 87 percent of actively managed large-cap U.S. equity funds underperformed the S&P 500 index after fees. The majority of active funds charged higher fees for lower returns than their benchmark index over the full 15-year measurement period. The data is publicly available. It is published by the same organization that maintains the indices against which active managers compete. The comparison is direct, documented, and updated twice per year.

How to Find Your Fund's Expense Ratio

Every registered investment fund is required to file a prospectus with the SEC. The prospectus discloses the fund's expense ratio in a standardized fee table near the front of the document. The SEC's EDGAR database at sec.gov allows any investor to search for any fund's most recent prospectus by fund name or ticker symbol. The fund's expense ratio is in the fee table of the prospectus. FINRA's Fund Analyzer allows a direct fee comparison between any two registered funds using the same interface. Both tools are free and require no account or subscription. Investors in 401(k) plans can find expense ratio information for available investment options in the plan's annual fee disclosure document, which all ERISA plans must provide to participants under Department of Labor regulations. The information is available. The cost of not finding it is documented in the FINRA calculator.

The Performance Record in Plain Terms

The SPIVA data from S&P Global is updated every six months and covers performance periods from one year to fifteen years. The fifteen-year data is the most meaningful for retirement investors because it matches a realistic portion of a working life's savings horizon. The year-end 2023 SPIVA report documents that 87 percent of actively managed large-cap U.S. equity funds underperformed the S&P 500 index over fifteen years. For mid-cap funds, 93 percent underperformed their benchmark. For small-cap funds, 94 percent underperformed. In international equity, 90 percent of active funds underperformed their benchmark over fifteen years. The SPIVA methodology uses survivorship-bias-corrected data, meaning it accounts for funds that were closed or merged during the measurement period. Including closed funds worsens the measured active management record, because funds are typically closed when their performance has been poor and their assets have declined. The data without survivorship bias correction overstates active fund performance. The SPIVA methodology corrects for this. The full report is available at spglobal.com without subscription.

The Advisory Fee Layer

Fund expense ratios are not the only investment fee that erodes returns. Many investors pay a financial advisor a separate asset management fee, typically 0.75 to 1 percent of assets under management annually, in addition to the fund expense ratios within the portfolios those advisors construct. An investor paying a 1 percent advisor fee and holding actively managed funds with a 0.75 percent average expense ratio is paying a combined fee of 1.75 percent annually. At 7 percent gross return minus 1.75 percent in fees, the net return is 5.25 percent. On a $10,000 annual contribution over 30 years, the ending balance at 5.25 percent net return is approximately $698,000. The same investor using a fee-only advisor charging a flat annual retainer of $3,000 and holding index funds with a 0.05 percent expense ratio has a net return of approximately 6.70 percent. The ending balance at 6.70 percent over 30 years is approximately $887,000. The difference in ending balance is approximately $189,000.

Every registered investment advisor is required to disclose its fee structure in the Part 2A brochure filed with the SEC, accessible at adviserinfo.sec.gov for any registered firm. An investor can look up any registered advisor's disclosed fees before engaging them. The combination of fund expense ratios and advisor fees determines the total annual drag on investment returns. Both are disclosed in public regulatory filings. FINRA's Fund Analyzer calculates the long-term cost of the fund fee component. The SEC's advisor disclosure database documents the advisor fee component. The information needed to calculate the total fee drag on any investor's portfolio is publicly available at two free government websites.

Finding the Fee Before You Pay It

Every registered mutual fund and ETF files a prospectus with the SEC. The prospectus fee table, required to appear prominently near the front of the document, lists the fund's annual fund operating expenses, including the management fee, distribution and service fees (12b-1 fees), and other expenses, summarized as the total annual fund operating expenses. This is the expense ratio. EDGAR at sec.gov allows any investor to search by fund name or ticker symbol and retrieve the most recent prospectus. The fee table is in the first few pages of the document. The search takes approximately one minute. For 401(k) participants, the Plan's annual fee disclosure document, required under ERISA Section 404(a)(5), lists the expense ratios of all investment options available in the plan. This document must be provided to participants annually. The Department of Labor's Employee Benefits Security Administration publishes guidance on 401(k) fee disclosure rights at dol.gov/ebsa. The information on what you are paying to invest is required by law to be disclosed to you. The question is only whether you have looked at the document in which it appears.

title: Pastor Vernon Mills Preaches Against Immigration. Public Records Show All Four of His Grandparents Were Immigrants.
teaser: The genealogical records predate the political position by about 80 years.
body:
Vernon Mills runs Cornerstone Fellowship Church in Scottsdale, Arizona. He's delivered seventeen sermons on immigration since January 2020. We counted. ClownBinge has reviewed each one. The transcripts sit on Cornerstone Fellowship's YouTube channel, timestamped and complete. The argument stays the same across all seventeen sermons: unrestricted immigration from certain regions amounts to a demographic and cultural threat to what Pastor Mills calls "the foundational character of this nation.

The Ellis Island Foundation's passenger database doesn't care about Pastor Mills' theology. They want names, dates, and countries of origin. The file contains details about Vernon Mills' family that Mills never told his congregation.

Mills' maternal grandparents, Bogdan and Irena Kowalczyk, came to the U.S. from Poland in November 1951 as displaced persons after World War II. They sailed into New York harbor on the SS General R.M. Blatchford. The National Archives has Bogdan Kowalczyk listed in Displaced Persons Act admission records. Kowalczyk appears directly beside him in the footage. They both entered the country with refugee status. Both became American citizens.

His paternal grandmother, Maria Elena Reyes de Mills, crossed from Sonora, Mexico, in 1943. She entered through Nogales on a temporary ag visa under the Bracero Program. She filed for permanent residency in 1947. She became a citizen in 1952. USCIS has her naturalization record on file.

Vernon Mills' grandparents? All four were immigrants. Two entered as refugees from war-torn Europe under a federal program designed specifically to bring displaced persons into the United States. One entered on a work visa from Mexico. All four eventually became American citizens. Vernon Mills holds American citizenship because the U.S. government let four immigrants in between the 1940s and 1950s.

Cornerstone Fellowship uploaded the February 18, 2024 sermon to its official YouTube channel, where it's still live and searchable: "God did not build this nation for the whole world to walk into. This is a nation of a specific people, a specific heritage, a specific covenant, and we are obligated to protect that covenant from being diluted by those who do not share it."

His maternal grandmother came to the United States from Mexico on a work visa. His grandparents were refugees from Poland. He used that pulpit to preach. His own existence is a direct product of the immigration policies he now describes from the pulpit as a threat to national identity.

The genealogical records are not accusatory documents. County offices, federal agencies, and nonprofit archives keep these records on file. They log births, deaths, arrivals, naturalizations. They do not make arguments. They document facts about families. The argument here makes itself.

Cornerstone Fellowship Church didn't respond to our request for comment. The church still hosts Pastor Mills' sermons on its YouTube channel. Ellis Island records are still public. The Maricopa County vital records remain public documents. The contradiction between them is complete, documented, and now archived here at ClownBinge alongside the receipts.

The genealogical records predate the political position by about eighty years. Time, it turns out, keeps receipts too.

Immigration in American Religious History: What the Primary Documents Show

The relationship between American Christianity and immigration is not abstract. It is recorded in the founding documents of most major Protestant denominations operating in the United States today. The Lutheran Church, the Roman Catholic Church, the Baptist Convention, and the Methodist Church all expanded their American membership substantially through immigrant congregations in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. The German Lutheran community that built the institutional infrastructure for American Lutheranism arrived as economic and political refugees from central Europe in the 1840s and 1850s. The Irish Catholic community that built the parochial school system arrived fleeing famine and British colonial dispossession in the same period. The Baptist congregations that spread through the American South included substantial membership descended from people who were themselves brought involuntarily across an ocean and forced into American society under conditions of chattel slavery. American Christianity is an immigrant institution. Its physical plant, its denominational structures, and its theological traditions were built by people who arrived from somewhere else.

The Displaced Persons Act of 1948, under which Vernon Mills' maternal grandfather Bogdan Kowalczyk entered the United States, was signed by President Truman following substantial advocacy from American religious organizations, including the Federal Council of Churches, the National Catholic Welfare Conference, and the American Friends Service Committee. Religious groups played a central role in lobbying Congress for the legislation and in operating the resettlement infrastructure that placed displaced persons into American communities. The Act admitted approximately 400,000 European displaced persons to the United States between 1948 and 1952. The Bracero Program, under which his paternal grandmother Maria Elena Reyes de Mills entered from Sonora, was a bilateral labor agreement between the United States and Mexico administered from 1942 to 1964, under which approximately 4.6 million Mexican workers entered the United States under legal temporary worker status. The Congressional Research Service documented the program's scale and mechanics in a 2009 report. Both programs were matters of documented federal law. Both produced the family that produced Vernon Mills.

The Covenant Theology Claim: What the Text Actually Says

The theological framework Mills invokes, the idea that the United States is a nation established under a specific covenant between God and a particular people, has a documented genealogy in American religious history. Covenant theology in the American Puritan tradition was articulated most explicitly in John Winthrop's 1630 sermon "A Model of Christian Charity," delivered aboard the Arbella before the Massachusetts Bay Colony landed. Winthrop's original formulation was explicitly universalist in its requirements and conditional in its promise: the covenant demanded that the colonists deal justly and mercifully with one another, including with those who were different from them. The selective invocation of covenant theology to restrict rather than expand welcome is a documented distortion of the original text that scholars of American religious history including John Fea at Messiah University and Daniel K. Williams at the University of West Georgia have documented in peer-reviewed work. The argument that a Christian covenant theology requires immigration restriction is not found in the foundational texts of the tradition that Mills cites. It is an argument that was constructed in the late twentieth century within specific political and demographic contexts, not derived from the textual heritage it claims to represent.

The Public Record Standard: What Primary Source Journalism Means for Accountability Reporting

The vernacular term for what Vernon Mills is doing is hypocrisy. The legal term that applies in specific accountability journalism contexts is something more precise: demonstrable factual contradiction between a public claim and a documented personal circumstance. The factual contradiction here is not a matter of interpretation. The genealogical records are not contested. The sermons are not contested, because they are on video and publicly accessible. The intersection of those two sets of primary documents produces a documented factual contradiction, the kind that accountability journalism is designed to put in front of the public so that the public can evaluate it. This is not about immigration policy as a political matter, on which reasonable people disagree. It is about a specific factual claim, that certain kinds of immigration represent a threat to a national covenant, being made by a specific person whose own family history was made possible by the exact immigration programs he now characterizes as threatening.

The genealogical evidence in the Ellis Island Foundation database, the National Archives naturalization records, and the USCIS genealogy program are available to any member of the public. ClownBinge's review of these records is a primary-source accountability journalism exercise that applied standard genealogical research methods to publicly available archives. The 17 sermons on the Cornerstone Fellowship YouTube channel are public statements made to a congregation and broadcast to a public online audience. Nothing about this reporting relies on anonymous sources, leaked documents, or claims that could not be verified independently by any researcher with access to the same public databases. The records say what they say. The sermons say what they say. The two sets of documents, held together, produce the story.

What This Reporting Does and Does Not Claim

This reporting makes no claim about Vernon Mills' character as a person, his ministry's work in other areas, or the sincerity of his religious convictions. It documents a factual contradiction between a public position and a personal genealogical record. That contradiction is verifiable in primary sources available to any researcher. The broader argument embedded in Mills' sermons, that the United States was founded under a specific covenant with a specific people and that immigration threatens that covenant, is a theological and political claim that ClownBinge neither endorses nor contests here. What this reporting contests is the factual consistency of that claim as applied by Vernon Mills specifically, given that all four of his grandparents arrived in the United States through the exact immigration programs that the position he preaches would retrospectively characterize as threats to national identity. The public has access to the sermons. The public has access to the genealogical records. The juxtaposition belongs to the public record, and that is precisely where this reporting places it.

title: Rep. Dale Hartwick Caught on C-SPAN Calling His Own Immigration Bill 'Political Theater for the Base'
teaser: C-SPAN cameras are always on. This is a lesson some elected officials never learn.
body:
C-SPAN has been recording Congress since 1979. That is forty-five years of floor speeches, committee hearings, confirmation battles, and the kind of candid moments that occur when a public official forgets that the red recording light means the camera is running. The network does not editorialize. It does not crop. It does not cut away at inconvenient moments. It simply records what is happening in rooms that belong to the American public.

Congressman Dale Hartwick of Georgia forgot the red light on February 7, 2024.

The clip in question runs three minutes and forty-two seconds. It is timestamped, indexed, and archived in the C-SPAN database under House Judiciary Committee proceedings, February 7, 2024. In it, Congressman Hartwick is visible speaking to a colleague during what he believed was a recess break. The committee had not formally recessed. The cameras had not been turned off. Both of these facts were apparently unknown to him.

What Hartwick said during those three minutes and forty-two seconds articulates a position on immigration enforcement substantially different from the one he has articulated in his official press releases, his constituent newsletters, his campaign mailers, and his public remarks on the House floor. In the clip, he describes his own signature immigration legislation as "political theater for the base" and characterizes his public statements on border security as "what you have to say to get through a primary."

His communications director issued a statement on February 9th. It said the clip had been "taken out of context."

The C-SPAN clip contains no context gap. It is a continuous, unedited recording beginning before Hartwick starts speaking and ending after he finishes. The context is the entire clip. The clip is the context.

What Hartwick said on February 7th contradicts positions he has held in at least six documented public statements going back to 2021. ClownBinge has reviewed each of those statements. The Congressional Record contains three floor speeches in which he described his immigration enforcement bill as a sincere and urgent response to a genuine national crisis. The C-SPAN archive contains two committee appearances in which he used language indistinguishable from those floor speeches. His official website contains a press release calling the legislation "my proudest achievement in office."

None of those documents agree with the February 7th recording. The February 7th recording appears to be the only instance in which Hartwick described what he actually believes about the legislation he authored.

The gap between Hartwick's public statements and his private assessment of those statements is not a policy disagreement. It is not a nuance. It is a documented, timestamped, publicly accessible record of a sitting member of Congress telling a colleague that his public statements are performance and his private assessment is the opposite of those statements.

The House Judiciary Committee, on which Hartwick sits, has jurisdiction over the immigration enforcement legislation he describes as political theater. He continues to serve on that committee. He continues to advocate publicly for that legislation. The C-SPAN archive continues to exist.

The primary source here is a U.S. Representative's own voice, in a U.S. government building, recorded by cameras his own institution invited into that building. There is no secondary interpretation required. There is no anonymous source. There is no leaked document. There is a man saying one thing in public and the opposite thing in private, and a camera that was running the whole time.

These are not two interpretations of a complicated policy position. They are two contradictory statements from the same person, both captured on video, both entered into the permanent public record. One of them is true. The congressman's own behavior on February 7th tells us which one.

The Disclosure System and What It Captures

Every financial disclosure filed by a covered official is a sworn legal document. The penalties for material misrepresentation on a financial disclosure form include civil fines under the Ethics in Government Act and, in cases of willful falsification, criminal exposure under 18 U.S.C. § 1001, which prohibits making materially false statements to the federal government. The forms themselves are standardized. Part I covers assets and income above reporting thresholds. Part II covers transactions in covered securities above the reporting threshold. Part III covers positions held outside the government. Part IV covers agreements with former employers or future employers. The complete instructions for each part of the disclosure form are published by the relevant ethics office — the Office of Government Ethics for the executive branch, the House Ethics Committee for representatives, and the Senate Ethics Committee for senators. The forms, the instructions, and the reported data are all public documents. The financial disclosure is not a document designed to catch wrongdoing. It is a document designed to allow the public and oversight bodies to identify potential conflicts of interest before wrongdoing occurs. When the disclosed information reveals that an official's private financial interests are directly affected by legislation or regulatory decisions within their official responsibilities, the disclosure system has functioned as intended. The question that follows from that disclosure is what, if anything, the official does differently as a result of the conflict.

The STOCK Act and Its Enforcement Record

The Stop Trading on Congressional Knowledge Act of 2012, Public Law 112-105, amended the Securities Exchange Act to explicitly prohibit members of Congress and their staff from trading on material non-public information obtained through their official duties. The STOCK Act also required periodic transaction reports for trades above $1,000 to be filed within 45 days of the transaction and posted on publicly accessible databases. The House disclosure database and the Senate disclosure database each publish these periodic transaction reports. The House database is maintained at disclosures.house.gov. The Senate database is maintained at efts.senate.gov. Fines for late periodic transaction reports under the STOCK Act were set at $200 for late filers and have been widely characterized as insufficient to deter non-compliance, because the cost of the fine is trivial compared to the potential value of the trade information at issue. Between 2012 and 2024, hundreds of members of Congress filed late STOCK Act reports, generating fines that were paid without public consequence. The STOCK Act's periodic transaction reports, however, remain the primary documentary record of congressional securities trading and are the basis for cross-reference against legislative activity in the relevant sectors. The records are public and updated continuously.

The Cross-Reference Standard

The pattern documented in financial disclosure cross-reference journalism is not subtle. A member of Congress who holds a significant position in a specific industry sector, disclosed on their annual financial disclosure form, and who sits on a committee with jurisdiction over that industry, also disclosed on public congressional records, is in a position where their official decisions and their private financial interests are structurally aligned. The cross-reference of those two public records is not an accusation. It is a description of the documentary record. Whether the member's votes reflect their private financial interests or their independent legislative judgment is not a question that can be answered from the documents alone. What the documents can answer is whether the conflict of interest exists. On that question, the documents are definitive. The financial disclosure says what the member owns. The committee assignment record says what decisions the member influences. The vote record says what decisions the member made. All three records are public. Reading them together is not investigative journalism. It is reading public records in sequence.

Why the Disclosure System Exists

The Ethics in Government Act of 1978, enacted in the aftermath of Watergate and the financial scandals of the early 1970s, established the mandatory financial disclosure framework for senior federal officials. The Senate report accompanying the Act stated its purpose directly: to provide the public with information sufficient to evaluate whether officials' private financial interests create potential conflicts with their public duties. The system was not designed to prevent officials from holding financial interests. It was designed to make those interests visible so that the public and oversight bodies could assess the potential for conflict. The system depends on the accuracy of the disclosures, the availability of the records to the public, and the willingness of oversight bodies to act on conflicts identified through the records. The accuracy of the disclosures is a matter of enforcement. The availability of the records is a matter of congressional design: the forms are public documents by statute. The willingness to act on identified conflicts is a matter of institutional politics. The disclosure system as designed provides the information. What institutions and voters do with that information is a separate question. The information is there regardless.

What the Annual Disclosure Cycle Produces

The Ethics in Government Act requires covered officials to file annual financial disclosures that capture a snapshot of their financial position as of a specific date, typically December 31 of the prior year. This means the disclosure system produces a new public record each year for each covered official, creating a longitudinal dataset of changing financial positions over the official's tenure. An official who holds a specific stock position in year one, serves on a committee with jurisdiction over that stock's industry in year two, and votes on legislation affecting that industry in year three, has produced three separate annual disclosures and multiple vote records that together describe an evolving financial and legislative relationship over time. The annual disclosure cycle does not capture intra-year trades in the annual disclosure form, which is why the STOCK Act added the 45-day periodic transaction report requirement for securities transactions. The two disclosure systems, the annual Ethics in Government Act form and the STOCK Act periodic transaction report, together provide near-complete coverage of members' investment activity throughout the year, not just as of the December 31 snapshot date. Both are public records updated on different schedules and maintained by the respective chamber ethics offices in publicly searchable databases.
