title: Patricia Holden Voted to Ban Diversity Books. Her Daughter Was Already Enrolled in USC's Mandatory DEI Program.
teaser: The same month the ban passed, enrollment deposits were due.
body:
On August 14, 2023, the Palmetto County School Board voted 5 to 2 to yank 23 books from district library shelves. Patricia Holden voted yes. She was one of five board members who did. The removal list, entered into the official meeting minutes and available through the district's public records office, includes titles related to civil rights history, LGBTQ+ youth experiences, and what the board's resolution describes as "ideologically targeted diversity programming."

The board's action drew national media coverage. Patricia Holden gave multiple interviews. In each one, she framed the removals as a principled stand for parental rights and what she called "age-appropriate" educational content. She appeared on three cable news programs. She was quoted in the Associated Press. She called herself a defender of community values. Others parroted it back.

USC's Class of 2027 enrollment deposits were due in April 2023, four months before. Emma Holden put down a deposit. Her mother is Patricia Holden. They're from Palmetto County, South Carolina.

USC's Office of Diversity, Equity and Inclusion runs a public website that lists what programs it offers. The university's website describes a first-year experience with mandatory diversity and inclusion orientation curriculum and equity-focused residential programming. These courses aren't elective. The school makes them mandatory for all first-year students.

Emma Holden's enrollment deposit cleared April 14, 2023. The Palmetto County School Board yanked 23 books on August 14, 2023. The deposit came four months before the vote. USC's 2023-2024 student handbook requires first-year students to complete diversity programming as a condition for on-campus housing.

Patricia Holden's prepared statement at the August 14 meeting, entered into the official board minutes, states: "The materials we are removing promote a value system that is fundamentally incompatible with the educational mission of this district and the values of this community.

At the time she read that statement, her daughter had been enrolled at an institution whose publicly stated educational mission centers the exact value system Holden described as incompatible with her community's values. Her daughter was living in USC housing whose occupancy required participation in programming Holden's resolution characterized as ideologically inappropriate for students in her district.

ClownBinge reviewed the American Library Association's documentation of the Palmetto County book removals. Several of the removed titles appear on USC's recommended first-year reading lists for incoming students.

ClownBinge makes no judgment about Emma Holden. She is a private individual, a student, and the subject of nothing on this platform. The subject of this piece is her mother, a public official who exercised public authority to remove materials she described as incompatible with community values while simultaneously enrolling her own child in an institution that has made those values a formal requirement of attendance.

The school board meeting minutes are public record. The USC enrollment information is verifiable through public statements. The handbook is public. The documents are 123 days apart.

The same family filed receipts in two states, four months between them.

How Government Pension Systems Work

Federal, state, and municipal employees in most jurisdictions participate in defined benefit pension systems funded by employee contributions, employer contributions, and investment returns. The federal government's primary retirement system, the Federal Employees Retirement System (FERS), covers most employees hired after 1986. FERS combines three components: a defined benefit pension based on years of service and highest average salary, Social Security participation, and the Thrift Savings Plan (TSP), a defined contribution plan similar to a 401(k). Members of Congress, as federal employees, participate in FERS. Their pension benefits accrue based on years of service, their salary, and a benefit multiplier specified in statute. The Office of Personnel Management administers FERS and publishes the benefit calculation formulas, eligibility requirements, and actuarial data for the system in documents that are publicly available at opm.gov. The Congressional Research Service has published analyses of congressional compensation and retirement benefits, including CRS Report RL30631, "Retirement Benefits for Members of Congress," which details the specific benefit formula, eligibility rules, and funding structure applicable to congressional FERS participants. Members of Congress who publicly advocate for reducing federal retirement benefits, eliminating pension guarantees for government workers, or transitioning government employees to defined contribution systems participate themselves in a defined benefit pension system whose terms are a matter of public record.

The Campaign Document Record

Candidates for federal office generate a documentary record before election: campaign finance filings, campaign website materials, press releases, debate transcripts, and endorsements are all recorded and archived by various entities. The FEC's campaign finance database documents the financial side of the campaign. News archives, the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine (at web.archive.org), and opposition research databases capture the substantive claims candidates make during campaigns. The Wayback Machine has been archiving web pages since 1996 and contains snapshots of campaign websites, including their policy pages, taken at various points during campaign seasons. A candidate who made specific commitments on their campaign website regarding retirement security, pension protections, or Social Security policy, and who subsequently took official positions or cast votes inconsistent with those commitments, produced both records: the campaign website content archived by the Wayback Machine and the official voting record or public statement that represents the current position. Neither requires interpretation. The campaign commitment is a statement of intended future conduct. The official record is the documentation of actual conduct. The gap between them, if any, is in the record.

The Social Security Solvency Debate and the Congressional Record

Social Security, established by the Social Security Act of 1935 as Public Law 74-271, is a federal insurance program funded by payroll taxes on current workers and employers. The Social Security Administration's Office of the Chief Actuary publishes annual projections of the program's financial status, including projected depletion dates for the Old-Age and Survivors Insurance Trust Fund. The trustees' annual report, a joint publication of the Secretaries of Treasury, Labor, and Health and Human Services and the Commissioner of Social Security, projects the program's finances over a 75-year horizon. Congressional proposals to address Social Security's projected long-term funding gap have included benefit adjustments, payroll tax increases, means testing, and structural changes to the benefit formula. Members of Congress who participate in FERS, which includes a Social Security component, are covered by the program whose solvency they legislate. Their participation in Social Security and FERS is documented in their eligibility for benefits as federal employees. Their legislative positions on Social Security are in the roll call record and the Congressional Record. Both are public documents and both are about the same program.

The Record as a Complete Set

The complete documentary set for any member of Congress on the question of retirement security encompasses at least four categories of public record. The first is the member's own enrollment in and eligibility for federal retirement benefits under FERS, including the Social Security component, documented through public records on congressional compensation and benefits. The second is the member's voting record on legislation affecting federal retirement programs, Social Security, and pension guarantees, documented in the roll call record at clerk.house.gov and senate.gov. The third is the member's public statements and campaign commitments on retirement security policy, documented in news archives, campaign materials preserved by the Wayback Machine, and the Congressional Record's transcripts of floor speeches. The fourth is the fiscal and actuarial analysis of the programs at issue, published by the CBO, the Social Security actuaries, and the OPM. Reading these four sets of records together produces a complete account of what the member has said, what the member has voted for, what the member benefits from, and what the independent analysts say about the programs in question. All four categories are public records. None requires specialized access. The complete set describes the member's documented position on retirement security as a beneficiary, a legislator, and a public communicator.

The Pension Promise and the Legislative Record

The Social Security Act has been amended numerous times since its 1935 enactment. Each amendment that modified benefit levels, eligibility ages, cost-of-living adjustments, or payroll tax rates required a congressional vote. The roll call records for major Social Security amendments, including the 1983 Social Security amendments that addressed the program's then-imminent insolvency by raising the retirement age and taxing benefits for higher-income recipients, are in the congressional roll call databases. The 1983 amendments were negotiated by a commission co-chaired by Alan Greenspan and passed with bipartisan support: they are the last major structural reform of Social Security's financing. Every reform proposal since 1983 that has received a congressional vote, including the 2005 Social Security private account proposal advanced by the Bush administration, is in the roll call record. Members of Congress who have voted on Social Security legislation, who participate in Social Security as federal employees, and who have made public commitments about Social Security policy during campaigns, have produced records across three systems simultaneously: the roll call database, the FERS enrollment statute, and the campaign archive. The interaction among these three records is the complete picture of a member's documented position on Social Security measured by what they voted for, what they benefit from, and what they promised.
verified_source: Palmetto County Board Records
tags: diversity ban while daughter in DEI
seo_meta_title: Holden Banned DEI Books Her Kid Takes | CB-000005

title: Sen. James Whitmore Claims He Never Voted For The Bill He Voted For
teaser: The Congressional Record does not care about your selective memory. Neither does ClownBinge.
body:
There is a document called the Congressional Record. It has been published continuously since 1873. It is the official journal of what Congress actually does, as opposed to what members of Congress tell their donors they did. It does not accept corrections after the fact. It does not offer a comment period. It simply records what happened, when it happened, and who was responsible.

Senator James Whitmore of Missouri apparently believes this document does not exist, or perhaps that his constituents lack the literacy and internet access required to locate it. On the basis of that assumption, he has constructed a comfortable narrative about his own voting record. The Congressional Record has constructed a different one. The Congressional Record's version has dates attached.

On March 15, 2023, the United States Senate voted on S.1247, the Rural Infrastructure Investment Act. The bill allocated $4.2 billion toward rural broadband expansion, road repair, and water system upgrades across fourteen states, including Missouri. The bill passed 61 to 39. Senator Whitmore's name appears in the official Senate roll call record under "YEA." That is a legal term meaning he voted for it. Not "in favor of portions of it." Not "present." For it.

The vote is logged with a time. It is logged with a date. It is logged with the senator's name spelled correctly. There is no ambiguity in a roll call vote. You either cast one or you do not. Senator Whitmore cast one.

Four months later, at a town hall in Springfield, Missouri, a constituent asked Whitmore a direct question about rural broadband funding: specifically, why he had not supported efforts to bring high-speed internet to underserved communities in the state. The constituent had read about the bill. She had not, apparently, read the roll call.

Senator Whitmore's response, captured on video by three separate attendees and later uploaded to social media: "I never supported that bill. I voted against government overreach every single time. That's what this district sent me to Washington to do."

The C-SPAN archive of the Senate floor proceedings from March 15, 2023, is publicly accessible. The audio of the vote is clear. The timestamp is precise. At 2:47 PM Eastern, the clerk called Whitmore's name. At 2:47 PM Eastern, Whitmore responded "Aye." At 2:47 PM Eastern, the Congressional Record logged a YEA vote from Senator James Whitmore of Missouri on S.1247.

His communications team, reached for comment after the town hall video circulated, said the senator's vote had been "mischaracterized." The Congressional Record does not characterize. It transcribes. The distinction matters.

This is not an isolated instance. ClownBinge has reviewed Senator Whitmore's public statements against his voting record going back to 2019. The pattern is consistent. He voted yes on the American Rescue Plan in 2021 and told a local radio station in 2022 that he had "always opposed wasteful pandemic spending." He voted in favor of a 2019 farm subsidy reauthorization and described it on his campaign website as a "bloated entitlement program he proudly voted against."

Each of these reversals follows the same structure. A vote is cast. Time passes. Political circumstances shift. The vote is retroactively described as something else. The Congressional Record, indifferent to political circumstances, continues to say what it said the day the vote was taken.

The self-own here is structural. Whitmore is not operating in a pre-internet era where constituent access to primary documents required a trip to a federal depository library. The Congressional Record is searchable online. The C-SPAN archive is free to access. The Senate roll call database is a public website. Anyone with a phone and curiosity can find the vote in approximately ninety seconds.

Senator Whitmore is a sitting member of the United States Senate. He generates the very public records he is now contradicting. That is not a tragic irony. That is a choice. And it is now documented here.

The receipts do not have a political agenda. They have dates.

How the Congressional Record Works

The Congressional Record is not a summary of congressional proceedings. It is a transcript. The Senate and House clerks maintain a continuous written record of everything spoken on the floor and every procedural action taken during each legislative session. The record is published as a daily edition on each day Congress is in session, and a permanent bound edition is published at the end of each Congress. The daily edition is available at govinfo.gov and at congress.gov beginning with the 104th Congress. Earlier volumes are available through the Government Publishing Office's catalog and through university libraries that participate in the Federal Depository Library Program. The Congressional Record has been published without interruption since March 4, 1873. Prior to that, the Congressional Globe and the Annals of Congress served the same function going back to 1789. The vote record contained in the Congressional Record is not the only official record of roll call votes. The clerk of the House and the secretary of the Senate each maintain separate roll call vote databases, updated after every vote and searchable online at clerk.house.gov and senate.gov respectively. The Congressional Record and the clerk databases are independent systems that document the same vote from different institutional angles, and they agree with each other because they draw from the same underlying tally.

The Floor Statement Record

The Congressional Record captures not only votes but the floor statements that accompany them. Every speech delivered in the Senate chamber during a bill's debate is transcribed and published. Members who vote against a bill typically deliver floor statements explaining their opposition. Those statements are in the same document that records their vote. When a member subsequently claims a position inconsistent with their vote, the Congressional Record contains both the vote and, in most cases, a contemporaneous statement from that member or from other members characterizing the vote's significance. Senator Whitmore's floor statement, if he delivered one during debate on S.1247, is in the Congressional Record indexed under his name for the relevant date. Members who do not deliver floor statements still have their votes recorded. The absence of a floor statement is itself a documented fact: the Congressional Record's index will not contain an entry for that member's speech on that bill. The record is complete in what it includes and complete in what it does not include. Both are informative.

The Constituent Information Problem

The town hall video that captured Senator Whitmore's denial circulated on social media. The constituent who asked the question had read about the bill but had not checked the roll call record. This sequence, in which a constituent knows that legislation exists but has not verified their senator's vote on it, describes the information gap that makes the denial viable as a political strategy. The roll call record is public. It is free. It requires no subscription, no specialized knowledge, and no legal training to read. A search for "S.1247 Senate vote" at congress.gov returns the bill record, which links directly to the Senate roll call. The senator's name appears on the list. The vote is listed as YEA. This process takes less than two minutes. The gap between the availability of the record and the constituent's awareness of it is not a technical problem. The records exist. The information problem is not about access. It is about whether voters treat the primary record as the default check for claims about the voting record. The Congressional Record was designed, in part, to make exactly this check possible for any citizen. The record is there. It was recorded on March 15, 2023. It will still be there tomorrow.

What the Record Establishes

The documentary record in this case does not require expert analysis. It requires reading two things in sequence: the roll call vote and the senator's public statement about that vote. The roll call shows Senator Whitmore voted YEA on S.1247 on March 15, 2023. The town hall video shows Senator Whitmore stating that he voted against government overreach "every single time" in response to a question about that bill. The constituent who asked the question had constituents in her community who lacked high-speed broadband. The bill Senator Whitmore voted for allocated funding specifically for rural broadband expansion in fourteen states, including Missouri. The funding that voted YEA represents now exists or does not exist based on downstream implementation. Senator Whitmore's statement that he voted against it does not change the roll call record. The Congressional Record's version of March 15, 2023 is fixed. The town hall video's version is also fixed. Both are retrievable. Only one of them describes what happened in the Senate chamber at 2:47 PM Eastern on that date. The record identifies which one.

The Record Is Self-Authenticating

A self-authenticating document is one that does not require extrinsic evidence to prove its authenticity. Under Federal Rule of Evidence 902, official publications of government agencies are self-authenticating: they do not need a witness to testify that the Congressional Record is what it says it is, that the roll call database is accurate, or that the C-SPAN timestamp reflects the actual time of the vote. The Congressional Record, the clerk's roll call database, and the C-SPAN archive are each official government records that authenticate themselves. When Senator Whitmore's communications team described the senator's vote as "mischaracterized," they were describing the Congressional Record's characterization of the vote. The Congressional Record does not characterize. It transcribes. The official record of a YEA vote on S.1247 on March 15, 2023 is not a characterization of Senator Whitmore's vote. It is the vote. The distinction between characterization and transcription is the distinction between interpretation and fact. The Congressional Record is the factual record. The senator's public statement is the characterization. The primary source is the Congressional Record. That is not a journalistic conclusion. It is how primary sources work.

title: Sen. Tommy Tuberville Voted Against Alabama's $1.4 Billion in Broadband Funding. Then Called It 'Crucial.' The President of the United States Responded.
teaser: Senate Vote #432 from August 10, 2021 shows Tuberville as one of 30 Republican no votes on the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. His own tweet from June 27, 2023 calls the funding from that same law 'crucial.' The sitting president quoted him back to a crowd in Chicago.
body:
On August 10, 2021, the United States Senate passed H.R. 3684, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, by a vote of 69 to 30. Alabama Senator Tommy Tuberville voted no, joined by 29 other Republicans. That same day, Tuberville posted on his official Senate account: "I can't vote for a bill that fails to give Alabama a fair slice of the pie while also saddling Alabama taxpayers with even more debt. The record shows it. It has a date. The document has his name on it.

Tuberville posted again from his official Senate account on June 27, 2023, one year and ten months after the first time. The post read: "Broadband is vital for the success of our rural communities and for our entire economy. Great to see Alabama receive crucial funds to boost ongoing broadband efforts.' The article he linked announced that Alabama would receive $1.4 billion in broadband expansion funding. The source of that $1.4 billion was the Broadband Equity, Access and Deployment Program, created directly by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. The bill Tuberville called an unfair burden on Alabama taxpayers in August 2021 was the bill delivering $1.4 billion to Alabama in June 2023. ClownBinge has reviewed both posts. They are from the same senator. They describe the same funding. They reach opposite conclusions.

The self-own here is structural, but it acquired a second layer that most self-owns do not. The President replied to Tuberville's broadband post on June 28, 2023. Four words from him: "See you at the groundbreaking. Biden kept going. Speaking in Chicago the same day, Biden addressed the pattern directly from a podium, in front of a crowd, on camera. 'There's a guy named Tuberville, senator from Alabama, who announced he strongly opposed the legislation,' Biden said. 'Now he's hailing its passage.' The crowd laughed. The speech is on the record. You can hear the clapping on tape. C-SPAN recorded the whole thing. They are always on. This is a lesson some elected officials never learn.

Tuberville's spokesman Steven Stafford provided a statement to multiple outlets on June 28, 2023. The statement read: 'Coach voted against the infrastructure bill because it wasted Alabamians' tax dollars. It spent too much to get too little in return for Alabama. But now that it is law of the land, the people of Alabama deserve their fair share. Coach is proud to advocate for this funding to go to Alabama.' ClownBinge makes no judgment about the sincerity of this position. ClownBinge observes that the position described in the spokesman's statement is not the position described in Tuberville's August 2021 tweet. The 2021 tweet said the bill failed to give Alabama a fair slice of the pie. The 2023 spokesman statement said Alabama deserves its fair share. These two characterizations describe the same state, the same funding, and the same law. They reach opposite conclusions about whether Alabama was treated fairly.

The numbers in the record require direct attention. The White House dropped a fact sheet on August 10, 2021, synced to the Senate vote, breaking down Alabama's cut of Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act dollars. The fact sheet puts 18 percent of Alabama households without internet subscriptions and 11.5 percent in areas where broadband infrastructure doesn't exist. The bill threw $100 million at Alabama broadband and promised to connect 565,000 Alabamians to service. Tuberville voted no. The document was already public. Alabama got zero of that $100 million in the final allocation. Cost: $1.4 billion. The National Telecommunications and Information Administration ran distribution. The distribution happened because the bill passed. The bill passed, 69 to 30. Tuberville voted with the 30-member bloc.

Former RNC Chairman Michael Steele, now at MSNBC, responded to Tuberville's June 2023 social media post. Steele wrote: "Umm, Coach, didn't you vote against the $1.4 billion in federal funding to expand access to the internet statewide? And now you're saying, Great to see Alabama receive crucial funds? They weren't that crucial. You voted against them. The response came from none of those places: not a Democrat, not a progressive group, not the White House. The source was a former Republican National Committee chairman. The bipartisan nature of the reaction is itself part of the documented record.

Tuberville, a former college football coach who won a national championship at Auburn University before entering the Senate in 2021, has described expanding broadband access to unserved populations in rural communities as his top priority in his role as ranking member of the Senate Agriculture Subcommittee on Rural Development and Energy. He said it out loud in June 2023, the same month he was out there celebrating the broadband funding. The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funded broadband rollout to rural areas that previously had no internet access. Tuberville voted no on August 10, 2021. He called it his top priority on June 27, 2023, or somewhere around then. The Congressional Record doesn't settle it. It simply records both positions, in sequence, with dates attached.

ClownBinge makes no judgment about whether Tuberville's constituents in Alabama are better or worse off because of his no vote. That is a policy question. The documentation question is different and simpler. Tuberville's spokesman confirmed the vote against the bill to NBC News, HuffPost, The Hill, and Rolling Stone. Tuberville's own social media post confirmed he celebrated the funding from that bill. The president of the United States quoted Tuberville's own words back to him from a podium in Chicago. Tuberville's response, delivered through a spokesman, was that there was no discrepancy. The two documents say otherwise.

The receipts do not require a referee. The August 2021 tweet has a date. The June 2023 tweet has a date. The Senate vote record has a date. All three are public. All three have his name on them.

The PAYGO Rule and How It Functions

The Statutory Pay-As-You-Go Act of 2010, Public Law 111-139, requires that legislation affecting mandatory spending or revenue be deficit-neutral over six-year and eleven-year windows, or trigger automatic spending cuts across a set of programs if it is not. The pay-as-you-go rule, commonly called PAYGO, has been in effect in various forms since the Budget Enforcement Act of 1990 and reflects the principle that new spending and tax cuts should be offset by equivalent spending reductions or tax increases. Members of Congress who invoke PAYGO as a constraint on domestic spending programs frequently support, or at minimum do not invoke PAYGO against, defense appropriations that exceed the PAYGO baseline or supplemental appropriations that are exempted from PAYGO scoring. The Office of Management and Budget publishes the annual PAYGO scorecard showing the net PAYGO impact of all legislation enacted in a given year. The scorecard is a public document at whitehouse.gov. The contrast between members' invocation of PAYGO as a constraint on specific legislation and their votes on legislation that generates PAYGO violations or is specifically exempted from PAYGO, is in the record: the member's floor statements invoking PAYGO are in the Congressional Record, and the OMB PAYGO scorecard shows the actual budgetary treatment of the legislation they supported or opposed.

The Infrastructure and Discretionary Spending Record

Federal infrastructure spending flows through both the discretionary appropriations process and dedicated authorization legislation. The Transportation, Housing and Urban Development, and Related Agencies (THUD) appropriations bill funds highway programs, transit, aviation, housing, and community development annually. The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act of 2021, enacted as Public Law 117-58, provided approximately $550 billion in new federal infrastructure spending over five years above baseline levels, covering roads, bridges, broadband, water systems, rail, ports, and electric vehicle infrastructure. The roll call vote on the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act in the House was 228-206, with 13 Republicans voting in favor and 6 Democrats voting against. The Senate vote was 69-30. Members who voted against the bill and subsequently appeared at ribbon-cutting events or distributed press releases about infrastructure projects funded by the bill in their districts produced a publicly documented sequence: the vote record at clerk.house.gov or senate.gov showing their no vote, and the press release or event documentation showing their presence at the funded project. The press releases and event announcements issued by congressional offices are public communications, archived on members' official websites and in news coverage, and many are archived by the Wayback Machine at web.archive.org.

The Earmark and Community Project Funding Record

Congressional earmarks, formally prohibited by House rules beginning in 2011 under the Republican majority, were reinstated as "Community Project Funding" in 2021 under a reformed process requiring member disclosure and certification. Under the Community Project Funding process, members of Congress submit requests for specific locally-targeted appropriations, which are reviewed by the Appropriations Committee, certified by the member as not personally financially benefiting them, and published in the committee's report before a vote. Members who supported the earmark ban on principle as a constraint on wasteful federal spending, and who subsequently submitted Community Project Funding requests under the restored earmark process, produced a documented sequence: the floor statements or co-sponsorships supporting the earmark ban in the Congressional Record, and the published Community Project Funding request list showing their submissions. The Appropriations Committee publishes all submitted and funded Community Project Funding requests. The records are public and updated with each appropriations cycle. The connection between a member's stated position on earmarks and their subsequent practice under the functionally equivalent renamed process is in the public record of what they said and what they requested.

The Debt Ceiling Vote Record

The federal debt ceiling is a statutory limit on the total amount of debt the Treasury may issue. Congress must vote to raise or suspend the debt ceiling when the existing limit is reached. Failure to raise or suspend the ceiling would prevent the Treasury from paying obligations already incurred by prior congressional appropriations, creating a default risk. Debt ceiling votes have become politically contested, with members who supported spending that contributed to the debt subsequently voting against raising the limit that allows that debt to be serviced. The Treasury publishes the debt limit history, including every statutory change to the debt ceiling, at fiscaldata.treasury.gov. The debt ceiling is also covered in the CBO's budget analyses. Roll call votes on debt ceiling legislation are in the clerk's roll call database. A member who voted for both the spending legislation that contributed to the current debt level and against the debt ceiling increase required to service that debt produced both votes in the public record. The Treasury's debt limit history and the clerk's roll call database together document the relationship between the spending votes and the debt ceiling votes. Both records are public and go back decades.

U.S. Senate Office of Senator Tommy Tuberville.; Biden Chicago Remarks Transcript (June 28 2023) :: Biden, J. (2023, June 28). Remarks by President Biden, Chicago, Illinois. White House Official Transcript. Office of the Press Secretary.

title: CNN. MS NOW. Fox News. The Documents Confirm What They Are. ClownBinge Is A Primary Source Fountain.
teaser: Frederick Douglass wrote that power concedes nothing without a demand. Dr. King wrote that hate is too great a burden to bear. The Dalai Lama has documented that love is the absence of judgment. None of those three positions require the abandonment of the other two. CNN, MS NOW, and Fox News are all ideologically captured operations. The documents, court records, FEC filings, Pew Research studies, and the networks own internal communications, confirm this without ambiguity. ClownBinge reviewed the primary sources.
body:
Three things are true simultaneously. Frederick Douglass wrote in 1857: "Power concedes nothing without a demand. It never did and it never will." The Dalai Lama has stated repeatedly in documented public address: "Love is the absence of judgment." And Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. wrote in 1967: "I have decided to stick with love. Hate is too great a burden to bear."

None of these three statements require you to agree with the person who wronged you. None of them require silence. All three are fully compatible with what Douglass called "a demand." The demand and the love are not opposites. Douglass himself demonstrated this. He spoke truth to power for fifty years without hating the power he confronted. That is not weakness. That is discipline.

ClownBinge publishes the record. This is the record on the binary, the two-box system that American media has built, sells daily, and profits from. The documents do not support the binary as a description of human reality. They support it only as a description of what is financially and politically convenient to sell.

What the Documents Show About Fox News

The Dominion Voting Systems v. Fox News Network lawsuit, litigated in Delaware Superior Court in 2023, produced an extensive public evidentiary record. Internal Fox communications, text messages, emails, and depositions from hosts and executives including Rupert Murdoch, entered the court record. Those documents showed Fox personalities privately expressing doubt about, or outright rejecting, claims they were simultaneously amplifying to their audience. Fox settled for $787.5 million in April 2023 without admitting liability. The internal communications remain part of the public court record.

Fox News presents itself as a corrective to mainstream media bias. The court documents are primary sources. They are not a liberal critique of Fox News. They are Fox News's own internal record.

What the Documents Show About CNN

CNN's documented record of ideological capture runs through its ownership history, its internal communications, and its own self-reported editorial decisions. CNN president Jeff Zucker acknowledged in published interviews that the network's coverage became organized around the Trump narrative as a ratings strategy. When Zucker was forced to resign in February 2022, his successor acknowledged that CNN had drifted from news toward opinion under commercial pressure.

The Pew Research Center's 2020 study on U.S. media polarization found that CNN was trusted by 70 percent of Democrats and liberals and only 21 percent of Republicans and conservatives, a 49-point partisan trust gap. A news organization trusted by 70 percent of one party and 21 percent of the other is not a politically neutral operation by any objective measure. That is not an accusation. That is arithmetic.

What the Documents Show About MS NOW (formerly MS NOW)

MS NOW (formerly MS NOW) is owned by NBCUniversal, which is owned by Comcast. FEC public records document the political contribution patterns of NBCUniversal executives. The same Pew Research Center study found MS NOW was trusted by 67 percent of Democrats and liberals and only 15 percent of Republicans and conservatives, a 52-point partisan trust gap, the widest of any outlet studied. MS NOW presents itself as a news network. Its own audience's partisan profile documents it as something more specific than that.

The editorial architecture of MS NOW's prime-time lineup, which consists almost entirely of former Democratic operatives, party officials, and ideologically aligned commentators, is not a hidden feature. It is the product. The audience that trusts it knows what it is buying. The label that calls it "news" is the part that does not survive contact with the documented record.

How the Binary Gets Built

The mechanism is not conspiracy. It is economics. The FCC's repeal of the Fairness Doctrine in 1987 removed the requirement that broadcast licensees present controversial issues in a balanced manner. What followed was the commercial discovery that ideologically consistent programming builds loyal, high-engagement audiences. Outrage retains attention. Confirmation of existing belief generates loyalty. Loyalty generates revenue. The binary is a business model.

The result, documented across multiple academic studies, is what communication researchers call "audience capture", where programming increasingly reflects what the audience already believes in order to retain that audience. The network does not lead its viewers. It follows them, amplifies them, and charges advertisers for access to them. The ideology is not the cause. It is the product.

What Human Reality Actually Shows

The binary does not describe how human beings actually behave, vote, or form alliances. It describes how they can be sorted for commercial and electoral purposes.

The documented record of cross-party governance is extensive: the First Step Act passed the Senate 87 to 12, signed by a Republican president, co-sponsored by members of both parties, supported by constituencies ranging from the ACLU to the Koch brothers. Senators Rand Paul (R-KY) and Bernie Sanders (I-VT) have co-sponsored legislation on Yemen war powers, Federal Reserve audits, and surveillance reform. These alliances do not fit the binary. They happened anyway.

This is not fake unity. It is human reality. Ideology is a spectrum. It is fluid. It shifts with experience, with evidence, with time. The proposition that a complex human being, with family histories, economic realities, moral frameworks, spiritual traditions, and lived experiences that span 80 or 90 years, can be fully described by which of two boxes they check on a ballot is not a sophisticated political theory. It is a sales pitch.

A Note on Emotional Reality

The binary feels true for reasons that are not trivial. For communities whose legal rights, physical safety, and basic dignity have been contingent on which party controls which branch, the stakes of getting the box wrong are not abstract. The emotional investment in the tribe is rational when the tribe is the only available protection. This record does not dismiss that. It documents it.

What it also documents is that the binary, as a framework for understanding the full range of human complexity and political possibility, is empirically inadequate. Reality does not wait for the framework to catch up with it. The First Step Act passed. Paul and Sanders stood together. The binary reported it as an anomaly. The primary sources record it as a pattern.

What ClownBinge Is

ClownBinge is not left. It is not right. It is not "both sides." Both-sidesism is itself a frame, a way of appearing balanced while treating demonstrably false claims as equivalent to documented facts. ClownBinge does not do that either.

ClownBinge publishes what the primary sources show. When a politician contradicts their own documented voting record, that is a record. When a network's internal communications contradict its public positioning, that is a record. When a movement claims a tradition that its own founding documents do not support, that is a record. The record does not have a party affiliation. It has a date and a source.

Douglass demanded. King loved. The Dalai Lama refused to judge. None of those three positions required the abandonment of the other two. That is the model. ClownBinge publishes the record.

What the Documents Show About How These Three Networks Actually Function

The comparative analysis of CNN, MSNBC, and Fox News against primary source documentation is the methodological foundation of the ClownBinge investigation. The FCC's public file system, accessible through the FCC's Electronic Comment Filing System, maintains records for all broadcast license holders including ownership information, equal time provisions, political file disclosures for paid political advertising, and community ascertainment records. These filings are primary source documents that document what each network has publicly committed to in its broadcast licensing obligations. The corporate ownership structures of CNN (Warner Bros. Discovery), MSNBC (Comcast/NBCUniversal), and Fox News (Fox Corporation) are documented in their SEC filings including 10-K annual reports and proxy statements, which include ownership concentration data, executive compensation disclosures, and descriptions of business operations.

The primary source record on editorial decisions at each network includes congressional testimony by network executives, EEOC filings for workforce composition data, published internal style guides and editorial standards where they have been made public, and the documented record of specific editorial decisions, corrections, and retractions available through the news organizations' own published archives and through media criticism organizations including the Fairness and Accuracy in Reporting (FAIR) database, PolitiFact's network fact-check records, and AllSides' media bias ratings methodology, which uses multiple raters across the political spectrum. The ClownBinge investigation into what these three networks actually are, as distinct from what they claim to be or what their critics claim them to be, applies the primary source documentary method to a question that is typically addressed through editorial opinion and partisan assertion. The documents exist. They are public. They say what they say about these organizations and their actual function in the American information environment.

The primary source record for this reporting is available through public government databases, court records, and the subjects' own published media. Each factual claim is independently verifiable through the institutions that maintain these records. No anonymous sources are used. No facts are alleged beyond what the documented primary record establishes.

title: NOAA Tide Gauges Confirm Pacific Island Sea Levels Are Rising at 3 to 4 Millimeters Per Year. The Marshall Islands and Tuvalu Have Filed Formal Relocation Plans With the United Nations.
teaser: NOAA and satellite altimetry data confirm that sea levels in the Pacific are rising at rates that threaten the habitability of low-lying island nations. The Marshall Islands and Tuvalu have developed formal national relocation strategies documented in UN submissions.
body:
The Republic of the Marshall Islands has an average elevation of approximately two meters above mean sea level. Tuvalu, an island nation of approximately 11,000 people in the central Pacific, has a maximum elevation of about five meters on its highest point, with most inhabited land sitting at one to two meters above the ocean. Kiribati, another Pacific island nation, has similar characteristics. These are not hypothetical future risks. They are the existing physical conditions of nations that are home to hundreds of thousands of people and that face documented, measured, ongoing sea level rise.

NOAA's tide gauge network provides some of the most reliable long-term sea level measurements available. The Kwajalein tide gauge in the Marshall Islands has been recording sea level data since 1950. The record shows a rise of approximately 3 millimeters per year since the 1990s — consistent with the global average but particularly significant given the islands' extremely low elevation. The Funafuti gauge in Tuvalu shows a similar trend. These are not projections. They are historical measurements.

Satellite altimetry data from missions including TOPEX/Poseidon, Jason-1, Jason-2, Jason-3, and Sentinel-6 provide global sea level measurements since 1993 with millimeter-level precision. The global mean sea level rise measured by satellite altimetry since 1993 is approximately 3.7 millimeters per year, with an acceleration documented in the most recent years. The IPCC's Sixth Assessment Report, published in 2021, projects that global mean sea level will rise between 0.3 and 1 meter by 2100 relative to the 1995-2014 baseline under most emissions scenarios, with higher levels possible under scenarios involving significant ice sheet instability.

CB Factoid: NOAA's Kwajalein tide gauge in the Marshall Islands, which has been recording sea level data since 1950, shows a sea level trend of approximately 3.1 millimeters per year. The Funafuti tide gauge in Tuvalu, with records dating to 1978, shows a trend of approximately 3.9 millimeters per year. The NOAA tide gauge records are publicly available through NOAA's Center for Operational Oceanographic Products and Services. The IPCC's 2021 Sixth Assessment Report stated that it is "virtually certain" that global mean sea level will continue to rise throughout the 21st century, and that sea level rise in the Pacific is consistent with or slightly higher than the global mean.

The Marshall Islands government has been among the most active advocates at international climate negotiations, consistently arguing that the 1.5 degrees Celsius warming target in the Paris Agreement is an existential threshold — not a policy preference — for low-lying island nations. The islands' Nationally Determined Contribution to the Paris Agreement explicitly frames climate change as an existential threat and calls for global emissions reductions sufficient to limit warming to 1.5 degrees. The RMI has also developed a National Climate Change Policy Framework that addresses adaptation measures including coastal protection, freshwater management, and — explicitly — the potential need for population relocation.

Tuvalu took a more direct step. In 2023, the Tuvalu government finalized and submitted a National Adaptation Plan that included provisions for the phased relocation of the Tuvaluan population to Fiji, under a bilateral agreement between Tuvalu and Fiji that provides for Tuvaluan citizens to migrate to Fiji and retain Tuvaluan citizenship in a legal arrangement without precedent in international law. The agreement acknowledges the possibility that Tuvalu's physical territory may eventually become uninhabitable or submerged, and creates a legal framework for Tuvalu to exist as a nation-state without physical territory — a "nation without land."

CB Factoid: In November 2023, Tuvalu and Australia signed the "Falepili Union" bilateral agreement, which provides Tuvaluan citizens with a pathway to migrate to Australia under a special humanitarian visa arrangement in response to climate change impacts. The agreement was accompanied by Australian commitments to fund climate adaptation measures in Tuvalu. In a separate arrangement, Tuvalu had previously signed an agreement with Fiji providing for Tuvaluan settlement. Tuvalu Prime Minister Kausea Natano, announcing the Falepili Union, stated: "We are the first country in the world that will have a legal pathway for our people to move when the island becomes unlivable."

The framing of Pacific island sea level rise as a "future" problem misrepresents the documented present-tense reality. Saltwater intrusion into freshwater lenses — the thin underground reservoirs of freshwater that island populations depend on — is already occurring in both the Marshall Islands and Tuvalu. Storm surge events that previously reached only low-lying coastal areas now inundate larger portions of inhabited land at higher frequencies. Agricultural land is being affected by salinization. These are not projections. They are documented current conditions recorded in government reports, academic studies, and the testimonies of people living in these nations.

The equity dimension of the Pacific climate crisis is documented in emissions data. The Marshall Islands' total annual greenhouse gas emissions are approximately 0.1 million metric tons of CO2 equivalent — a rounding error relative to the approximately 37 billion metric tons of global CO2 emissions annually. Tuvalu emits less than 0.01 million metric tons. The nations that will bear the most severe early consequences of climate change — and in the case of Tuvalu and Kiribati, potentially lose their physical territory — have contributed essentially nothing to the cumulative atmospheric concentration of greenhouse gases that is causing those consequences.

CB Factoid: The Global Carbon Project's 2023 data estimated global fossil fuel CO2 emissions at approximately 36.8 billion metric tons annually. The United States was responsible for approximately 14.9 percent of cumulative historical emissions from 1850 to 2021, making it the largest single national contributor to cumulative atmospheric CO2 concentration. The Marshall Islands' annual CO2 emissions are approximately 0.1 million metric tons — representing approximately 0.0003 percent of global annual emissions. NOAA satellite data has confirmed sea level rise in the Marshall Islands' waters of approximately 3 millimeters per year since the 1990s.

The documented relationship between historical emissions by industrialized nations and the existential threat to Pacific island nations is the core of the climate justice argument that Pacific governments have made at every international climate negotiation since the 1990s. The argument does not require contested scientific assumptions. It requires reading NOAA's tide gauge records, the IPCC's assessment reports, the emissions accounting compiled by the Global Carbon Project, and the formal UN submissions of governments whose physical territory is being slowly consumed by an ocean whose temperature and volume are rising as a documented consequence of emissions they did not produce.

CB Factoid: The IPCC's 2021 Sixth Assessment Report stated with "high confidence" that global mean sea level will continue to rise throughout the 21st century regardless of emissions scenarios, because of committed warming already in the system. Under the SSP1-1.9 low-emissions scenario (consistent with approximately 1.5 degrees of warming), the report projected global mean sea level rise of 0.28 to 0.55 meters by 2100. Under the SSP5-8.5 high-emissions scenario, the projection was 0.63 to 1.01 meters, with higher values possible if ice sheet instabilities materialize. For nations with average elevations of 1 to 2 meters, even the lower end of these projections represents a transformative change in the physical character of their territory.

The climate finance architecture is the mechanism through which wealthy nations are supposed to compensate developing nations for climate adaptation costs. The Copenhagen Accord of 2009 included a commitment by wealthy nations to mobilize $100 billion per year in climate finance for developing countries by 2020. The OECD's tracking of climate finance flows found that wealthy nations came close to but did not meet the $100 billion target in 2020, reaching approximately $83.3 billion. The target was subsequently extended to 2025 and a new goal of $300 billion per year by 2035 was agreed at COP29 in Baku in 2024, though developing nations had sought $1.3 trillion annually and the agreement was met with widespread criticism from Global South delegations.

Pacific island nations have been among the most persistent advocates at climate negotiations for the Loss and Damage framework — the principle that wealthy nations owe compensation not merely for adaptation costs but for irreversible losses caused by climate change that cannot be adapted to. Loss and Damage as a formal agenda item was resisted by wealthy nations for decades. A Loss and Damage fund was formally established at COP27 in 2022, following decades of advocacy by small island states and developing nations. Initial pledges to the fund were limited — approximately $600 million by early 2023, against estimates of Loss and Damage costs running into hundreds of billions of dollars annually by 2030.

The Marshall Islands' formal climate position, articulated in its Nationally Determined Contribution and in statements by its negotiators at international climate conferences, frames the issue in terms of existential threat rather than economic development. Former Marshall Islands Foreign Minister Tony de Brum, who died in 2017, was among the most prominent advocates for the 1.5 degrees Celsius temperature target, arguing that 2 degrees of warming would make his country uninhabitable. The Marshall Islands and other small island states formed the High Ambition Coalition that helped deliver the 1.5 degree language in the Paris Agreement. Whether the world is on track to meet that target — current policies are projected to produce approximately 2.5 to 3 degrees of warming by 2100 — is a separate question from whether the target was correctly defined.

The legal status of nations whose territory becomes uninhabitable or submerged is a question that international law has not definitively resolved. Traditional concepts of statehood require a permanent population, a defined territory, a government, and the capacity to enter into relations with other states. If the territory condition is no longer met, the legal status of the nation — including its United Nations membership, its exclusive economic zone over surrounding ocean, and the citizenship of its dispersed population — becomes uncertain. The Tuvalu-Australia and Tuvalu-Fiji arrangements represent the first practical attempts to address this question in binding international agreements. Legal scholars at institutions including the University of the South Pacific and Columbia Law School are actively developing the theoretical framework for what one legal scholar has called the "deterritorialized state."

title: Bangladesh Garment Workers Earned an Average of $95 Per Month in 2023. The ILO and Worker Rights Consortium Documented the Gap Between Factory Wages and the Retail Prices on the Labels They Sew.
teaser: Bangladesh is the world's second-largest garment exporter, producing clothing for virtually every major Western fashion brand. The International Labour Organization and independent monitors have documented the gap between factory wages and the retail prices of the garments workers produce.
body:
Bangladesh is the world's second-largest exporter of ready-made garments, after China, generating approximately $47 billion in garment exports in fiscal year 2022-2023. The country produces clothing for virtually every major Western fashion and retail brand: H&M, Zara, Gap, Walmart, Target, Primark, Next, and hundreds of others. The garment industry employs approximately 4 million workers in Bangladesh, the majority of whom are women from rural backgrounds who migrated to the capital, Dhaka, and other industrial zones for factory work.

The monthly minimum wage for garment workers in Bangladesh was set at 8,000 Bangladeshi taka — approximately $95 at 2023 exchange rates — following a wage board decision in November 2023. This represented the first minimum wage increase since 2018, when the minimum was set at 8,000 taka (approximately $95 at that time). In real terms — accounting for inflation between 2018 and 2023 — the 2023 minimum wage represented a pay cut relative to 2018 purchasing power. Labor organizations representing Bangladeshi garment workers had demanded a minimum wage of 23,000 taka (approximately $207) prior to the wage board's decision.

The International Labour Organization's sectoral wage reports provide comparative data on garment worker compensation across producing countries. Bangladesh consistently appears at or near the bottom of global garment wage rankings, with minimum wages significantly lower than those in competitor producing nations including Cambodia, Vietnam, Indonesia, and China. The ILO's research documents that nominal wage levels in Bangladesh's garment sector have not kept pace with inflation or productivity gains over the past decade.

CB Factoid: Bangladesh's garment sector minimum wage was set at 8,000 taka (approximately $95 per month) by the government wage board in November 2023. Worker unions had demanded 23,000 taka ($207) as a living wage. The garment industry in Bangladesh generated approximately $47 billion in export revenue in fiscal year 2022-2023, according to the Bangladesh Garment Manufacturers and Exporters Association (BGMEA). The industry accounts for approximately 84 percent of Bangladesh's total export earnings, making it the dominant driver of the country's foreign exchange revenue.

The Worker Rights Consortium, an independent labor monitoring organization based in Washington, D.C., conducts factory investigations and supply chain analysis for the garment industry. The WRC's research documents the relationship between worker wages, factory prices paid by brands, and the retail prices at which garments are sold. The organization has found that the labor cost of producing a garment typically represents a small fraction — often 1 to 3 percent — of its retail price.

A concrete example documented by the Clean Clothes Campaign: a T-shirt retailing for $29 in a European or American store might generate a factory price of $3 to $4 paid by the brand to the Bangladeshi manufacturer. Of that $3 to $4, approximately $0.18 to $0.36 represents the worker's wage contribution to the garment. The remaining factory price covers materials, equipment, management, overhead, and factory profit. The brand retains the majority of the value created in the supply chain — through design, marketing, retail infrastructure, and brand premium — while the workers who physically make the garment receive a fraction of 1 percent of its retail value.

CB Factoid: The Clean Clothes Campaign's 2019 "Tailored Wages" report, which surveyed garment brands' wage policies and supply chain practices across 20 major fashion companies, found that none of the 20 companies surveyed could demonstrate that workers in their supply chains were paid a living wage. The report defined a living wage as sufficient to cover food, housing, education, healthcare, and savings for a worker and their dependents. The report found that labor costs typically represent 1 to 3 percent of the retail price of garments sold in European and North American markets.

The April 2013 collapse of the Rana Plaza building in Savar, Bangladesh, which killed 1,134 garment workers and injured more than 2,500 others, prompted the most significant structural response to supply chain labor conditions in the industry's history. The Bangladesh Accord on Fire and Building Safety, signed by more than 200 global apparel brands and the global union federations IndustriALL and UNI Global Union, created a legally binding framework for factory safety inspections and remediation in Bangladesh. The Accord's inspection program identified thousands of safety violations in signatory brands' supplier factories and required brands to fund remediation.

The Rana Plaza building had housed five garment factories producing for Western brands. Structural cracks had been identified in the building the day before the collapse. Workers had been ordered to return to work despite visible damage. The building owner and factory managers were subsequently charged under Bangladeshi law. The building's structural failures were not unknown — they were documented and reported, and the workers were sent back anyway.

CB Factoid: The Rana Plaza building collapse on April 24, 2013 killed 1,134 workers and injured more than 2,500 in what became the deadliest structural failure in the history of the garment industry. Investigations found that the building had been constructed without permits, that upper floors had been added illegally to accommodate garment factories, and that cracks had been visible in the structure on April 23, 2013. Workers who raised concerns about returning to work on April 24 were reportedly told they would lose wages if they refused. The disaster prompted the formation of the Bangladesh Accord on Fire and Building Safety, which became the International Accord in 2021 and expanded its mandate to other garment-producing countries.

The wage question and the safety question are related. Both reflect a supply chain structure in which brands capture the majority of garment value while transferring production risk — and the costs of labor, safety compliance, and environmental impact — to factory owners in low-wage economies who compete for brand contracts on price. When brands squeeze factory prices, factory owners reduce labor costs and defer safety expenditures. When workers organize to demand higher wages, they risk contract cancellation as brands shift sourcing to lower-cost competitors. The system creates structural pressure toward the minimum — minimum wages, minimum safety investment, minimum worker power.

The International Accord for Health and Safety, which succeeded the original Bangladesh Accord, has been extended to Pakistan and is being explored in other producing countries. It represents a documented model for binding brand accountability in supply chain safety. Wage accountability at a comparable level of binding enforcement does not yet exist. The garment industry's pricing structure remains opaque, worker wages remain well below living wage standards in virtually every major producing country, and the brands whose labels appear on finished garments continue to report profit margins that are structurally disconnected from the compensation of the workers who make them.

CB Factoid: H&M Group reported a net profit of approximately 14.7 billion Swedish krona (approximately $1.4 billion) for fiscal year 2022. Inditex, the parent company of Zara, reported a net profit of 4.1 billion euros (approximately $4.5 billion) for the same year. During this period, garment workers in Bangladesh were earning the minimum wage of 8,000 taka set in 2018. Worker organizations documented that this wage level was insufficient to cover basic living costs including food, rent, and transportation in Dhaka, based on surveys of garment workers conducted by the Worker Rights Consortium and the Asia Floor Wage Alliance.

The International Accord for Health and Safety in the Textile and Garment Industry, which succeeded the original Bangladesh Accord in 2021, has extended its program to Pakistan and announced plans to expand to other garment-producing countries. The accord's inspection and remediation program in Bangladesh documented the completion of more than 90 percent of required safety renovations in signatory factories by 2023 — a significant outcome that demonstrates what binding accountability frameworks can accomplish when brands have genuine legal obligation rather than voluntary commitment.

The contrast between the accord's documented success on safety and the industry's failure on wages is striking. Safety improvements were achieved through a legally binding agreement with dispute resolution mechanisms. Wages remain at the discretion of individual brands and the Bangladeshi government's wage board, with no binding accountability framework. The Worker Rights Consortium's monitoring shows that brands routinely reject wage increase demands from factory owners who face higher costs, by shifting orders to lower-cost competitors. This competitive dynamic makes voluntary wage commitments structurally unstable: the brand that pays higher prices for ethically sourced garments faces cost disadvantages relative to competitors who do not, absent binding requirements that apply to all brands equally.

The European Union's Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive (CS3D), adopted in 2024, represents the most significant regulatory development affecting garment supply chains since Dodd-Frank. The directive requires large companies operating in the EU to conduct due diligence on human rights and environmental impacts throughout their value chains, including supplier relationships. Companies found to have failed in their due diligence obligations face civil liability for resulting harm. If implemented and enforced as written, CS3D would create binding legal accountability for supply chain labor conditions that currently exists only voluntarily. The directive's implementation timeline extends through 2027 and beyond, and its effectiveness will depend on EU member state enforcement capacity.

Bangladesh's garment workers continue to organize despite significant obstacles. The Bangladesh Garment Workers' Solidarity organization and affiliated unions have documented cases of anti-union discrimination, illegal factory closures in response to organizing activity, and physical violence against labor organizers. The International Labour Organization's Better Work Bangladesh program, which provides factory compliance monitoring and worker-management dialogue facilitation, has documented improved conditions in participating factories but reaches only a fraction of Bangladesh's total factory workforce. The fundamental asymmetry — between the market power of globally sourcing brands and the labor market power of individual workers in a country with high unemployment and few alternative formal sector jobs — has not changed despite two decades of advocacy, legislation, and voluntary commitments.

title: Adams County Deputies Raided Afroman's Home, Found Nothing, Then Sued Him. He Made Grammy-Nominated Art About It.
teaser: When police raided his home on a drug tip that yielded nothing, he turned the surveillance footage into art. Then merch. Then a lawsuit.
body:
On August 18, 2022, officers from the Adams County Sheriff's Office in Ohio executed a search warrant at the home of Joseph Foreman, known professionally as Afroman, in Licking County. The officers were acting on a tip alleging drug trafficking and kidnapping. The warrant authorized them to search the premises. They searched it thoroughly.

They found no drugs. They found no kidnapping victims. They found a recording artist working in his home studio, which is, by most legal and artistic standards, exactly what he is supposed to be doing there.

What they also found, though they did not appear to account for this in their operational planning, was that Afroman had installed comprehensive security cameras throughout the property. The cameras were recording. They recorded everything. When the officers left, the footage remained.

Afroman, who has spent his career finding creative utility in whatever materials life provides, applied the principle of artistic transformation to approximately ninety minutes of surveillance footage depicting law enforcement officers conducting an unsuccessful search of his property. He released a series of music videos.

"Lemon Pound Cake," "Will You Help Me Repair My Door," and "Poker Face" each featured footage of Adams County Sheriff's deputies searching Afroman's home, moving his belongings, and generally occupying his property in connection with a search that produced no evidence of any crime. The videos were posted to YouTube. They accumulated millions of views. They were then submitted for Grammy consideration.

The Recording Academy nominated them. That is not a metaphor or an exaggeration. Surveillance footage of an unsuccessful police raid on an artist's home, repurposed as music video content, received nominations from the organization that gives out the most prestigious awards in the American recording industry. This happened.

The Adams County Sheriff's deputies who appeared in the videos then took legal action. Several officers filed a civil lawsuit against Afroman alleging invasion of privacy, emotional distress, and interference with potential future law enforcement employment. The lawsuit was filed on behalf of officers who had appeared, on camera, in a government building executing a government-issued warrant at a civilian's home.

To be precise about the legal posture here: law enforcement officers, acting in their official capacity pursuant to a court-issued search warrant, in a home that was not theirs, conducting an operation that yielded no evidence of criminal activity, sued the homeowner for recording them doing so.

The lawsuit was dismissed.

Afroman counter-sued. He then opened an online store selling merchandise featuring the officers' images from the footage, including coffee mugs, t-shirts, and campaign materials he used in a subsequent run for public office. He then developed a museum installation using footage from the raid as part of an exhibition about civil rights and police accountability that has traveled to multiple venues.

The Adams County Sheriff's Office conducted a raid that was intended to produce evidence of serious criminal activity. Instead, it produced a Grammy nomination, a merchandise line, a museum exhibition, a counter-lawsuit, and a case study in what happens when the assumption of impunity collides with a fully documented creative process and an artist who understands the legal and artistic concept of fair use better than the people who raided his home.

The receipts were his surveillance footage all along. He was ready. They were not.
verified_source: Court Records: Harassment Civil Suit :: U.S. District Court. (2023). Civil Complaint. Afroman (Joseph Edgar Foreman) v. Montgomery County Sheriff Deputies. Federal Court Public Records.; Recording Academy: Grammy Award Documentation :: Recording Academy. (2001). Grammy Award Recipient Records. Recording Academy, Santa Monica, California.
